If you are happy with just having the most updated results, you can stop here. But if you want to go the extra mile and also automate the distribution of your processing results, grab a coffee and read through to the last step.

I decided to use powershell to email my results with outlook to other coworkers. Here is how:

Open up PowerShell ISE from windows search.

The following script is an example for my case, which is to mail the results separately to all recipients with a body and a subject line. You can modify your script to suit your needs.

$OL = New-Object -ComObject outlook.application

Start-Sleep 5

$recipients = @(“recipient_one@company.de”,”recipient_two@company.de”,”recipient_three@company.de”)

foreach ($recipient in $recipients){

$mItem = $OL.CreateItem(“olMailItem”)

$mItem.To = $recipient

$mItem.Subject = “Automatic Email: Weekly Report”

$mItem.Body = “This is an automated email sent using powershell. In case of any questions please contact Me. Dieses Dokument enthält… “

$mItem.Attachments.Add(“\This\is\where\my\python\output\lives\file_to_email.xlsx”)

$mItem.Send()

}

Step 5. Set up Task Scheduler to email your results to the recipients

Similar to step 3, you will now schedule your powershell script to execute every week soon after your python script is executed.

Go to New Task in your task scheduler

Name and describe your task

Go To trigger and set up your schedule

Go to action. In the program/script bar, enter the address to your PowerShell application. It is usually here:

C:\Windows\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\powershell.exe

In the argument bar, enter the path to your .ps1 script written earlier.

Voila! You sent an email without lifting a finger!

So to summarize, you automated an entire chain of events starting from receiving an excel file, saving it to a location, processing it with python and sending the results in an email. This is a pretty neat hack that saves me a bunch of time at work. Hopefully, it helps you all too!

title: Short Story: Life has meaning
content:
Short Story: Life has meaning

Little Rhyme’s way home.

Rhyme felt safer alone. Though other kids invited her to play, she was suspicious. They only want to fill a role in the game, they didn’t want her. They were all 8 years old, but somehow everyone else in the class was too pretty and too smart for Rhyme.

She perched on the long steps of the school Auditorium and waited for the Physical Education class to be over.“Nobody will even notice that I am here.”She hoped.

Even the teachers knew she was weird. She talked to nobody and sat where she couldn’t be seen. Her disheveled hair cut way too short made her look like a little boy in a skirt.

Many students and even a few teachers found her revolting.Why doesn’t she answer the simplest questions, why is her shirt so yellow, why is she so fat?Rhyme just accepted it all and stayed out of their way if she could.

That Tuesday the science class ended 20 minutes too early. Rhyme gathered her books into her bag and rushed to the school bus. This would be the day she gets to sit by the window and watch the road pass by.

She hated sitting on the aisle. The other kids talked over her as if she were invisible. But at the window, she could ignore everyone and look outside. The only way to get to a window was to get there first. Today, finally, Rhyme made it.

She clung to the seat and avoided all temptation to leave it even for a second. The peanut seller, ice cream cart, even the urge to take a closer look at something shiny on the ground. Rhyme stay put. The only teacher who treated her kindly passed by, but Rhyme didn’t rush to wave goodbye.Tomorrow, she told herself. Today was for the window. She shall not budge.

The bus slowly filled up with kids emerging from their classrooms. Extremely proud of her little achievement, Rhyme waited for the sound of the engine starting. Maybe some kid would even notice and make friends with her?

“Hey, would you like to trade your seat for this bar of chocolate?”,the popular kid. Rhyme was squeamish. She didn’t know how to say no. Or what it was like to have something that someone wanted. Though they traveled in the same bus for years now, this was the first time this kid spoke to her.

“Oh, you can have the seat after we go halfway home. No need to trade your chocolate, keep it.”Rhyme gloated at her good deed. She would tell mom all about her heroism when her mother comes home that weekend.

The engine revved twice and stopped, a sign that the bus would start soon. The driver always did this to make the last of the kids rush to the bus on time. Among the kids that poured in at the last moment was puny but vigorous one. He was a picture of energy and noise. He rushed in and walked straight up to Rhyme.

“That’s my seat! get up!”Rhyme was shook.What was happening?All color drained from her face. She swallowed and said“But I came here first, a long time ago…”

“Get up, you gross thing! that is where I always sit.”

By now everyone was staring. Rhyme’s eyes were filled to the brim and words were hard to find. She held tightly to her school bag and sunk even more into the seat.“No!”

The puny kid then did the unthinkable. He reached for Rhyme’s arm and dragged her by it. A punch landed on her face. By now Rhyme was on the floor, face down and wailing loudly. She felt terrible, not just for losing the seat but for becoming a spectacle. Some kids laughed and some were quiet. All were thankful they aren’t Rhyme.

She dashed out of the bus and out of the school gates. She couldn’t possibly sit in the bus until she gets home. She made up her mind to walk the 10 kilometers, even if it means she should walk all night.

What seemed like a quick 3 stops in the bus took Rhyme hours. She wouldn’t know how long, she couldn’t yet tell the time.

It was getting darker. Dad would come home soon and notice Rhyme wasn’t home yet. He’d be furious. Maybe he’d beat her up? Rhyme didn’t look forward to making it home before dark.

She was tired. On the busy Hyderabad road, Rhyme sat down on the dirty sidewalk thick with soot from passing vehicles and the thousands of people who walked on it all day.

For once, Rhyme found herself fully alone. No one seemed to care she was there at all. She felt oddly calm. She was going to do this every day.

She didn’t care about the stains on her white shirt. She even loved the dusty and polluted wind in her shabby hair. Nobody was watching her.

If her dad was going to beat her up, it was still hours away. She could walk away in a direction that did not lead home, if she wanted. The adventure in that thought was exhilarating. Finally she was free, even if only in her imagination.

Rhyme was smiling again. She was nearly home, all by herself. She was a hero.

title: Dear People Who Re-read Your Own Articles All the Time…
content:
Dear People Who Re-read Your Own Articles All the Time…

In support of potentially toxic self-love

I love you all.

I know you love you too. So it doesn’t matter what I think, really.

Some call us narcissists, but what is a life where you did not once kiss your mirror? After all, everyone’s lips are tired of hitting the same skin-textured cushy round surfaces time and again.

If our own articles are a drug, we are all junkies. (If it is someone else’s work we also offer services as constructive critics.)

We all put “avid reader” on our resumes. Little do they know.

Burn that Custard to the ground who says our work needs work

Actually don’t burn anything, Just say — “**smiley** Thank you for your feedback *smiley*, I’ll think about it the next time I write *smiley* *smiley*.

On second thought, make this the welcome note on your voice mailbox.

If someone calls you out while you passionately lick your furry paws and bite lovingly on your own back, here are some templates you can use.

“You get paid THAT MUCH for just writing English?”,Actually no. That covers the time I spend on reading, re-reading, standing back and admiring, reaching out and kissing my article, and then wiping my spittle off the laptop screen. And I charge by the minute, you see.

“Is writing a real skill?”.No, it is not. please get off your keyboard and off my comments section. It is not a competition.

“ I have more readers than you do”.Not if you count the number of times I read my own work. I am in the Top 30 under 30 of all writers I read. No way you are going to make that cut.

“Anyone can be a writer”.And also anyone can be struck by a lightning, but just not everyone does.

“Writing isn’t a real job”.Would you rather I were a hitman? That is definitely a real job and I have a special discount for the bounty on internet trolls.

“Writing isn’t a real job”.Yeah, sex is cool but did you ever write without getting any red underlining on Ms.Word and Grammarly?

“But you could do that on the side”I would if I didn’t have a naked contempt for those who order ketchup on the side. That just killed all “sides” for me.

“That is just toxic self-love”.Yes. It is. Stand back because it is also highly inflammable and a potential bio-hazard.

Thanks for putting your mirror down for a few seconds. You may now go back to re-reading your own work.

Bye now.

title: Things I Tell Myself When I’m Not Writing
content:
Things I Tell Myself When I’m Not Writing

I and my procastinosaurus are on the same side

This is research, going through a block is definitely research.

How am I to create great content if I don’t consume great content?

I learn so much about screen-writing from Netflix-watching.

All great writresses (Umm..is that a word? that should be a word) need a glass of wine to get creative.

That wine made me uncreative.

It is natural to have some periods of lull.

I’m not as awesome as that other writer. Maybe I shouldn't try anymore.

Writing for a living is not a great idea. Maybe I should do something else.

No, I should get over there and write!

Nobody cares for real talent anymore. It is all about clickbait. (sigh).

I could write a clickbait.

I don't want to write clickbait.

Maybe a notebook and a pen will help me.

I have to buy a good-looking block to write.

I’ll shop on Amazon for a cute book.

That’s a cute book. But I have too many books, when will I fill them?

Stop shopping and write!

There, this is what I can do today.18 is better than nothing.

Hope your writing process is going well. Kick the demons and shut the other tab. Bury the self-doubt and run out of excuses. Get in there and write!

title: Every Writing Advice You Ever Got
content:
Every Writing Advice You Ever Got

And what it really means…

This is what you see

This is what you should read

Choose a catchy title

You’ve got 3 seconds to make your pitch. Go!

Use pictures to boost your post

This will take 1 second off your 3-second pitch. Trust me, there is no better way for that second to die.

I made 10000000000$ on Medium last month

I will make another 100 off your reading this.

Anybody can write

Come to me thou wretched rag, ye shall be lead to riches.

Provide useful information

Please don’t turn this into Facebook, I love this place.

Keep it short

Do not do unto others what you don’t want done unto you.

Do not write for money

Do not, I repeat,DO NOTquit your day job. Expect like 47 cents on an average.

Choose a suitable publication

Do you have 1000000 friends? No? didn’t think so. They do.

Although other posts might tell you…

This is NOT one of the other posts. This is the Truth. I cannot stress this enough. (So did all the other posts, but THIS is the one)

Write for what you really care about

People on the internet have subconscious hogwash detectors. #Truestory.

No, write for your audience

Is there a non-hogwash topic that you love and people love too? That’s the sweet spot.

Be honest

Of course, you love your family, you love your job and you have a great body. There is Instagram for this. Add to it a tad bit of honesty, and BAM, great blog post!

Make your presence known on the platform

I learned this on instagram. I’m going to spread it on Medium. Like for Like, Follow for Follow is the unspoken truth (I mean, it might work!)

What writers can learn from house-elves and Burger-King

That title is not on me. You wouldn’t have clicked otherwise.

Although my country is not a part of the Partner Program…

At this point, I’m talking to the Medium guys, you just move on to #5 in the 8 things that was going to make you a good writer

title: Reference
content:
Reference

So there are these kind of polarized issues in social science. And the one that I touch on inBlink— the polarized issue I touch on there — is probably the race stuff, the racial prejudice stuff. InOutliersthe polarized stuff I touch on is the IQ stuff. And there are IQ fundamentalists out there. You know like[Harvard psychology professor Steven] Pinker, who in a kind of hostile wayreviewedWhat the Dog Saw. You know, am I ever going to see eye-to-eye with Pinker? No. And at a certain point I’m bringing my own values and perspectives to bear on my interpretations of this literature. And I am placing my bets with the[University of Michigan professor Richard] Nisbettsof the world over the Pinkers of the world. And I feel comfortable doing that. And if you are a die-hard IQ fundamentalist, you’re not going to like that part of my book, and I’m fine with that. You know, I don’t pretend that this is…. There’s always points in social science, or in any kind of science — no, not any kind of science — but particularly in social science where you take a stand on the basis of your values.

HERE THE FULL INTERVIEW

I think what they’re doing is they are plugging this new knowledge into their existing explanatory system. So they already have an explanatory system that has many variables, that has a kind of lay-notion of variance attached to it…. So inTipping Point, [there’s] a discussion of crime in New York. I don’t think many readers came away from that thinking, “Oh, crime dropped in New York just because they cleaned the graffiti off the subway.” But I think what people did is they said, “Oh, I need to add that variable into my lay-account of what happened in New York.” I think that’s an appropriate [response]. That’s how they read that. When you tell the story, you’re taking for granted that they have an existing set of variables in their head, and you’re just adding to it.

This is an interesting topic. But it goes to the issue of: What do people do with the things they read in books such as this? And my working assumption is that what they do is they talk about it. The reason people read a book like, books like ours — as opposed to novels — is that books like ours are fodder for continued thought and conversation. And I hear this all the time from readers: “I talk about this with my family, we have arguments about this.” And that makes me feel that it’s OK to do, to tell stories the way we do, because like I said, the experience doesn’t end on the final page. It is feeding into an ongoing conversation that people have about their lives.

I’ve learned that if you tell your story properly, people are very, very open-minded — far more open-minded than I would’ve thought. Or to put it in a more sophisticated way: People are information-rich and theory-poor. If you can give them a way of organizing their experience, then their minds are wide open. Which I would not have not have necessarily thought. And if you can frame questions appropriately you can overcome all kinds of ideological — what you would have thought of — as ideological constraints. So I’ve been continuously surprised. I always thought my book, because I am a political liberal, that my books would have heavily liberal audiences. But in fact they don’t

I’ve learned that if you tell your story properly, people are very, very open-minded — far more open-minded than I would’ve thought. Or to put it in a more sophisticated way: People are information-rich and theory-poor. If you can give them a way of organizing their experience, then their minds are wide open. Which I would not have not have necessarily thought. And if you can frame questions appropriately you can overcome all kinds of ideological — what you would have thought of — as ideological constraints. So I’ve been continuously surprised. I always thought my book, because I am a political liberal, that my books would have heavily liberal audiences. But in fact they don’t….

Ariely:But they are also not very liberal books. Do you read them as being liberal?

Gladwell:I read them as liberal. But this is another case of: How I read my books is irrelevant to how they are read.

Ariely:But it’s interesting that you think it’s the theory, not the data. That I think is fascinating. That it’s about the organizing principles.

Gladwell:It’s about the principles. The books always give people these kind of broader theories, because that’s what they go to first. Then they’ll fill in the gap with the data. But they want some way of reorganizing their view of things. That’s what potent.

title: Seven Fun Books Which Made Me a Better Data Analyst
content:
Seven Fun Books Which Made Me a Better Data Analyst

There is humor in numbers, harness it

As historian Yuval Noah Harari puts it, the TV series Black Mirror brings the reality of AI closer to the general public than would a Turing prize-winning paper by Geoffrey Hinton.

Last week I made a dapper presentation about some key insights into (a secret) of the factory to my boss who has no experience in the field of analytics but in planning how to run a factory.

His face told me “I’m one hundred percent listening, this curve makes so much sense and this presentation is not at all boring”.

Turns out, there is a lot more to the field of analytics than just being really good at math and understanding data. It is important for a data analyst to generalize the results to non-experts in data science makes insights usable by people who have the power to make decisions.

Many ideas for the structure and tone of that presentation came from the books I read in the past couple of years, which made many perspectives of data more accessible.

So here are some educational and entertaining books in Data Science that would tickle every Analyst.(The order doesn't mean anything)

1. How not to be wrong

by Jordan Ellenberg

As compared to the other books on this list, How not be wrong addresses the hidden mathematics of many everyday life statistics sometimes with sound mathematical equations and theorems.

Politics, fear-mongering TV, and “scientific journals” which at first look correct are actually just tools to fool you.

It talks in detail about various methods of measurement of probability and likelihood, terms of comparison, various biases in the interpretation of statistics, the Law of large numbers, and the meaning of numbers brought back from various real-life scenarios.

2. Fooled by randomness

by Naseem Nicolas Taleb

This is a relatively difficult book to read unless you want to earn a lot of money on the stock market. Naseem Taleb makes you and everyone else in the market realize that they are really stupid and that predicting the stock market is a fool's endeavor.

He throws an arrogant but hilarious light on how the everyday world is ridden with randomness and how few things there are that you can really be sure about. Giving copious examples of failures of reliance on randomness and how insignificant a successful prediction might be, Taleb successfully convinces you that you, sir, are an idiot.

3. Factfulness

By Hans Rosling

Factfulness is the project of a man who made it his life’s work. Hans Rosling wrote it while he was on his deathbed. It was LITERALLY the last thing he chose to do before he left the planet.

It points a finger at all the fear-mongering news channels and hate-mongering politics and proves them wrong by showing that numbers, actually say that we are doing quite well as a planet and species.

There is hope. There is in fact not just hope but a million reasons to celebrate. You will learn here how to show the full picture to an audience of statistics so that people can decide whether to be happy or sad about the happenings. On a global scale, turns out it is not that obvious what is “good”.

4. Thinking fast and slow

By Daniel Kahnemann

Thinking fast and slow is a brilliant book on cognitive biases written by noble prize winning economist Daniel Kahnemann.

As an analyst, it is not just enough to know numbers but also what your client would choose given all the options, therefore how to package information not just report it.

It tells a lot about our emotional tendencies as primates which clearly surpass our logic halves the brain. If it is of any consolation, you will learn the next time you make an illogical choice, that you are predisposed by evolution to do so, and may be, if you’re smart enough, will choose otherwise.

5. Everybody Lies

By Seth Stevens Davidowitz

We behave differently when people are watching and when people are not watching. The tone of the book is outright “spit your drink funny!”. Just from the topics Davidowitz chooses to write about, such as brutally honest questions people type into google.

It might reinforce some stereotypes such as whether men or women are more interested in pleasing the opposite sex orally (umm, if you know what I mean), and whether we vote like we say we do, and how different age groups fare in priorities at different stages in life. This book is pure humor masquerading as science.

6. Freakonomics

By Steven Levitt and Stephen Dubner

Levitt is an eccentric economist and Dubner is a witty journalist. Needless to say, Freakonomics is a controversially delightful book. Levitt chooses the most random-arse topics on the planet to apply data science to. Why? In the words of George Mallory,“because it is there”.

The book talks about Economics of the drug dealer money-chain, effects of school with or without laurels on your child’s career, whether the “colors” of names play a role in your future, Sumo wrestlers vs American teachers and other things that you wouldn't dream of in your waking day. If you want to know how to ask the correct questions, then read this.

7. Outliers

By Malcolm Gladwell

Malcolm Gladwell never disappoints. He is a good journalist and a great storyteller. At the end of reading a Gladwell book, you may or may not have learned something new, but you will definitely collect some proof for what you already knew.

He puts layman data analytics on tens of social situations until you can nod your head only in the direction of agreement. It convinces you that you are not good enough but great, and gets your proof with data science from the most random places on earth. If you ask the correct questions, there are answers everywhere. This is what you will learn from the book.

title: Go to Belgium — The Dreamy, Beery, Chocolaty, Romance
content:
Go to Belgium — The Dreamy, Beery, Chocolaty, Romance

Travelogue: A Long Weekend in Brussels (& Brugges)

Resting pretty to the west of Germany, east of France, and south of Amsterdam, Belgium does not garner as much attention as its neighbors. I spent 4 days in this beautiful country, 5 if I count travel.

Belgium was SO satisfying. It gave me one of the dreamiest experiences I’ve had.

I spent two days each in the capital, Brussels, and Bruges (Brügge in German). I’ll tell you about Brussels here, and Brugges later.

Brussels, the mini metropolitan with a rich old town

The goal was to have the most fun without breaking the bank. We arrived at the Brussels airport quite early in the morning.

The train station was directly in the basement of the airport. We were greeted by very friendly train staff ready to help us in any language we like — English, Dutch, French, or German.

I could already tell that this place is a customer-service paradise compared to its German neighbors. I once had airport security in Germany scream at me because I delayed herFeierabend(End of the work shift) by deciding to fly.Ehm..what?

Belgium, needless to say, is a land of waffles, chocolate, and beer. With a couple of hours to go before we could check into our Airbnb we dashed into the first open waffle shop.

We got our first taste of vanilla, Nutella, and strawberry waffles with cream quite easily. Turns out it was a great decision, the shop was bustling with people queuing up like ants for the rest of the day.

Getting around and being tourist in Brussels

We soon checked in and washed up. The first goal is to explore until we drop.

The old town of Brussels is massive and full of tourists. I was keen on exploring the both in and out of the touristy old town.

This city is most definitely an artist’s canvas. Every wall and building had a handsome mural or a clever comic strip. They had a special Magritte art showing in their art museum(Remember Magritte ? Those paintings of guys in hats and suits with hidden faces?). The city was appropriately decorated with Magritte artworks in the most unexpected corners.

After the old town, we went to the beer museum - a chilly respite from the beating heat of the Belgian summer. It cost us only 5 Eur. to enter and they even offered a glass of Beer of our choice!

Here, I had my first Belgian Blond Beer, and the first that evening. I had already developed a taste for their blondes. (Beers of course.)

Keen to see the city outside, we walked a few kilometers across the town and beyond the river to the Town Church. The city square, unlike in many European cities did not have a church, It had the traditional marketplace and king’s palace, but no church.

Compared to most large German cities, Brussels is modern. Public transport is easy and cheap. You only have to scan your card or phone every time you get on a bus, and the rest is taken care of. It cannot cost you more than 7 Eur. a day no matter how much you travel. Even the train-travel to the nearby cities like Ghent, Brugges, or Antwerp had a two-for-one offer.

Easy peasy!

By the end of the day, we had walked about 20 kilometers in all. Though we really wanted to party in the famous Madam Moustache Club and the Belgian Beer Project bar, the previous sleepless night got to us, and we hit the hay by 23:00. Our friend was to join us by morning. We better be awake to let him in.

A little walking tour

On Day 2, we did a Free walking tour. There are hundreds of websites you can book these, and you pay what you like. (About 10–20 Eur. is legit I guess?)

The sun beating down was hard enough, but our anxious tour guide was shouting the history of the town in our faces with the force of a military commander. In the hot sun, this march was not exactly relaxing.

It was cool to get glimpses of the folklore and the history of the city. I often wandered off from my tour group trying subtly to escape our commanding officer’s gaze without being too rude. Unfortunately, I cannot reproduce the city’s history here.

The one thing that stood out for me in the story was how little Belgian Dark History is spoken of compared to its Dark Chocolate. Coming from Germany, where the war and the Holocaust are repeatedly, profusely, and dearly apologized for, the Belgian silence was puzzling. (Google What did Belgium do to the Congolese people).

About Beer, Chocolate, and Comics

An undoubted highlight of the city was Belgian Chocolate. We walked into one of the multitudes of chocolate shops where we could “pick and mix” at 5 Eur for 100 grams.

These were the most delicious bits of chocolate I’ve had, no exaggeration. My favorite is the Belgian Truffle chocolate. They look sticky and are coated with cocoa powder that feels like it’s about to coat your dress a dusty brown the moment you touch them. But no. They melt and disappear without even leaving teeth residue. No wonder people flock from all over the world to this chocolate. It’s flock-worthy chocolate.

A second highlight was the Beer. The Germans are sticklers for “Pure Beer”. No kidding, they have a law of purity when it comes to beer — That it MUST only contain Hops, Malt, Hefe and Water. My favourite is theHelles,or the Bavarian Light beer.

But Belgian Beers. They have no rules. They brew them how they like it, and as strongly as they like it. While most German Beers have about 5% alcohol, maybe 7, the Belgians go to as high as 12% with their double, triple, and quadruple Brews.

They have beers with Lemons, berries, herbs — you name it! (Here is a picture of a Beer-meter, a meter-long tray with every-flavor beers for your taste.)

In Belgium, I did not miss Bavarian beer like I did in the USA, India, or Turkey. Beer was all great, and sometimes even better here. If you happen to go to Brussels, make sure to check out Delirium, the super cool bar nearly in the city center.

A Trip to the Comic Book World

Another cool place to be is the Comic book Museum. You will already get a hint of history when you step into Brussels, because Tintin and The Smurfs Merch is literally in every corner.

Visiting the museum only made me respect comics more. The work that goes into creating and distributing comics, especially at times when digital art was more difficult than today, is no small feat. Plus, who doesn’t love the Smurfs and Tintin?

That’s not all

After two full days in Brussels, we made our way to Bruges in the B-train. Equally impressive, Bruges is older, quieter, and more relaxing.

To keep this article short, I have to make a cut somewhere. But that is not all about Brussels.

In a previous visit, I went to the Royal Museum of Fine Art. This museum is right next to the train station with classical art, Special Display and historical sculptures. The visit mighth ake an entire day. I cannot recommend it enough.

There are multiple museums and bars, which you can visit with a Brussels ticket- a 32 Eur ticket for all you can visit in 24 hours. (But mind you, the museums close at 5 p.m). Since we were there only for 2 days, we skipped the Atomium.

The city is extremely famous for its party scene and clubs, which you should definitely visit.

Also make sure to do the chocolate workshops and museums (Which I visited in Brugge, also a great idea).

If you did visit Brussels, let me know what else you did!

title: Vande Bharat: The South Indian ICE Train Experience. But better.
content:
Vande Bharat: The South Indian ICE Train Experience. But better.

Like the German train, but with 5-star service in under 17€.

The two women blocking the entrance were surprised that we opened the doors by ourselves with a button press.

Somebody had told them they have to be let in by the train-master. I giggled.

This week, the Indian Railway launchedVande Bharat Expresssuper fast train in between Hyderabad and Tirupati, about 600km of distance. (An 8-hour journey as per plan)

We booked one of the first trips on the shiny blue train, strikingly similar in appearance and design to the German Inter City Express, the ICE.

A few moments inside I realized that it isn’t “similar" to the ICE, it is the same, down to the T.

It is the same train Including the color, announcement screens, toilets, even the pattern of the print on the seat covers.

..but wait, it gets better.

The train comes with a steward-service AND snacks, all included in it’s ticket of 1500 rupees (about 17 Eur). This meant coffee, tea, breakfast, lunch and snacks brought to your table without needing to additionally reserve or ask for it!

I thought about the time when the Board Restaurant lady in the Deutsche Bahn was mad at me because I “ordered something difficult (on it’s own friggin menu), so please stand in that corner because such things take time, this isn’t a restaurant, it’s a train”.Apart from that, I have fond memories of high heart rate and 2 AM train station stints from the ever delayed DB trains.

While awed passengers surveyedVandeBharat’snew interiors, the stewardess stood about smiling in the executive coach. (There was a stewardess on an Indian train, Wut!!!!).

The executive coach had movie-theater- style wide cushy seats and more legroom. It costs twice as much as the regular ticket.

“Chengaavi rangu cheera…” a Telugu vintage song to my right.“Aglaawan majboori noo.."aPunjabi love song somewhere up front.Desipassengers all around me are immersed in the world of Instagram reels and YouTube shorts out loud.

Though from the corner of my eye, I see the German ICE universe — blue seats with lighter blue dots; these unmatching sounds shook me back into the reality of a vivacious India.

At one point I couldn’t take it anymore. I screamed, albeit anonymously, “Turn it down! I don’t want to hear it!”

I heard some weak protests from the offenders but my dad’s deep booming voice followed, with severe eye contact — “Turn it down!”.

Only absolute silence thereafter.In India, alpha-ness helps very viscerally.

It was 7 am and about 35 degrees outside. It would go up to 40 later in the day. The AC in the coach was cranked up, and I was shivering.

I sat tight accepting the temperature made for everyone. Just then, an announcement went through saying “AC too cold in cars C1 & C3, please go down a notch".

And it did! I love these desi flexibilities.

I just discovered that I can connect to the WiFi and watch pre-selected movies and music with it. Wow!! (I cannot use it for personal use, only the provided infotainment)

A few moments later came the steward with the day’s newspaper, cardamom- tea, and biscuits.

He returned in another 20 minutes with breakfast (idly, vada, vegetable omelet & veggie cutlet with a side of apple juice). He topped that off with a bag of Indian munchies to save for later. Until lunch, that is. (AllKostenlos ;))

I have never been a fan of copying European and American models into India’s environment without change.

For example, the electric bus that had carried me home from the airport was European, made to survive their deep winters. There was no way to open windows, with all glasses sealed and insulated. But the AC was broken. I nearly threw up from the heat during my 45 min ride.

Another example is modern Indian architecture. The full glass buildings in the city designed for the West suck our limited electricity supplies for mandatory air conditioning. They can turn into killer-green houses in our 40+ degree summers in case of power shortages. Western models DON’T always work for us.

Surely enough, the toilet on Vande Bharat proved me right. Somebody had got everything wet with the bum gun (or whatever the water hose for the butt is called). Everything was wet including the toilet paper.

I remember this video that circulated during the first trips of this train.

A man climbed into the new train wanting to take pictures, while the doors closed behind him. He meant to jump off after it starts and before it picks up speed — the classic way if telling “bye” in India.

He didn’t know that he couldn’t control the doors on this one once the train started.

The baffled train-master told him he was an idiot and the next stop was be 5 hours and a few 100s of kilometers later.

The novelty and culture shock that this train has brought to the country is charming. Unfortunately, it is currently thoroughly patrolled by armed officers because curiousvandals have pelted stones at it to see if the glass exterior cracks.

Like the metro network in Hyderabad, the train has so far surprised me immensely and positively. It made my and my family’s visits to relatives easy and comfortable at an affordable price.

The Indian government way is often characterized by an exuberant start and gloomy upkeep until things fall apart. Nothing would scream “progress” more to me, than if the train remains this way in a year. If it does, it truly is a segue to India’s future of easy transport.

PS: I took the Vande Bharat after 3 weeks again. This time, I booked the executive coach, which cost twice as much. The seats were uncomfortable — the headrest too high for where the average Indian head rests when it sits, and the chairs were uncomfortable to sleep in. The rest was the same. I recommend booking the “regular" seats.

If you enjoyed this article you might also like this one:

Join me on Medium for all you can read and write. It costs 5$ a month and changed my life.

title: Indian Mantra — Food for soul
content:
Indian Mantra 2 — Food for soul

Your body is a temple, and the food you eat makes up the temple you live in. Ancient Indian wisdom tells us that the body is balanced by seven energy-centers, in the form of energy disks or wheels, called Chakras. Each chakra is responsible for a different aspect of your physical and mental wellbeing. When the seven Chakras are balanced, your soul is in balance. These are the Chakras depicted in our Logo.

At Indian Mantra, we offer food not just for your body, but your soul.

The Chakras

The root chakra, or Muladhara located at the base of your spine, is responsible for your sense of security and stability.The sacral chakra, or Svadhisthana, is a chakra for your creative energy.

The solar plexus chakra, or Manipura, stands for your confidence and self esteem. The heart chakra, or Anahata, stands for your ability to love and show compassion.

The throat chakra, or Vishuddha, is for your ability to articulate and communicate. The third eye chakra, or Ajna, is located between your eyes. It is responsible for your imagination and intuition.

The crown chakra, or Sahasrara, is located at the top of your head. It stands for your role in the universe and life’s greater purpose.

Indian Mantra’s goal is to bring harmony to your body and soul through our food. The spices we use in the Indian kitchen are said to have healing properties and find medicinal use in ancient Indian medicine called Ayurveda. At Indian Mantra, we use authentic recipes and source our spices right from their places of origin.

title: Pro Tip: Nobody Buys Writing for the Writing of It
content:
Pro Tip: Nobody Buys Writing for the Writing of It

So here is what to sell instead

Buying writing is not different from buying anything else. To help you understand what a buyer looks for in your writing, think about the last time you bought a piece of cake.

Step 0: You wanted a cake.

You imaginedthat spongy, juicy, sweet something that would melt the moment some tongue-juice lays its hands on it. Your brain led you by tongue to the bakery.

You wentto the store. You looked in the glass.

You sawthat smooth, bald, creamy icing work on layers of oozy sponge, cut through the cross-section, separated by thin chocolate-vanilla cream layers.

You pointedand said “That one”.

Lesson 1

Nobody, EVER, went to the store for cake thinking about icing.

The icing, dear writer, is your writing.

The raw skill, with which you embellish information, which makes your reader’s eyes bounce on top of intelligently used words, freshly cut sentences, and delightfully simple explanations.

Of course, it sells your work. Of course, it defines you to the world.

Your writing is the photo they are going to use for the poster on the railway station which says “Wanted, criminal writer for illegal levels of awesome”. Writing skill is important.

BUT.

But, If your reader wanted only to pick something that looked delicious, without anticipating what is underneath, he might just as well have bought soap.

He needs something to dig his teeth into. He needs an anchor called chocolate or vanilla or strawberry cream to toss around in his mouth on the way to the baker.

Lesson 2

Your cake needs a heart. The icing is not it.

When people click on your work, more often than not is for the juicy, invisible sponge — the heart of the matter, the thing he is going to remember in 5 years from your text, the story he is going to tell his wife that night before going to bed, the thing he is going to talk about in cocktail parties to look smart.

What you write about, is why you write. Your writing itself, on the other hand, is the bread you hide your dog-pills in. Don’t believe me? Think about this:

If you had to choose one word for what makes the following authors bestselling:

Malcolm Gladwell — Extensive Research

Steven King — Understanding of Human fears

Agatha Christe — Twisted Plots

Dan Brown— Geographical and historical realism

Yuval Noah Harari — Objective Perspectives on facts

I can go on, but every bestselling author has probably developed a measurable, fact-based, knowledge databank (physical or otherwise) and worked story-telling into the mix. The heart of every piece of the writing is this database.

The enormous, copious, genius, collection of simple facts.

This is what you need before you go write that best-performing article.

Lesson 3

Nobody orders cake from a restaurant that sells cakes, pizza, shampoo, and Indian food.

Niche.

Get a niche. There are multiple reasons this makes sense.

It is why employers ask for 3 years of experience before hiring a beginner. Research needs time. Developing a niche needs time. The more time you take, the deeper your knowledge.

Agatha Christe did not write love stories. She probably would have done a brilliant job if she did, but we only live for so long.

Raw writing skill needs time too. But that’s the thing you can “pick-up on the job”, or from reading other niche writer’s works. If you want to be a writer, you are probably a little better than the global average anyway.

Spend a couple of years canoodling with different topics. Dabble, philander, flirt. But at some point, Marry. Settle down.

Of course, there was Shakespeare, The maninventedmany words. There are poets, songwriters, and the like who are wildly famous for their way with words in their pure form. Believe it or not, it is a side effect.

It could have gone this way or that. For them, the Shakespearean lingo stuck and the plot of King-Lear disappeared to the bottom of the page.

Nevertheless, if King Lear or Julius Ceaser received tomatoes and rotten eggs on the dias in their time, his linguistic extravagance would never have reached us. It was the plot, it was always the plot.

title: 5 Massively Overrated Bestsellers
content:
5 Massively Overrated Bestsellers

Books that were not worth it.

I read anywhere between 30 and 50 books a year. I try to review them all on Goodreads. The average of all ratings I gave on Goodreads is 4.22 out of 5. This is to say I am not one of those internet trolls screaming indirectly “It is unfair that you did something which I didn’t, I should be more famous”.

The books I don’t enjoy reading, I would still rate high if they have a new thought, perspective, or interesting research.

If a book is a literary disaster but offers a nice perspective, I would still rate it well. For example, a lot of information in “Astrophysics for People in a Hurry” could have been cleaned up better, but I love it anyway.

Despite all that, there are books I thought were just a number up on my Goodreads list, and nothing more. Here they are.

5. The Internet Is a Playground: 80,000 Copies Sold in the First Month of Release

The author of this book David Thorne was shot to fame when he tried to pay his bank dues with a hilarious cartoon of a 5-legged spider attached to his email. The internet went wild. Thorne wrote books. The internet is a playground became a thing.

The book is mildly funny if any at all. As you go through pages, you wonder if many people were rudely inconvenienced just to make material for the book.

While some instances described are funny and harmless, most jokes are on unsuspecting office secretaries, bank tellers, and landlords who were just doing their job.

I felt sorry for them. I returned the book.

4. The Beauty Myth: Sunday Times Bestseller

I grit my teeth and try to keep going as I read the statistics from the bestsellerThe Beauty Myth,a book on feminism by Naomi Wolf.

That is not how math works!

A simple google search told me that I am not the only one to realize these details. Thank heavens. There are papers and explanations likethis oneto debunk her made-up facts.

I later learned that her recent bookOutragesis also under fire for false information.

Another aspect of The Beauty Myth I had to cringe through is herconspiracy-theoristview of the world where she imagines a group of men sitting in a room control every law being made who make sure to level it against women. I simply don’t think that is how the world works.

Save yourself some time and don’t read The Beauty Myth.

3. The Why Cafe: Bestseller in Singapore, Taiwan, and in Germany for 3 years straight.

I read the German version of this book,Das Cafe am Rande der Welt.

It is intended to be a “moral compass” to determine what you want from life and how to get there.

We all have questioned our own motives and we have tried to streamline our lives to make ourselves “truly happy”. For that relatability, I acknowledge that the thought behind the book is mighty. When it comes to details the book is little more than a fable for a primary schooler.

Everyone with a morsel of self-awareness immediately sees that the philosophy here is extremely shallow. It doesn’t bend its back and delve into the complexity of life and mind. It ignores all of life’s greater dilemmas, struggles, values and states platitudes like “Do only what you like”.

Not a recommended read if you are over 13.

2. This Is Water: Times Magazine’s Best Speech at a Commencement

This is water was suggested byJames Clear,the author ofAtomic Habitson his website as the “Top 10 non-fiction to read”. It is a high-school graduation speech by novelist David Wallace. It is around 130 pages long.

Since it was originally written as a speech, I cannot judge the book harshly on thought-complexity.

Had I listened to it as a speech I might have enjoyed it but I couldn’t read it as a book for the utterly palpable lack of complexity. Although one must admit, more complexity can be delivered in the text as compared to in a speech.

Time magazine credited this speech to be one of the best commencement speeches ever delivered. It talks about empathy and small gestures of kindness to the world around you — A well-intended message delivered with sheer over-simplification of life.

If you are not looking to learn speech writing, this book is not it. Pick up something else.

1. Tuesdays with Morrie: 14 Million Copies Sold.

The theme of this book is extremely moving but Mitch Albom didn’t make me cry.

It was a dry chronicle of Mitch’s meetings with his dying college professor Morrie. Morrie had only a few months before cancer got to him.

Mushy as the theme is, any intellectual ruminations about the meaning of life have been left out. After the last page, you still are left hanging.

Morrie focuses on human interactions, relationships, and love as central to a meaningful life. The narration of the same has been done in a very “Dora-the-explorer” fashion and did not touch on the gravity of the situation.

If you are looking for insights on the true meaning of life, I’d suggest that you rather read Paul Kalanithi’s “When Breath Becomes Air”.

Meet me,Sruthi Korlakuntafor more such stories.

Stay in touch throughLinkedInorMedium. Write me a note, it would be awesome to meet you!

title: Subtle Curry Arranged Marriages: Germany Edition
content:
Subtle Curry Arranged Marriages: Germany Edition

Part 1: Boys on fixing matches and finding love.

For non-curries reading this

For many Indians and their culture-twins from the subcontinent, the question ofarranged marriagelooms in their later 20s.

Unlike in the western world, in the subcontinent, the idea of “letting life take its course”is often regarded as an unwise solution to the problem of marriage. Hence in many cases, the process is meticulously arranged — through acquaintances, self-certified matchmakers, priests, or websites.

Matrimonial websites have databanks that let you filter for education, income, skin color, likes and dislikes, and caste, among larger life goals. Every year, 10% of a billion people search in the pool for their better halves.

Meanwhile in Deutschland

Young emigrants in Germany look at a major challenge after graduation. The ordeal of arranging a marriagewhile being away from home, where the pool is larger.

One interviewee (not among those featured here) mentioned that the German matrimony websites are skewed towards the brides, with one bride for every three grooms who are registered on these platforms.

A few men were of the opinion that ladies increasingly imagine a fairy-tale love story for themselves, making the process of arranged marriage harder for the boys.

Furthermore, the 3-second interval in which boys have to make a woman swipe right is building up the pressure. One candidate found the “trick” for an attractive image — a lens of 15 mm diameter and an aperture ratio of 1.2 for his profile pictures.

Fighting challenges of getting enough right-swipes, spending years with the “wrong profile picture”, having to make lively conversations, the boys are exploring new ways of wooing the ladies both on and offline.

Do women overwhelmingly prefer to fly towards the west, mostly to the United States? Are boys in Germany at a loss?

I asked people from different parts of India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh (in part 2) the following questions.

Here is the full story.

1. Why do/did you want to marry?

2. How do you feel about marrying someone you don’t know?

3. What are your Matrimony profile filters for the other?

4. What questions do you ask to choose “the one”? What are your No-gos?

5. The question about dowry

Some states of India have a culture of dowry. The family of the woman “gifts” the to-be-wed couple valuables such as real estate, automobile, gold, or other precious property, called dowry.

6. Are you enjoying the process? Why/Why not?

7. Have you had any weird experiences on the way?

8. Do you have advice for those just starting the search?

Subtle Curry, in the name of this article, is a series of hilarious pages on Facebook where about 1 million young Indians share memes, referring to Indian idiosyncracies as “curry culture”.

It is not intended as an offense, it is a bunch of people not afraid to laugh at themselves.

Those were the stories of 3 boys who are and have been in the process of finding a suitable girl in the traditional way.

I leave it to the reader to understand the world from a foreign perspective without harsh judgment.

To see what some ladies are saying, watch out for Part 2, coming soon!

Follow meMediumor write me a note anywhere you find me, it would be awesome to meet you!

Here is my social, I amSruthi Korlakunta.
