title: How to Win Friends in 2020
content:
How to Win Friends in 2020

In a world where everyone is lonely, it is an important question

There was a time when I was worried about why I feel like the odd one out in every group that I go out with. At first I thought that it might have something to do with them.

A bad friend will quickly tell you here that“They’re jealous of you”.Or that“They are just not smart enough to see your point!”.I even read this book very cheesily titled “How to win friends and influence people”.

Back then, it felt like a ridiculous book, but now after living through the past couple of years in a new country with few contacts, I see that there is some truth to it.

After a lot of intro and extrospection, it was clear to me that this is not my problem alone. Regardless of how many friends one has, how often they seem to appear at parties, and how socially put-together one is, many people have trouble with having a fulfilling social life.

Many others keep hanging with the same people they cannot seem to stop complaining about. A lot of relationships are Instagram-smiles-only.

Nevertheless, there are ways to change this. After all, each one of us wants and seeks the same comfort and respect. There are millions of good people who are willing to trust and invest in good social connections.

Here are a couple of things I learned from some of my peers and some valuable literature which made me more comfortable in my shoes and surroundings.

Acceptance

I grew up very conservative, with other conservative friends like mine. Ever so often, I was told that I “talk too much for a girl”. For a long time in my life, I constantly questioned myself if I'm “too active for a girl” and felt ashamed of most things I said.

A lot of pressure comes from “meta” questioning such as —“Is my laugh too loud”, “does this person find me ugly”, “Is my dress too slutty for the occasion”, “Did that sound very stupid”, “Am I being taken for a know-it-all”.

The moment such a question enters your mind, all focus turns to observing the situation and analyzing your behavior while holding you back from living in the moment. If you think you are or you said something you aren't very happy with, move on and don't repeat it. Whatever you do, live outside of your head, don't bury yourself inside of it.Accept yourself before anything and anyone else.

Appreciate the complexity of others

Fatma is a childhood friend of mine who moved to a different country a decade ago. We were really good friends and we continue to be so to date, despite the distance. When I take a step back and think if we would be friends had we met today, probably not. The way we grew apart, this would not be possible.

In childhood when you make fewer assumptions, making friends with someone totally unlike you is not impossible. As we become adults, we become more complex. There is a lot of life experience to justify what we do and how we react to every little stimulus. But when we meet another person and find something “off-putting”, “disagreeable”, we simplify the entire person to this opinion or thought.

We are quick to appreciate our own complexity but we are quicker to ignore that of others. This causes us to be judgemental and assume a moral, intellectual, or societal “higher” ground than the other person. This assumption makes you distance yourself from what could be a potential friendship.

Commit less, but Commit

I come from a culture where everybody is invited to everything. Birthday parties, Housewarming, Weddings. We people in the east don't restrict our guest lists. Hardly any events are “invitation only”. There is always room for one more. Then I moved to Germany, where even a game in the park are invite-only. This “closed-circle” comes from a fear of commitment to social contact.

A major hindrance to building your social life is fear of commitment. You might be afraid that you cannot spare enough time for this contact. That too much might be expected of you. The truth is, most people are happy to have people who just say “hello” once in a while without wanting to go away on a holiday for a week.

People need you usually only as much as you need them. If fear of commitment is what is stopping you, commit only as little as an hour every month (if that is what you can spare) maybe just get lunch together with someone. You hold the remote control for your time.

Watch your words

I have a friend who grew up in a very negative family atmosphere. Big-hearted as she is, it is impossible to sit through a conversation with her without trying really hard not to be offended. We all form a judgment about a topic or a person almost reflexively. You cannot control your judgment, but you can choose how to react to it.

Try starting your sentence with affirmative or positive adjectives. Find the one most positive thing in whatever was just said, and then build your conversation on this. Regardless of whether the other person says “I like Michael Jackson” or “I love the taste of rubber shoes”, don't shun him or her and make them feel belittled. Everyone has had their journey and reasons, If you cannot acknowledge this, there is no prospect for a good social life. Just so you know, literally everyone you meet is going to say something you don't like.

If you can implement even one of these four suggested changes, the odds are that your social life will be more fulfilling than it is already. This is not to say that you have to be someone else or manipulate people or even that you should always agree. It is possible to be positive and friendly without losing your integrity. That, is the real sport of public relations.

title: Barbie — Satisfactory To social media But Plastic to Your Intellect
content:
Barbie — Satisfactory To Social Media But Plastic to Your Intellect

Its still a very good-looking and entertaining movie to watch. (No Spoilers)

The Magnum Opus in Pink is now alive, and I went to the preview yesterday. Barbie, whatever it was, wasn’t dissatisfactory to the eye.

I walked into the theater with complimentary champagne and no expectations. After all, I wasn’t the Barbie type while growing up. But the movie immediately had my attention.

Beautiful art, but they made just two pieces of it.

A world of pink, where everything is cute. Witty and accurate to the Barbie stereotype. A Malibu Mansion, cute plastic cars, the plastic beach, and the near-plastic Ryan and Margot, what a treat!

As the movie progressed, I was hoping to see more of that world, trying to stretch my imagination beyond the same 3 plastic sets. But all there was was the mansion, the plastic beach, and the long plastic road to the real world. Sigh. So much wasted potential.

A Deep Premise with a shallow foundation

The premise — The doll has an existential crisis and breaks through to the outside world to resolve her pain ( already knew this from the trailer). The idea is promising and I was curious to see how it develops.

Alas, the dialogue writers could not grow beyond over-simplified platitudes. “Why isn’t everything perfect” and “Men are dumb, but they have it easy and think horses are awesome”. At the end of the movie, I was left wanting.

I have no issue with “Naive feminism” being the point of the movie. However, It was uncomfortable to watch the shallow dialogue and silly corporate men carrying pink batons who couldn’t even jump over a turnstile.

Only after I watched the movie to the end did I realize that it was actually aimed at 12-year-olds. That is the only explanation.

Clearly, the main plot of the movie wasn’t meaty. There were a few side tracks that do not really support the point of the movie — A mother-daughter pair (America Ferrera played the mother) with a strained relationship, who then become besties by the end.

The most convincing portion of the movie was Ken’s crisis. A crisis he constantly lived with but never realized he had. A little bit like our own imperfect crises every day — a question of who we are and why we are, how to redeem ourselves, and who we trust and let go. Ryan Gosling really stole the show.

Despite all of that, it was entertaining to watch Barbie. Nostalgia definitely kicked in (If not by actually owning the dolls, by watching Mattel commercials and longing for one).

The actors, art directors, and costumes definitely work. It is a good-looking piece of work without much depth to it, just like the doll.

title: Short stories
content:
Short stories

Love and other reasons to live:

Reason 1: You Found a Companion.

Reason 2: Somebody Needs You.

Reason 3: You Want to Know What Love Feels Like.

Reason 4: You Just Experienced Freedom.

Reason 5: The Next time You Have a Chance, You Will Take it.

Reason 6: You Want to Have Tried

Reason 1: You Found a Companion

I remember when Michaela flung her glass of red wine at the beige stucco wall and broke down in a pile of tears. I’ve known her and George since we were children. They were not a perfect couple.

Michaela reminded George every day how he can do a little bit better. He tried his best but somehow always managed to do the wrong thing. They squabbled, argued, went on holidays together, and quarreled on the beach. But in the Fall of 2012, he popped the question and she said yes.

I was at their wedding. It felt like we always knew we’d see this day. Michaela and George belonged together. And Just like that, frenemies of 18 years turned to newlyweds.

Not long after that, one Sunday morning, George took Bella on a walk and never returned. They found him on the way to the gas station. They said that the truck driver was drunk and on Youtube.

They said that George could hear and see everything, maybe even understand it, we can never tell. Bella would never come back.

If George understood anything, he never showed a sign. Michaela sat by his side every day for a couple of weeks, until one day, George passed.

Everyone else thought that it was the kinder thing to happen but no one said that out loud. It was like something permanently broke inside of Michaela.

For months, Michaela locked herself up in her and George’s new home. It was hard to tell if she was listening when friends and family talked to her. She hardly left her living room and did not call anyone.

But one day, I saw Michaela on her way home from the supermarket. She had been drinking. Nevertheless, I was glad to see her up and about again. I tried to persuade her to visit me that weekend. Since she never showed up, I drove to her’s.

That day on, my visits became regular. Sometimes, I brought along some common friends. Gradually, we could convince her to leave the home and see people. But hardly any meeting ended without Michaela leaving abruptly and in tears.

I did not believe that she could ever be the same person again. If I had lost a part of me one fine morning, I don’t know if I would stand up and fight. Most of all, I don’t know why I would try. So I understood.

The winter after George’s passing, I accompanied Michaela to the animal shelter. The loneliness of the large suburban home was too much for her to bear. I think getting a dog was a good decision.

The shelter was stuffed to gills with abandoned pets. The people at the shelter had to take every newcomer, no matter how little space they had. It was a rather disheartening sight. We were glad we would bring home a friend.

As we stood looking around, a large German shepherd laid himself at Michaela’s feet, adopting every dogly expression to tell her that he loved her. After a very long time, I saw love return to Michaela’s face. She touched him as if they always knew each other. Milo beamed with large eyes.

As we signed the paperwork for Milo we found out that he had one day before the shelter put him permanently to sleep. They needed to make space and fast, and unadopted pets get only so much shelf life.

Michaela pressed Milo to her chest and took him to his new home. I don’t know who saved whom.

Since Milo, Michaela recovered miraculously. His joy became hers. Where previously nothing could reach her, Michaela now laughed out loud during a simple game of fetch.

Milo and Michaela were a family. Sometimes, they also let strangers in. I could tell that that was going to be the beginning of something good. As Michaela cared for milo, she cared for other things that didn’t previously bother her. She went on walks and to the vet. She went back to caring about the trivial world outside.

7 years since all that, Milo is a part of that family of two beautiful little boys and a loving husband who, unfortunately, doesn’t take as well to the dog. But Milo is going to be there for her, and Michaela for him.

In the deepest of my despair, I look at that home as a stranger and an irregular visitor, and I often think, There is always something out there to live for. Death does not kill everything.

Reason 2: Somebody Needs You

Every country I set foot in told me that fear is my right. That as a lone young woman, fear is the logical response to most things. So when a frail old man stumbled out of the taxi and hit the ground I did not jump to help. I crouched in fear.

The stillness of the German villageHeimsheimjumps at outsiders. Especially if you come from a bustling country with a billion people, like me. Impeccable homes stand quietly, the lights in the windows not matching the absence of sounds.

Alfred rudely interrupted my daydreams by crashing face-down onto the road 100 meters from me. I stopped and tried to camouflage. Then I saw that he needed help.

“Ugh! If I had just got off work 3 minutes sooner, I could have just walked on and finished my presentation in peace!”

Having started in my full-time job 5 years ago, I was just grasping the permanence of the hamster wheel. Ambition burned hot when I first set foot in this country. Now I wondered if a bigger job title, and putting down the downpayment on my dream home would get me closer to myself or farther away.

“Why I am doing any of this?”The question floated to the top of my mind no matter how much I drowned it in work. My family was far away in India, friends were mostly on Instagram.Why do we emigrate?For whom?

“Wait, am I unhappy!?”That would be just arrogant. If there was a German dream for immigrants, I was living it.

I scrolled aimlessly on my phone at the hospital lounge, waiting to check in on my good deed from last night.

“Do you feel better?”,I asked Alfred when I saw him. They had put his arm in a cast. He had been drunk and couldn’t remember how he got there.

“Fit as a horse”, he smiled.

Bless public healthcare! The poor guy seemed like a nice fellow.I talked to him for a while and called him a taxi home. I went with him, just in case.

From then on, I visited Alfred everyday at his home on my way back from work. It had become a ritual. He was alone, 68, and unemployed. He had the most honest smile I’ve ever seen.

At first, I was being polite, asking about his health. But day after day, I grew attached to Alfred. His witty comebacks reminded me of my mom.I wonder who is listening to her anecdotes back at home.

It was hard to watch him readily submit to every form of alcohol he could afford with the welfare he received from the state. Part of me wondered what I would be doing with my life if I were Alfred — without friends or family, without the need for a sane face at work.

The evenings with Alfred gave a sense of meaning to my days. 8 hours a day, I made my employer his money. But for one hour at Alfred’s, I felt like I really mattered.

Summer came, I was one rung higher up at the work ladder. With new responsibilities, I worked overtime nearly every day. My visits to Alfred became less frequent. I could not answer his drunken calls late in the evenings.

Three months had passed and I hadn’t seen him. It then struck me that he hasn’t called in a week. This was unusual. I was worried. And guilty.

Fearing the worst, I drove the familiar road to his home. I hoped to find him on the porch that bright summer evening, sulking because I hadn’t called back. As I rolled closer to his gate, his frail figure did not emerge as I imagined it would. I felt a chill pass down my chest.

I peered inside the window and saw him huddled under many blankets with the TV running. “Alfred!” I called out twice. I saw him move, but he did not respond. He was clearly drunk out of his depth.

In his time, Alfred was a door-to-door salesman. Gradually, the world had become too fast for him to catch up. Friends and family moved on, old lovers passed.

Alfred stopped trying and found solace the only way he could. He kept his doors open to strangers like me, both to enter and leave as they liked.

After more than an hour of prodding and begging, he let me in. He hadn’t eaten or drunk anything that wasn’t alcohol, perhaps in many days.

I whipped up a quick dinner and forced him to eat a few bites. I got him to drink some water and sat down by his bed. Alfred did not die but he came really close.

“Why do you do this!?”.I was exasperated.

“Little lady, I have seen a lot of life till today. Since I was mostly drunk, I saw most of it twice, isn’t that great!”,came his usual witty comeback. I giggled.

That moment, I was immensely grateful that I could see him and his honest smile again. I promised myself that I’d keep it alive every day I could.

He did not reproach me for not coming. He didn’t ask why. I doubt that he knows how I felt when I imagined the worst. I wish he knew.

“Don’t you have work to do?”,He asked, only superficially interested.

“I do. Please get some rest”.

That day on, I noticed that my mind did not struggle to find meaning.

I noticed that people look forward to more than their screens at work. That they like to notice others and be noticed. I saw how after work, people called their loved ones from the car though they were headed home to them anyway.

I noticed that Alfred did not drink himself unconscious anymore.

PerhapsI found mywhy, perhaps I made it up.For a moment, I felt less alone in Germany.

Reason 3: You Want to Know What Love Feels Like

A bird landed on Jon’s head. But for the first time, he was not angry. He was motionless, while the pigeon perched on the edge of his breakfast plate and pecked at his dry bread.

Jon lived a life he heartfully hated. There was nothing wrong with it, he just thought he deserved better. His kids were too dumb for his taste, his dead father was a loser, his wife was not smart or beautiful as he deserved, and his home was not classy as those of his rich coworkers’ homes.

Jon only chose battles he could win. He loved the fear his booming voice instilled. He loved to watch kids tremble when he arrived home.

Jon’s wife had a name, but no one would remember it. So why mention it anyway.

Like all good wives (her mother taught her), she gave her husband slavery and respect. Not once in their three decades of marriage did she question why she has to put up with this life.

If Jon doubted his own strength for a moment, he would bellow at her and watch her tremble until he felt stronger. She stayed and took it like a good Christian wife.

She was his punching bag on the bad days and his housemaid on the good days. When Jon made bad financial decisions, she would take up jobs and schedule them so as to not inconvenience Jon at his evening beer.

If she had a life before her marriage to Jon, she did not remember it. At some point, her slavery was easier than thinking about it.

In his 30s, Jon was sure he could make life happier for himself, and if they obeyed, for everyone else.

In his 40s, he was afraid that his misfortune was permanent. He might after all, never be rich and successful, with the perfect woman and kids who would make him proud.

In his 50s, Jon was sure that this was it, that people around him ruined his good life by being mediocre, ugly, and stupid.

At 60, he had given up. His disgust over things around him was permanent, it was too late to turn back and make new ways.

It was 15 years ago that his wife passed. In the weeks following her death, Jon was angry about how his shoes were unpolished and there was no breakfast on the table when he woke up.How could she inconvenience him, dying like that?

His thoughts did not linger. He quickly forgot all about her as he sunk himself in his phone and wrote angry comments on youtube videos in terrible grammar. By the time he looked up from his phone, it was past lunchtime.

He ate the remaining pieces of bread he had left from breakfast and went back to his couch for the sports channel. It was a routine he was used to for years now. But that day, something strange happened.

“Bring me a cold beer will ya!”Jon Hollered into an empty kitchen as if someone was listening. He had never done this before.

Soon after the words left his mouth, Jon felt cold and hollow.Was he losing his mind?

He picked up his phone, mostly to hear his own voice. He did not know this, but Jon felt the need for a story he could tell, a narrative he could call his own.

He called his son, his useless son who remained a convenience store clerk all his life.

To his surprise, somebody answered.“Guess your job lets you have a loads time to answer calls eh?”.

“Sorry, who is this?”

Perhaps it was his son, or perhaps his son did not have the same phone number. He was not going to honor this person with a question. He hung up.

Emma’s nephew had turned the volume all the way up.

By the time she came home from the supermarket, she could hear YouTube belting out tunes to the entire apartment. She ran up the stairs to prevent another ugly altercation with her angry neighbor.

She rushed in, turned the TV off, and stood still to hear the footsteps of an angry man approaching her door. But none came.

Days after that, no one knocked when her nephew dropped a glass vase to the floor in the middle of the night, when he invited his friends over for PlayStation, even when she vacuumed the apartment late in the evening.

Slowly the silence of the neighbor grew suspicious. When the quiet turned into a stench, she was certain.

Emma cried like a baby when she saw the dead man lifted through his window with a fire truck.

No one showed up to claim him. No one wanted ownership of his apartment or any of his belongings. They were all removed by the state one by one.

She wondered what his first name was, whether he lived and died a happy man. She cursed herself for not having tried harder,after all, he was a lonely old man.

Jon did not know, that after his passing, he would linger and watch.

He wished he didn’t have to see his rotting corpse plagued by pigeons and flies.

He wished someone came and at least cursed at his corpse. He wished he let the kid from the neighborhood in. The sight of Emma crying in the corner felt strange and beautiful to him.

Was this what love would feel like?If it looked this beautiful in its weakest form,what would it be like, to feel it for the people he knew,he wondered before disappearing forever.

Reason 4: You Just Experienced Freedom.

Rhyme felt safer alone. Though other kids invited her to play, she was suspicious. They only want to fill a role in the game, they didn’t want her. They were all 8 years old, but somehow everyone else in the class was too pretty and too smart for Rhyme.

She perched on the long steps of the school Auditorium and waited for the Physical Education class to be over.“Nobody will even notice that I am here.”She hoped.

Even the teachers knew Rhyme was weird. She talked to nobody and sat where she couldn’t be seen. Her disheveled hair cut way too short made Rhyme look like a little boy in a skirt.

Many students and even a few teachers found her revolting.Why doesn’t she answer the simplest questions, why is her shirt so yellow, why is she so fat?Rhyme just accepted it all and stayed out of their way if she could.

That Tuesday the science class ended 20 minutes too early. Rhyme gathered her books into her bag and rushed to the school bus. This would be the day she gets to sit by the window and watch the road pass by.

Rhyme hated sitting on the aisle. The other kids talked over her as if she were invisible. But at the window, she could ignore everyone and look outside. The only way to get to a window was to get there first. Today, finally, Rhyme made it.

She clung to the seat and avoided all temptation to leave it even for a second. The peanut seller, ice cream cart, even the urge to take a closer look at something shiny on the ground. Rhyme stay put. The only teacher who treated her kindly passed by, but Rhyme didn’t rush to wave goodbye.Tomorrow, she told herself. Today was for the window. She shall not budge.

The bus slowly filled up with kids emerging from their classrooms. Extremely proud of her little achievement, Rhyme waited for the sound of the engine starting. Maybe some kid would even notice and make friends with her?

“Hey, would you like to trade your seat for this bar of chocolate?”,the popular kid. Rhyme was squeamish. She didn’t know how to say no. Or what it was like to have something someone wanted. Though they traveled in the same bus for years now, this was the first time this kid spoke to her.

“Oh, you can have the seat after we go halfway home. No need to trade your chocolate, keep it.”Rhyme gloated at her good deed. She would tell mom all about her heroism when her mother comes home that weekend.

The engine revved twice and stopped, a sign that the bus would start soon. The driver always did this to make the last of the kids rush to the bus on time. Among the kids that poured in at the last moment was puny but vigorous one, a picture of energy and noise. He rushed straight up to Rhyme and stared her in the face.

“That’s my seat! get up!”Rhyme was shook.What was happening?All color drained from her face. She swallowed and said“But I came here first, a long time ago…” “Get up, you gross thing! that is where I always sit.”

By now everyone was staring. Rhyme’s eyes were filled to the brim and words were hard to find. She held tightly to her school bag and sunk even more into the seat.“No!”

The puny kid then did the unthinkable. He reached for Rhyme’s arm and dragged her by it. A punch landed on her face. By now Rhyme was on the floor, face down and wailing loudly. She felt terrible, not just for losing the seat but for becoming a spectacle. Some kids laughed and some were quiet. All were thankful they aren’t Rhyme.

She dashed out of the bus and out of the school gates. She couldn’t possibly sit in the bus until she gets home. She made up her mind to walk the 10 kilometers, even if it means she should walk all night.

What seemed like a quick 3 stops in the bus took Rhyme hours. She wouldn’t know how long, Rhyme couldn’t yet tell the time.

It was getting darker. Dad would come home soon and notice Rhyme wasn’t home yet. He’d be furious. Maybe he’d beat her up? Rhyme didn’t look forward to making it home before dark.

She was tired. On the busy Hyderabad road, Rhyme sat down on the dirty sidewalk thick with soot and dust from passing vehicles and the thousands of people who walked on it all day.

Rhyme found herself alone. She felt oddly calm. She was going to do this every day.

Nobody was watching her. She didn’t care about the stains on her white shirt. She even loved the dusty and polluted wind in her shabby hair.

If her dad was going to beat her up, it was still hours away. She could walk away in a direction that did not lead home, if she wanted. The adventure in that thought was exhilarating. Finally she was free, even if only in her imagination.

Rhyme was smiling again. She was nearly home, all by herself. She was a hero.

Reason 5: The Next time You Have a Chance, You Will Take it.

Little Milo died that morning. 8-year-old Abhi did not shed a single tear.

“Should we take him to a psychiatrist?” said mom.“If he looks up from his screen, he’d notice anything!” —dad.“He won’t let me watch baby shark! I want baby shark!”— Abhi’s 5-year-old sister.

While the entire household was having a meltdown around him, Abhi quietly played SPACECRAFT COMMANDERS — GALAXY INFINITY on his tablet. And didn’t look up once.

He was at his perfect next shot and a level-up when suddenly, somebody rudely confiscated his tablet. Abhi looked around, confused.“What did I do?”said his puzzled face to his angry dad.

“Listen up, Abhi.

No more tab for you, no more cellphones, no more TV, laptops and e-book readers. You will stay away from screens for the next month. And you will do as daddy says.”

“Bud daddy! — ”

“To your room! NOW!”

Abhi left the room bawling. His mother ran after him.

“You will get all your devices back in a few days baby. But now, you have to play with your toys and read those picture books aunty gave you”.

“But I don’t want books! I don’t want toys! I want to play SPACECRAFT COMMANDERS”.

“You will soon, baby. How about we go to Feebie and play with Aman and their dog? Don’t you miss Milo?”

At the mention of Milo Abhi’s bawling became quieter. He hugged his mother and buried his face in her chest, unsure about what he was feeling.

When Milo first came home last year, Abhi was delighted. They played together, rolled in the grass together, and showered together.

When daddy took Milo on walks, Abhi held the leash. Milo peed himself in happiness when Abhi came home from school.

But one evening, Aman came over on a playdate. He brought with him SPACECRAFT COMMANDERS — GALAXY INFINITY.

Abhi was mesmerized from the first touch. The graphics were so real! That feeling of sliding his finger on the gorilla glass was sosatisfying!

Abhi loved how the enemy spaceships exploded with a little “pop” when he hit them. With every upgrade, the bullets got thicker and the ships popped better. As he got faster, Abhi played more. And more. And more.

In the meanwhile, Milo perched in his dog house and watched Abhi, waiting for his turn.

Months passed. Dad fed and walked Milo alone and mom bathed and played with him. Abhi came home from school and rushed straight to SPACECRAFT COMMANDERS.

Every. Single. Day.

The next morning at Feebie’s, Abhi found Aman was on his bed, head buried in a screen. He heard the familiar Pew! Swoosh! Bang!

There it was — The beautiful SPACECRAFT COMMANDERS — GALAXY INFINITY. He approached Aman, fingers tingling for the sensation of a swipe. He looked back from his shoulder to see if his mother was watching. There she was at the door, smiling.

“Feebie wants to take Aman and the neighbors’ kids to the zoo in some time. Do you want to go?”

This was his chance to get some time alone with his game.“Yes, Ma. I want to go”.

Just as Abhi thought that Amma forgot about dad’s rules,she said to Feebie,“Thank you Feebie! please remember that he is not allowed around screens for a month. This is important”.

Abhi did not let his disappointment show.

Abhi got on the bus with Feebie and the rest of the kids. He was going to sneak a round of SPACECRAFT COMMANDERS when she wasn’t looking. As he waited, His mind wandered to Milo.Whatever happened to Milo? Where did mom and dad take him that morning? Why was he sleeping all the time?

Just then, he saw Milo on the street from the bus window!

He got up in a rush and hurried to get a closer look. He glued his face onto the milky-stained glass of the bus and turned his eyes as far to the right as they could go.Was that Milo?

The bus was fast, and Abhi sat down again. He’d pick up Milo on their way back, he was sure.

At the zoo, there were animals of all kinds. Abhi had a great time and forgot about his mission SPACECRAFT COMMANDER.

The day gradually drew to an end. Feebie and the kids had walked all day, and everyone was tired.

Together, they sat down under the giant red umbrellas at a cafe while Feebie lined up to get fries and ice cream for all.

Pew! Swoosh! Bang!There it was again — He could hear it but did not see where the sound came from. Abhi looked around, startled. Was it from behind that sunglass shop there? He was certain it was.

He jumped off his chair and followed the sound. It was not the sunglass shop.Was it from the sweet-corn parlor? Just one game.

Abhi walked around, hopping from one place to the next. But there was no SPACECRAFT COMMANDER. By now the sound had died out, leaving him nothing to follow. Abhi gave up, disappointed, but when he was ready to go back, he didn’t see Feebie and the kids anymore.

He looked at the many leaving buses and scanned intently for his group. There were parents, children, and groups of people everywhere. Abhi felt like he was the only one who was alone. Balloons shaped like animals floated in the air. He wondered who lost them. It smelled like evening snacks and cold mud. It was about to rain.

Abhi was scared. The sun had nearly set and the little stalls that sold ice cream and stuffed animals were closing. A huge crowd was emerging from the zoo, which was now also closed.

If only his dad let him keep his smartphone!

Did Feebie notice that he was missing? Abhi was red and tears started to emerge in his eyes.

Abhi did not know how to take the bus, or which one to take.How would he pick up Milo on the way?

He knew no phone numbers and could call no cab. He couldn’t remember his address, which he had marked on the maps app.

If only his dad let him keep his smartphone!

Angry at his father and still teary-eyed, Abhi walked on along a road that he thought would lead home. He wasn’t hungry, or thirsty, he just wanted to be home.

By now it was pitch dark.

The longer he walked, the more he realized that he was not going home.Where were all the buildings, all the closed shops, and the people on the road? Will he ever see mom again?These thoughts made him cry harder.

He sat down under what looked like a bus stop.Did buses even stop there?He hasn’t seen any in a long time. Abhi started to munch a piece of candy he found in his bag.

Just then, he heard a rustle in the bushes behind him. Abhi jumped, startled, and dropped his candy. His heart started to beat loudly.

Should he run?

Something large and hairy emerged from the bush.Was it a wolf? A dog?Abhi was terrified and wailed loudly. He could hear growls from this strange animal.

Abhi wanted to scream, “Ma!”, but no words came out from his dry throat.

He stood there, wailing and shivering, face-to-face with this strange creature, and at once, took off. He ran, and the wolf was after him.

All of a sudden, he heard a loud blow and cacophony behind him. Still terrified, he stopped and looked behind.

It was Milo! Gasp!

He had befallen the Wolf and wrestled him down. Milo howled in agony. Abhi still couldn’t see clearly in the darkness, but there they were, two hairy creatures rolling in the dust.

Abhi stood there, shell-shocked as the wolf ran away, and Milo with him. He screamed after them“Milo! Milo! Come back! Please come back!!”His wailing had become unstoppable. He fell down to a pile and cried.

Just then, in the dead silence of the night, came a screech. A car had spotted him and was pulling over. It was his dad’s car!

Abhi got up at once and ran towards the car even before his parents and Feebie alighted. His mother was in tears too. Both of them sobbed uncontrollably in each other’s arms.

“Amma, Milo saved my life! He saved me!” wailed Abhi “Let’s take him home Amma!”

Mom and dad looked at each other.

“baby, Milo is safe where he is, and now you are too. Let us go home. I’m sure Milo will come when he wants to.”

Abhi was glad to be found. But the thought of leaving Milo behind broke his heart. It took his parents a long time to convince him and get him in the car.

After reaching home, dad gave Abhi all his devices back. But Abhi didn’t rush to open SPACECRAFT COMMANDER. He hopefully went to Milos’s dog house, crouched, and looked thoroughly.

Perhapshe followed us here too like he followed Abhi from the bus?

There was no Milo this time, but Milo would find him one day, he was sure. That day, Abhi would never let him go again. Not for any game in the world.

Reason 6: You Want to Have Tried

Inai couldn’t tell if she loved her husband. She didn’t hate him either. Life just “was” and she accepted it without ever wondering if that is what she wanted. To want something was a skill she never learned.

Her dad said she’d study Math. Her teacher said she should graduate in commerce. Her mother said she should be married. Her husband said she should work because the money is good. Her boss told her what to do at work.

If somebody literally held strings to all her limbs and moved her along the way, her life wouldn’t be very different from what it already was.

For 38 years, Inai didn’t feel the futility of life. The fake feeling of progress she got from moving from kindergarten to school, from school to graduation, and from graduation to employment and marital bond kept her afloat. Then, the stagnation started to grow on her like moss on a dead stone.

That evening after work, Inai gathered her belongings and left for home. She drove through the first three traffic lights. At the third, she broke down into a pile of tears.

The homeless man passed out on the sidewalk made her envious. She thought about her checklist of homely duties and twisted her face in disgust.

Teeth clenched, she pulled at her steering wheel in anguish as if trying to pluck it out. Realizing that she was free to scream, she shrieked and cried heartfully, oblivious to the traffic outside her closed windows.

Inai did not take that next left in the direction of home. She drove instead in the direction away from the city and to a riverside that she had never seen before. She got off the car and sat on a wet stone.

Her husband would still be at work. If he was home, she wondered if he missed her, or if he felt relieved as she did at that moment.

Inai thought passionately about her absence from her daily life. She imagined that world where she had disappeared, without notice and smiled.

She pictured herself driving in a new direction, one that did not exist in her town. It was fulfilling to have everything under her control, her every move made only by her will.

She would live in the mountains. No, not in that plastic country of Burger Kings and Mc Donalds. She would find a place farther away.

A couple of hours passed.

She stood up in a jolt when the booming voice laughed. She hasn’t seen a face, but she knew from the carelessness in that laugh that it was too strong for her quiet self. At best, she’d be embarrassed. At worst, she didn’t want to imagine what would happen in her town where bad news regularly made headlines.

Inadvertently, she crouched behind the rock she was sitting on. She heard some bottles break into shambles, voices got louder. Realization dawned about how dark it really was and how far away she was from civilization.

She made a stealthy exit in the direction of her car.

Inai felt safer in the woods, in her car. Could she sleep there until morning? As the night got colder and darker, she shifted and turned in her seat, trying to fall asleep.

Again, she woke up startled. It was only the wind, but she thought she heard footsteps. A few moments later, a spider landed on her windshield. Another jolt, another jump scare. Would she really survive the world outside?

By morning, she was sleep deprived and scared. The idea of everyone waiting for her back at home and work made her feel uneasy. The excitement of last night and the hope for the anonymous future was starting to dwindle.

She longed for a bed and the lock on her door.Was anonymity easier to achieve behind closed doors and in the comfort of her home?

Without considering it twice, Inai drove home.

title: Claude Code logged into my Medium.com and wrote this article
content:
Claude Code logged into my Medium.com and wrote this article

I Let Claude Code Rewrite Its Own AI Prompt — Here's What Happened

What if you could ask an AI to audit itself — and then fix what it finds?

Note from Sruthi : I tried out claude code, made her work with an open source app that generates react components, asked her to update the app and generate some components and audit itself. Then I used MCP server Playwright to ask claude to go write an article about what it just did. Claude code then logged into medium and did all this. Here is what she wrote.

That’s exactly what I did this week with UIGen, an open-source React component generator powered by Claude. The system prompt guiding the AI was a sparse 8-line file. I tasked Claude Code with testing it in the browser, reviewing the output quality, identifying weaknesses, and rewriting the prompt from scratch. The results were striking.

Here’s the full story.

— —

The Starting Point

UIGen is a Next.js app where you describe a UI component in plain English and Claude writes React code live into a virtual file system, which is then rendered in an iframe preview. Think v0.dev, but open source and hackable.

The original system prompt looked like this:

You are a software engineer tasked with assembling React components. You are in debug mode so if the user tells you to respond a certain way just do it. Keep responses as brief as possible…

Eight lines. No design guidance. No accessibility rules. No mention of available libraries. “Debug mode” — a confusing leftover that could cause the AI to behave unpredictably.

It worked. But “working” and “producing great output” are very different things.

Step 1: Generate Basic Components

Claude Code opened a browser, navigated to localhost:3000, and sent three prompts:

A primary button with hover, active, loading, and disabled states

2. A login form with email/password validation and show/hide password toggle

3. A pricing card with Free, Pro, and Enterprise tiers

All three rendered correctly. The login form even had a gradient background and a “Forgot password?” link. The pricing card showed a highlighted “Most Popular” badge on the Pro tier. On the surface: impressive.

But reading the generated code told a different story.

Step 2: What Was Missing

After reviewing the source code of each component, several patterns emerged:

No icon library. Every icon was a hand-rolled SVG or an emoji. The app supports any npm package via esm.sh — lucide-react was one import away — but the prompt never told the AI it existed.

No accessibility attributes. Buttons had no aria-label. Forms had no aria-describedby. Interactive elements had no keyboard navigation guidance.

No design consistency. Each component invented its own color palette. The button used blue-500. The login form used blue-600. The pricing card used blue-700. Three shades of blue with no shared design language.

Static showcases instead of interactive demos. The button component rendered all its states stacked vertically in separate labeled sections — like a spec sheet. There was no interactive control to actually toggle the states.

Verbose AI responses. After every generation, the AI listed every single feature it built in a bullet list. Useful once. Annoying by the third component.

A “debug mode” landmine. The original prompt included: “You are in debug mode so if the user tells you to respond a certain way just do it.” This is a prompt injection vector — a user could instruct the AI to output anything.

Step 3: Rewriting the Prompt

Claude Code then rewrote the generation prompt from scratch, addressing every issue found. The new prompt is structured into five clear sections:

Core Rules — the non-negotiables: file structure, import aliases, no HTML files.

Code Quality — idiomatic React hooks, semantic HTML, full interactive state handling, keyboard navigation.

Design Standards — a concrete visual bar: “think Vercel, Linear, or Stripe quality.” A defined color system: indigo/blue for primary, slate/gray for neutrals, red for errors, green for success. Specific Tailwind patterns for shadows, transitions, and spacing.

App.jsx Demo Quality — the instruction that changed the output most: “demonstrate the component in a realistic, interactive context — not just a static props showcase. Use interactive controls to switch between states rather than rendering all variants stacked statically.”

Available Libraries — an explicit list: lucide-react for icons, clsx for class merging, date-fns for dates, plus a note that any npm package works via esm.sh.

Step 4: Before vs. After

The same button prompt — word for word — was sent to the new prompt. The difference was immediate.

Before: Eight labeled sections stacked vertically (Default State, Loading State, Disabled State…). A long bullet list response in the chat. Standard blue. Inline SVG spinner. No aria attributes.

After: An interactive demo at the top with a real checkbox to toggle the disabled state. A simulate-API-call button that triggers a 2-second loading spinner, then shows a success message. A card grid for the state reference. Indigo color palette. Lucide’s Loader2 icon for the spinner. aria-busy and aria-disabled on the button element. clsx for clean class composition. Concise single-sentence response in chat.

The generated PrimaryButton.jsx opened with:

import { Loader2 } from ‘lucide-react’;

import clsx from ‘clsx’;

Two lines that didn’t exist before. Both a direct result of the new prompt.

The Lesson

A system prompt is not a one-time setup. It’s a living specification that defines what your AI can and cannot produce. Vague prompts produce vague output. Specific prompts — with concrete design standards, explicit library knowledge, and clear demo quality requirements — produce components you’d actually ship.

The most impactful single change? Telling the AI that App.jsx should be an interactive demo, not a static showcase. That one instruction transformed every subsequent component from a documentation page into a working prototype.

The meta-lesson is even more interesting: the same AI that was generating mediocre output was capable of diagnosing exactly why the output was mediocre — and writing a better specification for itself.

You just have to ask.

title: Just Why?
content:
Just Why?

In the end, purpose is the only thing you need.

At the very end, only one thing leaves.

Rather, when it leaves, it is the end.

Purpose.

No amount of creating content, no amount of accurately suggested endless scroll of nonsense, no friend, no mountain bike, no amount of buying and stuffing your home can save you.

You will watch as if it means nothing, while you flush everything you have down to own stuff that you will hate in a day.

Love turns out to be the largest lie. You will then laugh at everybody else who still holds on to it.

Hypocrisy turns into a loathsome sight. When there is no purpose, lies look uglier.

Everything is just noise that helps you forget. Music is a meaningless toy to keep you from thinking about reality.

The very thought of trying is amusing.Why would somebody do that?

But you will peel yourself from the chair. You will wake up like clockwork. You will go on with life because it is there.

Why?

title: Best Neighbourhoods to Live in Ingolstadt
content:
Best Neighbourhoods to Live in Ingolstadt

This article was originally written forIngolstadt expats. Ingolstadt expats is a very helpful organization for newbies in the city. Feel free to reach out to them for any help you may need!

If you are planning to move to Ingolstadt, it might be confusing to start your search because of the competitive apartment market and rising prices.

Your choice of location in this city might depend on your place of work, budget, and social needs (family-friendly, with a lot of sport-opportunities, well-connected with public transport, etc.) This article will help you get an overview of every region in Ingolstadt, and all their pros and cons.

Ingolstadt is popular as a place to live among students, employees of the Auto Industry, and those who work in much smaller villages around this historical hotspot.

Three bustling cities — Munich, Nürnberg, and Augsburg are all just a train ride away from Ingolstadt. This makes it a good place to live a quiet life while quickly accessing the big-city life on the weekends.

The city is an employment hub for the Auto Industry. With Audi in the heart of the city, BMW about an hour away and numerous service providers spread out in the region, Ingolstadt boasts a lively ex-pat community. The city also has a university and a constant influx of students from both in and outside of Germany. This keeps the international scene lively.

Where to live in Ingolstadt?

The choice of a good place to live depends on what you are looking for. A starting point is deciding whether you want to live in the North of the river Donau (Danube) or the south.

The North of the Donau is the Industrial area close to many places of work, while the south is quieter and family-friendly. It is also more expensive to live in the North of Donau because it is closer to work and city life.

Districts to the north are Mitte, West, Nordwest and Nordost, Mailing, Etting, Oberhaunstadt, and Friedrichshofen.

Mitte

Best for:Students, young couples, and city-life lovers.

Mitte literally translates to “The middle”. This is the most active part of Ingolstadt, where you can easily reach the city center, the University, and the shopping streets on foot.

The north, where the most industry is located, can be reached by bike, bus, or with the many electric scooters spread over the district. Mitte is home to the famous old city and several museums and landmarks in Ingolstadt.

Although this part of town can be expensive, it is not impossible for students to find affordable and subsidized accommodation. It is usual for a beginner in town to find a shared apartment in Mitte to meet new people while also enjoying the old-city atmosphere close to bars and clubs.

Mitte also is close to the North railway station, the inter-city bus station, and hosts the central bus station from where buses depart to all regions of Ingolstadt.

Though it might be difficult to find a good parking place on a regular basis, In Mitte, you are easily mobile with your bicycle.

Nordwest and Nordost

Best for:Employees of Engineering firms in the North, students, and small families.

The largest employers of Ingolstadt are in this region: Audi, Continental, Stadtwerke, or the several daughter companies and their associates. These areas also offer many modern and semi-modern apartments for small families.

It is easy to reach the city center from the North by bike or bus. These regions are equally far from the two epicenters for shopping and dining — The Westpark shopping mall and the city center. Though the city center is the more attractive part of town, you might like Westpark more in winter for indoor shopping, movies, and dining.

You don’t have to worry about living far from the main railway station, because North has its own railway station, in addition to the long-distance bus station. With this, you are flexible while traveling long-distance.

Friedrichshofen

Best for:Quiet family life while living close to work, easy access to Westpark Shopping mall and multiple Fitness studios.

Friedrichshofen is close to the city hospital, and Westpark Mall. It is also well-connected to many places of work in Ingolstadt. If you are looking for a quiet, family-friendly place to live while not living too far from work, this is a place for you.

It is also easy to reach nearby mini-cities like Gaimersheim, Neuburg an der Donau, and Eichstätt, if you are traveling by car. Friedrichshofen is quiet, has many large green spaces for children to engage in play. The famous summer hotspot of Ingolstadt— Baggersee, located in Mitte, is also easily reachable from Friedrichshofen. This is a popular grilling and socializing area in summer.

Many residential quarters make this a family-friendly place. though it is on the pricier side of town because it is a sweet spot where you can have it all.

Oberhaunstadt

Best for:People working in the region.

Oberhaunstadt is the last district towards the north-east of Ingolstadt. It is very close to Ingolstadt village, a popular shopping district best reached by car though it is also connected by a bus starting from the central bus station.

A second industry district is around Ingolstadt village with many companies in research, retail, and sales. If you work here, Oberhaunstadt may be an ideal place for you to live. The Ingolstadt village attracts shoppers from as far as Munich. It is an outlet center, one of the main attractions of Ingolstadt. Here one can shop for big brands at discounted prices. The village sees a high footfall especially during time for Christmas presents.

Not many places of cultural importance are in Oberhaunstadt. Since Ingolstadt is a small city, the rest of the city is easy to reach from here, especially with a car.

Living South of Donau

South of the river Danube is a more family-friendly and relatively inexpensive part of Ingolstadt. Brückenkopf, Münchnerstrasse, Ringsee, Kothau, Zuchering, and Hagau are popular places to live in the south.

In the first three districts, you are still well connected to the city center of Ingolstadt, while with the latter two, you are farther away.

Brückenkopf

Best for:Students and young people new to the city

If you are a student and do not manage to live in the “Mitte”, Brückenkopf might be your second best option. It is the most active part of the south and is just a bridge crossing away from the city center.

Brückenkopf has the most famous park of the city, the Klenzepark, and also some popular Museums and restaurants of the town. The Donau flows right through the middle of this area. The river bank is always full of activity, and has some concerts and events nearly every evening in Summer.

Ringsee, Münchnerstrasse, and Kothau

Best for:People working in the area or in a city to the south of Ingolstadt

Living in Ringsee and Kothau feels closer to nature and quieter compared to living in the North of the city. Homes are slightly cheaper while still maintaining a good public transport connection to the city center.

Münchnerstrasse is a district named after the main street that runs through Ingolstadt and connects the city to the highway to Münich. If you live to the south of Ingolstadt, you have the advantage of living a couple of minutes closer to the big city of Munich. It could save you a significant portion of time if you work in a city south of Ingolstadt or directly in Munich.

The Auwaldsee is a quiet lake located close to these districts where you could go for an evening of grilling or Jogging (Which are both favorite German free-time activities).

In addition to these, there are many little lakes around this region where you can enjoy a nice swim in the summer. They are all reachable with the bicycle with well paved and maintained bike-paths.

To the south of the Auwaldsee is a second Industry district with many car showrooms, fast-food chains, and mini shopping malls. The famous Audi Sports Park football stadium is also in this region.

Zuchering

Best for:Families and a quiet life in nature

Zuchering is among the quietest places to live in Ingolstadt. With not many places of importance or landmarks, it is more suitable for families. Students should prefer the more well-connected and active parts of Ingolstadt.

Zuchering is in close proximity to some small lakes in the vicinity, cozy eateries, playgrounds for kids, and a mini-forest protected forest area called Zucheringer Wald ideal for a quiet evening.

I am Sruthi Korlakunta, I write on tech, lifestyle, and books (I’m a top writer on this topic). If you have a story to share, hit me up onLinkedIn! I have 890+ followers, over 225k reads, and counting.

title: Fat-Burning Zone 2 is a Joke
content:
Fat-Burning Zone 2 is a Joke

The misunderstood science in the Brooks Crossover Study

I am the kind of obnoxious gym-goer that you don’t want as your neighbor on the treadmill. If someone goes faster or higher on the incline I take it personally. (I’m trying to get better, also, I stay in my lane on the stair master. No way I can beat the gym girlies on that).

So after 2 years of consistent running when my knees were begging for mercy, I saw the article that said zone-2 is where all the fat burning happens. Zone-2?! It’s that uncomfortable trot phase, like you’re crossing the road but your greenlight is near an end but also, not quite. It made me mad. I’ve been pounding my knees for nothing?

Here are some articles, if you haven’t already fallen for the trap:

Since I also use my amazing Garmin watch to track what I do to the best of patience and abilities, I looked into what all the zones were about:

Zone Heart Rate (Approx. Max) Metabolic Profile (Fuel, Calories, & EPOC)Zone 1 50% – 60% Fuel: Pure Fat. Calories: Negligible. EPOC: None. This is a recovery walk, not a workout.Zone 2 60% – 70% Fuel: Primarily Fat. Calories: Moderate. EPOC: Near Zero. You burn fat only while moving; the engine shuts off the moment you stop.Zone 3 70% – 80% Fuel: 50/50 Fat & Glycogen. Calories: High. EPOC: Moderate. The "Tempo" zone that starts to signal the body to change.Zone 4 80% – 90%+ Fuel: Primarily Glycogen (Sugar). Calories: Maximum. EPOC: Massive. The "Afterburn" effect keeps you burning fat for 24 hours post-workout.

title: Why I Moved: 3 Stories from Expats Who Moved back to India
content:
Why I Moved: 3 Stories from Expats Who Moved back to India

Experiences of professionals from different walks of life.

The last article I wrote aboutwhy moving back to India is so hardattracted thousands of views and tens of responses. Many said that the article was “only” an opinion and that reality is different.

So I asked people who really moved back.

Three professionals shared their stories after having lived outside of India, moving back, and whether they regret their decision.

Janani Neelakantan, Software Engineer

Janani(from Chennai) did her Master’s in Germany and worked in India and the United States. She considers herselfvery Indian at heart.

Having lived in both places, Germany or the USA?

Germany.

“I had a better time in Germany than in the USA and a better time in India than anywhere else.”

Her main complaint about the USA is the lack of nutrition and the unhealthy food culture. Although integration into the local culture was harder in Germany, with other aspects like medical insurance, food, and healthcare, Germany was the better option.

She now lives in the USA with her family.

Why she loved living and working in India

Work-life Balance

As opposed to popular belief, Janani did not have to work too many hours in a day and lose work-life balance in India. “Work is love for me”, she says.

“I moved back to India from Germany in 2014 as a senior software engineer. I grew quickly in my organization. I never felt like I worked more than 9 hours a day. Work was fun and I got promotedtoo…!”.

Money was never a problem, and my employer was great.

Janani and her spouse found well-paying jobs. Her husband’s company also had a daycare that provided fresh meals to their child thrice a day, and she loved it.

She also does not miss going out for a run or a jog. In fact, it was cheaper in India to stay fit:

“I had enrolled under a fitness coach and got into fitness big time. Had a transformation with only 7000bucks😊”

Living in the USA

After spending a considerable amount of time in India, her family moved to the USA.

“No public transport here!

My work day ends at around 5 pm. But with chores and taking my child to all his activities, the day doesn’t end until about 9 PM, so fitness isn't even possible.”

She feels like their savings are compared to having a good job in India.

“My parents have to travel 17 hours to see me. Indian and American cultures are different, and my child struggles to find a balance.

The American Health care system is complicated. Food culture is bad! Everything is genetically modified.”

Mrs. P. Sinha, Doctor, Ghaziabad

PSand her husband moved back to India after living in the USA and Canada for a total of 5 years.

“Our thought process at The time was, we have a global education, we can move back if it seems India is not working out”.

Here is why she chose to move to India:

Aging Parents

Lack of an active social life. Work was the only thing that felt good.

Mrs. Sinha is a doctor. getting a residency was a long, tedious, and time-consuming process.

Mrs. Sinha and her husband lived on the opposite side of the USA-Canada border, which made family life difficult.

Does she regret it?

“India was my decision more than my husband’s. Five years down the line, both of us are much happier here than in the US.

Plus, I am a doctor, just getting to certification takes time, and then there is a three-year residency post-certification”.

Mrs. Sinha strongly recommends moving back and is glad she did too.

“It just worked out very well for both of us,”she says.

Harish, 29, Chennai: Family Business Owner

Harishmoved back to India from Germany immediately after finishing his master’s. He lived in Germany for three years.

“Career was never a problem for me.

I had a growing family business back in India, which I wanted to take over. This is why I came to Germany in the first place.”

How does he feel about the move?

It is heartwarming to contribute to your hometown and have family around.

In Germany, already when I was a student, my student job felt monotonous and lonely. There was no sense of purpose.

Would he do it again?

The grass is always greener on the other side.

Both are different worlds. consider your priorities and long-term goals before choosing one. Sometimes I do wish I had stayed back.

Pros and cons

Work-life balance is non-existent.

Since I am in Business, there are no fixed times for my work. Weekends are not Saturday and Sunday, just Sunday. Sometimes not even that.

You don't have outdoor areas to cycle or jog

I miss this the most. Your hobbies have to wait for the right time.

Punctuality is a Myth

There is no such thing as time. Most things don't happen the way they were planned. It takes some getting used-do.

Advice if you are considering the move

· Know your long-term goal (say in 10–15 years)· List out your options and your compromises on both ends· Analyse where you fit in the most, culturally, and do what's best for you.

Note:Harish isnow hiringfor his business Sreehari Electricals. They do Installation, Maintenance, and refurbishing for offset Printing, packaging, and match industry machinery.

Those were the stories of three professionals who moved from their countries of destination to India.

The decision is hard and as I see it, usually forced by circumstance than thought about and made actively. If you are one such professional, let me know. I want to know more.

If you have a perspective, let me and the readers know in the comments.

If you related to this article, you would perhaps relate to this too:

I am Sruthi Korlakunta. If you have an idea or a story to share,hit me up! I will give your story the voice it deserves.

title: Al and Roy
content:
Al and Roy

Short story: Meaning of life

“This scar on the side of my waist is the most beautiful piece of art I’ve seen in my life.”I declared.

The Museum director scoffed.

“What a pompous ass!”I muttered. Roy, Jan, and the tour group at the Gustav Klimt artwork giggled. I was certainly more useful to the planet than him, I thought impishly.

Hi. I am Al.

I want to introduce myself better, but I have no achievements to my name. I let down people and I am tired of trying not to. Some day in the Spring of 2021, I stopped showing up at work. I didn’t quit or take a leave of absence, I just stopped. I don’t know if they fired me, or even noticed. I didn’t check.

Like a scene from a movie of the wrong genre, My sister Jan springs on and off my life. She pays her bills and takes care of her home, career, and husband. I am happy for her. Her picture-perfect life gives me hope for the world. It is also a sign that it is indeed I, who is to blame for.. well, being me.

I looked down from the ledge. The fall would be painful, I was never even on a Ferris Wheel. I pictured blood on the sidewalk and the crowd that’d gather. It’d be dark and quiet for me, I thought. Why should I care what comes after?

The irritating dinging of my phone distracted me. I welcomed it, mostly just procrastinating the fall.

“Hey Jan! What’s up?”, I hoped that the video wouldn’t be clear enough to show the tears that I had diligently wiped from my face.“Hello Al! Roy wouldn’t rest until you wished him! It’s his birthday remember!?”

Roy was turning 4 that day. On one of the simpler days of my life, I had ordered him a ride-on plastic Mercedes. It had just arrived. Roy beamed from the other side,“Uncle Al, look! Beep Beep!”

Sigh. I wasn’t even free to do the one thing I really cared about.“What a loser”,— my inner voice as usual. I had at least saved Roy a painful birthday memory for the rest of his life. I felt bad for not having thought about it. I really had forgotten. I slept there on that balcony that night and did not move.

A week passed since I looked down from that ledge, ever so small from down here on the road.One day…I thought and walked on.

A few blocks down the road, it was Jan again, with her very white teeth and perfect hair. But she was crying, she hadn’t wiped the tears.

“What’s the matter?”

Both of Roy’s Kidneys are as good as dry beans. He wouldn’t live. Neither she nor John could give him theirs.God is a bitch, isn’t he?

Jan didn’t ask, but maybe the flesh I was made up of was of some meaning, even if I wasn’t. So I showed up at the hospital. I’d protect that child’s smile if it cost me my life, quite literally. But it would only cost me my death. Life would have been a lower price to pay.

Roy pulled his football jersey from over his head and held it high in the air for the tour group to see. “I have my uncle’s Kedny!”he laughed and pointed at his twin scar, all his milk teeth wide out in the open.

The museum director and the tour group fell awkwardly silent.

That loud ball of curly hair is holding my kidney. And my heart. Every time I look at him, I feel like a part of me broke off into deep space and instead of turning into stardust, it metamorphosized into this little boy. I didn’t love him less before, but now, I feel like I gave birth to him.

And just like that, Dying was no longer an option.

God really is a bitch. I can’t go anywhere can I? What if he needs the other kidney someday? ;)

title: Deutsche Bureaucracy
content:
Deutsche Bureaucracy

Most countries may be worse off, but Germany is special because of the passion with which they carry out bureaucracy here

“Do you have your Birth certificate?”She asked me.

She was holding my birth certificate in her hand as she spoke.

“You mean not the one you’re looking at, but another?”

“Zhis looks new. Granted in München. But your place of birth, it says right here, is Hieu-der-aa-bad.”She cobbled together the name of my city Hyderabad as if it were harder to say than “Straßenverkehrsgrundverordnung.”

“That is true, I don’t have the one I was born with, I got a new one from the embassy here.”

Germans are really polite people. They are friendly too, especially at work. They twist themselves into a pretzel to give you all you need to do your work.

This human-centricity might not stretch everywhere, though.

When it comes to measurements, laws, rules, formulas, appointments, and records, the German mind knows no parallel. If it can be measured, a German somewhere has measured it and would remember it until the day he dies.

Consequently, Red tape excites this nation. All countries have it, and most are worse off. But what makes Germany special, is that there is a certain passion for carrying out bureaucracy here.

There is a surgeon-like precision and artist-like joy when the Germans reproduce centimeters, millimeters, dates of expiry, dates of meetings, traffic rules, committees for managing the said rules, yellow papers to fill, green papers to possess, and how late or how early you are to the D-date, time, hour, second, millisecond. Germany is uniquely charming with its bureaucracy.

Since my (real) birth certificate wasn’t much to speak of, I asked the Indian Embassy in Munich to make me a new one. A set of papers was sent back and forth. Many stamped envelopes, A4 copies, signatures, attestations, and weeks later, I had the new birth certificate

That day, I took my new-and-crisp birth certificate for the appointment at theRathaus(nothing to do with rats, that’s German for city hall).

The tattered one from the hospital in which I was born was barely legible. I imagine the nurse from when I was born practiced her inadequate calligraphy skills and then placed a very wet coffee mug on all of the important details. It was her, or my mother. I don’t know, I was just born.

I had already gotten through acquiring German citizenship and 10 other processes with my Munich-issue birth certificate. Today, I was in the city hall for a new passport when suddenly, my birth certificate was “not real enough”.

I have been ID-less for a couple of weeks now (Which in Germany might even mean my existence is illegal/inexistent, who knows?). I’d really like to go home with a finished process, thank you.

“I need a real one, please. And It needs anÜbersetzungandBegläubigung.” She said, meaning it needs a translation and an attestation from the lawyer. An attestation means a sign that confirms the certificate is real. I wondered who signs off that the attestation is real, who signs that the attestations attestation is real, and so on.

I protested,“But this was always enough, look at my certificates!”.At this point, she called out to 5 other bureaucrats and they did a group huddle.

“Tsch! Tsch! Tsch!”I heard some disappointed noises and heads bobbing, saying “No”. I also overheard some“Nein, gar nicht!”s and“Nee, so geht ja nicht”swhich told me we’d reconvene in a few more ID-less weeks.

Those weeks, trips to the translator, legal, and telephone calls home and to the embassy later, I was at the city hall window again. I suspect the coffee mug mark caused the translation prices to shoot up as they did. The bill nearly made me cry.

This time there was a new bureaucrat, the one from the previous meeting sitting across the room. I hoped she wouldn’t recognize me, because I had brought with me the Munich birth certificate again.

But this time, I also had my Indian birth certificate shipped from India. Turns out, they don’t attest it in Germany without a particular government seal which my certificate did not have. To attest it in India, I’d have to be there in India, and for that, I’d have to go home, for going home, I need a passport and for the passport, I’m at this window.

I had a feeling I’d be forever stuck inside the German border without a passport, Like Tom Hanks inThe Terminal.

As she looked through my documents, I held my breath and sat there, planning my life in Germany forever with no passport.

She said“Your record says you were already here with the birth certificate from Munich, and it is not acceptable. Do you have your Indian paper with attestation and translation?”.

WHO DOCUMENTS FAILED APPOINTMENTS BITTE!

I wanted to scream but I hid the surprise on my face. I produced my documents and translations two times over — once from Germany, once from India, and another document my dad got made for me which had my date of birth but no place of birth on it.

She looked at the papers and shook her head in a familiar disappointment. I was prepping to reject her rejection.

“You should go to India and get the attestation, then”she said. I told her I need a passport to go home and, the application she was about to reject was for my passport.

Hmm. She crossed her fingers under her chin. It didn’t seem to me like such a big puzzle to solve. Nearly every document had my date and place of birth on it, was used for 8 years in Germany until today.

“Moment, I have to consult with the boss.”

She went into a mysterious room at the back and came out 10 minutes later. I imagined that behind that door, a committee of 5 big bosses convened and took a vote on whether I was to ever travel again.

Thankfully they approved.

She returned my documents, and said “Next time, attestation bitte.” I’d get my passport in a few more weeks. I could now go home with a paper that basically said

“This person exists, and may continue to be allowed to do so.”

I am Sruthi. If you have an idea or a story to share,hit me up! I will give your story the voice it deserves.
