title: 5 Hilarious Books on Data Analytics Everybody Can Read
content:
5 Hilarious Books on Data Analytics Everybody Can Read

Bed-time stories for data-engineers

Today, nearly every profession involves Data Analytics of some form.

The following books are from professionals who ask questions that we probably should also be asking our numbers. Feel free to read them as bedtime stories too, they are brilliant and easy to read.

1. FREAKONOMICS — Rogue Economics

Levitt and Dubner are a delightful duo. While Levitt is arogue economistand ahuman slide-rule. The book, written in Dubner’s witty tongue and Levitt’s research is a must-read for all data-lovers.

It offers a data based perspective on cause and effect relations to the most random topics such as:

What do Sumo wrestlers and teachers have in common?

What is the economic model of drug dealers?

Does it matter what school you go to if you want to make it in life?

Why are there Black names and white names,how do they play out in society?

The book won’t teach you how to data-analyze or do math. However, it will nudge you into asking the right questions. Read it. It is fun.

2. Everybody Lies — Really hilarious lies.

If you thought Freakonomics was entertaining, this might be even better.

This take on human beings based on google-searches throws a not-so-impressive light on humanity.

There is no arguing that google searches are honest. I wouldn’t type into google “How to pick my nose in public without being caught?” without genuinely being interested in the answer. People, in the confines of their bed and smartphone, are brutally honest about everything.Google knows!

Davidowitz analyses, among others, people’s sexual fantasies, their real and proclaimed voting habits, liberal (or extreme) ideologies, people’s genitalia-related insecurities, and all the things we are interested in (apparently).

3. Factfulness — Data Therapy

Factfulness is a data-based therapy for optimism.

Hans Rosling, a proven data visualization diva, having worked with the world bank, WHO, and who’s who of United Nations, left this piece of hope to the rest of the world days before he passed away.

Factfulness proves with brilliantly intuitive charts how amazing the world is. The number of children that are healthy today, the number of people not dying of hunger, reduced number of war-related deaths, and improving world education levels.

You name a parameter that is a benchmark for civilization, you have a chart for it that tells you we are at our ABSOLUTE PEAK in the history of existence.

There is an imminent need to recognize this and build on facts instead of on the purely devastating worldview offered by the media. The book is a must-read.

4. Fooled by Randomness — Being primate and living with it

If you classify the rest of the books into the genre data science, this would be on the rack opposite under “anti-data-science”.

Nassim Taleb explores how misused data analysis is in his field, as a trader on the stock market — A field that is a minefield of surprises.

He makes valid arguments about how your wealth acquired by investing in stocks that “magically” went up has absolutely nothing to do with how awesome you are and goes on to statistically prove that the odds are that you are an idiot. He also briefly talks about how NOT to be one.

Taleb is deaf to the entire world, including his editor. That is also the tone assumed.

Although it is heavily data-based, the book talks only in the context of investments and stocks and occasional philosophy and mostly arrogance. It is a fun read.

5. Thinking Fast and Slow — Why we do what we do

If these books were a Quentin Tarantino movie, you would immediately spot that they are all playing in the same universe and reference each other all the time.

Thinking Fast and Slow is the mother of all other works that talk about human behavior in number terms.

Kahneman also won the Nobel Prize for this economics-psychology masterpiece.

He divides the mind into a slow-thinking analytical brain (for example, while calculating 345x12), and a fast-thinking automatic one (deciding where to put the next step while taking a walk.)

Testing various specifics of human behavior on intuition, spending, risk-taking, assumptions in situations where our logic circuits are expected to come to play but don’t, it explains, very objectively and with all proof, the primates we are.

title: Business Process Decomposition — Your Key to a Successful Test Automation Strategy
content:
Business Process Decomposition — Your Key to a Successful Test Automation Strategy

The best way to achieve faster, error-free, high-quality software that slides smoothly and quickly into production is to automate as much of the process as efficiently as possible.

A crucial step inautomating everything that is automatableistest automation.It improves testing efficiency by eliminating human errors and exponentially improves the quality of the product. It is a long-term solution that saves hours of effort while radically reducing the costs and results in shorter development cycles.

Although test automation has been the industry hot-favorite for the past couple of years, many teams are yet to adopt it to its full capacity. This could be due to various considerations.

Some of these might be:

Retraining a team to learn the automation process.

Finding experts with the right skills to implement the test automation process.

Time consumed in understanding system complexity and developing a suitable test automation strategy that meets all needs over a long period of time.

There is no hard and fast rule as to what kind of tests are the most efficient and what constitutes a complete and accurate test. These decisions have to be strategically made based on the system environment.

The above reasons are why there is a need to implement an effectiveBusiness Process Decompositionto understand the system environment before deciding on a Test Automation Strategy.

What is Business Process Decomposition?

In order to develop an ideal test automation strategy, it is essential for all the stakeholders of the business process to understand the structure and model of the business.

Complexity is an intrinsic part of any design activity and test automation is not an exception. A test strategist should anticipate complexity regardless of the environment in advance and be deal with it with deserving attention to detail.

A Business Process Decomposition model encompasses the description of the organization in terms of people, technology, and goals.

A business process decomposition model is the breakdown of a business process into the component data that makes it up.

Example for Business Process Decomposition

Imagine you have a food delivery business. In order to understand the business and develop an ideal strategy for its software testing, you want to decompose it into component parts.

These components may comprise of:

GoalsRoadblocksSystemsMetrics and KPIDatasetsData Attributes

Your goals as a delivery agent are to:

receive orders accurately.

make the payment process easy.

Provide an accurate tracking system.

In order to make your goals,you will have to identify what problems are in your way. In this example,

Your ordering interface might be complex for a customer to use on cell-phone.

Your payment processes might not be up to date

Your maps may not have traffic information.

In order to check your metrics or KPIs, you might look at

Customer reviews

Delivery time trackers

Calls made to your call center

You will then reach out to the dashboards and databases of your company in order to retrieve information regarding previous orders, incidents, reviews, call center records, and other relevant information.

You set a benchmark of 4.5 starts for your business which you want to reach within the next quarter. These are your metrics, by which you decide the steps to be taken to improve your business.

That brings us to people. In order to retrieve and understand the information provided, you will then set up meetings with people responsible in various areas of your business, such as data experts, call center database experts.

All the above components — your goals, problems, metrics, KPIs, databases, systems, and people are a part of your business process decomposition.

It is imperative that you understand all these components of your business in order to understand the degree of automation that might be needed for your use-case.

Business component decomposition will help you identify and breakdown the functional aspects, processes, scope, problems, and deliverables in your organization into smaller aspects.

You can then go on to identify who in your organization would be responsible for which area of automation, what is the effort involved, how your automation can scale and be simplified.

What to watch while performing business process decomposition for your test automation strategy

One major roadblock in implementing a test automation strategy is the effort needed to develop a strategy and program for every part of the system. This effort can be minimized by a consistent decomposed model.

If the semantics of the organization are understood within the decomposed model uniformly it is easy to modularize larger systems, build partial models, and reuse them to fit different scenarios of the organization.

This also results in easier understanding of the models and easier change management.

The scope and depth of the decomposed model should also be suited to your test automation strategy. You might have to understand your business models based on systems, based on roles, and based on functions.

The following are only some examples of the models of decomposition that you could implement.

Functional Decomposition:Details the activities being performed in the context of a process

Informational Decomposition:Details the different datasets and their manipulations at various steps of the process

Behavioral Decomposition:Describes the flow of activities and how they are performed.

Organizational Decomposition:Describes the goals of the organization at every performed activity.

While building a model, it is important to answer the following questions:

Who, when, where, how, and why at every stage of the process.

It is undebatable that for fail-proof software, it is necessary to tighten all possible loose ends in software testing. Poor software testing has in the past resulted in many known catastrophes in the world, some involving major crashes of aircraft carriers and nationwide power outages. Testing is by no means to be taken lightly.

In order to catch bugs in time and make the software successful and safe, the go-to method is test automation. The major stepping stone in the process of test automation is business process decomposition.

title: Why Click-baiting is OK and How to do it Right
content:
Why Click-baiting is OK and How to do it Right

An Objective Mirror to Click-bait Culture

Let’s begin with addressing the cringe we hate to admit — Clickbait works. that’s the larget reason it exists, IT WORKS.

Just look at the Medium’s Guideline for good writing that addresses this issue:

There are a lot of places on the Internet for clickbait. Medium doesn’t want to be one of them.

The other guidelines being “Don’t Copy”, “Write a Clear Headline”, “Clean it up”,the choice of words on Click-bait avoidance seems like a mild threat from playful girlfriend. Granted, Click-bait on Medium looks and sounds different than on shady facebook feed or Buzzfeed stories but it baits just the same.

While onalternative mediayou might find baits that hook you up with“You wont believe”, go on to promise a bite-size read with“…these 6 things”and promise a high point with“(#4 will kill you)”, on Medium things are much subtler. Here is what my medium feed looks like, I made minor edits to the original titles so that the authors are not offended:

7 Reasons Why You Will End Up Being Mediocre in Your Life

There is a secret to Creating Good Content, Here it is.

Here is How I made a $100000 doing what you could totally do

9 things that MUST happen RIGHT NOW

If Click-bait is :

“A title with mild or strong exaggerations that make people click ”

these titles are clickbait. Here is how I define an honest title as opposed to a click bait —

“A title that is given bearing primarily on mind the content of the article and the intention of the author as opposed to it’s sales pitch. A non-clickbait title could itself serve the purpose of being an embellishment to the content.”

Clickbaits are sometimes acceptable. Live with it.

The onus of selling the article to readers lies on the writer, and the title is his only pitch.

There was a phase of time in the world where mild click bait has become not only acceptable but also an expectation from the content creator, Just like the the time when everyone just accepted it was OK appear at the airport in casuals.

Look at what the writer of the popular book “Design of Everyday Things”, and ex-Chief Design Officer at Apple, Don Norman, said when he was forced to change the title of his book :

“The final nail in the coffin (before changing the title) came when the senior executive who introduced me to the audience praised the book, Damned the title (then Psychology of Everyday things, Now Design of Everyday Things) and asked the audience to read the book despite the title”

He was forced toBuy-baithis readers! Clickbait stems from the same roots. It exists not only because content creators are coy, but also also because we are stupid and dance to the tune of our emotions at the drop of a hat. If this wasn’t true, Click-bait would have been a thing we would have outgrown already just like we outgrew emails from the Nigerian Prince.

Having defended the demon, here are somethings that you should know if your title is targeted more at “Kaching$!” of clicks than honestly mirroring your content :

1. The more click-baity your title, the more your content should pop.

Just like how when you tell an offensive joke — the offensiver it gets, the better it be for you to get off the hook. If no one laughs, you’re just a jerk. If no one likes your content, the click-baiting just made you look much worse than just the article would have. Remember, some of the best selling books have simple titles.

2. Honest baits are OK to hang

If it is an experience unique to you, worth sharing, insightful and VERY RARE, it is OK to tell it in all it’s glory in the title.“Here is how I survived a Month without Food and Water in the Amazon”is perfectly alright IF it is true. You don’t have to downplay your achievements for the fear of looking like a bait.

3. Write about unintuitive but verifiable facts

And, pick these up as titles.“Yeast was the trending search on Google today, guess why?”.This is a verifiable fact that was trending during corona since people were all about home-baked bread. This is a perfectly acceptable title since it is the truth — not a penny more.

4. Write for more than clicks

If shakespeare wrote for clicks we wouldn’t have hadJulius Ceaserbut “5 Reasons My Best Friend Backstabbed me”, if Mark twain didnt care for the adventure, we would have had “5 times I played hookey at school, #3 will shock you” and not Tom Sawyer. Sit back, relax and write like you mean it. The clicks will come, and oh will they come from the right places!

5. Recognize that people read beyond titles

If there is anything I learnt from my writing on medium, it is that people read much more beyond the title than you assume they do. They know what’s a bad bait and whats worth the bait. Build a faithful audience by consistent writing and type away without bothering too much about the title.

Clickbait is a gray zone and you can explore and find out what works best. What is an absolute no-go is bait and not make the minimum effort at delivery. You are then no better than someone that sets up website like foogle.com and hopes that atleast 1% of internet population finger-stumbles on their keyboard. Make your time on the internet count. Use titles wisely.

title: 11 Tiny Excel Tricks to Crunch It Like A Consultant
content:
11 Tiny Excel Tricks to Crunch It Like A Consultant

Lesser-known excel tricks for being awesome

1. Don't touch the mouse if you don't have to.

If you hit alt, you will get all the keyboard shortcuts. Navigate only with the keyboard.

2. The apostrophe in front helps you retain your formula without executing it.

Use it to write complex long formulas to retain it while you drift away for a while.

3. Toss the mouse and the point-and-click.

Go into editing cell text mode by pressing F2.

4. Concatenate using the &.

Instead of

=CONCATENATE(A1,”-”,B1"-”,C1)

go

=A1&”-”&B1&”-”&C1

5. Use F4 to “Repeat the last action”

Use this until your F4 disintegrates. Thick lines, color cell, change text color, you name it, F4 it.

6. Leave the Shift and the Caps key alone.

Type your formulas in the small case. They will auto-capitalize if you write them correctly. Your fingers are free-er to do the right thing then.

7. Formulas you must know on fingertips

VLOOKUP, LEFT, RIGHT, TRIM.

Remember, YouMUSTsort first for VLOOKUP

8. When in doubt, SUBSTITUTE

If you are not sure how many leading and trailing spaces you have in your cells, use the substitute function instead of trim to remove ALL spaces.

sidebar: Use clean to remove all the non-printable messy outputs and retain ASCII texts.

9. If you are writing complex formulas, document them there.

Write documentation for formulas adding a

+N(“Your Documentation Here”)

Example:

10. With F9, check partial formula results

First, be very careful with the f9 key because, if you don't select any part of your formula, it will replace your formula irreversibly with the result value. Press escape to exit debug mode.

That said, select the range of your formula, you will see the actual numbers in the range instead of cell references. You will see the results for the selected parts which you can use to evaluate your formula.

11. If you have a german keyboard, I’m throwing in a bonus here.

alt gr + any digit is automatic super scripting ;)

That's all for now. Here is mylinkedinand here is mymediumprofile. See you soon!

title: Indian Mantra — Nahrung für die Seele
content:
Indian Mantra — Nahrung für die Seele

Ihr Körper ist ein Tempel. Die Nahrung, die Sie zu sich nehmen, bildet den Tempel, in dem Sie leben.

Die alte indische Weisheit besagt, dass der Körper durch sieben Energiezentren in Form vonden Chakren, im Gleichgewicht ist.

Jedes Chakra ist für einen anderen Aspekt des körperlichen und geistigen Wohlbefindens verantwortlich. Wenn die sieben Chakras im Gleichgewicht sind, ist auch Ihre Seele im Gleichgewicht. Dies sind die Chakren, die die im Logo vonIndian Mantradargestellt sind.

Bei Indian Mantra bieten wir nicht nur Nahrung für Ihren Körper, sondern auch für Ihre Seele.

Die Chakren

Das Wurzelchakra oder Muladhara, das sich an der Basis Ihrer Wirbelsäule befindet, ist verantwortlich für Ihr Gefühl von Sicherheit und Stabilität.

Das Sakralchakra oder Svadhisthana ist ein Chakra für Ihre kreative Energie.

Das Solarplexus-Chakra oder Manipura steht für Ihr Selbstvertrauen und Ihr Selbstwertgefühl.

Das Herzchakra oder Anahata steht für Ihre Fähigkeit zu lieben und Mitgefühl zu zeigen.

Das Kehlchakra oder Vishuddha steht für Ihre Fähigkeit, dich zu artikulieren und zu kommunizieren.

Das dritte Augenchakra oder Ajna befindet sich zwischen Ihren Augen. Es ist verantwortlich für Ihre Vorstellungskraft und Intuition.

Das Kronenchakra oder Sahasrar befindet sich über dem Kopf. Es steht für Ihre Rolle im Universum und den größeren Sinn des Lebens.

Das Ziel von Indian Mantra ist es, Körper und Seele durch unser Essen in Einklang zu bringen. Den Gewürzen, die wir in der indischen Küche verwenden, werden heilende Eigenschaften nachgesagt, und sie finden in der alten indischen Medizin, dem Ayurveda, Verwendung.

Bei Indian Mantra kochen wir authentische Rezepte und beziehen unsere Gewürze direkt von ihren Ursprungsquellen.

title: Why Medium Should Have Ads
content:
Why Medium Should Have Ads

It’s why everyone is hanging out elsewhere

Selling is not tacky. Selling is not a crime.

Writers, both on and outside of Medium would agree. While brands approach and interact with influencers on Instagram and YouTube, Medium writers are left longing for 2$ at the end of the month.

Why isn’t any money coming in here?

Because Medium wants to stay elite.

Selling is beneath Medium.

Unless you pay, your eyeballs don’t generate revenue on here. Unfortunately for the general public, paying for yet another subscription service on top of their streaming, TV, magazines, music, professional networks, and all the other Gaga, is not an attractive option. The internet is practically free. Good luck selling blog posts here!

As a result, Medium’s writers are its largest reader base. The platform practically exists for itself without motivating the world outside to join the party. Each time another reader comes here, he is engulfed as an individual, without necessarily motivating new readers to open their wallets.

So far, every attempt Medium made to make the platform better was targeted only at the writers. More bonuses, more designs, fewer publications— All without improving the main agenda point— Reaching more REAL readers. (Those who are genuinely interested in the content, not because they can clickbait— think Instagram)

How should a platform sustain itself with a bunch of creators and no consumers? Why aren’t the reads by non-members through Google searches worth any money?

Medium’s elitism is ridiculous. Banning ads does not mean that the platform has high standards. On the contrary, turning a penny through advertising proves the platform's real worth.

Circulating money is not making money

Contrary to making their writers generate value out of thin air, Medium’s current policy just circulates a fixed pot of money that their writers pour into the platform.

The platform just takes from one set of writers and gives to the other. It There is no “new money” through investors, advertisers, and marketers who see value here.

This policy causes the platform to stagnate into a set of pointless clickbait articles — Which are ironically, also about the platform.

As a writer on Medium who respects your own content, you cannot escape these titles in your feed:

“How to make money on Medium”, “How to get 100 followers”, “How to make 6 figures by writing”, “More platforms to write for money”, “Writing about writing”, “How I quit my job writing”.

Medium has become a go-to place for clickbait Gurus to become rich.

I think it is about time Medium gives its writers some serious visibility.

Medium has the potential to become a long-text blogging platform for celebrities, companies, markets, and beyond. As of now, it seems only limited to “elite” intellectual celebrities (Barack Obama, Naseem Taleb, and all) and clickbait gurus.

The platform hardly makes it into the books of “normal” people who have no interest in writing. It offers no big steps than (maybe) 100 dollars if any at all, to serious writers.

Get rid of this elitism, Let Medium shine through ads. Let them welcome talent instead of clickbait.Tony Stubblebine, please oh please, give us real readers.

title: Best Mouse to Prevent Carpal Tunnel
content:
Best Mouse to Prevent Carpal Tunnel

Vertical mouse vs. a static mouse with a track-ball vs. a regular mouse.

I have sensitive hands. After a long day at work, my hands seem to have reduced “tension” in them and I felt the constant need to stretch my forearms and wrists.

I wanted a durable ergonomic mouse that doesn’t make my palm feel like it is “hanging” in the air after a long day. I needed one only for office work and not a gaming mouse.

I tried a track-ball mouse ( The one in the middle above), and a couple of weeks later, a vertical mouse. Here is what worked and what did not.

tl;dr:For a healthy hand, get all three of them and switch them up. If you want only one, get the vertical mouse.

Trackball Mouse

I got theLogitech M575 Wireless Trackball Mouse ERGObecause it had the most reviews on amazon among those of its kind, and 4.5 stars. It also was in the “middle” of the price range at a decent 35 Eur in Germany.

The next option wasLogitech MX Ergo, which was twice the price but also offered adjustable tilt-angle. I chose the former for the price.

This mouse took some getting-used-to. I could not easily digest the fact that a mouse does not move at all. I had to control the cursor position with my thumb.

At the beginning it felt like a lot of work. I switched very often to my regular mouse. I left both of them connected to the computer, in case I have to panic and reach for the regular mouse.

As time passed, I could tell that not moving my wrist was actually very relaxing. The stability of this mouse grew on me and now I don’t reach for a regular mouse at all. Here is a summary.

Trackball mouse experience:

Pros:

Yes, IT DOES HELP in relaxing the hand and helped my symptoms.

The tilt of the mouse is alright. If you want something adjustable, trythis, which offers flexible tilt but at twice the price.

The fact that the wrist doesn't move a lot helps my arms relax.

Has back and forward buttons for page shift.

Once you get used to it, it is great for office work.

Cons

The side of the palm does not rest completely on the ground. Maybe my palms are too small.

Because of not completely being able to rest my palm in its “natural position”, I sometimes take a break to tilt my palm to rest vertically.

The mouse looks like it is built for larger hands.

Took about two weeks to get used to.

When colleagues walk up to my desk, they need a regular mouse to use my computer.

Vertical Mouse

I wasn’t sure if I’d get used to a track-ball mouse. I got a vertical mouse to test if that was the better option. I bought theTrust Verto vertical mouse, because it also had a large number of ratings and an overall 4.5 stars.

This mouse hardly needed any adjustment time. It was the same old mouse with a 180-degree tilt. The side of my hand rests completely on the desk and it is a very pleasant feeling.

The build is very comfortable, with a smooth surface finish that feels amazing in your palms.

Pros:

Yes, it helps relax my arm.

No adjustment time needed

The side of the hand rests completely on the ground, feeling very natural.

Good looking mouse.

Fits perfectly into my rather small hand.

Cons:

This particular model is a wired mouse. They have a wireless version for about the same price.

Wrist movement is still the same as with a regular mouse.

The forward and backward buttons are awkwardly placed and I cannot really use them.

In the end, I’d recommend that you get one of each kind and switch them up so that your arm does not stick to one motion over a long time. Both these mouses are a good alternative to your regular mouse. But if you want only one get the vertical mouse.

I am actually contemplating getting an ergonomic keyboard too because the ergonomic mice were actually value addition to my hand health.

Currently, I use all three of them. The two “special mice” more regularly than the normal mouse. If I had to get rid of one mouse permanently, it’d be the regular mouse.

Edit: Update after a year, the trackball mouse is infinitely more comfortable after getting used to it.

I am Sruthi Korlakunta, I write on tech, lifestyle, and books (I’m a top writer on this topic). If you have a story to share, hit me up onLinkedIn! I have 890+ followers, over 225k reads, and counting.

Read all you want on Medium by joining with my referral link here. It costs 5$ a month and changed my life!

title: How Reading 50 books a Year Changed My Personality
content:
How Reading 50 books a Year Changed My Personality

and how to read more

Today is the Golden day. Since last July, I picked up, devoured, and laid back down 50 books in all. Age, weight, time, distance, length, whatever the form, I am guilty of being infected by the popular number fever. This spread also to my reading habit.

[I won’t go on and preach about how it is important to or not to have a number, do what floats your boat. But what I can do, is tell about what this number did to me. 49 might have done it too, 51 equally well. Maybe so would have 3. (But nobody got a Ph.D. reading 3 books, just saying.) ]

What did I read?

Mostly non-fiction, some biographies, some trashy novels, 2 Books of short stories, One book of poetry, some self-help, and some classics. so, everything. Here is a list:

1. What it did to me

It is mind-blowing how well-researched the world is, how many people elucidate the most complex life problems, and hand it to you like a peeled out banana.

Peace out.

If you are high-activity, border-line ADHD like me, reading a tonne will definitely help. No, it won’t make you multitask less. But it gives you the calmest possible single-focus hobby you could practice your peace on.

Come to terms with the drama of life

I was seriously depressed for a bit. My work needed me to travel quite a lot, which ruled out the option of therapy. Reading the right books really put me back on track and in good terms with my self. The thing with advice is, none of it is good until you realize what works for you in your own daily-puzzle of life. If you keep on it long enough, you will realize that all advice has some amount of truth to it, even the bad ones. You will appreciate your smallness in the massive world of problems and solutions.

I empathize more

Straight from high-school, many of us ship with a tonne of unresolved issues. Women getting jealous of other women, bad relationships, macho-men, all sorts of mental disorders follow us like a swarm of bees. All because you cannot be in other’s shoes. Reading more puts a perspective on things. It makes you ask “why is this person not how I want him to be, oh may be he needn’t be". True, a real-life experience never matches up to a book, but reading prepares you for life like the world’s most empathetic coach.

I listen more

Maybe it is that every interpersonal relation based book seems to have a central theme, or maybe it is just that reading develops curiosity — I listen more since I read more. You might feel like you are already the master of social skills and a magician of an orator but trust me, there is more to learn. Both from books and other speakers. Reading helps me wait and listen.

I am comfortable in my shoes.

I was a depressed woman. There was no place on earth that felt like mine and no one that felt like mine. Reading more brought me at peace with myself and others. The vast amount of experiences you go through while reading helps you see the bigger picture. I now accept myself for how I am more than I did before and also understand that not everyone has to like that. I learned to let go and to leave gracefully when the time, people and place aren’t mine. All with the help of some books.

How to read more

Pick your books with love

What makes you depends on what you read, not just the fact that you read. You are the master of what you pick and choose, so I trust you to choose wisely. Not by force, not necessarily self-help. There is a universe of help out there to choose good books. I find my next book with the help of the app Goodreads, Newyork times lists and Reviews on seller website. If you don’t like what you are reading, put it down and move on. Don’t force it down your eyes.

I e-read

Getting an e-reader massively upped my book-consumption. Now I want a book. voila! Now I have it. No waiting, no packaging material, no space in the shelf where already-read books sit lonely. My mother owns an e-reader too, and we share books. So we get more per book each time I buy one. It fits in my coat pocket and goes with me everywhere. So I read more and am never bored at a train station. I read about 150% more since I have the e-reader. I use Amazon’s kindle, but take your pick.

After the current TV-series, start a book and not another series

Whichever show/shows you are binging right now, finish it. Then, pick a show with shorter episode time or shorter run so that you have more time to read. (Come on, we all know that is why you don't read). I promise you will also sleep better.

Read less on Facebook, Insta and Twitter

This is a chicken and egg problem. Do you read fewer books because you read stuff on the internet, or do you read more on the internet because you don’t have something legit on your hands?

I for one have consciously minimized my social media consumption to binge on some well-researched and written books instead. No regrets there.

If there are only a bunch of things left for the Human race to keep and the rest to be thrown away, we should definitely keep the ability to read. I’m thankful I found a way to keep alive far from home and family. I’d totally recommend actively reading more, if not a thousand books a month. It pays off.
