title: 5 Incredible Books To Understand the Current World
content:
5 Incredible Books To Understand the Current World

Stay on top of world affairs, it might just save you.

Will China dominate the world economy? What does it mean to identify as they/them? Will Climate change cause you to move? How will this war end?

There is a lot on the news but you don’t know how it is going to affect you until you pay those additional 100 dollars of raised energy prices. Things change very fast. Not all values you learned in school are valid now. Cautionary tales of back then might even become your worst enemies.

To understand the world as it is, to stay abreast of right and wrong, or even to pick a side, you should inform yourself. Here is a list of books that will help you start.

Different: Gender from the eyes of a Primatologist, by Frans De Waal

Age of Ambition: China, by Evan Osnos

title: To India: I’m Home Again. This Time I’m 30+
content:
To India: I’m Home Again. This Time I’m 30+

Hyderabad as a woman in your 30s

“Lady, go put yourself at the very back of the line. That’s how queues work”, I blocked the way of three defenseless women at the metro station who were quite taken back by my audacity. I would have normally started the sentence with “auntie”, but I just realized this lady could be anywhere between 30 and 50 years old.My peer? Oh my God.

The women gave up pretty much without contest but a whole bunch of men in the queue behind me, encouraged by a woman (me) on their side, ganged up against them. The three women were scared. I felt like an arse ‘ole for starting it.Well, always good to be home.

I collected my tickets and took the metro to the mall for some clothes. Everything there was priced, sized, and weather-coordinated more to Europe and the USA than to Hyderabad, but with better quality.Okay why?

While I casually browsed, I ran into a woman I used to know at college 10 years ago. I barely recognized her. She looked, well, Old. That was enough to jolt me into the trial room and evaluate my own face and body, whether my own age showed up in my sunken eyes and/or love handles just like hers. “Man!” I refreshed all my fitness resolutions in that instant.“Also why does everyone look angry and stressed when I run into them but never on their Instagram?”.

The sales guy approached me to help with my shopping and explained all the offers they have. I was enjoying the premium Indian service after getting used to the famously dysfunctional customer-centricity in Germany. That was until he added “oh, fyi, you can also redeem your offer in the men’s and kids section too, for your husband or something...”first of all, Ouch. “Thank you, I’ll look by myself from now on.” I picked clothes which I planned to wear in Europe anyway and made my way out of the mall, playingguess how old he/she isat strangers.

As I ubered home I noticed how everyone was always coughing and sneezing, winter -appropriately. I realized I can stop withholding my coughs to not scare people, no one seems to care here unlike in Germany. I thought about the last conversation I had with my colleague in Germany —Oh you have a cold? Where did you get it from? Was it at the Christmas party?The Super-Spreader event.

People of Hyderabad simply didn’t care where their colds come from or where they go. They just live them out like it’s Tuesday. At least the cold seems to be age-agnostic, thank god.

Another day, another series of old friends, most of them advising me to finally settle the F down. Marry. I look inside my heart for signs of fear and empathy at what they’re saying. “What part of the story should I unpack here to this person I’ll see again just once next year?”I let it slide.

Meanwhile I found out that my dad’s been telling the people of the town that my mom’s “bad upbringing” is why I’m still unmarried. I went from 😶 to 😐 to 😡 to 😒 very quickly, and let that one slide too. I’m too old to fight this shitty battle. “You deal with your husband lady. You’re the one who married him.”I told my mother and walked away.

I reluctantly agreed to a ‘pooja’, a religious ceremony I attended for inaugurating my new apartment (which I paid for, by the way). The priest duly informed me that as an unmarried woman, I cannot preside over the ceremony and my parents should instead. I clenched my teeth and agreed. It is to appease them that I agree to this little-girl-tea-party anyway.

That night I read a passive aggressive Whatsapp status from my friend about how you should be selfish and tough as a woman in an Indian household, and how people are judgemental and unfriendly.Umm, I thought to myself, what could have happened there? She married a man of her mother’s choosing at 23 and has a beautiful child and an amazing career.Turns out, she visited India during her Christmas break and was told “to try again, this time for a boy child”. Wow. I understand her fury. In fact I was surprised at how she wasn’t madder. But it is comforting to know that it isn’t just me, that there is really no escape from thisScheisse.

Everyone is showing up to meetings either with a partner orinspiteof a partner. Some were going throughmarriage-proposalsthrough their parents, meeting boys and girls of their age they’ve never seen before. Others were going through divorce from a marriagetoo early. Some were going through couples therapy. Some were new parents. Unanimously, they all agree that I should be married.

I wonder why it comes easy to them and why they took the plunge. How such a love looks, and if it’ll ever find it’s way to me. Well, whether it does or not, one thing is certain — life goes on.

I took one long look at my bank account and decided I still have a while to go before having actual problems. That means a good night after all.

title: Visits to India are Heart-breaking and NRIs Are Not Honest About It
content:
Visits to India are Heart-breaking and NRIs Are Not Honest About It

Notes from my last trip to India — Bangalore and Tirupati

As an Indian by birth, I’m not going to rave about how life-changing it is to live abroad, study abroad, or work abroad. Primarily because it triggers anyone in India who hasn’t. Claiming that something has bettered one’s life, and it wasn’t in India, is somehow offensive to a lot of Indians.

This, I presume, is also the reason why NRI (Non-Resident Indians) are not honest about what they actually feel when they go to India. According to friends and family there, you’re supposed to be “guilty” For choosing a different life, for “abandoning” your responsibility towards the remainder of your family and the society at large.

Everybody talks of “stay and work for your country” as if it were a clearly laid-out path that we chose to abandon. Far from it, in India, even the highest levels of education and clearing the toughest competitive exams in the country leaves you groveling — first for a decent job, then decent treatment at a job, then the inifinite hurdles you need to jump if you don’t want to be utterly corrupt (both my parents worked in the government, and being not-corrupt is seen as a thing of cinematic arrogance).

On top of all this, god forbid, you are a woman — Every breath you take is a “how dare she!”, but ofcourse, we are supposed to pretend that everything is perfect andviolence against women happens everywhere so why talk about only India. It does not matter to the discussion that women living abroad report feeling safer on a day-to-day basis, are taken seriously at work and at home, and relieved from prejudices of the family and judgment about what they wear.

In truth, Indians and, more unfortunately, the Indian Government, treats “bare-minimums” as undeserving luxuries to its people. This “undeserving” POV begins at home, where people give “inferior” cutlery and plates to household workers. But they do not seem to notice that the same inferior treatment extends to everyone in the family by virtue of their age, sex, who they are, and whatnot.

Cut to the bigger picture, the general population is never deserving of roads, clean water, air that doesn’t reek of sewage, and nights that aren't blaring with traffic noises and stray dogs fighting. Why should anyone fight against these? These are “normal”, and anyone claiming otherwise is just an arrogant prick that has been “westernized”.

God knows how hard it is for people to leave behind their moms, family ties, and familiar homes permanently, just to have a fair chance at life, career and family. No, NRIs don’t do this wholeheartedly.

“Then why don’t you stay and change the country?”

The shortest answer is that India doesn’t want to change. For any change to take place, for any “development” to happen, the prerequisite is that people should want it.

I remember my father having a conversation with a cab driver about how privatizing bus traffic will mean cleaner and more reliable transport, but it will also mean that there will be no free travel for women (An unsustainable freebie introduced to win votes while not letting the country develop), so the cab driver shouldn’t vote for the idea.

The irony is that neither my dad nor my mother uses that transport because of how horrendous and unsafe that experience is. Yet, they don’t want it to be better because :

The government that is introducing the change isn’t their caste and community.

It doesn’t matter to them; they can afford a cab ride over long distances.

(Ironically, the cab rides are equally unsafe, because none of the roads outside of big cities have bare minimum rules, and it always feels like a high chance you won’t make it to the destination with the amount of ghost riders on the road.)

Long story short, people in India don’t want a better country with regulations, order, and a safe world for everyone. They specifically want a world that is safeonly for them, and is unsafe and unlivable for everyone else.

Another story. I was watching a movie with a few neighbors — It glorifies domestic violence against women and preaches that women should just “brush it off” and stick with their husbands. (This movie happens to be a big hit among the local population and claims to solve the problem of “divorce” — It is titled“Mana Sankara Varaprasad Garu”)

When I mentioned how horrified I was listening to this perspective and that physical violence should be a valid cause for separation, the room got very uncomfortable.How dare I suggest that divorce is a good thing? How can women today be so outrageous?The reactions were from all men and some women in the room.

That's when I understood, they don’t get it because they don’t want to get it. They know it is bad, but it is convenient that way, and those who suffer the consequences are too small to fight back.

The reason so many Indians who left the country willfully choose to stay out, even if they can go back, is that one simply cannot lead a life expecting to start a revolution at every turn. Sometimes, people just want to be, and it is ok to move to places that let you do this in peace.

In this visit, family, connection, and love were all great to experience. But it was very hard to look away from all the normalized abuse in society. Abuse of people by the government, of water bodies, of children who are deprived of clean air and moral values (a child of 5 told me flat out that he won't clean up because he is not a girl), of women in their homes, of people travelling on unsafe roads, of lack of amenities in the busiest and richest cities of the country. It was heartbreaking to visit India knowing that it won’t change — primarily because it doesn’t want to.

Read Also:Coming Home to India After a Long time

title: The Chia Seed Fallacy
content:
The Chia Seed Fallacy

The proof is in the pudding

No, I’m not telling you to that they’re going to give you cancer, or that some country in Europe banned them for finding lead. Chia seeds are healthy today as they were yesterday.

I have been bloated for a while now. No amount of weighted-vest cardio has helped me, and for the love of god, I couldn’t figure out if I’m working out 4 times a week, why I look like I never got off the couch in the last 4 years.

Enter Chia pudding.

Here was my recipie for the disaster:

Every morning, without fail, I consumed the following overnight pudding:

3 tbsp of chia seeds

1 scoop of Protein powder

about 50 ml of milk

Blueberries or mangoes to top off.

I was so proud of my creation!

On top of this, since I was trying to lose weight, I added broccoli to my lunch, like I was competing in a superfood consumption triathlon.

While this is not exactly a disaster, it explains why I look and feel like a balloon on most days. The chia seeds are already causing “traffic jam”(they are like sticky sponges) in the narrow hallway of my gut, and by adding Broccoli, I welcomed a gang of gas-producing teenagers (a cruciferous vegetable that contains a sugar calledraffinose, turns out,that bacteria love to ferment). Add to this an occasional Coke Zero or sparkling water. OMG!

In my tummy, the daily drill has been that the broccoli is stuck behind the chia, fermenting and creating gas, cementing my stomach pooch into a hard rock that won't budge.

Calorie Monster in Disguise

Besides the “traffic jam,” turns out, the math just didn’t add up for a fat-loss goal. I thought chia seeds was good fat and great protein, with the protein powder on top too! but I was actually eating aCalorie Bombthat left me hungry two hours later.

The Math of My Morning Disaster:

3 tbsp Chia Seeds:~180 calories (and a staggering 15g of fiber)

1 scoop Protein Powder:~120 calories

50ml Milk:~30 calories

Mango/Blueberry topping:~70 calories

Total:~400 calories!!!

For a 400-calorie investment, you’d expect to feel full, right? I can inhale that pudding in about 4 licks. It is physically tiny. It fit in a small glass jar. It didn’t need chewing.

In the world of satiety,volume matters.Compare that 400-calorie sludge to a plate of three scrambled eggs with a massive pile of sautéed spinach and mushrooms.

The Fibre overload

Then there’s the fiber. A woman of my size needs about 25g a day, My breakfast was15g or 70% of my daily limit — before 9:00 AM.

My poor intestines were dealing with a massive “fiber hit” that they couldn’t possibly hydrate fast enough. Instead of “cleansing” me, the chia was sitting there, expanding.

Add to this the “Superfood Triathlon” lunch of broccoli I was basically a walking chemistry experiment.

Stop, breathe, debloat.

Once I realized I was essentially concrete-filling my digestive tract, I had to stop being a Wellness Warrior. I staged a gut intervention.

If you’re currently rocking a Chia Pooch and feeling like a human parade float, here is my 3-step rescue plan:

1. Downsize

Drop the seed-size in the pudding, I dropped from 3 tablespoons to 1.That goes from15g of fiber(a total gut-bomb) to a manageable5g.

To make up for the lost “fullness,” I cut the protein scoop and added two boiled eggs on the side. I stayed full until 2:00 PM atleast.

2. Grease the Wheels

It took about 3 days for the bloadt to clear. Cue Hot Ginger Tea. And water. For every tablespoon of chia, drink an extra 250ml of water. If you aren’t drinking it, the seeds will steal it from your body. Chug warm water like it's your day job.

4. The Gym Flip

If you add the stress of high-intensity cardio (I was doing high incline walk and 2.8mph speed for 40 mins with a weighted vest), your body will store tummy fat. It is also a cue that muscle is a pain to carry, since it slows your cardio. So I rearranged my gym schedule.

I first lift, then do a 20 min cardio without the vest but at incline, and at the end, close with my high-pump weighted vest cardio.

The Moral of the Story

You can’t out-train a digestive disaster. My unfit look wasn’t a lack of discipline. Within 72 hours of cutting the chia overload and flushing out the broccoli-gas, my stomach physically flattened. I didn’t lose 5kg of fat overnight, but I look like I did. I lost trapped air and “sticky sponge” seeds.

Listen to your gut. If it feels like a rock, stop throwing more “superfood” stones at it.

title: Tinder has an 800 Page File On You
content:
Tinder has an 800 Page File On You

Including data about exactly when you get horny.

Judith Duportailfirst installed tinder after she went through a break-up. Reading through the 1700 sent messages on her tinder report, she looks slightly embarrassed. On one occasion, she had used the same pickup line on 16 matches and then ghosted all of them.

After reading some horrifying truths on tinder’s practices, Judith decided to ask tinder for her personal data. She wrote Tinder, hoping to find out herattractiveness score.

She received 800 sheets of paper with every last conversation she had, pictures she liked both on and off of tinder, and some more intimate information best left undisclosed.

They decide whether you are hot or not.

Tinder’s Elo algorithm (They claim that they no longer use it, but identical alternatives are likely still in place) rated users based on their“league”.

This means the higher number of right swipes you get, the higher is your score. It does not stop there. If someone with a high attractiveness score swipes right on you, your score goes up. However if someone “unattractive” swipes left on you, you are doomed.

They then use these scores to show you people in yourleague.Your profile, attitude, interests, and other details seem hardly important.

What they have on you.

People tend to disclose more information on dating apps than elsewhere since it is often a place they go when they feel vulnerable.

In her widely sharedarticle that appeared inThe GuardianJudith wrote about the scary amount of detail on her report. She says:

“I took a trip into my hopes, fears, sexual preferences, and deepest secrets. Tinder knows me so well. It knows the real, inglorious version of me…”

Tinder states in its Terms and conditions that the information you share on the app may not be secure. The app had information on Judiths music tastes, likes on Facebook, the kind of men she was interested in (Race, age etc). Tinder also knows the times at which you are most active, deducing when you are vulnerable and when you feel lonely.

In return, tinder claims to give you perfect matches and suitable profiles. It is up to you to choose whether you are willing to pay the price because the terms and conditions are clear.

Every app you use talks to others and shares data about you. There might be thousands of pages of information describing your persona out there. You have no realistic way to find out what they do with your information or how they manipulate what you see and who you meet. It is not easy to find out either.

People who go through the pain of getting their data from these companies hire professional lawyers and data privacy experts.Read this articleby Judith’s lawyer, Paul Olivier Dehaye, the co-founder ofPersonalData.IO— a company dedicated to data protection. On top of all that, Tinder gave in only because she was a journalist.

In the end, Duportail did not find out her attractiveness score, but she wrote her findings in her book, originally published in french — L’amour sous algorithme.

title: The Story of Miss India 2020, Manasa Varanasi
content:
The Story of Miss India 2020, Manasa Varanasi

An interview with Manasa’s family.

What if all your dreams come true?

What if you considered yourself a “commoner”, and one day you are shot to fame, and the world realizes that you are a star?

This is the story ofManasa Varanasi, 23, crownedVLCC Femina Miss India 2020this February.

Her cousinAdvaitValluri is a friend and lives across the street from me. Advait and Manasa grew up together and talked regularly right until the night before her winning.

I followed this thread to see whatAdvaitand his wifeSivaniSaraswatula had to say about their sister’s sensational rise to fame.

Everybody knows what happened to Aishwarya Rai and Priyanka Chopra in the years following their title as the most beautiful women in the world. But what happens in the days following the massive win? What did life look like before?

The big day

February 10, 2021.

Manasa’s family— her grandmother, mother, father, and sister were huddled at the TV watching her on her walk of fame.

Her mother was excited and terrified as she realized hers was no longer a “common” family.

She couldn’t tell whether she wants her daughter to be crowned the prettiest woman in the country or if she’d prefer a quiet life. Either way, it was too late to ask that question.

“I myself was as delighted as I was surprised!

We are a traditional and academic family, with almost everybody, including Manasa, being engineers, you see”, says Advait, himself a Project Manager at Audi in Germany, as he recollects the excitement in their family that night.

“The year leading up to her crowning moment, we saw her do modeling assignments. But the magnitude of her determination did not really sink-in with the family until she came home one day with the title of Miss Telangana.

Now she was Miss India! ”

How it all started

“It was initially difficult for our grandmother, who lives with us and has until then only known a traditional lifestyle. Often, there were strangers who rang up Manasa’s mother to tell her to watch what their daughter is doing”.

During her time in college as a student of Computer Science Engineering, Manasa was active in extra-curricular activities like dancing and fashion shows. She garnered attention as a pretty and talented young woman.

Unsurprisingly, she drew eye-balls. She was approached by local businesses to model for their enterprises.

“She was happy to support boutiques of independent women in Hyderabad through her assignments. That’s how it all started”.

Since then, the word spread and change came fast.

The news on the TV connected with the glamour world is not always in the best light. Manasa’s family was understandably apprehensive through her days as a hobby-model.

For somebody with no experience in show-biz, there is a valid fear of what is“on the other side”,especially with their little girl now involved.

They had to deal with this uncertainty and the hail of advice from strangers. There were often doubts about where the path should end, but Manasa, like always, won gracefully.

“She was never the one for stressful argumentation. She was always clear about her goals and has been politely assertive”, says Advait, clearly proud of his sister.

The family often had to assure Manasa’s parents that“it’s going to be okay”. Such assurances were important for the parents to come to terms with the new world their daughter has opened to them.

Paving her way, one step at a time

She knew she had the X-factor. She wanted to live it out.

The year was 2019.

With little experience in the field of glamour and modeling, Manasa had no formal training for the state-wide pageant of Miss Telangana. However, she was a trained singer and aBharatanatyamdancer and was familiar with the field of performing arts.

She followed where her passion took her and participated for the first time in the Miss Telangana Pageant of 2019. Though she did not win the first time, she learned everything there was to learn.

For the Miss Telangana pageant of 2020, she trained herself in her presence, public-speaking, and demeanor. She won.

“I am proud of how much she has evolved since her appearance at Miss Telangana 2019. The calm and grace with which she received the winning announcement at the Miss India pageant, it’s just beautiful”,

says Sivani, who has seen her sister-in-law grow as India’s top beauty queen.

Gearing up for Miss India

The winners of all 31 state-level pageants of India went on to participate in an online pageant (thanks to the pandemic). The best 15 were invited to the final walk in Mumbai.

Throughout the process, Manasa was her own make-up artist, camera person, and coach.

“She even paid for everything by herself from her savings. Winning the Miss Telangana title was helpful. It put her on the radar for sponsors.

But the actual support came only after she was invited to Mumbai. She practically managed everything single-handedly until then.”

Her colleagues and bosses were supportive of her goals and respected the fact that she needed time to practice for the pageant. For that, says Advait, Manasa is immensely thankful.

Once the realization set-in about how big her endeavor is, and that she achieved what is many a girl’s dream, Manasa’s parents are now immensely proud of their girl.

What is Manasa doing now?

The winner of Miss India is to represent the country in the Miss World pageant in Puerto Rico later this year.

Manasa is busy with her preparation. She, along with other contestants is now in Mumbai under the care ofFemina Miss India, by theTimes group.

She wants nothing but to give India its best representation in the Miss World pageant. She quit her former role as a financial analyst to be able to prepare for it.

“She loves her job. But for very valid reasons, Manasa decided to focus on the Miss World pageant. It is about representing India now.

If she doesn’t, she says that it is unfair to anyone who could have won in her place. Going by how quickly and drastically she has grown, I am sure she will make it!” says Advait.

How has the family been taking it?

“Since the title, she hardly has the time to get in touch with the family. But thanks to the supportive organizers, she has a trustworthy, and friendly manager”.

Advait notes that every admonition and unsolicited advice from strangers has since become a word of encouragement and a pat on the back.

The 200-people strong extended family who received her at the Marriot in Hyderabad for home-coming made it clear that they want nothing but for her to win the Miss World title for India.

Support has been pouring in from friends all over the world. The family is overwhelmed.

“Her mother is now a tad more peaceful. Yet I’m sure she wouldn't mind if her daughter would continue to be academic instead!”.Advait says with a jestful grin.

“But now, she has her year full of assignments. She has in her own assertive style convinced her family that she can be in charge. I am sure she will do great!”.

Manasa has always been well-read and focused on the greater good of society.

She has been volunteering as a teacher for underprivileged children in Hyderabad’sMake a Differenceorganization andlearned sign languageto make society more inclusive for people with hearing and speaking disabilities. All this even before her entry into the world of pageantry.

She has always been a self-made woman, the independent woman we seek to see in today’s India.

The fact that she comes from the same home-town as I do, is something I am quietly proud of. My home-town needs more role-models like Manasa in all walks of life.

From myself, from everyone in Hyderabad, and as I am sure, from everyone in India, I wish Manasa the very best for her upcoming pageant for the Miss World title.

We are already proud of you, Manasa.

Note:In the article, Manasa was referred to as Advait’s sister, instead of cousin, because the Telugu (their mother-tongue) word for “cousin” is the same as a sister.

For more such features, meet meSruthi Korlakunta.

Stay in touch throughLinkedInorMedium. Write me a note, it would be awesome to meet you!

title: Toastmasters: A Gym For Your Verbal Self
content:
Toastmasters: A Gym For Your Verbal Self

From the member’s pen — Ingolspeakers

This is a write-up from my experiences in the German Speaking Toastmasters Club in the Bavarian City of Ingolstadt.

If you are anywhere around this town, feel free to leave a private note or comment. We could catch up for a nice evening of Toastmasters in Ingolstadt and feel at home in Bavaria!

See if you know the answers to these questions:What is 5x 6?What is the green pigment in plants?How many continents are there?Who is the president of the United States?

If you could answer all these questions, congratulations! You went to school and were a good student. There is one more unifying aspect to all the questions above:

Their contribution to improving your daily life is a loud and clear zero.

So now you are good at math or science or social studies, have a great job, contribute to society positively — This should be all about it, right? What they teach you in school is everything you need, no?

If that was true, very few of us would have social anxiety, unsureness while asking questions, a racing heart before we can open our mouth and speak to people.

Incidentally, the largest fear of people is public speaking, and only the second-largest phobia is death. Meaning that most of us would be dead than be talking.

You probably realized after school that you need a lot of googling-around for information, on the right kind of food and exercise to keep fit. Similarly, there is a lot of missing common knowledge in another vital area of life— Communication.

Unlike health science, far fewer people realize this need and make up for it far too late. Just like you go to a fitness studio to make up for the last night’s pizza, there is a club you go to to make your guilty bad soft skills go away and prepare you for a better tomorrow.

It is called Toastmasters. I discovered this international club about 2 years ago and eventually after a year of dilly-dallying made the plunge and became a member.

I never felt free-er in life to make more mistakes more confidently.

What happens in a Toastmasters evening?

Think about it like a group of ants. Each one has its own job in the colony. Likewise, each member of toastmasters takes up a role for the evening, ranging from Timekeeping, Grammar Notes, moderation, “Um” counting, to Full-fledged speeches for about 7 minutes.

Based on their grit and resolve, each member takes up a role, some needing previous preparation and some not so much (Like keeping time).

At the end of the evening, everyone, including the speaker gets loving, constructive feedback on their speech and performance for the day.

To someone who keeps asking themselves “How did I do! How did I do? How did I do!!?” This is a godsend. Because you will definitely find out how you did.

Beyond communicating and public speaking, toastmasters for me was a symbol of utmost resilience.

The group of members, ranging all from ages 20 to 70, pulled through the pandemic with online meetings with extremely high motivations and turn out. I could not be happier to meet these members. If you also want to check out the Ingolspeakers evenings, feel free to drop them a linehere.

I am Sruthi Korlakunta, living in Ingolstadt since 2019. I work full-time for Audi as a Data Analyst.

If you moved here recently, Welcome to Ingolstadt. Feel free to get in touch onLinkedIn. If you want to be a part of Medium,join here.

If you are new to the city, check outhttps://ingolstadtexpats.com/for some great contacts in the city. It is a blog run by and for expats in the town and has some great content to get started

title: Clean Thinking: 9 Rules For a Mentally Clean Life
content:
Clean Thinking: 9 Rules For a Mentally Clean Life

The truths I found on my path to mental peace.

Stress comes from within. Not from work, from friends, or circumstances. It comes from the expectations you set for yourself. It comes from building an alternate reality in your head which you think “should actually be the case”.

If you get your facts right and ethics in place, it feels like having a pair of clear new spectacles to see the world. It may not solve all your problems but it will help you settle down in your seat and be calm. It will help you think clearly and factually, instead of running in circles with the same problems over and over.

Here are a couple of things that helped me clean my mental clutter. Commandments which I discovered to be always true. My heart now beats slower and it helps me think clean.

1. Don’t adopt ideologies. Discover them.

So many “isms” and still no world peace!

Feminism, communism, nationalism, liberalism. All isms are just many people's attempts to find a huge box to dump their ideas to find them easily later.

A lot of people have dumped a lot of ideas into the same boxes. You might not agree with all of them. Don’t be an “ist”. Don’t frantically run for the box to determine what is correct and what is not.

Wait. Look at the situation, think, and decide. Values, morals, decisions, and feelings are nuanced. Take time to find them within yourself before adopting a Size-M from the one-size-fits-all box.

2. Stay away from symbols and close to individual ideas.

Symbols are there for a reason. You will like an idea more when you see your favorite symbol on it.

“Get them!” written with your country’s flag on it sounds empowering. With someone else’s flag, it sounds terrifying. Symbols are a cover to get away with what you say. They ask for blind trust, blind loyalty, and unthinking support.

Religion, region, language, cult, or sport — every time you come across an idea, strip it of its symbols and see what it means. If the symbol is the only part of it that is convincing, run.

3. Don’t accept incomplete logic circles.

Logic is universal. Closing your eyes to what is logically true will only make you blind, even if you don’t like what you see.

White-peoples jokes are racist as black-people jokes. Sometimes you do things more out of obligation than love. Going to church or temple during corona is the same as going to a party. They are all uncomfortable truths.

You may not always choose to take the most logical step, but it is important to know that you are operatingdespiteknowing the truth, not by obliterating the truth.

4. Don’t try to solve Morality with logic.

People have the strangest notions about right and wrong. A story —

A friend once stole from me because he assumed it is okay since I “have more”. (I don’t, really).How can someone I trusted steal from me! If I am nice to someone, should they not be nice to me!?

It threw me off-balance.Should I continue to be friends, or should I cut off someone for just small money? Should I have tolerance? Should I invest trust in this person to show him that the world can be a nice place?

You cannot solve questions of morality with logic alone. Logically, helping someone by going out of your way is risky. Forgiveness is not always logical. But life is not worth living if everyone constantly stole and one-upped.

I did not come to terms with my friend, but I came to terms with the disgust I felt. I let it go. I am more careful. It is a better place to be than eternal hate.

5. Expect no relationships. Let them form by themselves.

When you meet another person, it is human to see where you stand with respect to the other.

You might see a potential friend, you might want to run far away, or you might be indifferent. What you see affects how you behave and this, in turn, affects the reactions you cause.

Relax, and let time decide. Not every interaction might go the way you want to. Sometimes there might be potential friends in strangers you don’t relate with. At other times, someone just like you might end up being surprisingly different. Keep an open mind and let relationships take their course. Expect nothing.

6. If you have it better, be nicer.

Like it or not, we have already been dealt some cards at birth. Sometimes it is a rich family, sometimes a highly educated environment, and sometimes good looks.

I used to be a chubby girl. Life wasn’t easy. Some 8 years down the line, I don’t see the same world that she saw. That girl had a hard time. The world treats people differently based on how much they own and how they look.

If you know that you have been dealt a better hand, acknowledge it. The world looks dramatically different from the other side. No amount of theory can convince you of the glaring difference. Empathizing with another makes you mentally peaceful.

7. If you don’t know, say “I don’t know”.

It is easy.

By saying I don’t know, you will build the confidence to accept your littleness. You don’t know most things, and that’s ok. A smart person does not know everything, he knows what he knows and what he doesn’t.

If you instead try to come up with fake facts, you will feel small as you say it. If you talk about something else to draw attention away from “I don’t know”, you will inherently feel unintelligent.

If you admit to yourself that you don’t know, you will go down the road of truth much sooner. Until then you will just be kidding yourself about “knowing” and you will not be searching.

corollary:If you know for sure, say it. But only if you can distinguish between fact and opinion.How do you distinguish?Know your logic circles.

8. Love animals

In a world where you are successfully out of natural selection and food chain, you have no excuse but to love creatures that don’t have it better.

Animals don’t plot evil. A lion hunts, but never out of hate. No matter how you feel about creepy-crawly creatures, they don’t have the faintest idea of what causes your inbuilt dread, or that you feel it at all.

You took everything from every other species on the planet just by being born into the right species. You have no right to hate.

9. If your mind or body tells you something, believe it.

If you want to see a human, don’t be afraid to pick up the phone and call. If you want to stop doing something, give in and stop — a bad habit or even just a forced hobby.

“But how?!”Just like how you get up from bed every morning. There is nohow.Just doing it. If you can’t stop — either thinking or doing things you don't like, don’t let it bother you.

The next time, listen to yourself.

I amSruthi Korlakunta, I write here onMedium.

In my day job, I am a Data Analyst for Audi. During and after work, I vigorously explore the world and my mind for the truth. I’d be happy to connect!

title: The Secret Ingredient to Raising Successful Adults
content:
What do Elon Musk, Tara Westover and Jeanette Walls have in common?

Most adults of today grew up in an age where parenting wasn’t as much a science as it is today. Children were not as much individuals as a mild nuisance that needs to be“put under control”.Love was tough. The display of emotion was considered weak. Tenderness, if at all, was the job of a woman. The biggest lessons on how to live and love came to children from movies, where love was presented as one big gesture that sets all things right. Family memories were made one catchy dialog a time. The Result — A whole generation of adults that spend their entire lifetimes getting over the systematically induced childhood trauma.

It is hardly surprising that today, parenting is all about over-compensating the trauma induced by parents of the bygone era. What constitutes good parenting these days is summarized by these platitudes —

“Every child is a winner”

“You are a princess”

“You are in trouble? It is definitely someone else’s fault”.

Is either coddling or shutting-off a child playing a role in the development of effective faculties of the child?

Let us start by asking some basic questions:

Why do some adults, having endured tougher childhoods than most, come out, if not unscathed, bright and successful? What is the secret ingredient?

I think we all agree that the best gift we could have received before we started assimilating life in all its blemished glory isa tool that helps to say “I can go on”. Knowingly or unknowingly, bothGoodandnot-the-bestparents pass it on to their children.

Let’s talk in the context of the following Biographies of some achievers of the day.

What sets them apart?

Elon Musk

Elon Musk needs no introduction. A founder of multibillion-dollar companies in extremely different fields, he dreamed up Tesla, SpaceX, and co-founded Paypal. A less famous fact as noted by Biographer Ashlee Vance is that Musk has his share of childhood experiences he would best like to sweep under the carpet. He doesn’t talk about his dad or his childhood experiences while growing up with his dad. He has vowed not to let his five children (with his first wife) ever meet his Father Errol Musk, with whom he grew up after his parents divorced.

Jeanette Walls

Walls lived her childhood as a nomad and was in a large part homeless. Her unusual parents with an eccentric lifestyle hopped from one city to another escaping poverty and sometimes law. She grew up on the run and barely made out alive in multiple circumstances. She is now a columnist for MSNBC and the author of a New York Times Bestselling Autobiography called Glass Castle that stayed on the chart for over 8 years.

Tara Westover

Tara Westover gother first real textbook as a teenager and never put pen to paper or attended school before going to college. She grew up in a family that believed apocalypse is around the corner and lived as such. They were off the government records and lived without hospitals or schooling. She is nowa Cambridge graduate and the author of a New York Times Bestselling Autobiography — Educated, which is also one of Bill Gates’ favorite books (says he).

None of the above remarkable individuals seems to have had a rainbow-unicorn-flower garden childhood. Aside from the fact that they all had traumatizing childhoods, what else sets them apart? See if you can guess:

Elon Musk as described by Biographer Ashlee Vance was born into a very well to do and adventurous family in South Africa. He had already poured himself through two entire encyclopedias before high school out of sheer curiosity. He convinced his reluctant and Luddite father to buy him a Computer at 10 and taught himself BASIC programming language in an unbelievably short time. His grades were never the best in class but his enthusiasm for what interested him was extraordinary even by the global standard. He taught himself what he craved for despite being bullied and beaten to unconsciousness by his peers.

Jeanette Walls’ father was not merely an eccentric drunk but also an oddly loving parent. Jeanette’s father taught her skills that were beyond her age. She was forced by her circumstances to teach herself cooking at three, to survive in a desert without supervision while she was barely 10 and pick up skills to survive bullies in the extremely shady neighborhoods where the family always ended up.

As a child,Tara Westover spent hours teaching herself all kinds of skills. At times motivated by sheer personal will and at times because she needed them to survive her extraordinary circumstances. These ranged anything from what should have been her right to learn as a child, to bizarre things for a girl of her age. Examples being the ability to read and sing, operate heavy machinery in a scrap metal yard, helping new mothers in birthing, even canning fruit for a possible apocalypse.

If you haven’t noticed already, what the parents of these extraordinary minds gave them, knowingly or unknowingly was not hours of practice. It was not unlimited resources to buy the moon, not a vision and mission that never swayed.It was the tenacious ability to teach themselves!They were people that grew up free to be tormented by their idiosyncrasies.

This obsessive zeal to learn appears naturally in every child. Children constantly find themselves in dogged pursuit of comprehending the world they live in. This pursuit is interrupted, alas in the form of aiding, by adults who interfere with the free-flowing thought process and contain it into preconceived conventions. The right way to teach a child is, ironically, to let her be. It might seem counter-intuitive to today’s style of excessive coaching, after-school tutoring, and multiple hours of structured teaching. What characterizes a good method of learning is that it is self-taught. A child needs space, time, and self-application before an adult needs to interfere and feed along with his intelligence, his ignorance of the child.

The parents of these extraordinary minds gave them, knowingly or unknowingly the tenacious ability to teach themselves!

As anthropologistYuval Noah Hararisays in his bookHomo Deus, modern schools are built along with the model of the industrial revolution — In squared classrooms that all look alike, children in uniform who appear alike move from classroom to classroom on the sound of a scheduled bell. It is no coincidence that a system built for aiding the assembly-line culture looks exactly like a mass-manufacture factory. This system needs a gradual upgrade to the discoveries and needs of the current world, which makes scientific and experimental discoveries around parenting common knowledge.

To underline the bottom line of the text, children are naturally curious and intelligent beings that, if let and aided by the resources and surroundings, would find their way. Self-learning is the key ingredient for an individual to develop at a young age to be able to apply one’s self fully and completely. Creating a safe environment where this can happen is the best a parent can do to aid the growth of the child and her zeal to learn.

title: Chandler Bing
content:
Chandler Bing

The Painfully Transparent Biography of Matthew Perry.

“I deeply cared what strangers thought about me. Infact, its one of the key threads of my life. I asked my mom to paint the backyard blue so that people flying in planes overhead would think we had a swimming pool” — Matthew Perry

I read this quote and felt it lodged somewhere deep in my chest. It’s funny in the way only real pain can be, just like Chandler — howling beneath the surface, dressed up as a joke. And it’s painfully relatable.

I was seriously surprised to see how much of the real Matthew Perry made up Chandler Bing — his upbringing, childhood trauma, people-pleasing-jokeman-personality — all him in real life.

Matthew Perry’s memoir,Friends, Lovers, and the Big Terrible Thing, was not the usual tell-all. It felt raw, self-deprecating, and heartbreaking. With a sort of vulnerability that made me extremely uncomfortable, Matthew wrote on fame, addiction, loneliness, and self-worth (or lack thereof).

His story began with a ruptured colon caused by years of substance abuse. A part of Chandler (I mean, Matthew) is very selfaware. He knows of the hole he is trying to fill with his drugs. Even as he talks about his parents in the present tense, there is a palpable sense of distance, and a distinction between the parents he has on his mind and those in reality. All his life, Matthew has struggled to belong, and be accepted. First into his family and then everywhere else.

Just like Chandler, Matthew jokes about even the darkest moments — It seems to be both his deflection and coping. That tension between humor and despair is the emotional undercurrent of him as a person, and this book. It’s what makesFriends, Lovers, and the Big Terrible Thingso human.

Perry not only acknowledges but understands the magnanimity of his achievements and the privilage they brought him. He by no means underplays the role of FRIENDS in his life, nor does he gloat in the situation. If anything, he seems haunted by how he was at the peak of his career while sinking into the depths of addiction.

One of the most affecting aspects of the memoir is how often Perry confesses to sabotaging good things — relationships, opportunities, even his health — because of his deep-seated insecurities and compulsions. He chronicles countless stints in rehab, failed romances, and near-death experiences with a kind of blunt sincerity and quite openly asks for understanding.

I did hope for a bit of a celebrity-centered juicy gossip and sitcom-era tidbits. After all, I wanted to read this book because I started to rewatch Friends. But Perry offered something different and far more valuable: a cautionary tale about how even immense fame, money, and success only fill so much of your void. Sigh, I always wished that were not true.

Of course, there are moments when his perspective feels skewed by ego or defensiveness. He admits this himself. He’s a flawed narrator, but that’s part of the book’s authenticity. He owns his contradictions.

If you’re aFriendsfan, of course there are some glimpses of the show’s magic. But that is not what this book is even remotely about. If you’re interested in the psychology of addiction or the fragility of fame, you’ll find this memoir eye-opening. And if you’ve ever felt like you were performing a version of yourself to be loved or accepted, Perry’s story will likely resonate on a deep level. It did with me.

title: Spectacular Netflix Series on True Nonviolent Crimes
content:
Spectacular Netflix Series on True Nonviolent Crimes

No blood, just corporate crimes and “catch-me-if-you-can”s

Netflix is hands-down the best streaming service there is. The quality of their interface, the shows they curate, their originals — everything made me fall in love with the platform.

Netflix's design and interface make Amazon Prime Video feel like someone’s disorganized personal video library. They certainly have shows I don’t love, but they always tend to have a minimum standard. Some shows on Amazon are simply atrocious.

True crime is many a viewer’s guilty pleasure. I am averse to watching rape, murder, abuse, or personal trauma. It makes me fear that I am taking a voyeuristic pleasure in another person’s pain. So, I steer away when I can. The troubling aspect of such crime shows is that the “heroes” somehow end up more famous than infamous. It is a disgusting reflection of an uncaring society.

Having said that, there is something genuinely intriguing about criminals. The odds-defying behavior, the guts to game a system, and their wish to play outside of the rules in enticing and entertaining. I satisfy this curiosity by watching non-bloody crime.

Here are some excellent shows where the crime isn’t bloody:

Pepsi where’s my jet —A Quirky Lawsuit

Imagine you told your daughter she could get a real-life unicorn for Christmas if she scored a million million gazillion points on her test. And then she comes home with a report card that says million million gazillion points… what would you do? You might walk away, but Pepsi couldn’t.

This documentary series,Pepsi: Where’s My Jet?is a weird story of a young boy (I think about 17 at that time) demanding Pepsi to fulfill an extravagant promise — of delivering him a Harrier Jet plane. Why? Because the ad said so, without fine print.

This young man John Leonard sets off on a legal rollercoaster when he collects the seemingly impossible 7 million points in a Pepsi challenge advertised on TV. What starts as an innocent ad campaign offering merchandise in exchange for Pepsi points takes an unforeseen turn when John decides to hold Pepsi accountable for its extravagant claim.

The story is an interesting ‘David and Goliath’ narrative in a legal setting where both sides possess reasonable arguments. The series is an entertaining and quirky piece of corporate history that unfolds at the time when Pepsi had caused a bloodbath with its botched lottery campaign in the Philippines.

D.B. Cooper: Where are you!?

The story of DB Cooper, the mystery man who hijacked a plane in 1971 and parachuted away with a ransom, has been the subject of documentaries and has inspired various retellings. This show on Netflix is one of them.

The series is a mix of real footage from the past aided with artsy recreations and animations to fill the gaps. It opens with a gripping heist. D.B Cooper, a fake name, has now become immortal as a folk hero, feeding into the public’s fascination.

The series focuses on the DB Cooper “industry”, spotlighting detective Tom Colbert’s relentless pursuit to prove his suspicion that a certain Rackstraw was Cooper. As Colbert’s obsession intensifies, the documentary morphs into a true crime narrative akin to the unsettling evolution in “Zodiac.”

Unfortunately, if you are expecting closure,DB Cooper: Where Are You?!may disappoint. The case is unsolved.

Operation Varsity Blues

There is some weird pleasure in watching the rich pay for their crimes. (Is it biological? Why are there so many “eat the rich” scenarios in society?)Operation Varsity Bluesmight just satisfy that itch.

Operation Varsity Blues: The College Admissions Scandaldissects the notorious college scam masterminded by Rick Singer, shedding light on a corrupt system where money bought access to prestigious universities. The Netflix docudrama, spearheaded by Chris Smith, combines storytelling with journalism detailing how the gullible rich fell prey to the scandal orchestrated by Singer.

Singer’s “side door” method exploited wealthy families, promising admission through athletic channels by doctoring photos to create false athletic profiles so that the kids got into prestigious colleges. The documentary even employs FBI wiretap recordings of Singer’s voice.

The documentary is a solid watch, offering insights into a scandal that rocked the academic world.

Inventing Anna

Shonda Rimes has done it again.Inventing Annais a captivating exploration of the antics of a con artist. Based on a true story, the series navigates the world of Anna Delvey, a fake German heiress. Julia Garner plays Anna. Her portrayal is compelling and draws viewers into the complexities of identity and manipulation.

Anna slept in 5-star Hotels, made friends with millionaires, and even stole a private jet — all using only her deceptive charm and allure.Inventing Annamight even inspire awe, be it for a criminal.

Tinder Swindler

What if you went on a dating app and immediately met the man/woman of your dreams? They’re charming, beautiful, kind, and turn out, also a millionaire and they invite you on a holiday in their private jet, which you gladly accept. The jet is impressive, the roses were fresh and there was love. But after a month, you get a text on your phone asking for a loan of, say, £1000. What now?

The Tinder Swindleris an exposé of Simon Leviev’s elaborate deception using online dating. Leviev manipulated and exploited unsuspecting women he met through Tinder. Through compelling interviews and reconstructed events, the film paints a vivid picture of his calculated schemes.

The documentary captures the emotional trauma experienced by the victims but really does not dig into the “why” and “how” of it all. I was left with so many unanswered questions at the end of the show.

Nonetheless, if you are on dating appsThe Tinder Swindlercould serve as a much-needed cautionary tale.

Fyre: The Greatest Party That Never Happened

The Netflix seriesFyre: The Greatest Party That Never Happenedis another unbelievable narrative surrounding the disastrous Fyre Festival. Through real behind-the-scenes footage and interviews, the documentary tells the story of the festival’s catastrophic failure.

All such stories have an antihero. Here we have Billy McFarland, the festival’s mastermind, a picture of recklessness and deceit that fueled the event’s conception and subsequent collapse. The series provides a stark portrayal of the power of social media in shaping perceptions and marketing, ultimately leading to a colossal misrepresentation of reality.

Netflix effectively exposes the chaos and disillusionment experienced by attendees and workers of the Fyre Festival, leaving the viewer to question the “Instagram reality” they might be living in.

Nonetheless,Fyreremains a compelling and cautionary tale, a reminder of the importance of critical thinking in a world dominated by the allure of image and hype on social media.

title: Jitsi Meet — The Anonymus, Free and Open Source Alternative to Zoom Conferencing
content:
Jitsi Meet — The Anonymus, Free and Open Source Alternative to Zoom Video Conferencing

Video Communication Software Trends World-Wide

Here is what the world is doing, according to google

Who has not already made a video call on Zoom the last 3 weeks, unless they have been living under a rock. Skype and Zoom are about the top key words I started to hear on a daily basis over the past couple of weeks, paralleled only by keywords “COVID-19” and “Lockdown”.

A quick look into google trends told me that I am not alone. Take a look at the interest on google over the past 90 days in the following search terms on Google Trends:

The Correlation between the Video communication search and Covid-19 search terms is uncanny! It is not difficult to guess why. Now, look at the region-wise break-down for the same search terms.

The searches on communications software by no means pale against those for the pandemic itself. Turns out Europeans (and Argentinians) are much more social than one thinks! (Or hard working? Alternatively, tech savvy.)

The relatively small but fast-growing blue-dot on the above bar isJitsi-Meet, A completely free, open-source alternative to Zoomthat I loved using. If you are looking for an unlimited, quality alternative video communication form, Jitsi is the way to go.

I Tried Jitsi Meet and Here is How It Went

Most of you reading this have already been on a zoom call and are familiar with the 40-minute limit on free meetings. Jitsi Meet is a completely free, open-source solution to this, with a promise of new features added every day. (May be we will see a zoom equivalent with End to End encryption soon!). Here is how it went :

The basics

First things first, I like the video quality. If you happen to find yourself in a place starved of high internet speeds, Jitsi has an option to lower the video quality to suit your needs. Thoughtful touch. Options for screen-sharing, audio and video options, toggle menu, and others are handy.

No Registration, No download.

I DID NOT HAVE TO REGISTER! How great is that! No need to register, install or go through any other process where you are not comfortable giving out your email or set another password. This was for me, the best part. Open your browser, type jitsi.org, and dive in.

Signing in

You can join a meeting on Jitsi with a unique handle that you choose.I strongly recommend that you make this handle as unique as possible and set a passwordat the beginning so as to avoid “intruders”. A generic meeting name like “hello” not protected with a password might welcome some members that you were not expecting. This is done in Zoom at the start by default, but Jitsi leaves you the option of choosing your meeting name and password.

User Interface and options palette

The user interface of Jitsi is similar to that of zoom. It offers a far wider range of options to make your life easier.

It also offers cool features such asLive streamingyour group calls to your youtube channel, share a youtube video with yourco-jitsers, look up speaker stats at the end of the meeting to get a picture of how much you spoke, and a whole bunch of keyboard shortcuts that are in-built so that you don’t have to fumble with the mouse each time you want to mute or unmute yourself.

Number of participants, Video Recording and Meeting Stats

Jitsi Offers good conferencing experience for up to 75 users. For a bigger audience, Jitsi advises the use of its Live streaming option on YouTube, which in-turn supports millions at a time. In my opinion, that is a cool option to have on a video calling service. Although that is not an optimal “conference”, I cannot imagine a situation with about 300 audience members where everyone needs to speak on an ad-hoc basis.

While Zoom’s Video Recording feature is limited to it’s paid members,Jitsi Allows you to record all your conferences while still being free. The recording is then backed-up to your dropbox or a location on your computer if you choose. Congenial!

Privacy

Jitsi-Meet, like zoom, is not yet end-to-end Encrypted. Each of its meeting instances is created and destroyed as and when members enter or leave the meeting. I cannot stress here enough, how important it is toset a strong password and a unique meeting name on Jitsi. Jitsi also provides a random name generator for this purpose. Here is an example :

What else is awesome

With Jitsi, Any user could mute/unmute a participant. While this might be considered a nuisance, I have been insufficient meetings where a caller was frantically moving his lips and arms unaware that his microphone is muted, If someone didn’t awkwardly scream into the micro and interrupt him. I welcome the change.

You can create your own video conferencing solutions!

With Jitsi It is possible to create your own meeting instance on your own server. You can integrate Jitsi service in your own mobile applications and cloud services and scale it as desired. An active open-source developer community provides support for these applications.

Instructions and docker images for self-deployment here:

That was my experience with Jitsi. If you try it out and/or use it in your mobile application or website, let me know in the comments. I hope this resolves your time limit restrictions and keeps you connecting. Happy conferencing!

title: Everything Worth Living For
content:
Everything Worth Living For

The story of inexplicable depression.

“I don't matter”, “I don’t matter”, “I don’t matter”.

I ran around the world hearing this in wake and sleep. It wouldn’t leave me, like a fly buzzing, refusing to be swapped. Even when I couldn’t hear it, I knew it was there. Like a noise in dog frequency. When it was loud enough, it was unbearable. My ears hurt. My existence hurt.

I was screaming in my head “Make it stop! Turn it off!”, looking for a switch that was nowhere. Every time I was around people, laughter, love, the volume went up, with that my heart rate. When it was the loudest, I stayed still. Hoping that if I made myself invisible, it won’t find me.

But it always did. Until I stood by a turbulent river on one cold winter night, trying to think about every face that will tell me not to do this. The noise of my ringing phone drowned the one in my head. The voice of my mother, I heard before I answered.

To her, I didn't answer the call. To myself, I did. I went home.

There on, days were numb. Empty. Devoid of feeling. I didn't remember what joy, anger, or sadness felt like. I was a shell of a person, watching everything that came and went.

I only saw as a bystander, failing to comprehend. I saw scorns devoid of their meaning, joy devoid of its life force, hate devoid of its power, love devoid of its softness. I saw will, want, ambition, and all their frivolity. I watched like a stranger on a train watching the landscape pass by. It wasn't mine, it just was.

I watched as human complexity unfolded itself in its stupid meaninglessness. I watched in wonder.

Strangers smiled at me, not knowing I am a facade, lacking the infrastructure that makes a full human.

As emptiness turned to wonder, I saw art. Like an outstretched hand assuring a cub. I looked up at the Goliath extremely aware of my smallness. It struck me then, not like a lightning; like reality, half mixed with a dying dream.

“I see, so I must be!”

Realization turned to reality. Faces turned to people. Hatred turned to humor. love turned to light. I see it.

I saw colors on canvas, words on paper, tunes on what used to be air. I had nothing to give back. No courage to love, no stature to compliment, no strength to support.

But I was there. For the first time again, Iwas.

I condensed my helpless thanks to a wordless clap, to everyone making life a little less miserable. I clapped, joining a million others in being nobody.

I am nobody, therefore I am free.

title: How Stoicism Helps You Overcome the Hate You Feel
content:
How Stoicism Helps You Overcome the Hate You Feel

What to do when you are unsettled by hatred from or of someone

Hardly ever is the suffering of real-life worse than the one in our head.

Hate is intoxicating. Unlike love, which can leave your mind by the morning after, hate is a bath we like to stew in. Important loved ones ironically compete for space in your head with the peripheral-few who you love to hate.

How to get over someone who occupies an unreasonable amount of space in your head, rent-free?

Let’s explore what stoicism has to offer to get over the sickness of hatred.

1. Why we hate

Your hatred is triggered when you find in another person proof of your perceived threat.

If you already “smell” something fishy, the moment you find an iota of proof that this is true, your alarm system called hate goes off. More often than not, all the triggers are in yourself.

Nothing outside of you has physical access to your thoughts and perceptions.

- Marcus Aurelius.

For example, a person might attack what you already perceive as your weaknesses. A passing comment which could have been a joke turns ugly.

Your values and deeply-seated morals might be attacked by those who hold different values. You might feel like a champion of tolerance or you might feel justified in belittling and ridiculing those who advocate violence and hatred.

The wave of moral superiority rides high. from that height, we can’t see that more intolerance is a wrong response to intolerance.

Acknowledge your role in the story without forming emotional bonds with ideas. This way, you will allow for space so that your ego will not consume you as a whole.

2. The hurt, hurt.

The word on the street is that Bill Gates gave somewhere up of 40 Billion dollars in charity.

What one gets from the world, one gives.

Ironically, this is not different from how people take pleasure in other’s misery. People who feed on misery have been hurt in the past. Loving homes and loving families don’t raise people who feed on hate and ridicule.

Acknowledge your privilege which gives you the strength to get over the bad experiences.

Acknowledge the people in your life who led you to click on an article titled “I want to get over hate”.

Acknowledge also the fact that the “other” did not have this in their life. It is all a matter of time before a man meets his maker. Recognize that you grew past the same experiences. Everybody does.

Don’t judge. Accept and Empathize.

3. Don’t Retaliate

Hatred, harsh as it might sound, is a visceral response. You will notice your hatred when you willfully think ill of someone or wish ill upon them.

Catch yourself in the deed and acknowledge the hatred you feel. Don’t act on it. That is your first step towards self-awareness.

It may be hard to live with the fact that someone wronged you. But harder is the realization that you are evil. You can get away from someone else, but not from yourself. Avoid sabotaging another at all costs.

“Whoever does wrong, wrongs himself; whoever does injustice, does it to himself, making himself evil.”

― Marcus Aurelius, Meditations

Trust that one pays the price already by committing an act of hate. Don’t share this price by retaliating.

4. Determine if the situation is really controllable

In his brilliant article on stoicism,Louis Chewtalks about developing a “locus of control”. A sense of self-control, which chooses our reaction to events regardless of the nature of the events.

To quote Epictetus,

“Man is disturbed not by things, but by the views he takes of them.”

—Epictetus

In our unnatural world of perfection, the slightest inconvenience can cause our tower of order and expectation to topple in a moment. If this tower is willfully attacked by someone, we blame and hate almost reflexively(If we are the cause of the turmoil we somehow manage to find acceptance for ourselves).

One hundred percent of the responsibility for what we feel lies in our hands. To blame another for what we feel is not only foolish but also factually incorrect.

5. Being practical

Forbes has put together this fabulouslist of to-dosfor dealing with hatred or dislike on a daily basis.

The bottom line:Focus on the positive, be civil, sidestep the landmine areas, and avoid drama at all costs.

If you can’t handle it, it is important to minimize stressful contact and keep a healthy circle of people you love and trust.

This does not mean that you should make a show of being uncomfortable and make it known that you are avoiding someone.

You are not doing this as an act of revenge, you are doing this for your peace of mind. To be more positive, not less.

There is enough place in the world for extremely different people and ideologies to co-exist. Respect your own and another’s right to hold on to their thoughts unless they themselves voluntarily change them.

6. Get a whiff of nihilism

No matter what we achieve, where we go, what we believe, and who we are, in the big-picture, all we have is a couple of years and some consciousness to realize it.

Nothing else matters.

Like John Lennon put it, there might not be a heaven, country might be a vague belief, possessions might not be as absolute as you think. It is easy if you can imagine it.

The more time you are going to spend feeling “not great”, the less you are going to spend knowing more, doing more, giving more, and loving more.

Your case of hatred is not going to help you in saving more time. The sooner you can give it up, the sooner you can move on to the more fun parts.

Nobody is going to meet all your expectations. Some are going to deviate more than others. Objectively, they all do not matter except for the ripples you let them cause in your head. Choose your ripples wisely.

To sum up, when you have a reason to hate,

Remember that intolerance is a bad answer to intolerance.

Acknowledge your role in the deed.

Develop empathy and trust that people can be good if they make good experiences.

At all costs, don’t retaliate.

Understand that what you feel about things is solely in your hands.

If you cannot handle it, sidestep and disengage.

Understand that in the grand scheme of things, all that matters is how you use your limited time in the world.

title: Netflix, Collagen and Insta— Shows for People in their 30s Who can't Stop Scrolling
content:
Netflix, Collagen and Insta— Shows for People in their 30s Who can't Stop Scrolling

Casual watching while looking at your phone

Let’s just get to the list, ok?

Friends From College

This is an extremely uncomfortable-to-watch kind of series about a bunch of friends who are married among themselves and to out-group partners, but everyone is cheating on everybody.

The series is full of self-centered and unfaithful leads but that’s what makes it an interesting watch. There are no Ned Stark-type perfect protagonists to root for. Everyone has a case for themselves (and that case is usually “yolo”) about why they do what they do.

The extent of diabolical life-messing-up decisions is as entertaining as it is uncomfortable to watch. It might be slightly annoying to see full-grown adults still hanging on to their college lifestyle and juvenile friendships and crushes, but hey, it's a TV show.

Beef

Beef is my second favorite series on this list. It is a well-made 10-episode limited Dark comedy.

It is the story of an escalating road rage incident between Danny and Amy that turns into an emotional spiral. Outside of the feud, Danny wants to bring his parents back from Korea, while Amy grapples with the pressures of maintaining her business and family.

Instead of letting the road rage incident go, Danny and Amy escalate it to compensate for their unresolved resentment. The obsessive vendetta causes them to have at each other’s lives — vandalizing homes, personal businesses, and sabotaging careers, relationships, and mental health.

This series also has my personal favorite final episode of all. Without spoiling, I’d say, watch it.

Glow

I have had a soft corner for Alison Brie since I watchedCommunity.So when I saw the trailer for Glow, I knew I would watch the show. A super unique periodic comedy set in the 80s, GLOW has the premise of a flashy female wrestling industry.

The chemistry between the cast, especially Alison Brie’s determined Ruth and Betty Gilpin’s powerhouse Debbie, worked well. Also a very unique set of circumstances and costumes, I must say.

Glow is full of sharp writing, retro flair, and surprisingly powerful moments. It also has not-so-subtle feminist undertones and is a refreshing watch.

Whether you’re into wrestling or not, GLOW is a wacky underdog story that makes you click on “Next Episode".

Ghosts

Ghostsis a refreshingly original sitcom that blends classic comedy and supernatural charm. The show is about Samantha and Jay, a young couple who inherit a mansion, only to discover it’s inhabited by a group of quirky ghosts from different historical eras.

Spoiler Alert for the first episode here though: After a near-death experience, Samantha gains the ability to see and hear the ghosts.

My favourite ghost is Thor, a Viking warrior with an adorable Khal-Drogo-ish charm, with murderous thoughts but a Joey-like delivery and innocent humor.

The storylines in this series are mostly lighthearted and don’t require a lot of “investment” from the watcher, like a much much much less intense Friends. It’s a feel-good series that doesn’t take itself too seriously. Every episode has a fun-spooky-heart-warming storyline. It had me wishing ghosts were real, and I could see them.

title: Surprising 7 Little Facts in English To Write Like a Pro
content:
Surprising 7 Little Facts in English To Write Like a Pro

Don’t make these rookie mistakes if you want to get anywhere with your writing.

During my diploma in Feature writing, I learned a few tips in classy writing. I wrote them down here for you.Grammarlyis a brilliant tool, and it can do a lot, but not everything. It pays to have a few tricks up your sleeve, even if you are only writing emails for work.

1. Cliches kill your writing.

Avoid over-used tropes even if they fit your case.‘at this moment in time’,‘in any way, shape or form’,and‘in this day and age’.

2. All these are singular:

Each, every, either, neither, everybody, somebody, none (short for no one).For example:You cannot say“Neither Jack nor Jillaregood at fetching water”.Correct:“Neither Jack nor Jillisgood at fetching water.”

3. Be aware of the apostrophe in its.

It’s is short for it is.“It’sa beautiful day!”Its is the possessive case of the pronoun it.“Taj Mahal anditsbeauty!”Its’is just plain wrong.

4. When to use me or I:

One is not just more “classy”. There is a correct context for both.Example:It is wrong to say “This couch is just for my dad and I.”Take out the words“my dad and", it should still be correct.

Examples:“This couch is for my dad and me”is correct.“My mom and I plan to go Christmas shopping”is correct.

5. Few and less are different.

Fewer relates to numbers, less to quantities.Fewer apples are collected in winter, but there is less collecting of apples in winter.

6. Comparing to and comparing with.

Things of a general class are compared with each other.“It is unfair to compare Donald Trump with Joe Biden.”

Things of a different class are compared to each other“In an audacious statement, the comedian compared Donald Trump to an orange.”

7. Drop antique words and phrases.

Drop antique words and phrases, like amidst, whilst, and amongst from your writing. User modern counterparts and remember that language is constantly evolving. Don’t shun everything new.

I am Sru. If you have an idea or a story to share,hit me up! I will give your story the voice it deserves.
