title: 6 Beautiful Song Texts About Love and Pain From Hindi Cinema and Translation
content:
6 Beautiful Song Texts About Love and Pain From Hindi Cinema and Translation.

1. Aaj Jane ki Zidd Na Karo

Waqt ki qaid mein zindagi hai magarChand ghaṛiyaan yahi hain jo aazaad hainIn ko kho kar miri jaan-i jaanUmr bhar na taraste raho

Translation:

Life lives in the prison of time,Yet, these few moments are free,Don’t lose this moment, my love,and wait a lifetime for another.

2. Churaliya Hain Tumne

Bahaar Banke Aaoon Kabhi Tumhaari Duniya MeinGuzar Na Jaaye Yeh Din Kahin Isi Tamanna Mein

Translation:

Maybe Someday, I can come into your life like the spring,Or perhaps, my days will pass in the hope of that day.

3. Bulleya

Ve main tan hoon pagdandi labhdi ae jo raah jannat ki,Tu mude jahan main saath mud jaaun.Tere kaarvan mein shamil hona chahun,Kamiya taraash ke main kaabil hona chahun

Translation:I am but a wanderer seeking the path to paradise,Should you turn, I’ll turn by your side,I just want to be a part of your journey,Chisel away my flaws, to be deserving (of you)

4. Lag Ja Gale

Aankhon se phir ye pyaar ki barasaat ho na hoShaayad phir is janam men mulaaqaat ho na hoLag ja gale ki phir ye hasin raat ho na ho

Translation:

What if you’ll never see love in these eyes again,What if we never meet in this lifetime again,Embrace me, what if such a beautiful night never comes again?

5. Abhi Na Jao

Sitaare jhilmila uthechirag jagmaga uthebas ab na mujhko toknana badhke raah roknaAgar main ruk gayi abhito jaa na paungi kabhiyahi kahoge tum sadake dil abhi nahi bhara

The stars are out, evening lamps flicker,Now, don’t stop me,I might never be able to leave,No matter how long I stay,You’ll forever want more.

6. Dehleez pe mere dil ki

Dehleez pe mere dil kiJo rakhe hain tune kadamTere naam pe meri zindagiLikh di mere humdu.Na sikha kabhi jeena jeena kaise jeenaNa sikha jeena tere bina humdum

The moment you entered the doorstep of my heart,I’ve made you my entire life,I learned to live,but I’ve not learned to live without you, my soul.

title: How to Spend: The Icky Spending Habits of Your Friends and How It Impacts You
content:
How to Spend: The Icky Spending Habits of Your Friends and How It Impacts You

You stay balanced for your own good.

“You spent how much on that smart-home thing?”

When she introduced me to her new Apple home assistant, I was pleasantly impressed.But wait, how much does this thing cost? Also, where’s my money?

Before you tell me that I don’t have the right to have an opinion on other’s spending, know that this person owed me a lot of money. The money I lent because she promised that she absolutely needed it to stay afloat. This was years ago. Now she can certainly afford to pay me back. I am also certain she’d be there for me too when I’m in need, but now I’m not sure she ever can afford to help when the time comes.

Another couple I know always “tags along” when we make group plans but exits to the bathroom when the waiter brings the check. They don’t mind turning up to parties with nothing in their hands and are usually the first to send you Venmo when they pay for as much as tissue paper. They make expensive choices when the group splits the bill equally and no choices at all when it is their turn.

To me, both spending extremes are icky.

I, My middle-class Wallet, and the 50-hour rule

I grew up believing that every penny we spend was very hard to make. Not a great outlook to start life with, but I’d prefer this to being people in the stories above.

After I made the first few bucks from my salary, my instinct was that I really didn’t deserve any luxury yet. I lived in little shared apartments and bought used furniture. I also bought affordable electronics and only what I absolutely needed.

If I spent on the things that I think are “luxury” like my super awesome ultra sexy white Mountain bike, I made it do its money’s worth.

I made a general rule for myself that I will spend money on a hobby only after I first invest at least 50 hours / other units of measure on it before I go on and buy accessories. Want new shoes to run a marathon? Run 50 kilometers with your current shoes first. Want an iPad to design art? Spend 50 hours drawing on free apps first.

This was a really helpful tool to keep my rampant cravings in check. I did, occasionally splurge for great products and services that absolutely amp up my work life. But where possible, I bought refurbished.

Their Carelessness in the light of my Frugality

A lot of my friends assume I’m rich because I bring gifts, pick up tabs for people who don’t earn (If they’re very close), and have my own little investments. (Many of my friends are starting out in their careers. I am too, with a couple of years of a headstart).

Once they did start out, I expected the same level of diligence in their attitude towards generosity since they were in the same financial boat as I was. I did not have a lot of money, and we were not family-rich.

Turns out, I was dead-ass wrong. People simply don’t remember these “tiny favors”. It might even be wrong of me to expect that they would. I certainly want to have a trustworthy friendship where we can count on each other, so I will continue to do this and expect less back.

My frugality was not so that you could splurge

What I certainly will continue to expect is that lent money is paid backand that returning it has the same level of priority as returning someone else’s or buying a Gucci shirt. I don’t care what psychological insecurity made you want that shirt in installments, but I will definitely judge you if you do it before making your dues even with me.

I cannot help but think, that had I prioritized my well-being the same way, I would not have had the money to spare to lend to you. My frugality was certainly not so that you could splurge.

I also don’t mind cutting off people who are slapping mad amounts of bills on me for expensive car rentals for group trips, and not paying their fairshare. I’ve sent some people go easier on these attitudes to save relationships but to me, it simply doesn’t add up.

I don’t mind contributing, but sorry, I do mind being ripped off.

What I hate the most about all this

The thing I like the least about all this is that I have to judge my friends who, otherwise, I perfectly adore. Not the ones sliding into the bathroom when the waiter comes, but the rest of them.

I also don’t like to “not be there for my friends”. I don’t want to say no when I can genuinely help. I don’t want to mistrust the human lot, because I had one bad experience. (To be fair, when money is involved, it is never just one bad experience.)

When I have no financial give and take with people, an expensive outfit or brand new cars merely amuse me but if you have expensive art hanging on your walls, and you show up in the newest fashion of the season, I expect you to be debt-free with me.

Am I evil? Is this too much to ask?

title: Equilibrium
content:
Equilibrium

A poem of survival

Still. Quiet.

A gust of wind from the left.

Thud. A moment spent on the ground.

Up.

You know what the floor looks like. You know. Knowledge.

Knowledge, Not shame.

Watch out! eyes that look down at you.

Watch.

Keep watching.

Still no sign of shame.

Distant memory.

The nostalgia of what fear looked like.

One long look at the imprints where shame had walked.

Now just a well-tread road. Nothing to see there.

But Future!

What about now?

Regret?

It is in the past.

Colors, stillness, beauty, smiles, love.

Where?

Now.

When?

Now.

Equilibrium.

title: Balagam Movie Review: An Authentic Tear-Jerker with a Very South Indian Soul.
content:
Balagam Movie Review: An Authentic Tear-Jerker with a Very South Indian Soul.

One of the few well-made movies this season

Death is an uncomfortable topic.

To tell the story of a death with humor, and yet to bring you to tears multiple times through the plot is genius.Balagam,by director Venu Yaldandi,was genius.

The plot without spoilers

The plot plays in a small village in Telangana. It follows the death of a grandfather and the subsequent drama that unfolds in the family. By the second half of the movie, the drama grows to engulf the whole village and the audience.

Balagam touches on the themes of death, love, family, and superstition. The movie is so convincing in its portrayal of superstition, that I hardly had the heart to question why people believe what they believe. I just understood and let it be, the way we do in our Indian families.

Hyper-realism and Telugu Culture

It was delightful to have Telugu culture portrayed well and without shame for once.

Most Telugu movies hide Telugu skin colors in whiter girls from the North of India, Telugu accents in poorly dubbed (and disturbing) Telangana, and our entire Telugu identity inHero-is-god-everybody-else-loserblockbusters. The makers of Balagam made Telugu look normal again. Thank you for that.

It was impossible for me to believe that the actors in the film were “just acting”. Every little nod, blink, and emotion flowed naturally from the cast- It could have been my hometown.

Not just the actors but the village, the costumes, homes, hospitals, everything to the smallest detail was convincing. It had me wondering why Telugu movies with hundreds of millions in investment and CGI effects fail to have my belief even for a minute.

The depiction of rituals around death in Telangana was accurate to the point of fault. Balagam brought back traditional elements like Oggu Katha, which are by now well forgotten bydesis(as opposed to Indians) — but still alive in India.

Visual Minimalism

One of the most captivating aspects of Balagam is its visual minimalism. The cinematography is ordinary and yet engaging, with the camera not stealing the show from the screenplay.

The color palette is normal like the one you’d find in an Indian village, without baiting the audience with the“extremely beautiful Telugu village”farce that movies usually try to sell us.

Dialogue and Music

Balagam’s dialogue and music fit seamlessly into the scenes. Much of its music is taken from traditional Telangana songs we hear on the street or on the radio. No fancy Devi Sri Prasad beats here, but the music still rings in your ears after the movie.

Dialogues are effortless and when the scene demands, funny. Except for one, the movie doesn’t force humor with side-track storylines composed for comedians. There were no face-pulling or memery tropes created by Brahmanandam and Sunil. Humor is somehow a part of this movie in a very intelligent way.

In all, this movie was a delight to watch. A masterpiece, taking after“Care of Kancharapalem.”

If you’d only watch one Telugu movie this year, this would be it.

title: Immigration: When is it Time to Move From Your Work-Country?
content:
Immigration: When is it Time to Move From Your Work-Country?

Lessons learned from the human-centric German immigrant Policy. (And a come-to-Germany-call)

A few days ago, I published anarticle about moving back home to India.Clearly, it resonated with many, with about 30,000 sightings and opinions pouring in from ex-pats worldwide.

Since then, I have been told multiple times how I am lucky to be in Europe and how it is different from the rest of the world. From Germany, I agree.

What all ex-pats talk about: “The next destination”

As an ex-pat, their country of residence hardly ever feels like a permanent home.

These are often the opinions and questions I hear:

Should they move to China from Germany, because that is the next big thing in the automotive industry?

Should they go to Europe (If they are currently in the USA), where a work-life balance is possible to achieve in reality?

Should they Emigrate from Europe to the USA because you can make exorbitant amounts of money if you are willing to work hard?

Should they consider the middle-east, with its obvious open arms for highly qualified workers and really low taxes, and its proximity to the Indian subcontinent?

Is Canada a good option, because it allows elderly parents to visit and stay permanently?

What makes sense?

One documentary about ex-pat workers in Qatar got me thinking (Link below).What makes a good choice for a country of destination?

Qatar workers get an entire day off only once every 3 or 4 years. One woman was even refused a day off even when her husband died. (I strongly recommend watching this video)

You might think this is only the case for blue-collar workers and doesn't apply to you.

But it does.

A country’s foreign policy reflects on ALL its immigrants. Here is how.

Policies that can make your life difficult in a foreign country

The following policies spell bad news for potential immigrants. Keep this in mind before you consider your move.

If a country shields its internet from the rest of the world, you don't want to make it your permanent home. Don’t move from a “more free” place to a “less free” place.

If a country does not allow you to ever become a citizen, no matter how long you live there, you will not be respected as a taxpayer either.

If you have no such thing as “sick days,” they are exploiting you.

You should never have to surrender your passports to the employer.

Many countries need you to have a local citizen sponsor you if you were to ever start your own venture. If you have a better option, don't go to this place.

Are you even allowed to start your own ventures? Are you free to, for example, write on the internet?

If you don’t feel free in a liberal world, you will feel even less accepted in nations that are overtly non-liberal. (Even if you are not liberal yourself).

Health care should be a part of your payment plan. If not, you should have at least an option of making it a part, or buying it at a price.

Even if you don't have a time-tracking system in place in your contract, ask your future co-workers about how much time they spend at work. 12 hours or more is a bad sign.

If you are working 13-hour days happily, make sure the compensation is that good. It should be VERY good compared to a country that has employment protection policies.

Having said that…

Every country has its own pros and cons. The above checklist makes European Countries like Germany, Switzerland, and Austria sound like dream destinations.

Expats in these countries complain that their cultures are insulated from immigrants as compared to those in the far-west like USA and Canada. Also, there is the additional bureaucratic and language barrier in Europe that is hard for many to cut through.

This is very true.

Learning a language is an easier barrier to break than habituated 16-hour work days. It is better to be able to own property, have health care, have hobbies and hustles apart from only your work, and also have opportunities to grow if you are willing to learn.

In short, when emigrating, Freedom is paramount.

Having said that, no country will be like home. But we all left home for a reason.

If you are planning to move to Europe, it will be a harder, slower, and less exciting life than in the rest of the world. But you will be safe.

Having lived in Germany, I am thankful for its foreign policy and how it treats me legally.

Personal struggles are real and they will always haunt you. You might even want to move out of Europe for this very reason.

but at least you won't sacrifice your human rights and dignity.

title: Showing up Matters the Most
content:
Showing up Matters the Most

Why it is important to stay connected despite your online-aversion

Times are changing, and fast.

A year ago, Toastmasters would not have been a thing if people were not to see each other. Today, when people found a way even to work their abs and glutes online, Toastmasters is just one of the many things that has been mastered without contact. Guess how much time was spent on making the change. Zero. That's right, The INgolSpeakers mastered the pandemic in less than one session and kept the culture alive.

For every one member that decided online is not their thing, two new ones joined. For every guest that didn't make it, one joined from across the continent. New blood rushed in to fill the gaps as soon as it could, membership still grew.

Myself, I complained about the loss of human contact because of the pandemic and sat unwillingly at a multitude of no-face meetings at work. I pulled back from a couple of evenings of the toastmasters online, because I was no more up for it.

This is what I figured, shutting out oneself didn't mean more human contact, it meant less. I missed the company, the enthusiasm of being there and doing it, most of all, I missed the fact that showing up matters the most.

What is “real”, changed from decade to decade. Real meetings got lonelier, with more and more friends looking with more enthusiasm into their phones than at each other. So what is the apprehension when digitally “present” people “really” look at each other?

Why not use this chance to be present, online or off? After all, presence is the greatest gift. Don’t let the pandemic get the better of you. Be online, be present, give and get presence, most of all,

Show up!

title: 4 Brilliant Ideas For Summer Activities in South Germany over a Long Weekend
content:
4 Brilliant Ideas For Summer Activities in South Germany over a Long Weekend

Bike, hike, or chill

How would you spend them if you had just 4 or 5 days of vacation — too little to go far, too much to stay home! Such are long weekends. If you live in the South of Germany, and looking to soak up the sun for a change, here are some ideas.

1. Allgäu: Immenstadt, Oberstdorf, Garmisch Partenkirchen and more

A bare 2 hours from Munich, and you are already in paradise. The beauty of Allgäu in the summer is one-of-a-kind. You can only take it all in on the bike or on foot — all with the 49€ monthly ticket!

This long weekend, just go to Munich and it’s all mountains from there.

I didn’t know where I wanted to go, and saw a train to Oberstdorf. I jumped on it with 3 minutes to decide. On my way, I saw that Immenstadt had cheap Airbnb’s, and got off there.

This place is an absolute paradise for canyoning, rafting, hiking — you name it. After I checked into the Airbnb, I found other fun solo-travellers like myself and we’re going hiking tomorrow! What a day! (It gets cold in the evenings even in summer, bring a jacket.)

2. Bike Road from Passau to Vienna

Since I bought my first Grand Canyon MTB in 2021, I’ve biked more than 2000 kilometers.

The second most beautiful experience of that was biking Passau to Vienna on theDonauradweg. The first was through the Italian Alps from Trento to Verona, via Claudio Augusti.

TheDonauradwegis a 300 km cycle path stretching from Germany to Vienna that follows along the Danube River (Donau in German).

A friend and I did the Donauradweg in 3 days, about 100km a day, -/+ 20. I’d recommend you take two or three more if you want to relax and enjoy the trip. If you want to know how to pack for a multi-day bike trip,here is a pointer.

With beautiful views, well paved roads, bike hotels and airbnbs, this is a 5-star road with no difficulty.

3. Enjoy a Therme, South Germany is full of them

GermanThermesare supremely relaxing hotspots. Beware if you are not into it, some of them have no-clothes-at-all rules. Others allow bathing suits.

You can purchase day-tickets to these places, some offer overnight stay with excellent food and services.

Inside these sprawling acres of wellness are hot pools, saunas, and steam baths — each with its own ambience, salt, scent and skin-benefits. By the time the day ends, your body will be a noodle.

Therme Sinsheim (the best I’ve been), Erding (second best), Beuren, Lindau, to name only a few, are excellent in the south of Germany. There are multitude more. (Czech Republic, not far from here also has excellent thermal baths).

The Dolomites

The Dolomites are an underrated travel destination. Super-easy to get to by car or train from Munich, dolomites offer spectacular landscapes south of the German border.

They are dotted with small lakes, high mountains, and beautiful views suited for long drives, bike rides or hikes. It is easy to find stuff to do here. The Italian food only makes it better. That feeling when Italian olive-oil and fresh tomato paste on the pizza hits your tongue is priceless!

If you don’t know where to start, Drei Zinnen (in the photo) is the most popular peak here.

I’ve been to dolomites hiking, it is still on my biking bucket list. It is not a place you can visit only once, there is simply too much to do!
