title: Reminder and Remainder.
content:
Reminder and Remainder.

చీకటితో వెలుగే చెప్పెను నేనున్నాననిఓటమితో గెలుపే చెప్పెను నేనున్నాననినేనున్నాననీ నీకేంకాదనినిన్నటిరాతనీ మార్చేస్తాననీతగిలే రాళ్లని పునాది చేసి ఎదగాలనీతరిమే వాళ్లని హితులుగ తలచి ముందుకెళ్లాలనీకన్నుల నీటిని కలల సాగుకై వాడుకోవాలనీకాల్చే నిప్పుని ప్రమిదగ మలచి కాంతి పంచాలనీగుండెతో ధైర్యం చెప్పెనుచూపుతో మార్గం చెప్పెనుఅడుగుతో గమ్యం చెప్పెను నేనున్నాననీనేనున్నాననీ నీకేంకాదనినిన్నటిరాతనీ మార్చేస్తాననీ.

ఎడారిదారుల తడారిపోయిన ఆశకు చెమటలధారలుపోయ్

నిస్సత్తువతో నిలబడనీయ్యక ఒక్కోఅడుగు ముందుకువేయ్

వందయేళ్ళ నీ నిండు జీవితం గండిపడదనే నమ్మకమై

శతకోటి సమస్యల ఎదుర్కొనేందుకు బతికివుండగల సాహసానివై

పరుగులుతీయ్ పరుగులుతీయ్ .... ఉరకలువేయ్

ఆకతాయుల నిహతి అనివర్యమవు నియతి(శిత హస్తి హత మస్త కారినక సవకాసియో)(క్రూరాసి క్రోసి హ్రుతదాయ దంష్ట్రుల దోసి)(మసి చేయ మహిత యజ్ఞం)అమేయమనూహ్యమనంత విశ్వంఆ బ్రహ్మాండపు సూక్ష్మ స్వరూపంఈ మానుష రూపంకుబ్జాకృతిగా బుద్ధిని భ్రమింపజేసేఅల్ప ప్రమాణంముజ్జగాలను మూడడుగులతో కొలిచేత్రైవిక్రమ విస్తరణంజరుగుతున్నది జగన్నాటకం

అణిమగా మహిమగా గరిమగా లఘిమగప్రాప్తిగా ప్రాకామ్యవర్తిగా ఈశత్వముగా వశిత్వమ్ముగానీలోని అష్తసిద్ధులు నీకు కన్వట్టగాస్వస్వరూపమే విశ్వరూపమ్ముగానరుని లోపలి పరునిపై దృష్టి పరుపగతలవంచి కైమోడ్చి శిష్యుడవు నీవైతేనీ ఆర్తి కడతేర్చు ఆచార్యుడవు నీవే

title: 5 Harari’s Tricks for Writing Exciting Non-Fiction
content:
5 Harari’s Tricks for Writing Exciting Non-Fiction

If you haven’t read Sapiens, you haven’t read non-fiction

If Yuval Noah Harari was to wipe his face on a paper towel and subsequently decides to publish it, I’d buy it. I bet the 12 million others who read his Sapiens would do the same. Harari has redefined what non-fictional writing means and feels like.

Sure there are other best-selling authors of non-fiction like Jared Diamond, Malcolm Gladwell, Bill Bryson, and Jon Ronson among my favorites. But I hardly remember devouring those books like those of Harari, and hardly remember having completely changed a perspective forever and for good. To be fair, Sapiens also sold 3 times as many million copies.

Here are some striking aspects of Harari’s writing that are a lesson to everyone who does some research.

1. Bibliography

The most striking aspect of Harari’s literature is his detailed and complete bibliography. All his books have nearly 20% of the total length dedicated to references. I hardly come across writers who do justice to their sources as Harari does. If you are writing non-fiction and would like to establish yourself as a credible source of information, quote, cite, respect the work of fellow authors, and NEVER steal ideas. Always put ethics first.

2. Limit your bottom line to one to three statements

Decide before you start writing, what is going to be the message of your book. What are to trying to convey? A novel argument (Like Harari)? A proof for some theory? examples for one particular argument from various perspectives? If you are writing a biography, why do you want your reader to read this book, what is in it for him?

Whatever is the question you ask from the above list, make sure your answer does not exceed three statements. This makes your writing precise and cuts out all the boring parts automatically. Structure your book around the backbone of these three ideas. The first 30% establishing idea 1, then idea 2, and so on.. while tying them all up in the end.

Harari ends his books with a clear and concise summary of what he wants the reader to take away from the book. It feels like a quick flip through the entire book and calls to mind the arguments he provided for each of these takeaways.

3. Facts are not your story, inferences are

This is where the platitude “Think outside the box” comes to life. What sets apart, per say Bill Bryson’s writing from Harari’s or Gladwell’s are that they are not a mere collection of facts. If you run these books through an AI, probably at least 70% of the total number of sentences are going to come out as opinions and inferences.

If one wanted facts, they’d just read the encyclopedia. But you as a writer of non-fiction bring value addition to facts by telling your savant inference to the ignoramus of the masses. Despite your extensive research, leave enough page space for inferencing. For every page of fact, add two or three of inference.

4. Stay on the point long enough

It is a natural tendency to over-edit. You end up cutting out what feels repetitive. If there is one thing I learned from Harari’s writing and from Toastmasters, it is that strangers to the topic appreciate repetition to a certain extent, when it is done tastefully and in context.

They don’t know your topic as well as you do. They haven’t broken their bones and heads researching every loose end. So if you repeat your bottom line here and there, it doesn’t hurt. In fact, it might make your writing seem more intense and compelling.

5. Be objective

Differentiate between fact and opinion. Know science and research from tradition and belief. Think about how different demographics of the readership may have a different perspective. As far as possible stay factual and neutral. After all, science and research are constantly subject to change. Your arguments might be disproved sooner or later. Be humble and stay objective.

Hope you enjoyed that read. If you haven't read all of Harari’s books already, I’d strongly recommend some for some serious learning.

If you’d like to connect with me, here is myMediumprofile, and here is myLinkedin. Happy writing!

title: Writer’s Definitions of Popular things
content:
Writer’s Definitions for Popular Things

Movies: Where books go to die.

Working day

7:00 PM to 2:00 AM.

Job

Anything that pays over 1 cent.

Book

The unfinished draft on your desktop, that will be backed up to the cloud “really soon”.

Bestselling author

That guy who wrote what you “definitely could have”.

Self

That dapper guy in the mirror who is sure going to be bestselling.

Others

Face-holes yapping unsolicited advice.

Cloud

That expensive home for my draft I can’t afford to pay rent for. (Is there a one-time payment plan, please.)

MS. Word

The white page that can’t shut up about “don’t” actually being “do not”.

Kindle

Tablet with cheaper books than paperbacks but you ended up buying more books, so turns out it was massively expensive.

Time

“Someday…they’ll see.”

Block

A wad of tiny papersAlso, that guy your head who pulls at your pen while you write

Rent

Life is meaningless and we are all a part of a simulation.

Netflix

That channel which is going to buy rights for your draft.

Medium

That place where you could definitely do better if only you didn't have other projects.

Depression

The dark place you dig at for your most authentic story.

Children

Little people, you are going to blame your writer’s block on.

title: An Analysis of My Journey on Medium
content:
An Analysis of My Journey on Medium

From a parasite to a creator, qualitative and quantitative changes

In this article, I will talk about:

Part 1: How Medium Improved the quality of my hobbies.

Part 2: True Score Metric to measure the real success of your article on Medium.

The True Score metric introduced in this article will help you measure and the improvement in your writing despite not having enough views or earning.

Part 1: How Medium improved the quality of my hobbies

I started writing on Medium 3 months ago. Since then I have been curated multiple times under Data Science, Writing, Humor, and Machine Learning. I have been published multiple times in major publications like:

Towards Data Science

The Writing Cooperative

The Start-up

Data-Driven Investor

Illumination

The Haven

Be yourself

But the major takeaway from creating on Medium was that I was no more a parasite feeding off Instagram, Facebook, and the like. I was a creator. This is a powerful feeling. It is uncanny how the reward-feeling of publishing is extremely similar to getting a double-tap on Instagram or a Thumbs-up on Facebook.The difference on Medium is that this reward comes after hours of thought and productive actionthat goes into making a piece of writing insightful and read-worthy to a wider audience. Here are some of my “hobbies” before I set foot on Medium,note how most of them are pure consumption:

Reading(I read about 30 to 40 books a year)

Obsessively watching Stand-up Comedy in all languages I can

Instagram, Linkedin, Facebook, repeat.

Toastmaster Club: Speech-writing and Presenting

Scripting with python

3 out of the 5 major activities that dominated my mind during the day-time were consumption intensive!Here is how Medium revolutionized every one of my hobbies to become more structured and productive:

Reading

One could argue that reading is not pure consumption since it changes you as a person.Reading is different from active reading.Writing on Medium showed me how much or how little of what I read I retain and can reproduce in context. This is the measure of its effectiveness.

Medium turned me into an active reader. Waltzing through pages aimlessly is different from making notes, quoting, and elaborating with your opinion on what you read. I have since publishedreviewsof about 10 books and wrote articles about the ideas thatarose from the booksas an inspiration. Active reading not only makes your comprehension better but channels your thought into the meta-

Why did the author write this? What methods did he use to collect this information? Why do I feel the way I feel about this book? How can I reproduce this effect?

2. Watching Stand-up Comedy

I have been a life-long enthusiast of Humor and devoured the literature in this area. Turning off your brain and watching comedy is different from active listening and taking notes. Thanks to Medium, I wrote somepiecesofsatireand created somecomicsmyself which were well received. I can proudly sayI am no more just a consumer of comedy.

3. Social Media

I was prey to Social Media. Mindless scrolling through pages of Facebook and Instagram to see what people are doing killed my time and sense of being.

Writing on Medium helped me find a positive writer’s support group and strategize effectively to improve my writing.

I learned about SEO optimization, strategic approach to good writing, and learned what are doable goals based on other writer’s experiences on the internet. The difference between using social media to learn and mindless scrolling is the same as the difference between playing hop-scotch in pajamas vs training for a marathon.

As for my speaking club and Data-science enthusiasm, Medium turned out to be a goldmine of learning. My writing and speaking complement each other. In both cases, I am trying to overcome the challenges of:

Minimal Verbosity:I’m cutting words faster than calories

Organization:I write speeches and articles every week

Valuable contacts:I recognize the importance of value-adding people and learn from them.

Like Elon Musk said, If you can’t collaborate with your family, collaborate with your friends. If you cannot collaborate with your friends, get better friends.

Believe it or not, My technology-averse mother is now a serious blogger and we make an amazing team!

Programming/Scripting:Having contributed to the top publications in this area, I am motivated to take on more. The extremely high quality of articles on medium for Data Science also keeps me up-to-date.

Part 2: Defining Quantitative Targets

While setting goals, make sure that they should be achievable andin your control.I figured that on Medium, there is no direct relation to earnings and quality of the article or the publication.

Making earnings a goal is not sensible on Medium

since it’ causes are abstract and uncontrollable. It might depend on uncontrollable factors like the number of paying members viewing your article, your article being recommended by an active member, the activity of members that are reading your article, it’s topic, the number of tags it is curated under, etc. the list is enormous.

But, do your best at the influenceable factors.

Here are some influenceable factors that are worth targeting:

Write More

Here is a summary of the number of words I have written since the start of Medium-ing. Since my articles are also being read, I have ventured out of mini-articles under 500 words to bigger ones, while simultaneously cutting the bull-sh*t. (These stats are of course verifiable from my profile. The stats for June include two complete but unpublished drafts, of which the current article is a part).

Marketing Strategy

Every upcoming bar on the My Stats chart above will be taller its left-side counterpart.

Good strategies are simple and verifiable. The above strategy streamlines my marketing goals for the articles. Earnings aside, I know exactly how much marketing is to be done on a given day to reach my goal number views. whether the views are internal or external does not matter to me. If I don’t make well on my target in a specific week, I try to average them out over the weekend or the week after.

Writing Quality

The metric Read Ratio is an accurate reflection of the quality of your writing. views might come from marketing but a high read ratio is not something you can trick people into. Read ratio reflects two things:

Length of the article

Writing Quality

Length, because shorter articles might have a higher read-ratio despite their quality. If my articles have an appalling read ratio, It either means:

The writing was not captivating enough to read on to the next line.

The verbosity was unnecessary, there are other mistakes in grammar/presentation which make the writing unappealing.

Read ratio is also my favorite parameter to watch because it does not depend on how many people click, how bad your title is or that it is not on a big publication.

If only 10 viewers read your article (Strangers, since friends might do it out of love) and 9 out of 10 read it through, the quality of your writing is very high.

To get a high read ratio you might have to:

Learn to build tension in your writing (without clickbait of course)

Relate to the reader on a personal level

Write concisely

Be informative

All of these are very deep skills that take time and growth to improve, unlike the number of views, which is just clicking on the share button. If you can hit both read ratio and views, bingo!My highest earning article has the highest read-ratio for its word count and also the highest number of views.

The True Score Metric

I invented the followingTrue scorefor myself to give my read ratio more meaning. (This is purely my metric, not a standard) The score has two goals:

To measure the actual success of your read ratio despite how long or short your article is.

To reward longer articles. Shorter articles have a goal of “not boring” the reader.A good article should have both length and substance.Also from my experience, shorter articles automatically have a higher read ratio

Here is how I calculate it:

I divide all the article lengths with the word count of my largest article and express them as a proportion to my largest article.

N = (100 *l ) / b

Proportional length of the article → N

Length of the article→ l

Length of the largest article→ b

My longest article, say of 1600 words, will get a score of 100. The shortest, say of 800 words, gets a 50. Now all my article lengths are below 100 and greater than 0.

2. I define my True score as:

T= r / 200— N

Read-ratio of the article → r

proportional length →N

True Score →T

200 is a constant value I chose since it gives an intuitive curve for reading ratios. Take the following example:

If an article of the largest length (say 1600) has a read ratio of 80, my true score would be:

N = 100 * (1600/1600) = 100

T = 80 / (200–100) = 0.8

If an article of word length 200 has a read ratio of 80, my true score would be:

N = 100 * (200/1600) = 12.5

T = 80/ (200–12.5) = 0.42

The read ratio is penalized by the small length of the article. For a 100% read ratio, here is what the true score values will look like at different lengths of the article:

Notice that for the same read ratio, an article of a larger word-count gets a higher True score. In the above chart, for a read ratio of 100, my largest article gets a true score of 1 whereas the smallest article (here of proportional word length 10) gets a True score of 0.5.

Here are my Actual True Scores for various articles and How they fare on True Score:

My highest earning article is the last bar on the above chart. (I removed comics in this analysis which have a word length of 0).It is also the second-highest True Score.The bar with the highest true score has only 29 views (Wasn’t marketed/Waiting on Curation). It is safe to assume that despite the good quality of this article, it has not reached as big an audience. The second highest true-score has reached an audience of 2.5k and has an extremely high read-ratio of 60% — clearly reflected in the earnings it brought.

If you want to be a good writer,your goal should be to Improve your True Score and Views(Marketing + Quality of writing).

All said and done, the cliche is true — Write because you love to write. It is impossible to put a metric on well-spent time and the amount of learning you incur in the process. I strongly recommend that you continue reading and writing on medium and keep growing!

title: 5 Unusual Documentaries on Holocaust Survival, WW2, and Jewish History
content:
5 Unusual Documentaries on Holocaust Survival, WW2, and Jewish History

Available on Netflix

There are hundreds of war movies. WW2 is a topic that is never exhausted of perspectives. Many horrifying, even incredibly inspiring stories came out of that era.

The following documentaries from the war, pre-, and post-war periods on Netflix offer fresh perspectives on the story. Despite watching all those war movies, these are topics I never knew about.

1. The Last Days: The Story of Hungarian Jews

Run time: 87 Minutes. IMDb: 7.9, Emotion: Touching

The Last Daysis a very touching first-person documentary on the topic of Holocaust survival.

Originally made in 1988, it is a series of interviews with Hungarian Jews who were captured during the last year of the war.

Following the survivors' stories from birth, childhood, their transport to German concentration camps, their lives there, and subsequent rescue, this documentary might make you tear up.

As noted byThe Guardian:

“The film’s thesis is that the Nazis were so fueled by hatred that they would sacrifice their position in the war in order to carry out the genocide, deporting 438,000 Hungarian Jews to Auschwitz within a six-week period.”

As a spectacular ending to the documentary, one of the survivors who lost every last member of his family got a special gift from his 2 daughters — 16 Grandkids! Another went on to become the first Jewish senator in the USA.

2. The Devil Next Door: The story of a suspected Nazi war criminal in the USA

Run time: 3h49 Minutes, 5 Episodes. IMDb: 7.5, Emotion: Unbelievable

The Devil Next Dooris a haunting documentary about the perplexing case of John Demjanjuk, an “ordinary” Cleveland grandfather accused of being the infamous Nazi death camp guard known as “Ivan the Terrible.”

This documentary covers his arrest, extradition to Israel, and trial. I’ve hardly ever experienced a roller-coaster of emotions like while watching this story unfold, where you are kept guessing if he is really “The Terrible” or just another grandpa.

You can form your own opinions about his guilt or innocence, but you will change it in every episode.

Through interviews with family members, survivors, lawyers, and journalists, the filmmakers humanize both the accused and the victims. The agony faced by Holocaust survivors, who confront their darkest memories with Ivan now in the courtroom — a man who cut the flesh off of Jews, who were already waiting to be gassed.

The use of archival footage and photographs adds authenticity and historical depth to the story. This is an excellent work of journalism.

3. Camp Confidential: The poaching of German scientists to the USA

Run time: 36 Minutes, ImDB: 6.6, Emotion: Bizarre!

In this bizarre story, three Jewish men who fled to America from Germany join the American Army. They are prepared to give their lives fighting against the Nazis, but end up in a kind of “resort”.

At this resort, the American government tries to woo German scientists, AKA Nazi war criminals, to build bombs and rockets for them instead. Sure enough, this is where Werner von Braun, the head of the NASA project which put Man on the moon, started working for the USA. (Yeah he was a Nazi).

4. Baghdad Remembers: The history of Iraqi Jews — all 5 of them (Yes, there were only 5 Jews left in Iraq by the time of filming)

Run time: 72 Minutes, ImDB: 7.5, Emotion: Informative, touching.

Bhagdad Remembersis a documentary on Jews of Iraq, and their gradual disappearance from the Iraqi landscape over the years. The documentary follows the lives of former-Iraqi Jews — born in Iraq during the “good times” but fled to London during various phases of political turmoil.

While the world looks at Germany for the history of anti-Semitism, many parts of the world are forgotten. This documentary throws light on the not-so-far past of Judaism’s persecution in Iraq.

Following various regimes throughout Iraq’s history and the developments that lead to a near-complete ousting of Jews from the Land,Baghdad Remembersis an enlightening documentary.

5. The Adolf Eichmann Trial

Run time: 90 Minutes, ImDB: 7,4, Emotion: Matter-of-fact with touching stories

The French documentary,The Adolf Eichmann Trial, is a compelling journey into one of the most significant trials in history. The trial of one of the key architects of the Holocaust — Adolf Eichmann, responsible for the “transport” of Jews to their concentration camps.

This is the first time in history where Jewish victims of the Holocaust took the stage and told stories first-hand while their perpetrators stood in front of them.

“The officer stuck a boy with a tight blow of his stick. I counted 80 blows,”one victim said. “But what I couldn’t believe was the 81st blow.”

One of the most chilling moments for me was when Eichmann denied to acknowledge his own and his fellow officers’ actions as criminal. The usual excuse, “We were following orders,” was a large part of the defense.

The Adolf Eichmann Trialis an engrossing and emotionally-charged documentary with meticulous attention to historical detail. It serves as a poignant reminder of the horrors of the Holocaust, the importance of justice, and the need to learn from our past to create a more compassionate and just future.

If you liked this topic, you might enjoy the book Man’s Search for Meaning (By Victor Frankl), and Night (by Elie Wiesel). Here is a related thesis.

I am Sruthi, I write on Immigration, travel, and lifestyle. Join me on Medium for all you can read and write. It costs 5$ and changed my life.

title: Data Visualization Essentials for Meaningful Insights
content:
Data Visualization Essentials for Meaningful Insights

Things to do, in that order

If you are a Business Intelligence Analyst, an Auditor, a Data Engineer, or anyone who cares about the “data” aspect of decision-making, you already deal with charts and plots aplenty. Tools like Tableau, Qlik, Power-BI, Python, R, Plotly, etc. dominate this world and make drawing insights from raw data easy (Thanks a LOT!). As I mentioned in my previous articlePre-process Like a Pro, the value-addition to work comes through the insights that the charts deliver rather than the charts themselves. This article focuses mainly on checkpoints at various steps to make sure your dashboards are effective and insightful.

Ask for proofs of accuracy and completeness

Step 0 of dashboarding is always to check foraccuracy and completeness of source data. If you didn’t extract the data yourself and got it from elsewhere, call-up/email/text/WhatsApp and get convincing proof.

Ask for an excerpt of the logic used to extract this data. Here are some questions that will help:

Were all the filters set according to your question?

Was the time period correct?

Do the parameters mean what you think they do?

Are these parameters correct representatives for the metric you want to observe?

This is your proof of accuracy.

Usually, data query results come with a message on the screen sayingX lines of results found-This should be the number in the data-extract that you have. You could compare it to the total volume of data/ data generated in the previous similar extracts to check if this is a plausible number.

This is your proof of completeness.

For every image, get a context

Let us assume that you have data verified for accuracy and completeness on your hand. You have now built a beautiful chart of your data. To draw insights from any chart (let us call thisimage), there should always be a second chart to make the scene complete and meaningful (let’s call thiscontext). Never trust standalone figures no matter how awesome or awful they look.

The moment you see an image, look for a context.

Context from the big picture

Always see what the bigger picture says.Here are some examples of how to look for context in the big picture withhighlightedprepositionsyour context should contain.Consider the following examples:

If you see that one machine produces 90% of all your defective parts(Image)before you go ahead and repair this machine you also need a second chart showing how manyof the total partsthis machine produces(context). If it also produces 90% of all the parts, of course, it has more parts that are defective, which means all your machines need fixing.

If you see that your company has made 100k$ a month(Image), look at your market sharein comparisontothe others on the market with asimilar targetcustomer base(context).

If 1 million of your parts came out defective(Image), look at what is the defective partsper millionon the machine and how many million you produced(context).

Remember context looks like a percent or a ratio. A number looks like it is there to incite emotion. Take this example from Factfulness, by the Data Guru and founder ofGapminder.org,Hans Rosling:

Without context: 4.2 Million Babies died in 2016.

Horrible, isn’t it?

With context: It was 4.5 Million in 2015, 4.4 Million in 2014 and 14 million in 1950. We suddenly have 10 million less dead babies than we did before. Is it a good or bad way that we are on?

4. If someone is trying to sell you information with an image but no context, RUN!

Context from Time

Time context shows seasonality and trend in your observations.Example:Are the data pointshigheveryWednesday (seasonality) in comparison to the rest of the week? Are theyhigher/lower everyweek than the previous (trend)?

How did your metricschange sinceYesterday/ last month / last year the same season? Do you have high sales just because the sun is high in June? Did you do something magnificent that you can repeat?

Another detail that time provides is therate of change.How dynamic is your metric?

While performing further research on your raw data (such as with Machine Learning),remember to normalize forandremoveseasonality and trend from the picture.

Look for patterns

Once you have your images with their context, start looking for patterns. The best way to start is by just looking at them with your eyes at various levels of zoom with at least two different parameters taken at a time.

For example, assume you have these parameters:

Profit from 10 products, the amount of money invested in the products, the height of the Department head, research revenue of these products.

Ploteach of these parameters against every otherand see what they tell you. You can never have too much visual information. Ask your self —“what is a cause”and“what is a coincidence”. (Yeah, the height of the guy vs anything probably was a coincidence…)

2. Look for like-sized, like-ranged parameters to see if they have common behavior in more than one context. This might (not must) help determine —what is a coincidence and what is a causality.

Make sure your charts are representative of all important information

It is human tendency to classify things as black and white, good and bad, 1 and 0. Before you apply this tendency to your data representation, see if your black-white buckets are representative of ALL information. For example, look at the following questions:

Are all students in the class good, bad, and average or do you need skill level and subject information?

Is adding subject information not changing your insight but slowing your graphics down?

Is a monthly representation a sufficient granularity or do you need a weekly bucket?

Be extremely careful while creating average buckets. If you use them to compare, take care to compare extremes with extremes, and means with means. Leave anomalies out of average buckets.

Some information is more important than the other

To check whether the parameters you chose are the correct representatives,check for parameters whose removal has the most drastic impactin your KPI or metric. This or these groups of parameters are the most important ones that you should want to build your talking points on.

Having said this, note that in the real world, cause and effect are much more complex than we imagine.Don’t fall prey to the simplest explanation there isin front of your eyes. This is the hardest and the most tempting block to overcome on the way to effective problem-solving.

title: Poem — I Want to Be Free
content:
Poem — I Want to Be Free

If there is such a thing.

How to be free? where are my wings?I dream about the outside but I am always in.Why is the clock always counting down?when I know that at the very end, I will still not be done.

Why can’t I abandon my projects?Why isn’t every day new?I want to name red rocks,Why should my interests be of any value?

I want to wander off, not have a map,sleep under a leaf, and never come back.Why did my mom not kick me from the nest?to fend for myself, and find my own friends?

Why can’t I go somewhere else?Why is everywhere a Burger king?Is there no challenge, no grit,no glory to keep living?

I want go, I want to leave,from unreal places and everyone fake,I want to f off, I want to srexit,Where is real freedom? Is there such a thing?

I amSruthi Korlakunta.

Connect with me onLinkedInandMedium. It would be awesome to meet you!

title: 5 Shallow Tips That Really Changed My Life
content:
5 Shallow Tips That Will Really Change Your Life

They say only personality matters. Shallow things too, count in a shallow world.

This article is not a provocation.

Body positivity is good. Looks are skin deep and money isn't the most important thing in the world. At the core of it, if you don't actually become a better, more positive, knowledgeable, and compassionate person, you are going to be left behind.

BUT. No matter how good your product (aka you) is, without the packaging it is going to be hard to sell. Imagine having to carry home a liter of Coca-Cola in a plastic bag instead of a bottle. Packaging matters.

If these shallow things helped me, be it. I am going to thank them and keep them going.

1. Get in shape.

There is a world of difference in how the world treats you in your different phases of shape. I am its biggest witness.

I was fat and thin, and fat again, and thin again. And people notice. Especially in Europe, looks matter a lot. But it is more or less the same in the rest of the world too.

Unfortunate as it is, more opportunities come your way if you “look the part”, regardless of the truth. The confidence boost that a good body gives you, is unfortunate and very real.

I regret to recognize that the phases of life where I was in my best shape have been the most memorable and kindest phases of life for me.

2. Dress Well.

If not working out, the second best option to look great is to dress well.

I hate dressing up. I hate pretty clothes, I hate trying hard to look good. It feels inherently weak and stupid if you have to dress up to look legitimate.but it works.

The most confident of the men and women I met in life were not the greatest dressers. But they had already made their place in the world and accumulated weight to their word by having a big name.

If you are just starting out, dress well. Buy well-fitting clothes and do your laundry.

3. Learn Proper Grammar (in any language).

You might have the best ideas but if they don't understand them, dim-wits are going to look down upon you. There is no end to dim-wits in this world.

I have been learning the German language for 8 years now. I notice that in Germany, non-natives who speak good German get treated differently than those who do not.

I have my good days and bad days when it comes to fluency in German. On my good days, I feel like a diva. People shower compliments on you as if speaking a foreign language is magic. On the more stammer-y days, I get awkward glances. Switching language to English is a weakness I’d like not to show here.

No matter what language you are speaking, try to learn good grammar. Keep your sentences simple, but use the right grammar. Read in that language, It pays off.

4. Always find something good in what you see, especially in other people. (Even if it is nearly impossible).

One thing I learned after finishing college is this — People disguise their dislike in positive words. and I HATE IT. I hate it because it confuses me. It feels like there is a curtain that is preventing me from seeing the truth.

Though it is ok to say no and to express your disapproval, mask your words to be as positive as possible. Negative language is a shortcut to destination-f*ked(language courtesy of Ozzy-man).

You might even find yourself thinking more positively, now that you eliminated that way of language.

Though it might feel untruthful, don't ever say anything negative. It is a win-win. After a few days, you will be thankful for your choice of words.

5. Look like you have money and don't be obviously cheap (in reasonable limits).

Obviously, showing your wealth off is unclassy.

But, don't be obviously cheap in a public setting. Don’t try to push the bill onto others, don't try to save a penny by ordering the cheapest, and don't wear the cheap-local store brands that wear out within days of use and remain worn out through their time with you.

Pay people back if you owe them. Pick up the check for the weaker ones sometimes. People notice these things.

As a student, I couldn't really afford to hang out with the full-time employees of my company. I am extremely thankful and grateful for those who picked up the tab when they knew I couldn't. Do the same for those weaker than you, at least once in a while.

This may not come naturally to you, but adopt it, it is a good habit even if you have to fake it.

I am Sruthi Korlakunta, I write on tech, lifestyle, and books (I’m a top writer on this topic). If you have a story to share, hit me up onLinkedIn! I have 890+ followers, over 225k reads, and counting.

Read all you want on Medium by joining with my referral link here. It costs 5$ a month and changed my life!

title: The Story Circle: A Genius Storytelling Format Making Viral Plots For Netflix
content:
The Story Circle: A Genius Storytelling Format Making Viral Plots For Netflix

The backbone of Rick and Morty, community and IT crowd

There are regular TV series and there are TV series that you dream about in sleep. They are so good, you sometimes pause and rerun the last 10 seconds to appreciate the intelligence of the dialog.

Such shows which had my full attention and respect areRick and Morty, Bojack Horseman, and Community. They piqued my interest in effective storytelling and play no small part in my attempts at writing.

These series, agnostic to mainstream wants and responsibilities, remain true to their characters and develop believable arcs.

Two of the above shows — Rick and Morty and Community, have been created by Dan Harmon, who also invented a brilliant and simplified story-mathematics called the story circle. The source of such formalization of narrative originates from “The Hero’s Journey”, a structure popularized by Joseph Campbell in his book “The Hero with a thousand faces”.

The story circle

Dan Harmon’s story circle is similar to the originalHero’s Journeyand majestically simple. In Harmon’s story circle, A character in any story goes through the following phases:

A character is in a zone of comfort,

But they want something.

They enter an unfamiliar situation,

Adapt to it,

Get what they wanted,

Pay a heavy price for it,

Then return to their familiar situation,

Having changed.

The beauty of this circle(as opposed to Campbell’s Hero’s journey)is that it puts the character in the center of happenings. Take a look at the stages of story development in the Hero’s Journey:

Everyday Life, Call to Adventure, Accepting the Call, Challenges/Action, Death and Resurrection, Transformation and Atonement, Return.

The first and the most immediate observation that meets the eye is thatThe Hero’s Journeytalks about whathappens tothe character. The phases take the Hero through most of the story as if he is a slave to circumstance and an effect of unknown causes. The emphasis is on the happenings. Now look at the story circle:

Here, the hero is the center of the happenings. He“is”an agencythat wants, searches, finds, and takes. Characters created by Harmon are also extremely human and go through internal turmoil while being casually“super-self-aware”.

Harmon lays emphasis on the circular format of this storytelling which makes the character return to a “normal”.Why is this important?In the creator’s words:

“Jack goes up the beanstalk, Jack finds some cool shit, Jack steals it, runs back down, and gives it to his Mom. We need go search- We need get fire, we need good woman, we need land moon- but most importantly, we need RETURN and we need CHANGE, because we are a community, and if our heroes just climbed beanstalks and never came down, we wouldn’t have survived our first ice age.”

The rhythm of the circle — Going down and rising above is a rhythm we all go through on a daily basis. Harmon likens the top of the circle to order: The beautiful living room which we are all consciously proud of and invite friends and guests into.

The dark chaos, into which we all descend is the subconscious. We make a trip into this dark “basement of our head” in order to bring into the conscious what we want and need.

For instance, imagine yourself at work. On the surface, you want a promotion, to be perfect, to be a great co-worker or a helpful friend. This is your ‘order’, the fact that the universe gets to see.

TheWhyof all this is not as pretty. It might be caused by your fear of being disliked or your need for validation or your feeling morally superior. This is the chaos you deal with, which in turn reflects in your conscious choices and actions.

The main characters in a story go through this rhythm of consciousness and subconscious, in order to get what they want — No matter how silly or serious the want itself may be.

They may need to fight the subconscious or actively kick it alive to come back up. The audience at the same time, tap their feet to the rhythm of this cycle, resonating because it is familiar.

For you to copy

Let’s get to the nitty-gritty of each of the phases of Harmon’s storytelling.

You

This is a protagonist that your audience is willing to change shoes with. They should relate to, feel, or understand this character. You can play around with changing this “relatable anchor” from character to character but eventually, you have to throw the audience a bone without letting them hang for too long.

Need

Here your character wants to feel alright but doesn't. He wants to change something so that his world is alright again.

Go

If you are making a movie, this stage is your poster. If you are writing a book, this is your cover. Is your character a painting? Did it come to life in step 2? Now it leaves the frame. Your character is a little girl? Did she want ice cream from an innocent van? Now she is kidnapped.

Search

This is where your character ventures into chaos and subconscious. Sink or swim. Make a choice, cut the crap.

Find

A time and place for relief. Love. Sex. Vulnerability. Hope. There is after all a “why” to everything. The little girl saw her favorite ice cream through a hole in the sack she is encased in.

Take

Here is where your character meets his maker. A struggle, fight, a demon, a car chase, a genius invention.

Return

You cannot stay in the basement forever. There is a reason why you lived through all that. For this very moment.

Change

You have conquered your chaos, congratulations until the next adventure to the dark side begins.

Structure removes the repetitive, drab, boring, process of reinventing the wheel every time you want to drive. It gives you time to put the meat and work into your story instead of swarming you with“what should happen now?”.

Once you decide what’s in the basement and what’s on the surface, you can dip your Hero into the most action-packed story there was. After all, nobody isnota story-teller.

title: I hit the impossible Level 80 on Duolingo — Can I be honest?
content:
I hit the impossible Level 80 on Duolingo — Honest Review

Final verdict on whether the owl is helpful or just a drama queen.

I reached level 80, the maximum level on Duolingo for the German language. I just wanted to see the end, and I did. I hitLevel 80 in the German course, the current summit of the Duolingo mountain in this language. Apparently, there are more levels coming, but they don’t exist yet. This is supposed to be equivalent to a B1 level, which means I should be able to have conversations in most daily situations.

German is, well.. an interesting language (Rindfleischetikettierungsüberwachungsaufgabenübertragungsgeset is a real word in German).How effectively can Duolingo teach this beautiful beast of a language that is softer than it sounds?

If it feels like learning, and looks like learning, it must be learning(?)

After the “Level 80 high,” I was waiting for a little song and dance, given that the owl is a drama queen and knows how to push your buttons. There was none, but I can live with that.

What I could not live with, is the feeling of emptiness and stagnation that I kept waiting to change. I kept waiting for a big test that would give me a chance to unleash my prowess and apply my beautiful skills. Something that makes me scratch my head and make enough mistakes to feel like I want to go back and check up on something.

But no. It remained the same old drab format of never-challenging, never-increasing complexity until level 80 came to an end.

It looks like learning, it sounds like learning, but turns out, it’s only behavioral conditioning. We are all Pavlov’s dogs, if Pavlov were an owl.

Mnemonics vs. Logic

I’ve always loved learning languages. Thankfully, I also took German classes on the side and worked with German colleagues enough to speak the language daily, so yes, I can speak German. Duolingo’s contribution pales in comparison.

Duolingo treats language like a list of matching labels. If you saw one of those videos where Dog owners teach their dogs how to “talk” by pressing buttons on a mat, that’s how Duolingo tries to teach you. But in reality, language is a system of logic, not unlike mathematics. This complexity is lost on the app that is too afraid to challenge the user, lest they might lose a customer.

It hopes that by showing you 500 sentences where “the dog” isder Hundand “I see the dog” isden Hund, you will eventually “vibe” your way into the Accusative case. This bypasses the analytical satisfaction of understandingwhya word changed.

It’s pattern matching, rewarded by XP and whatnots, and nothing more. Just like a dog learns to rollover. Does it really understand what “roll over” means? When you tell the dog “there is a cream roll over there”, does it roll over or get the cream roll?

But gamification is good!

This is how I imagine Duolingo tests their own efficacy: They don’t test “Did the user learn the accusative case today?” but they do test “Does a purple button make them stay 3 minutes longer than a blue one?”,

No matter what level you achieve, the app remains “too easy”. By keeping the difficulty curve flat, they avoid frustrating the user at the cost of ever making them better. We all know any progress involvesatleastsome level of pain, cognitive stress in the least. But the app is designed to keep you just hooked enough without making you feel stupid.

You’ll feel like you are crushing it, because you’re doing 10 lessons a day, but the mathematical intelligence required to actually synthesize a new sentence from scratch is never truly triggered.

The AI Calling feature

When I started using the video call feature, I thought it would bridge some of these gaps. A few calls in, this was no different from all their other features. To compare, I did a call with Gemini, trying to practise my language (I did this with my Spanish course), which felt like a much more realistic challenge, because real-life conversations don’t happen in your own knowledge range. But that's how you grow!

What to do if you are serious about language learning?

The most rebellious (and effective) thing you can do is break the streak. Duolingo is still a good tool to brush up on your words once in a while, but you are just pouring in time into another shiny app if you are doing it with the religious dedication to streaks on it.

If you want to actually use that mathematical brain, you might find more satisfaction in learning the language outside of the app for the majority of your learning, like tandems, classes, books, or self-learning from YouTube.

Maybe even usea dedicated AI chatbot. Tell it:“I am a Level B2 German learner. Let’s debate the use of atomic energy. Correct my grammar only when I make a case error.”

Basically, do something, anything, outside of the app to really become road-ready.

title: 3 Ways to Deal With Your Painful Thoughts
content:
3 Ways to Deal With Your Painful Thoughts

We all have unresolved issues of some kind. Sometimes it feels like it’s all behind you and you’re all fresh and new. At other times, it feels like chewing gum stuck to the bottom of your personality and it just won’t go.

Letting go of a way of thinking and growing a new one is a repetitive and tedious process. You have to keep at it. What is tough learning right now is automatic tomorrow.

If you’re sticking to a bad memory or bad luck of the past, remind yourself this and you’ll feel better.

1. Stop thinking it’s special

Flash news! Everyone’s got problems. Imperfect upbringing, financial troubles, difficult relationships, addiction, not enough smarts to get through school or college, a parent that ran away, violence, abuse. You name it.

Whoever you are — the good, the bad, the ugly, the beautiful and the perfect, is a result of ALL of that. Nothing is “wrong” , because “you” are not wrong.

2. Don’t Romanticize it

I intentionally didn’t use the word “trauma" in the title, and instead chose “painful thoughts". Isn’t it relieving to trivialize it if only for a bit?

Romanticizing pain it is giving it words. Fancy, big and beautiful words. Instead, make it easy. Use simple words to describe things. Keep it clear and concise. Stay away from cliches.

Romanticizing is wallowing in the memory. It is dancing with the devil. Don’t wallow in how “poor you" had to go through all that. You have to draw a line somewhere and say “it’s in the past". You’ll then have time to focus on the person you want to be instead of on the one you don’t.

If you romanticize, you’ll fall into the dangerous habit of evading responsibility for uncomfortable events in your present, and simply blame it on your past. Don’t.

3. Don’t make it someone else’s problem

Have respect for the people who love you. Have respect for the love they show you. Don’t reject it and throw it back at them.

Your friends may be willing to help you, but don’t make them your permanent therapists. Everyone needs respite and support, your friends and family too.

Help around the house, listen to their stories and give them your shoulder. It’ll help you feel better too!

4. Practice gratefulness

Try to see beauty and kindness in the world. Try to see everything that did go your way. If you can be honestly, genuinely, truly, grateful, you’ve already earned a gold star today ☺

title: Short Story: Half People
content:
Short Story: Half People

Being 17 where women count half and girls don't at all.

When you realize for the first time in years that your words have value, they don’t rush from your mouth like water from a sprinkler. They drip slowly and unsure of themselves.

In the beginning, She doubted whether her inner voice is sane. If it was, whether it was a correct guide to her choices.What if she causes her own downfall? What will they say?

At 17, Maya did not know that she was a full person. That she was thinking, feeling, deciding individual.

She did as she was told, gave them what they wanted of her. A young woman was only as good as her obedience.Who doesn’t want to be a good kid?

She was a good kid. She worried about problems not her own, wanted things she would never want by herself.

She lay in bed at night terrified of disappointing her dad yet again. She was not going to be at the top of her class, not even in the top 5.What a waste of existence she was! —the thought that made up her dreams.

Reluctant to go back home, Maya and her two near-identical half people hung around the school for hours.

They were all bright young girls with excellent grades, but it was just not enough for the family or the school. The pressure made them believe that they didn’t deserve college. Or respect.

Everything was decided for them — their clothes, what they could and could not talk about, what they ate, and how they spent their time. Except for this hour after school. The family thought they were in class, the school didn’t care where they were because they were not top of the class. In this hour Maya, Mira, and Zain did not matter to anybody.

They drifted past the streets, laughing at their self-deprecating jokes. Any words of self-esteem were arrogance. They talked about tests to come, only half-joking about how they were going to disappoint yet again.

They talked about their dads, who in the society were “generous” men who sent their daughters to school as if they were boys, let them take the bus as if they were independent.

An ice cream cart passed by.

The girls stopped him.“ Three sticks of mango, please”.Maya reached into her wallet for 30 rupees. There was none.

Embarrassed, she looked at Mira and Zain who hurriedly fumbled around in their backpacks for possible change left there and forgotten. The ice cream man stood by, waiting to dispense his three sticks of mango.

Maya shrugged and said“sorry brother, we don’t have money.”

“Oh? Don’t you girls walk around here some evenings?”

“Yes, you know us?”

“I think I have seen you before, Do you want the ice cream anyway? You can pay me back next Monday, I am here around this time.”

Maya was apprehensive. Why is a stranger trusting her? Why is this man talking as if she were incharge, and that she would do the right thing?

“Really? You would do that? Yes, please!”

As she reached for the ice cream Maya felt like a grown human. The gesture moved her.

For the first time, she did not feel like a culprit, like she did not matter. She had goosebumps being respected by a stranger, being treated as a full person.

Her want mattered, be it a mere stick of ice cream. It was a strange feeling. A full-grown man trusted her to choose rightly, this was unheard of.

title: Rangamarthanda Review: An Overrated Movie Glorifying Destructive Commucation inside Telugu Families
content:
Rangamarthanda Review: An Overrated Movie Glorifying Destructive Commucation inside Telugu Families

The Telugu audience is clearly suffering from an overdose of colorful sets, over-the-top-costumes and extremely shallow plot lines that characterise many blockbusters. This convinced Krishnavamsi that anything without joy, color and positivity will automatically become an art-movie and a hit among the audience. Hence, this movie.

While dotted with well written dialogues and great performance by the cast, Rangamartanda banks on stretching family drama to unreasonable lengths for shock-effect.

It was irritating to watch Prakash Raj and Ramya Krishna twist every normal conversation into emotional blackmail. This drama is the only fuel to the movie.

Summary:

Prakash Raj is an average 60 or 70-something dad, and a retired stage actor. He has two very reasonable children who have reasonable requests like wanting to raise their kids the way they like and to keep their professional and family life separate.

Prakash Raj and Ramya Krishna on the other hand, live life by one motto —“If my kids don’t behave the way we like, we will leave town and tell everyone that our kids kicked us out.”

Their son & daughter-in-law decide to have small liberties — like waking up late because they work night shifts, and raise their daughter in a British-affiliated school. This offends the old couple and they feel “restricted” in the house they built themselves.

While Prakash Raj raises valid points about how pretentious school systems misrepresent and kill Telugu culture, that is hardly the point of the movie and the argument at hand.

As a solution, their daughter-in-law suggests living separately in one building in different apartments. This offends the old couple too. So they move out and claim that their son “kicked them out”.

Their daughter gathers the old couple who try to flee town without notice and begs them to live with her instead. But this kindness finds no place or mention in the movie.

At her place, the same struggle continues. Prakash Raj shows up in his daughter in underwear while his daughter has a work meeting. When his daughter says (very softly),please think about us too, this is important to me,dramatic music plays and the older couple exchange “poor us” looks.

At the next very normal “confrontation”, which in the normal world would be a conversation — Everyone screams at each other in Krishnavamsi style. The couple then leaves home in the middle of the night to sleep on the street until a bus can take them to their town.

By the end of the movie, all old people die from depression and heartbreak. The subject of euthanasia is broached without nuance, or even explanation.

They leave their children with the guilt of“never being enough", though they always tried to communicate with love and respect. If I were one of the children or child-in-law, that behavior would have been enough to drive me to kill myself.

The movie ends with a note that one has to respect one’s parents. While the characters in the movie never showed any disrespect to their parents, it was in fact, the old couple who blatantly refused conversation at every opportunity.

If there was a moral to this story, I couldn’t find it. If there was a deeper meaning to any of the scenes, I didn’t see it. The entire movie could have been saved if they had a WhatsApp family group and TALKED.

Toxic communication styles, demonize everyone wanting to make their own life choices — no matter how small, and a stage-actor trope here and there. That was Rangamarthanda for me.

If you are looking for a realistic, reasonable family drama to legitimately cry at and also enjoy the story-telling, watch Balagam.

title: Finding Meaning in Despair —How to live through Impossible Moments.
content:
Finding Meaning in Despair —How to live through Impossible Moments.

It’s 8 AM, and the next meeting is in a few minutes. It’s 11:00, time for coffee. This weekend, we have a hike planned. Maybe next year, he’ll propose. I think we’re ready for a baby.

When meticulously planned moments pass from one to another like clockwork, it is easy to forget about how stagnant life is. The next distraction is only a clock-hand-swing away, so we can just pretend like now doesn’t exist. Or drown it in a cup of coffee, and the computer screen.

But now and then, when all that comes crashing down, Life stands still. You begin noticing how the walls on sidewalk have blackened, How familiar the street in front of your home looks, You find comfort in the familiarity of how light falls from the window, just like it did when you were 5. But at a different time, at a different place, with different people. It feels unreal that you lived through the time between then and now, and the next step seems mysterious. You will most certainly believe that it will never come. In that fix, there is nowhere to go but to stay where you are, and revel or suffer the moment.

In that calm and stillness of such times, you can choose comfort or long to leave it for the next moment. If you choose comfort, that is when you will see, that life doesn’t end at that bend.

That bend in time that comes in confusing times, like if you lost your job, lose a loved one, have your belief broken, or find yourself alone at the soul. All the things that hold meaning to you will lose their sense and time will suddenly acquire a new meaning. Days, weeks and months pass without rush and in that meaninglessness, time becomes beautiful again. When there is no where else to go and nothing meaningful to fill your time, you form a connection with yourself, if you don't desperately struggle to escape the moment. Your body and mind lead you to do only the most meaningful things and form only the most meaningful connections.

The race will be over, and you can relax again. There is a calm when you decide to no longer run. Embrace it, sit down, and listen to the silence.

title: Best Way To Begin Using Claude Code in Your Projects
content:
Best Way To Begin Using Claude Code in Your Projects

Learn here from Anthropic Academy. Claude Code is Copilot on Steroids.

It feels like AI coding assistants have no chill. A new development everyday and we are all just racing to catch up.

I thought Copilot was awesome, until Anthropic’s Claude code blew my mind. While Copilot felt like an intelligent autocomplete, Claude Code felt like a co-engineer. Like a fully capable human performed my tasks. Here is how I got started.

What is Claude Code?

Claude Code is an agentic coding assistant that lives on your terminal. I was surprised to find that Claude Code is aCLI tool,making it easier to grasp the ropes, given the familiarity we already have with terminals.

Unlike with Copilot, where context becomes everything(current file, current line etc.) Claude code takes very high-level commands — as you would give to an intern —for example, “Add a button on the right, choose the best color scheme”… and it doesn’t just show you the code; it:

Searches your codebase to find where the theme logic lives.

Proposes a plan.

Edits the necessary files (across multiple directories).

Runs your build commands to check for errors

Fixes its own mistakes if the build fails.

What the Course Covers

The Anthropic Academy course is a refreshing start for moving from “Chatting with AI” to “Engineering with Agents.” You no longer copy paste code or work with individual lines to achieve a larger goal. You begin with the larger goal.

In the course, you:

Understand how Claude interacts with your file system and shell.

2. How Claude uses bash, grep, and file-editing tools to navigate massive repos.

3.How to connect Claude to external data usingModel Context Protocol (MCP).You can connect to databases, Slack, or even the web — to bridge the gap between your code and the outside world.

4. How to use Hooks to prevent claude from reading certain files or taking some actions in case you want that check in place.

5. How to use the Claude SDK in python or typescript.

My Experience: Building an App and Integrating to Github

The course example walks you through auditing an existing open-source app. But you can build your own with Claude and perform the same steps.

Claude doesn’t just write the script; it sets up the folder structure, performs audits based on rules that you set, makes changes while checking with you if you want to proceed, allows for a plan mode where it tells you exactly what it’s going to do, and if that’s ok with you.

You can also integrate your GitHub to Claude and @ it, like you can ask claude“hey @claude, can you check the button on top, it is not working?”

The “Steroids” Moment: Claude Wrote Its Own Success Story on My Medium.com

What blew me away was its ability to useMCP (Model Context Protocol) Servers.

After finishing the app, I wanted to document the process on my blog. I connected Claude to an MCP server called Playwright( which allows Claude to interact with the the browser). I gave it a simple command:

“Claude, look at the React Generator we just built. Log into my Medium account via the MCP server and write a technical article explaining how we built it, including the terminal commands we used.”

Claude proceeded to:

Read the local files it had just created to gather “facts.”

Draft the post.

Use the MCP tool to format and publish the draft directly to Medium.

Add screenshots to the article it just wrote

Add title and subtitle formatting.

I didn’t open a browser once.

How to Get Started

If you want to move beyond simple code suggestions and start using AI as a true collaborator, here is the path:

Take the Course:Go toAnthropic Academy. It’s the best way to begin.

Install the CLI:Runnpm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code.

Use/init:Run this in your project root to create aCLAUDE.mdfile.

If you’re still copy-pasting code from a chat window into your editor, you’re living in the past. Welcome to the terminal. Give Claude code a chance.

title: For non-humans, Prison is the Only Safe Space
content:
For non-humans, Prison is the Only Safe Space

We need zoos and animal sanctuaries.

Yesterday after knowing him for 10 years, we found out that Sellie is actually a man. Somewhere down the line, a zookeeper mistook him for aherand the identity stuck. With Amazonian parrots, you can only tell with a DNA sample.

Sellie was blind in one eye, so he never learned to fly. He walked on the carefully arranged branches in his cage, using his beak as a walking stick. I often wonder — if he could fly, would I open the cage and forgetby accident?

When I look at the capuchins behind the glass as I clean it, I dread the idea that It could have been me inside.They look and act so much like us.

At our local zoo, marmoset monkeys, land tortoises, and Amazonian parrots live together in large enclosures. Nose bears have indoor and outdoor homes to suit the weather and their mood. Four capuchins live out their social life in a roomy enclosure which elsewhere would have housed eight monkeys.

The more intelligent ones like monkeys get hand-designed contraptions to play with and kill boredom. Others get care, company, and food, and space. I lovingly arrange the branches in Sellie’s 15 square meter cage so that he has new ways to walk on every week.

Familiar people often visit the animals with genuine love. Sometimes, the animals give it back. Zoo animals are nothing like their wild friends. They have evolved a lifestyle of their own, not unlike people like myself who move far away from home.

Nothing matters more to me in life than freedom. I grew up in a society where women are only as free as their menallowthem to be. My childhood taught me to cherish and value freedom. Obviously, I have mixed emotions about volunteering at the zoo — the very definition of confinement.

But what would life look like for Sellie, were she not with us?

Nature documentaries are lying to us. Yes, even those made by Sir Attenborough. There is no unexplored wilderness out there with nothing but cameras lurking in a corner. There is no pristine earth untouched by man where a lone polar bear ventures out into white infinity.Sellie would probably be dead.

If I could, I’d appeal them to stop lying to us by pointing carefully pointing and zooming. Don’t hide that group of tourists in straw hats in the background.

When I was young, about 20 sparrows fluttered into our front yard every morning demanding their usual breakfast. Now, I sprinkle some seed and wait. Nobody shows up.

Hundreds of thousands of Rhinos that roamed the Kenyan plains are now permanently gone. The last male Northern White RhinoSudanwas protected by three armed guards when he died.Three. armed.guards.As you read this, scientists are sticking rhino eggs into a tube in the hope tobring back the dead species.There is a lone orangutan perched on the one remaining tree top somewhere in Bornea, nowhere else to go, friends and family long gone. We discovered the vaquita as an animal only in the 1950s. There are now about 10 left.

As of today, nearly 38000 species are faced with permanent annihilation. Research inches closer to proving that animals are sentient beings, that their cognizance doesn’t differ from that of humans by nature. They feel and remember every bit of pain we inflict on them.

We have already proven ourselves incapable of coexistence with creatures that cannot speak our language. The advancements in science took us to Jupiter and beyond. But somehow, philosophy, religion, morality have failed to teach us the empathy necessary to coexist on this planet.

If I was a Northern White Rhino, I would not wish to be born into the wild. Actually, I hope I never see the wild Kenyan plains.

If I were a Chimpanzee, I’d rather be in a loving sanctuary with large outdoors, where I can see open skies, where my children will be provided for.

If I was to be one of the 800 surviving mountain gorillas, I’d give anything to not live among violent gunshots in Congo, watching my children carried away in cages for meat as I bleed to death.

In this real-world, when I walk into the zoo on Saturday mornings, I feel a ray of hope. Zoos in Germany are prohibited from capturing in the wild. In the case of many dying species, they breed and release these species back into the wild.

The animals there are under the watchful and loving eyes of people who care and do all they can to give the animals the best life they can afford.

There are sanctuaries like Chimp Haven in Louisiana, where primates retire after their career as lab-research workers. They live their life out under the sun, under supervision. More such islands of freedom are being built, where primates can socialize, swing around and live their best possible natural life.

In an imaginary pristine world, we can all be free. You, me, rhinos, sparrows, orangutans, and all others who think and feel. But the earth of today is a far cry from that paradise.

Zoos and sanctuaries may not be the ideal ways of life for any critter presumed to have free will. As long as we don’t have alternatives, let us give them part of “our” space.

What is a creature to do with freedom when its life crumbles around it?

Instead of pretending like there is an alternative, let us strive at making our zoos freer, our enclosures larger, greener, and more natural. Let us work in giving animal sanctuaries larger funding and stricter rules.

Let us give other creatures safe spaces to live and breathe.

title: Hi Anne.
content:
Hi Anne.

Yes, panrets want the best for us, that is what we tell ourselves to keep doing what we do.

In Germany the asian community is not as large as in the USA (I visited the USA once, it was very well suited for an Indian person to emigrate without much preparation.).

Life is much more restricted and people have only a small choice of social contacts in their own culture.

But Germany's immigration laws are friendly towards educated aliens. That part does make life easier as compared to the USA where I often hear about "visa issues".

haha yes, nosy relatives is something I can actually live without while being far away. But there is the occasional festival vibe and "someone to call on" that I dearly miss.

title: 6 Netflix Shows To Make You Feel Truly Alive
content:
6 Netflix Shows To Make You Feel Truly Alive

One-episode true stories in adventure and nature, rated.

Inthis list, I tried to pick shows and movies which felt new and raw. I listed them if they had an element of unexpectedness from the regular adventure and nature series, or If they stayed in memory for far longer than others.

In addition, David Attenborough’s Our planet, Life on our planet, Netflix’s own Night on Earth, the Netflix Original Unnatural Selection are all brilliant appendages to the list.

Here are my top picks. I tried not to spoil anything.

Everest — Movie, Adventure, Based on a true story

4 / 5 Stars

Many have tried the summit the Everest. Many have died.

1996 saw one of the deadliest such disasters in a guided expedition lead byAdventure Consultants,who offered toguide journalist Jon Krakauer, the journalist who gave usInto the Wild.

4 out of the team of 8 died, including the guide Rob Hall.Krakauer was among the survivors.Everesttells their story in his voice.

The movie neither states Krakauer’s bookInto Thin Airas the source nor him as the narrator. I suspect this is because of the backlash that ensued from the families of the deceased and those held responsible and multiple rebuttals that have since been made public. Nevertheless, it is hard to miss the red line that follows the events and dialogue as described in the book.

Krakauer writes of Mountains with awe and earnestness. Most of his writing draws life from his own experiences in the wilderness and his work as a journalist for the Outside magazine. He has been a vocal critic of the commercialization of summiting Everest, the story he intended to cover on this deadly expedition.

The movie is a brilliant watch.

Wild — Movie, Adventure, Based on a true story

4 / 5 Stars

Hiking is a deceptive sport. It looks like “something anyone could do”, but it could kill you.

The movie Wild is based on Cheryl Strayed’s 2012 memoirWild: From Lost to Found on the Pacific Crest Trail.

When Cheryl left for an 1800 Kilometer hike on the PCT, she carried heavy baggage. A painful childhood, the memory of having to put a loved one to sleep, addiction, anonymous sex, a destroyed marriage, and loss of her mother who was, until then, the only reason that had kept her going.

She had lost her purpose. She decided to hike the PCT alone.

The world feels different in the skin of a woman. When alone, she doesn't worry about her precious wallet or the money in it. Most of the time, she is happy to get where she wants alive.

On the trail with Cheryl, a part of you might redeem itself too. There is much in nature that you will be taught without being told.

Wildis a good watch.

Virunga, Investigative Journalism

4.5 /5 Stars

“130rangershave died protecting Virunga. I may die… though I don’t know when”.

Something you expect to hear at a war, but at a Gorilla Protection Zone?

This is the story of Virunga National Park of Congo — A treasure trove of natural resources, oil, diamonds, and unfortunately the world’s last 700 surviving Mountain Gorillas.

Virunga is investigative Journalism in the backdrop of civil unrest, genocide, corruption, and a war fueled by a European oil corporation. Tangled in the vile human intervention are the mountain gorillas with no one but the armed rangers on their side.

The documentary Virunga is something you will clench your teeth and sit through, hoping that things have changed since its 2014 making. But a quick search will show you a list of Park rangers who have since died protecting their land and resources.

Virunga is not Nature or a war documentary. It is a cry for help.

The Dawn Wall — Adventure Documentary

4.5 / 5 Stars

What if you killed a person to save your fellow people, but that is not even the most hair-raising thing you have done in your life?

The Dawn Wall is the story of Tommy Cladwell who free-climbed El Captain in Yosemite. Tens of times. And when he fell, he went back up again every single time until he made it 8 years later.

Tommy is not made of the same material as everyone else you know or heard of. It is like he has been programmed to do just one thing. He doesn't as much as see or think about anything else.

El Captain at its hardest looks like it has been poured at once out of a polished glass into its Behemoth form. Smooth and nearly without a crevice. If you are familiar with the physical drudgery that is climbing this story will impress you. If you have no relation to climbing at all, watching a man climb this rocky sheath will earn your respect.

Life in Colour — Episode 1, Nature Documentary

5 / 5 Stars

A classic David Attenborough, Life in Colour was made with cameras especially invented for the show so that you can see colors like a bee or a gazelle.

A tree is only as green as your eyes allow and a canary only as yellow. But why have these beings evolved these colors? What does nature see that we do not? Frogs, deer, mantis, birds of paradise, lizards, crabs — The world is made for each one of them and for each one differently.

Allowing you to marvel at things from a perspective that has never been seen before, Life in Colour shows you the world of animals with stereo vision, polarized vision, ultraviolet vision, and not the least, color blindness.

Attenborough attests that the climate change question he addresses in every one of his shows — Including our planet and life on our planet, has been left out in this show on purpose. This show is meant for you to celebrate and love nature for its brilliance.

For every minute you spend away from the natural world, Life in Color does everything to help you reconnect and bond with it.

My Octopus Teacher — Nature Documentary, a true story

5 / 5 Stars

My Octopus teacher is a personal story.

It is getting to know a person called octopus throughout her lifetime. It is watching an honest bond between individuals take leaf and grow until one of them withered.

Craig Foster, a cameraman and diver, seeks to rediscover himself and his life’s meaning. He takes a break from work for a year and goes diving every day off the coast of South Africa when he meets this intriguing being.

Craig’s relationship to the octopus is beyond any nature documentary. In this story, the Octopus trusts him back. It is as unusual as it is true.

Before we speculate about aliens, before we take a step outside of the planet, it is incredibly enriching to truly understand everything we already have.

My Octopus Teacher is a testimony to science that cannot be measured. It is undoubtedly the best nature story ever told and rightly won the Academy Award this year for the Best Documentary Feature.
