title: How Being Robbed of 3 € Taught me Tolerance
content:
How Being Robbed of 3 € Taught me Tolerance

On tolerance in animal and human societies.

So this happened.

I had leased a brand new car. The first-ever in my life, with my name on the papers. It obviously cost me a fortune, but what is a woman who has not lived when she could? I lived alone and was thrilled about exploring the world in my new Audi Q2.

I took a lesson fromInto the wildand decided that “happiness is true only when it is shared”. So I embarked on my travels with people I just met and drove hundreds of kilometers.

On one of these travels, I noticed that one of myfriends slipped some money in my car into his pocket.Ouch!

Why tolerance?

I had lost friends in the past.

At times I was dejected because people were all about taking and never about giving. At times that they lie way too easily. Sometimes that they were unkind or unreasonable or disrespectful or jealous. Sometimes I simply didn’t like their views.

I was tired.

Isn’t this exactly what was wrong with the world? Why do I expect everyone to be perfect? Why is acceptance SO rare, that an all-accepting human is equivalent to God himself?

I decided that it is going to be the end of this. I was decidedly going to be tolerant. Even to the one who stole from me.

What tolerance did to me

In the beginning, it was liberating. Probably because of this “virtuous moral high-ground”. I don’t think feeling good about good deeds is wrong, though.

I tried to think of people as well-intentioned, even when we were not getting along. People were visibly taken aback. They were nicer to me since then. I made many friends. People were opening up to me. It felt good.

It made me see more beauty in the world. I was more relaxed. I seemed to have fewer issues and felt more at home, wherever I was.

But then at times, it got difficult. Passive antagonism was easy to deal with. But active attacks were difficult to handle.

Why would someone exclusively give negative feedback, as if they take joy in it? Why would someone call me names, although all I did was help them? Most of all, why would someone steal from me while I was driving them places?!

I couldn’t keep it up.

The tryst with books and zoo parks

I didn’t want to strike off all the questions as “overthinking”. No.

This is going to be life. I watched my parents and grandparents have the very same problems. I watched too many movies, have listened to too much gossip. It is all the same.

Why do people do what they do? What does it take for people to respect each other?

If there is a definitive conclusion, it would be like having a cheat code. You would never expect too much. You wouldn’t be hurtful or hurt. You can focus on better things, create wonders and art. You can cooperate with awesome people. Seems like a dream.

All I needed was to know where I could get my acceptance to them all, once and for all. For good.

So I wanted to know more about what is“natural human behavior”,What is programmed into us? What can a human being not help but be?At the same time, I started working at the local zoo with a bunch of monkeys.

That was my pandora’s box.

Science is sure but dicey

Summing up the ways of the world through biology is a dicey proposition. Back in WWII times, they used the same kind of “scientific” arguments to claim that there were biologically superior and inferior races! This area of research called eugenics was even taught in schools and studied as a subject.

So one must tread carefully with respect to science. It is not absolute, it changes with times and is oftentimes severely inadequate to tell the whole truth.

That being said, people are, in the end, animals. They have to eat, drink, make love. We know these well because these are the surface symptoms of being an animal.

But there is more to being human. There are needs which we are not willing to notice because we are too ashamed to admit them. There are needs we are too proud to have. Like will to power, like the need for acceptance in a society, like the need to protect our kin. The list goes on.

Legend has it that when Nixon was forced to resign, he was devastated. He collapsed into a pile on the floor, sobbing. Henry Kissinger had to hug him and recite a list of his achievements until he calmed down.

Pulling power from a leader is like pulling a safety blanket from an infant. Or like an alpha chimpanzee who just lost its place to a younger chimp. The tantrums are normal as wanting food when you're hungry. We are all pulled by identical strings.

About order in the animal kind

When I offer food to a monkey (they have their order) at the zoo, he hesitates and looks at the boss. The dominant female (Macaques are matriarchs) walks over and takes the fruit. Then she threatens me, though I am holding her food, presumably for breaking their order.

Order is superior to food.

The other monkey doesn't fight. He doesn’t even attempt to take the food!

In another story, dog psychologist Ceaser Milan trains the most difficult problem dogs to behave. His diagnosis — The dog doesn’t see a leader around, so it tries to be one.

If the owner is all about smooches and affection? the dog has to secure the curb by himself. Ceaser trains beastly dogs with one simple tactic — show them who is the boss. He does not do this with aggression but by hold, grip, and tone. He is not a rude, uncaring trainer. He is simply assertive in the language the dog understands.

We are not that different from a bunch of dogs, wolves, or chimps in this regard. Weneedpeople to be assertive.

My experiences in the human society

People are pack animals. We don’t live alone because we can’t. A social animal needs an order. People may love kind strangers, but our safety blankets are those who look like leaders.

Just like a chimp or a dog, we need to hear assertive voices to know that a human can be in charge, at least of himself. Don’t let tolerance be mistaken for weakness. It should be your strength.

You are better off asserting yourself first as an individual with self and esteem, and only then moving on to being unquestioningly tolerant. Your exercise is not for people to like you, but for you to be respected enough so that you can give respect back.

Tolerance does not always mean letting others walk all over you, the same way, being a leader does not mean bossing over others and being uncouth.

The longest-ruling leaders in the chimp society rule for nearly a decade and a half or more. Guess who are the chimp leaders who enjoy the support of the clan? The tolerant leaders. Guess who gets overthrown at the bat of an eye? The ones who make macho displays to intimidate and launder fruit at disputes.Sounds familiar?

Before you go on the quest for tolerance, get some self-esteem. Don’t leave the turf of giving respect, but figure a way to cultivate assertive tolerance.

Sometimes, that fails too. But there would be your chance to flex your acceptance.

I amSruthi Korlakunta, I write here onMedium.

In my day job, I am a Data Analyst for Audi. During and after work, I vigorously explore the world and my mind for the truth. I’d be happy to connect!

title: The Trouble with Toastmasters
content:
The Trouble with Toastmasters

Should you join the club?

Limited focus on public speaking: While Toastmasters is well-regarded for its focus on public speaking, some people feel that the organization doesn’t offer enough opportunities to develop other communication skills, such as writing, negotiation, or conflict resolution.

Repetitive structure: Toastmasters meetings typically follow a similar format, which some members may find boring or unengaging over time.

The high degree of structure: Toastmasters emphasizes strict adherence to its rules and procedures, which can make it feel rigid or overly formal to some people.

Focus on the competition: Toastmasters’ speech contests can be a highlight for some members, but others may feel that the organization places too much emphasis on competition rather than personal growth.

Membership fees: While Toastmasters is a relatively affordable organization, some people may be deterred by the cost of joining and attending meetings.

Over-rehearsal: Some Toastmasters speakers may spend a lot of time practicing their speeches, which can result in a robotic or stilted delivery that lacks spontaneity or authenticity.

Following a formula: Toastmasters emphasizes a specific formula for speech delivery that involves opening with a joke or attention-grabber, incorporating a story or anecdote, and concluding with a call-to-action or memorable message. While this approach can be effective, it can also result in speeches that sound formulaic or cookie-cutter.

Nervousness: Toastmasters is often used as a platform for people to overcome their fear of public speaking. However, some speakers may still feel nervous or anxious during their presentations, which can manifest as a stiff or unnatural delivery.

Overemphasis on technique: Toastmasters places a lot of emphasis on the technical aspects of public speaking, such as body language, vocal variety, and pacing. While these skills are important, some speakers may focus too much on technique at the expense of their natural speaking style.

It’s important to note that not all Toastmasters speakers may sound “plastic,” and that this perception may be subjective. Ultimately, the goal of Toastmasters is to help people improve their communication skills, and the organization provides a supportive environment for people to practice and develop their speaking abilities.

title: Dasara Review: A Confused Plot Working Desperately to Make Nani a Mainstream Star
content:
Dasara Review: A Confused Plot Working Desperately to Make Nani a Mainstream Star

Is it about a bar? politics? cricket match? love? friendship? Revenge?

Dasara came into theatres this year on the 30th of March. It performed reasonably well at the box office, now already set for streaming on Netflix. I had the chance to watch this movie after a month of its release on the big screen. Was it worth it?

Dasara opened with captivating camera work that fully had my attention. The village scene, the “really local” feeling through music, accents, costumes, styling — all very convincing.

Great actors wasted on a terrible plot.

What stands out most in the movie is the cast. They rest more on their acting credentials than stardom.

Nani, Keerti Suresh, Samudrakani, Shine, and Sai Kumar, to name a few — are well-chosen for their roles and deliver great performances.

Unfortunately, except for Nani, the rest have no roles and character development whatsoever. Great entrance scenes for each of these actors fizzle out as soon as they appear. Their plot lines are carefully written until the interval, only to disappear without explanation.

The story of the first half of the movie is about the village and its dependence on the local bar for entertainment and politics. Nani and his friend are coal thieves —Here are some scenes copied from Bhaag Milkha Bhaag.Some local political drama.Here a little Rangasthalam. Add some social injustice scenes straight from Jai Bhim.Suddenly a cricket match comes in from nowhere.The movie shortly turns into Lagaan.Then the whole plot is dropped, and pivots to a love-revenge story.Then some Pushpa scenes, then some Kantara climax. Tadaaa!

Dasara did justice to costumes, song lyrics, and accents. The Music was terrible.

Telugu movies in recent times give actors fake-poor and fake-ancient costumes and accents. Actors wear clothes that clearly look new and “too carefully” put together, albeit artificially tattered and impoverished.

The same is done with their local, especially FakeTelanganaaccents. Dasara, on that front, can be credited for being honest in this department, except for the accent of Keerti Suresh, who speaks an outlandish Telangana.

Real Telangana songs in the movie had memorable and authentic lyrics. Unfortunately, the music did not play along. Tunes and instruments are haphazardly put together and do not add up.

The most popular song, “Chamkeela” turns into an entirely different, unrelated song at point 02:00 min, and becomes annoying.

The rest of the songs in the movie are the same — bad music with good lyrics.

Summary

The movie’s main point is to have “elevation” scenes to make a mainstream Telugu hero out of Nani. He is made a good-looking, sacrifice-all savior of the village and the heroine. He beats up piles of goondas in boring action sequences borrowed from other movies.

Bad direction aside, Nani does justice to his role. Keerti Suresh, in her role as a typical damsel in distress, delivers a stellar performance despite the bad writing. Samudrakani and Sai Kumar wasted their time signing up for this movie. Chinna Nambi, the main villain played by Shine, failed to Shine. It wasn’t his fault, nobody wrote him a character. The rest of the actors are entirely pointless.

In all, this is not a movie but a great collection of scenes. The scenes begin with enthusiasm and fizzle out in the next minute. There is no continuity or meaning in the storytelling.

Watch it like it is a sequence of ads before the movie — Roopa underwear followed by Everest Masala, followed by Chandana Brothers, followed by a trailer, and then a badly made ad for perfume. There you have Dasara.

title: 5 “Good” Habits That Are Actively Killing Your Personality
content:
5 “Good” Habits That Are Actively Killing Your Personality

Stop doing them, you secretly know you must.

1. You are burying your face in a book (or in Netflix, it is the same)

You might pride yourself on being an avid reader. Here is the truth — if you are not creating, you are consuming. And mere consumption is stupid.

Unless you balance your reads (and watches) by creating from them, you are just another consumer. There is glory in creating value, no matter how small and there is helplessness and stupidity in consumption.

If reading is your excuse for not creating, watch out, your personality will soon take a hit.

2. You put in a lot of overtime at work

If you brag about how many days a week you stay late at work, you are doing something wrong. Each hour taken from your personal time is an hour lost from becoming a better version of yourself.

You might never find out your true strength if your entire personality is defined by what you do for your employer.

Invest that time in developing a skill, or learning something new. Invest time in creating. Invest time in your family. It will add to your worth as an employee who also knows his self-worth.

3. You are very well informed on current affairs.

Ah, that feeling of “knowing it all” — so powerful, so satisfying!

It feels good to stay on top of the latest trends — Instagram, the war in that part of the world, the latest gossip in town, the newest movie reviews, and what else not.

If the conversation on current affairs and world events comes super-easy to you, you might be spending too long looking at your screen. It is good to stay current but it is better to filter the too much information internet throws at you.

Protect your brain from too much activity and find time to be calm. Let your Fear Of Missing Out not get to you.

4. Everyone in town is your friend.

This is the best feeling in the world — to always have someone to hang out with. It must mean you are social, amicable, and basically an awesome person to chill with. WRONG!

Having an enormous social circle costs you time. If you don't do it right, you have whiled away your days and have nothing to show for it. Use your time to build quality connections instead of trying to stay popular.

Balance your public time with adequate me-time. The constant din of partying might keep you from connecting to yourself.

5. You are an avid traveler

Travel makes you a better person — undeniable. Constant vacation might not. While it is alright to get away once in a while, it must not be a compulsion for the gram.

You should be ok with staying home for the next long weekend because you have taxes, your kitchen needs cleaning, or just want to stay home with your partner for a bit.

If you are constantly trying to escape the routine and get away from responsibilities, traveling is not all that glamorous. You are just a rich person who doesn't know how to manage time and money. Moderation is the key. Travel wisely.

title: The Bliss of Being Lonely
content:
The Bliss of Being Lonely

There is something about being alone that is pleasing beyond measure. Something I wouldn’t exchange for the security of love that just might someday come in handy.

There is peace to how you can quietly dream your shameful thoughts and how your clumsy self can throw itself into unsightly contortions. How you can laugh, cry or feel things that have not yet been named.

Closed doors are underrated, underappreciated, underused, and misunderstood.

There is blissful loneliness behind every closed door, unscathed by futile politeness and empty smiles.

There, the gaze is free to wander off without really looking or being followed.

Behind the door or is a bubble of freedom that everyone wishes extended far beyond the door, but themselves bear tools to break for everyone else.

If there is one possession I’d keep for myself, one treasure I can hold onto, I’d keep a bit of my loneliness, amidst the dazzling, loudly perfect world.

title: The Ability to Admit Not Knowing is A Sign of Intelligence
content:
The Ability to Admit Not Knowing is A Sign of Intelligence

I spent about two years of my life as a “traveling learner” as a part of my job. My job was to go places, talk to people, get to know the work, work culture, and foster relations among people inside of the company.

During my journey through the world as a qualified Engineer, I have learned to spot one common denominator among those who are reliable and trustworthy.

It is spectacularly simple and yet such a hard thing to do at work — to utter the sentence, “I don’t know.”

The problem with dodging a question you don’t know the answer to

I once met this lady who was an experienced project manager, newly hired, and new to the nature of the current project. An MBA graduate, she was evidently unaware of the computer-science details of the project we were both assigned to (which was, by the way, completely alright).

Not knowing the exact nature of her work or her role, I asked her for a technical detail that I wasn't sure about. Clearly, she had no idea what I was talking about because she was not the right person to answer this question.

Instead of pointing me to the right person, andtelling me that she did not know, she rambled on about the nature of technical difficulties. By then I had already realized that I approached the wrong expert.

To my utter dismay, she ended the conversation by filling the conversation with barely relevant jargon completely irrelevant to the context.

Not only was her answer not suitable to my question, but I was also utterly disappointed at how one can expect to ramble their way through real problems.

My respect would have remained at least unchanged for this probably very good project manager if she admitted to the truth and did not try to trick me into thinking she was a genius. Clearly, she failed.

Faces don’t lie

I have seen both beginners and people in place of power, who when asked for a detail or a plan, make up irrelevant “empty talk” in a place where they could have simply said “I don’t know”.

Humans are programmed to recognize emotions that pass over a face in a moment as small as one-fifth of a millisecond.

If we believe that we are fooling people with false pretenses and lies because we cannot own up to our lack of knowledge, we are kidding ourselves.

The power of saying “I don’t know”

Admitting that one doesn't know when one doesn't know is actually a sign of intelligence, not ignorance, for various reasons.

Saying “I don't know” is the starting point of admitting the current situation. Only when you come to terms with whatis,can you proceed to correct it or learn from it.

Moreover, it is mathematically accurate and clears confusion.

There are things you know, those you can find out upon inquiry, those you can calculate and immediately respond to, and those you simply don't know!

Stammering through a sentence around not knowing comes off as weak and fraudulent.

If you utter a lie confidently, you are still not a leader, but a liar. And Compromising ethics by lying is a big flashing sign that you are not meant to be a leader.

It’s not what you say, it is how you say it.

Just because you say “I don’t know” doesn't mean that you are a nincompoop. Instead, it means that you are capable of recognizing what you know and do not.

It means you are strong and reliable enough to own up to a situation. It means that you care enough about your job to want to know the truth, the whole truth and nothing but the truth. Most of all, it meansyou are willing to grow.

Regardless of what you say, it is mostly how you say it that makes you a leader or a liar.

Don’t look down at the floor and whisper it, because it shows that you are ashamed of your disposition. Instead, look into the asker’s eye.

Ask questions, find out more. If you need more information or if you think it is unknowable, state these things clearly. Ask questions about what you want to know to be able to answer.

What to do instead:

First and foremost, don’t treat the question like it is a threat, treat it as an opportunity to grow.

If you don’t know something, it is not a cause for shame. It occurs to everyone healthy individual every couple of minutes. This does not change just because it is a professional environment.

Find out what is the purpose of knowing the information and see if it is your domain and how you can help.

If you are curious dig deeper, ask deeper questions, and for god’s sake do some research. But first, say “I don’t know, it could be interesting to find out.”

If it is directly relevant to your job, trust that it can be learned. Figure out what sources you can approach. It is okay if at the end of your research it comes out as a non-trivial question.

Don’t jump into the defense mode. Work together with the question. Whether it is your boss or co-worker, you are on the same team. Answering questions is a part of your job.

If you are the wrong person to address the question, say so. It is not expected of you to know everything under the sun. Nobody on the planet pays anyone enough to do this.

Even if you are the lead on the topic, doesn't mean you know the world and everything in it. Acknowledge your own human self and respect it.

Trust the asker. He is a man with a doubt. Not an attacker.

The “make up something” mode is more detrimental to your respect and vocation than saying “I don't know.”

Whether you believe it or not, people have an inherent trust and respect for you, unless you explicitly do something to destroy it.

The listener in most cases empathizes and would be willing to work with you to figure it out if it is your job to find out.

Practice saying “I don’t know.” Not because you are complacent, because you are human. If it is something you are supposed to know, make the effort, and find out. If not, state the same clearly.

Truth, and doing the right thing have value in a society that is immeasurable. It may not show right away, but it brings you forward in ways you cannot see. Honestly, there is nothing like honesty.

If you are interested in articles on technology, or social competence or literature, connect with me. Here is myMediumprofile, and here is myLinkedin.

title: How To Get Rich: Netflix Show Review
content:
How To Get Rich: Netflix Show Review

**Gushes in green**

Like all millennials, I'm endlessly fascinated by the idea of not dying the way I was born — Not Rich. And like all millennials, I think about saving up, investing, owning a home, getting clean spending and donating habits, finding business ideas, and building side hustles.

I’ve reached out to people I respect for advice on the matter: old people, parents, friends, and family. The verdict is clear — no one really knows hoot. Most people freewheel through life without making a plan— it works for some but doesn’t for others. Those who do know how to make money are too busy making money, so they can’t really talk to you.

Naturally, when I saw the showHow to Get Richon Netflix, I clicked on it.

Did I like it? The title is clickbait, that much is clear. Because an honest title would be“How to manage your money and not be broke”.The show offers a refreshing take on personal finance and wealth-building. Though it really doesn’t touch on the subject of getting rich, it talks about living “a rich life” by the definition of the speaker — as long as they are not talking about bungalows in the south of France or owning a bunch of Lamborghinis by the end of the year.

After watching the first two episodes, I bought a book by Rami Sethi (the host), because the man knows what he’s talking. The show was slightly more than just telling people to “do better” (I’m looking at you, Marie Kondo). (And yes, if he would do an audit of my life, he’d find me recklessly clicking “buy” on the Kindle store.)

The Show

The show’s premise revolves around real people facing real financial challenges. Each episode introduces a new set of participants, each with their unique financial struggles, whether it’s crushing debt, lack of savings, or poor spending habits.

Ramit Sethi first looks into people’s checking, savings account statements, credit card bills, student debts, and whatnot, and makes an estimate about what he is dealing with. He then moonwalks into their lives and tries to not cringe as they casually share their money habits. Since most of these people are also in relationships with others who also manage money badly, the show kind of segues into a relationship counseling session very often.

The host touches on the subjects of whether couples should have joint accounts, home ownership, lowering credit card debt, Multilevel Marketing Schemes, trading on daytrade platforms, getting rid of ridiculous spending habits, and having open conversations in a relationship.

He specifically asks people what they want from their lives and helps them cut out the junk to get there. It was really rewarding to watch Ramit change their perspectives over the 8 episodes.

The participants

The show features people from the strangest and also the most normal situations in life. Millionaires, the poor, the rich, and the middle class are all a part of it.

It begins with this couple Matt and Amani — a super rich 27-year-old and her stay-at-home husband who do not even last an episode before deciding to quit the show. She makes over $200k and him nothing. Their net balance is currently about -$2000 a year despite the massive income.

Tensions are high when the couple discusses finances, and the audience is on the edge of the seat knowing that this marriage probably won’t last. But they do eventually make up and find a solution to their unique living situation. Thanks Ramit!

Another interesting mother-daughter pair comes from an episode on generational wealth — blown down from 500 million dollars of wealth to $5 left in the savings account. This might be the most ridiculous spending culture we have seen yet. The woman drives a Ferrari, lives in a 2 million dollar house, and doesn’t know she’s on the verge of being broke. What did she blow the $500mil on? Clothes, purses, and cars — until the daughter realized they could not afford her tuition.

Those are just two of the interesting stories that made the cut — probably for the pomp and drama they bring to the screen, but the show has a lot more to offer.

Ramit Sethi

What setsHow to Get Richapart is Ramit Sethi’s ability to connect with participants on a personal level. His empathy and understanding create a safe space for participants to open up about their financial worries to a million people watching Netflix.

I have yet to read the man’s book, but Ramit is a dashing, sympathetic, and really charming host to the show. In recent times, good hosting has not been that obvious. For example, I really could not relate to the hosts ofIs It Cake?,Buy My House, or evenTidying up with Marie Kondo. The minimalists were alright, but still not as relatable to the common man without sounding like tech-bros who just discovered recycling.

Ramit really stands out in that league. He is funny, good-looking, and stays positive, despite the atrocious stories he sometimes has to hear. He doesn’t look like he is judging a lot, except for those few times when the cringe on his face — though just for a microsecond, is too obvious. He is respectful, and friendly and doesn’t overstep his boundaries with his clients.

The Verdict

The show is a green flag.

You definitely won’t get rich just by watching the show but you might learn a tidbit or two about saving up and planning your finances. Or not, but it might be time well invested if you have never thought about these things, or just need some validation.

title: Don’t Waste Your Sweat: The Essential Guide to Weighted Vests Before You Buy One
content:
Don’t Waste Your Sweat: The Essential Guide to Weighted Vests Before You Buy One

The science, the safety, and the hype around weighted vests

It’s easy to convince me to buy fitness equipment. Like people who turn to snake oil for any hope to cure the incurable, I turn to anything to get my body in shape. (and often lose to midnight snacking and compensatory eating after losing a bunch of weight)

Last month, I bought the bookBurn: The misunderstood science of metabolismfor some hacks (Best 10$ ever invested in fitness). This month, I bought the weighted vest (about 50$).

I couldn’t help but wonder if it is just another fitness fad, or if there’s actually something to it. Some articles said it’s not all that, and others promised a miracle. So I dived into the real research on them (so you don’t have to).

Can they be counter-productive?

First and foremost, I wanted to know if it was causing me any harm at all, since it made me tired very soon and perhaps slouch a bit. That’s when I found out I need a properly fitting vest.

A weighted vest can cause harm if it is poorly fitted. It pulls your shoulders forward, ruining your form. It has to be snug and high on the chest. Also it can do more harm than good if you have joint-related complaints, like hip, knee, or back pain. The vest just adds more gravity for them all to cope with.

Additionally, if you have lower back pain from a weighted vest, you need to lay it off.

If these two boxes (fitting and healthy joints) are checked, read on.

Weight loss or muscle building?

Does it help lose weight at all?

The short answer to whether the vest helps you lose weight is — yes. But it’s not as simple as that.

I read in Herman Pontzner’s book (link above for deets) about metabolism that no matter how much you exercise, your body adjusts your daily burn to a constant number. He even proved this by measuring calorie burn in hunter-gatherer populations that walk 10–20 miles a day vs. sedentary office workers. They burn the same amount when averaged per day over longer periods.

Then how can a weighted vest "magically" increase calorie expenditure?

Weighted vest studies measure energy expenditure in the exercise session, not in a full year. So yes, you might burn an extra 100+ calories during your weighted vest walk, but your body might reduce your resting metabolic rate or unconscious movement later to partially offset that increase, just as is the case with any added exercise.

If you start wearing a weighted vest today, your daily burn will go up initially. However, over weeks or months, your body "finds" those calories elsewhere—it might dial down your immune system activity, lower your body temperature, or make you more sedentary for the rest of the day.

BUT WAIT!

The magic of the vest

Swedish researchers recently published a study in EBioMedicine suggesting good news: your body might have a biological "weight sensor" they’re calling the "Gravitostat theory."

Essentially, when you wear a weighted vest, you are tricking your body into thinking it has gained too much weight, so our body naturally reduces appetite and works to shed fat mass.

So the vest might be working through an entirely different mechanism than simple calorie burning. This would be a hormonal/regulatory pathway, not just a mechanical one. Your body might not "recognize" it as exercise in the same way it recognizes running or cycling.

How much do we burn?

Research suggests that for every 10% of your body weight you add via a vest, your metabolic rate jumps by about 14%. So if you weigh 150 pounds and add a 15-pound vest, you’re not just burning 10% more calories—you’re burning nearly 14% more. Remember, of course, this delta is only within the exercise session.

But the relationship isn’t linear. Doubling the weight doesn’t double the burn. Some vests weigh up to 66% of body mass, for example, those used by military personnel or professional athletes. They showed energy expenditure increases of over 40%, but the returns start to diminish from there.

These aren’t your regular gym/running vests anyway. They are only recommended for trained professionals.For the rest of us, the sweet spot seems to be a vest of around 10% of body weight. (Trust me, it is plenty in the beginning)

Do Weighted Vests Build Muscle?

You can’t just walk around in a weighted vest all day and expect to build muscle.

Vests are effective for muscle building only when used as an actual resistance training tool. You need to cause muscle atrophy to build muscle, and vests don’t do that by themselves.

A 2024 study on women with low muscle mass found that weighted vest circuit training led to a 7.5% increase in skeletal muscle mass. That’s substantial. But the keyword there is "training"—these participants were doing circuits, not just going on a walk with a weighted vest on.

Weighted vests for bone-density loss

There are studies that found that older women who used weighted vests during exercise for five years prevented the typical age-related loss of hip bone mineral density.

But vests aren’t powerful enough to override the bone loss that naturally happens when you’re in a calorie deficit. So if you are eating too little, bone density will be lost anyway.

So what does this mean? If you’re maintaining your weight and using a vest as part of your exercise routine, you might see bone benefits. But if you’re cutting calories to lose weight, don’t expect the vest alone to save your bone density.

Should You Actually Use One?

Personally, I feel like I’m getting 15 min more out of my 1-hour exercise, whether it is walking or resistance training. I’m happily drenched in sweat 10x faster at the end of my endurance training (weighted incline walk at 8-12% incline and 2.7mph), and resistance training feels more intense.

I work out with dumbbells, and some moves, like squats, cause improper posture since I cannot balance with the vest on. For these moves, I lay off the west. There are other activities, like dumbbell curls, which do not really benefit from wearing a vest. For these, too, I do without the vest since I want to minimize joint strain from the vest when I am stationary. But for other activities like bench presses, lunges, etc. I enjoy the added challenge of the vest.

If you know what it is like to lift and cardio to the point of exhaustion, you certainly see the value of those extra 15 minutes.

So I’d say hard YES — go for it. But only if you don’t have joint issues. If you’re looking to increase calorie burn during walks or workouts, add a vest of around 10% of your body weight. If you want to build muscle, use the vest during actual resistance training. And if you’re concerned about bone density, especially as you get older, a weighted vest combined with exercise might help, but not if you’re simultaneously trying to lose weight.

I’d also not wear the vest for prolonged periods daily, especially if you are unable to hold your form. In this case, go a few pounds lower.

title: True Love Really Changes Everything
content:
True Love Really Changes Everything

Go find it.

By true love, I don't meanone real soul matelike in a Hollywood rom-com.

Why do you feel safe around anyone?What is that about dogs that makes you feel fuzzy inside? (No, this isn’t about pets either). Something incredibly beautiful in a child’s happiness that makes you smile and watch longer.What is that something?

Simply put, it isauthenticity.

You know that a dog has no ulterior motive to show you love. It is true and real. The same with a child. You can trust their emotion instantly, unlike what you see from someone who wants something from you.

The problem in achieving authenticity: Childhood Issues

Authenticity takes trust. You need to trust the person for you to be able to be authentic and express your genuine self. Trust is an acquired taste. If you have not had the childhood environment to trust and open yourself to someone, it is harder to start later.

As a child, your entire world was your parent. To a small child, they are gods who know their way around the impossible universe. But to the rest of the world, these are just imperfect people who just gave birth.

Many adults carry with them unsolved childhood issues, fears, and trauma. Thanks to the very slow changes in the mental health domain, these went undiagnosed for the most part of history. As a result, they have been passed on across generations, even this one, even you.

This might have caused you to unlearn trust. You see the world as if it were wielding a weapon, and that you should protect yourself from it. Your inability to distinguish between a real threat and a perceived threat makes you wear a “fake” face.

It is therefore difficult to wear your heart on your sleeve. Especially when you have been ridiculed, criticized, judged, and hated for it time and again.

Love can change that

True love just trusts. Trusting someone enough to believe them, and trusting someone enough to tell them the truth and be yourself around them.

To feel true love for someone, you need blind joy at the existence of this person. Not because they are your partner, that they are there for you, or that they do things for you. Just because they are how and who they are.

Experiencing that wonder with which someone looks at you, that sense of admiration and joy in someone’s eyes changes you as a person. It makes you better by asking nothing of you.

It helps you to trust, and see clearly. It makes your values clear to yourself and helps you hold them closer to your heart.

Can you ever find true love

Before searching for true love, it is important to learn to give it.You will never recognize an emotion in another person which you yourself have never felt before.

To a romantic partner, a relative, an animal, or a friend. To give true love without judgment or expectation is the first step in training yourself to see true love when it knocks at your door.

When people say love is the purpose of life, it is that feeling of blind admiration that they are talking about.

To experience that is a joy. Meaning and purpose of life seem automatic from that moment and life seems like it has found its place.

I encourage you all to seek true love. To see it when it occurs. You may or may not be able to hold on to it, but it is important to experience genuine, honest, and true love.

I am Sruthi. I write on medium on tech and life experiences, dotted with some short stories. Join medium with my referral link, let’s create together! It costs you 5$ a month and can change your life in a big way.

title: Ditch Excel!  — A Primer to Python
content:
Ditch Excel! — A Primer to Python

Pandas one-liners for popular excel stuff

Excel is, was, and will be an excellent tool forever. Its ingenuity is awe-inspiring and elegance surpasses that of Bella Hadid. Since python has been around, it has become hard to ignore the ease and flexibility it offers. All said and done, moving on is still THE biggest Data guy-strong point. So regardless of what kind of data-guy you are, or if even you use it only once in a while, it pays to get used to python (It’s free). Why Python, you ask?

If being able to Excel is like putting together IKEA furniture, being able to python is like being a carpenter. You can do far more excellent wonders.

Because everyone else is doing it.

In the words of George Mallory, Because it is there.

Let’s begin with some basic functions. For simplicity let’s talk only of the pandas library. Startup python and do this:

import pandas as pd

After this, read in all your files into your filename variables:

file1 = pd.read_excel(‘path/to/your/file1.xlsx’)

Excel: VLOOKUP

pandas equivalent:merge

With VLOOKUP you are trying to look up a value from one excel column in a second file. You will first:

a. Sort both files by alphabet on the desired column

b. Use the formula:

=VLOOKUP(A2, Area in columns, column to be returned,FALSE)

to get the output.

The pandas equivalent is a much simpler and comprehensible,merge.It is the same as joining the datasets on the desired common column. After importing and reading in your files 1 and 2 as file1 and file2, it is a single statement:

file1 = file1.merge(file2[['columns', 'you', 'want', 'to' ,'return']], how=’left’,on=’common-column-to-look-for’)

Voila!

Now save your results with:

pd.to_excel('save/here/path/filename.xlsx')

COUNTIF

pandas equivalent:value_counts( )

Very often, you will want to count the occurrences of an element in a column. In excel you do it like so:

=COUNTIF(area-to-search,value-to-search-for)(#and drag it down)

Following, you will remove duplicates to get the value counts.

In pandas, you do this with the following oneliner:

file1[‘Column which needs counting’].value_counts()

voila!

TRIM

pandas equivalent:str.strip( )

Invisible whitespaces are a real pain in most files. In excel, we create a new column and apply the formula

= TRIM('cell which needs trimming') #and drag it down

In pandas, it is again a one-liner like so:

file1.column_that_needs_stripping = file1.column_that_needs_stripping.str.strip()

Deleting All Spaces

pandas equivalent:str.replace(“ “, “”)

Often, trimming is not enough. You have undesired spaces throughout the column. I am used to doing this is excel by selecting the column, searching and replacing the spaces.

Pandas offers a simple solution of replacing spaces in all strings of the column:

file1[‘column_to_delete_all_spaces’] = file1[‘column_to_delete_all_spaces’].str.replace(" ","")

CONCAT

pandas equivalent:+

String concatenation is another popular method in excel. Say you want to concatenate multiple cells, you do this in excel:

= CONCATENATE(Cell1,Cell2,Cell3,Cell4……)

It can be really tedious to find every cell and type it down. Pandas offers an elegant oneliner also for this as follows:

file1['new_column']= file1['first_col'] +’if_space_or_other_string_needed_here’+ file1['second_column']

CountA

pandas equivalent:.notnull( ).sum( )

Excel’s CountA counts all the non-zero cells in the selection and returns all cells which have some values in them.

= COUNTA(range)

The pandas equivalent one-liner to count non-zero values is as under:

file1[‘column_to_count’].notnull().sum()

If instead, you want to get all the column values which are null, you change notnull to isnull, and voila!

file1[‘column_to_count’].isnull().sum()

IFERROR

pandas equivalent:fillna( )

You handle errors such as zero-division errors in excel with IFERROR, by defining an error fill value.

= IFERROR(Formula that might have an error, “Value to return if error occurs”)

In pandas, you can do this kind of error handling by using fillna( )

file1 = file1.fillna('Value_you_want_to_fill')

LEFT/RIGHT

In excel, you can extract the left n number of digits or alphabets using the following formula:

= LEFT(Cell, number of digits you want)= RIGHT(Cell, number of digits you want)

The pandas equivalent for this is as follows.Read the “:” symbol as until or from:

left_five_digits = file1['column'].str[:5]

right_five_digits = file1['column'].str[-5:]#the minus indicates 5 from the end, without minus, it means 5 from left

Those were some functions I often used in excel on a daily basis, which I now regularly and with incredible speed do in python. I hope this primer motivates you to make the switch because it is worth every second.

title: Sadness is OK, Stress is NOT.
content:
Sadness is OK, Stress is NOT

Break the Drama cycle.

The Drama Cycle

Your train is late, breakfast burnt, the love of your life likes another woman, your boss seems to think you're invisible, your shins hurt from dancing in heels last night and today you have to walk 6 kilometers in snow.

Life is a dog, yet, it keeps going on. It is not always easy to get a hold of your emotions. Before you get to that “objective perspective”, a lot of tears have been shed and the heart seems to be sinking lower and lower into your gut.

Suddenly, it is not that bad anymore.

You can’t remember why you were distraught in the first place.“That’s not SO bad now, is it?”.You think about that documentary on homelessness you saw last month. That book on the holocaust you read last year. You see that boy dropping from a flying airplane in Afghanistan, your uncle who has had a shitty life and now is sick to the bones at the end of his life.

Your problems look like a joke.

So you start again, slow. Still sad, still pained. But somewhere inside, you're ok. You stop wallowing in sadness.

But then, your hair starts to fall out, skin breaks into acne, life has been drained from your face from all that stress, and you panic all over again.

What is and isn’t wrong with sadness

As humans, there are certain hardware features we cannot get around. We need things. To be loved, to belong, to have self-esteem, to have beauty and purpose in life are all little dots we like to collect. When we don’t have them, we are sad.(Look up Maslow’s triangle for fun. This is assuming your basics like food and shelter are met).

Sadness is a feature that you have so that you know what you want and go get it. Like, google maps. It is ok to be sad.

Why Zebras Don’t Get Ulcers

It is ok to be sad as long as it ends at some point. When your sadness drags on along for weeks, months, and years, that is when the problem starts. Chronic stress, unlike sadness, isn't benign and harmless.

Stress is a physical response your body has evolved so that you can react correctly to your surroundings. If a lion is chasing you down the savannah, you better be stressed.

Stress is a language your body uses to tell your muscles to run as fast as you can, while your heart beats like crazy and tells your adrenalin glands “keep pumping”, all while your glucocorticoids run the show by shooting through the roof.(For fun, read any book by Sapolsky, he is also pretty famous on YouTube, hit “Why zebras don’t get ulcers”).

When the chase is over, if you are still alive, stress disappears and your body goes back to what smart people calledHomeostasis, meaning its regular juice levels.

While stressed, your body’s “less important” functions like hair growth, food digestion, and horniness for that good-looking neighbor are actively inhibited. Neurons in your hippocampus (some brain part for remembering stuff) are atrophied and you fail to remember things.

Human stress is more than chases down the savannah. It is bills that are due, love that is unrequited, a loss that cannot be filled. They aren’t over after the chase. So your Homeostasis is nearly never achieved when your bodily fluids keep splashing helter-skelter for a long period of time.

Ergo, you lose weight, your digestion goes haywire, hunger takes a backseat, don’t even think about hair loss. Ulcers. Diesease. Immunity. Selfesteem. Ugh! Stress-related sickness becomes the cherry on top of your scheiss sandwich. (Who puts cherries on sandwiches anyway?)

Be sad, but kill it. It’s your job.

It isn't my place to tell you “don’t be sad”. Depression is real. Mental illnesses are real. Many of us have no control over how we feel. I acknowledge that.

Having said that, in the interest of your own health and the health of your beautiful brain and body, it is your job to actively try and lift yourself from your slump. People have committed worse crimes than you, People have fucked up in the past. People have corrected uncorrectable mistakes. You are not a lost cause.

Respect yourself enough to defend yourself when someone throws a stone at you. At the same time, don’t throw stones. There is a midway. Acknowledge your faults, but don't buckle under them. You are human. It is all going to be fine. Be sad but put an end to that Chronic stress you have been through.

It is enough now. Here is your permission.

title: Poem: Turning 30.
content:
Poem: Turning 30

I smiled and cried,laughed and hurt,as if it all meant something.I cared and bothered,loved and hatedlike everything was up to me.

I reached out and tried to changeeverything that wasn’t meant to beI suffered and took pride in it,The meaning of life waseverywhere where I could see.

I had my moments of shallow beauty,of surreal joy and pride.I felt many eyes upon me,A young woman whose time has come,Sometimes I hid andsometimes I took it in stride.

I had fear of what is to come,of uncertainty and failure.But I was sure and felt secure,with people all around me.And then the delusion broke,Until I saw, how each of us is truly alone.

I lived through pain,through hope and strength,I lived through joy and love and regret.30 years into my life,I wonder what is yet to come.what else could there be,If there are more highs and lows,More emotions than already.
