title: Murder on the 31st Floor
content:
Murder on the 31stFloor

Short Fiction.

“Bye Aish. see you later”

said Rajiv to his wife as she stood by his desk.“I’ll pick up Bhuvi from Tennis.”Aishwarya gave a curt response and turned to leave.

“Don’t forget to take your meds,”he raised his voice,But she was already gone. Rajiv wished everything was back to normal. He missed her affectionate touch and coy smile.If only she understood.

As the sky turned orange and then black outside their glass building, the sound of key taps and clicks thinned. Motion-sensing lights gave away one after the other.

Rajiv’s vicinity was now the only lit space. Dregs of light that fell from the far corner hinted at a busy janitor, perhaps the maintenance guy. He was too immersed to care.

The upcoming conference in Canada was world-renowned. If he could publish his research results there, it would change their life forever.Maybe that will get Aish to love him again…

As Rajiv scrutinized his results on the screen, something jumped at him. Something from a different era. A pop-up from what looked like Windows 98. ⚠️Then another. ⚠️And another. A deluge of pop-ups now flooded all his monitors. The awful sound of windows bleating the “error” note drove him crazy.

“Thank god everything is backed up”.

As he reached for the phone to report what he suspected was a hostile attack, another window popped up. Rajiv couldn’t believe what he was reading.

“What a nutjob! The audacity to pull that on me!”He got up and looked around for help. Not a living being was in sight. It was now past midnight. As he prepared himself to pull the plug on his computer and shut it down, His wife’s image flashed on his screen, tied up, naked, and bruised.

Rajiv felt a lump in his throat.This is not real, was it?He reached for his phone and dialed his wife and then his daughter. Both phones were unreachable, switched off.

Who was this, with a personal vendetta against him?A million questions ran through his mind. But now was not the time. If this were not true, he would find out soon enough.But if it was…

Scared, and unsure, he walked to the balcony with his phone and looked around. A message popped on his phone, unknown sender.

You are wearing a blue shirt with brown pants. I see you. If you don’t do what I say, I will see you. Fling all your phones out the balcony and walk back to your desk.”

With a hacked computer and no phone, he would be at the mercy of the hacker.

“Do you want to see your daughter too?” Popped another message. Along with it a picture of his daughter unconscious.

Should he just walk out of the building and run to the police? Will there be too much damage before all of that?Rajiv couldn’t take that risk. He was a researcher, not an FBI agent.

A wailing and battered Aishwarya appeared on the screen. She was missing a finger.

It hurts, Rajiv. Please don't let me go.Pleasetakemeawayfromhere, please.

Rajiv was terrified. He ran to the glass banister, dropped his phones and watched them disappear into a million pieces on the concrete. He screamed and wailed into the night, wondering if anyone heard him at all.

Defeated, Rajiv walked back to his desk.

He sat there, transfixed, helpless, and begging for a way to save his wife and child. By now he was fully subdued.

He did not want revenge. He did not want to find out who it was. He only wanted Aishwarya to come running through that aisle back to his desk, he wanted it to be 6 PM again, to go home with his wife and be with his family. He would never have let this happen.

With no way to talk to the hacker, he could only sit tight and wait for the next dreadful message. He considered reaching to the laptop on the desk beside him and reaching out to somebody.Was he still being watched? Would the hacker know?

Another pop-up. Rajiv looked through his red eyes and face drenched with tears. A picture of Bhuvi on his screen, unconscious. With that another message :

Someone clearly wanted to sabotage his career and prove him crazy. From where they were, the surveillance cameras wouldn’t see his screen. He’d look like a crazy researcher gone awry. He didn’t care.

Few laptops remained at work. Most went home with their owners.Would the janitor hear him?

Rajiv had wrecked the office. It was bright now, with all lights on, a picture of insanity. He was back at his desk, waiting for it to end, for the next command, for the news of his wife’s safety. He was willing to give everything.

Rajiv typed into the text box that appeared.

A picture of a severed finger then appeared on the screen. Rajiv hurriedly pulled out his credit card and wrote down the credentials, shivering.

After a very long pause came the next command.

Ifyou love your wifeasmuch, Jumpfromthat balcony.Letmesee you.Itisyouoryour wife.Any last words?

Rajiv sat there, dumbstruck. As he stared at the picture of his wife’s finger on the screen with their wedding ring on it, it hit him.

With tears down his face, he wrote his last words.

If anyone could access his sophisticated video and image generation algorithm at this early stage, it was his wife. If anyone had pictures of his wife that would perfectly morph into this algorithm, it was his wife. If anyone could hack into the IT system of one of the world’s most powerful tech companies, it was his Tech support — his wife.

Who else would recognize his password —his wife’s birthday and her favorite fragrance?Who else could know that he had to throw“All the phones”he had, not the one phone he had?

Aishwarya was Rajiv’s only love, his only wish in life.

When he saw her at the IT Service desk for the first time, he knew he wanted her. He would marry her. And he did. For her, he stole her ex-boyfriend’s research and got him fired. For her, he sabotaged their relationship and drove him to suicide. Aishwarya was blissfully unaware, until a few weeks ago.

But there was one thing she did not know. He was forced to build a “better version of reality” for his research. It had removed the dark pigment spots on his wife’s lovely finger. Marks which he loved so much because it reminded him of the day he put that ring on her. Marks which he would know and miss in an instant. It wasn’t a severed finger. It was a finger generated with his software. He knew.

When Rajiv hit that concrete pavement he didn’t feel pain. It was soft, as if into a bowl of jello. A long quietness then engulfed him.

Frustrated, he snapped out of his VR headset and saw Aishwarya walking toward his desk.

“I’m going home. How is your project coming through?”She asked, noticing her frustrated husband.

“Well, I programmed a safe death by falling today. It seems to work. But I don’t know what I should do after that point. Is it appropriate to put in an ad after death? I’m confused.

“You’ll figure it out, my super-smart husband. I have to go now, Bhuvi must be near the end of her Tennis class. See you at home, don’t be long!”

“Ok, see you soon. Love you.” “Love you”.

Rajiv did not tell her what he just experienced in his“Live your worst fears”VR simulator immersive experience. It was best kept to himself until the day she really found out.

title: The Best Book I Read on Human Behavior — Our Inner Ape
content:
The Best Book I Read on Human Behavior — Our Inner Ape

A strongly recommended read for this year.

Ifyou are obsessed with “whys” — Why we do what we do, why we feel things, why we work, why we have a moral code, and why things are the way they are, there is a book you should read.

The answer to these questions is hard because most theories in science and religion explain theHowsand theWhatsand only rarelyWhys.

The Whyitself is a question buried in the meta. The answer could be in philosophy, in metaphysics, in evolutionary biology, or just plain acceptance.

In search ofthe whyI found these relevant books which connect you to your deepest and the most primal human self like no other real-life experience. In no particular order, these are:

Man’s Search for Meaning(Psychology, philosophy)The Denial of Death(An opposition to the pan-sexual view of Freudian Psychology, convincing alternative theory of the “why” of human actions)Meditations of Marcus Aurelius(Stoic philosophy)Night(Memoir, Holocaust survival)Sapiens:A Brief History of Humankind (History)When Breath Becomes Air(Philosophy, memoir of a dying man)Thinking, Fast and Slow(Science, Behavioral psychology)The Man Who Mistook His Wife for a Hat and Other Clinical Tales(Medicine, Neuroscience, Memoir)The Psychopath Test: A Journey Through the Madness Industry(Journalism, about convicted psychopaths)Our Inner Ape:A Leading Primatologist Explains Why We Are Who We Are(History, evolutionary Biology)The Diary of a Young Girl(Diary, girl who succumbed to the Holocaust)The Body:A Guide for Occupants(Research, on the human body)All of Ayn Rand’s fiction(This one is fiction, but it is the strongest dose of capitalism!)The Autobiography of Malcolm X(Biography, on a man who fought tooth and nail, believed in a cause and held on to it until it gave him death)Into the Wild(Biography, Journalism, on a man who pursued the dream of leaving everything behind for a life in raw nature)All of Yuval Noah Harari’s books(History, relevance, and interpretation for today)Guns, Germs, and Steel(History, evolutionary biology)

All of the above books are in themselves masterpieces that shaped the way of thinking of millions of people. They are important, not because of the sheer numbers in which they are read, but the massive paradigm shift that comes with witnessing a seemingly minor thought.

Our Inner ape — The paradigm shift on being human

Of all the books above, the most effective, raw, and real mirror into being human was inOur Inner Ape, a book by Frans de Waal,a Dutch primatologist.

Since Darwin discovered the brutal truth of natural selection and uncaring elimination through survival of the fittest, the “selfish human being” narrative has reigned in our minds and behavior.

We consider everything vile and selfish as “natural”. Love, empathy, and altruism are seen as overwhelmingly human achievements. We find solace in this proof that the Homo Sapiens is a truly superior species to other animals.

The Selfish Gene Narrative

In Our Inner ape, De Waal challenges thisselfish genenarrative through the study of Bonobos.

Until the 1920s, we knew the killer chimpanzees as the closest cousins of humans. But then a strange new “chimpanzee” was discovered. It was friendly, peaceful, and lived in matriarchal societies.

This chimpanzee turned out to be a species in itself — the Bonobo, the closer of our ancestors, time-wise.

As opposed to the chimpanzee which we know to plot and murder other chimps and revisit the macabre site to celebrate their job, the bonobos are a peaceful species that resolve conflict through love and sex.

They are known to be uncomfortably promiscuous, a reason for which they have not received a lot of screen-time in animal documentaries.

In Our Inner Ape, de Waal explores the bonobo and chimpanzee species in the areas of power, sex, violence, kindness, and group dynamics. He exemplifies how community, love, and altruism are much a part of our genes as dog-eat-dog survival.

The subject matter touches on sensitive areas —does this mean murder is “natural”? Does it mean violence and rape, as commonly observed in chimps “justified”? (The answer is no).

De Waal shows ample examples of love, empathy, and egalitarianism in the bonobo species.

For example, a bonobo male once drowned trying to save an infant of his clan from the moat surrounding their cave in the Dutch Arnhem zoo.

Another bonobo was found to care for a bird that was hurt and dropped into its cage by flapping its wings and dropping it from a height, trying to get him to fly again.

There has been an instance where a bonobo secured an infant human that has fallen into her cage by protecting him and waiting for the zoo personnel and handing him over safely.

None of the above actions has any selfish or material gain (food, sex, or caring for your own young). This altruistic, inter-species empathy occurs more commonly in nature than one thinks.

While every page of the book is a strong reminder of our “encoded vileness”, it is also a reminder that humankind is not predisposed to a deadly means of life.

De Waal, from his time in the Arnhem zoo, shows us how to and how not to embrace the ape-ness in us.

Why are paradigm shifts significant?

Thinking back to Neil Armstrong’s“One step for a man and a giant leap for mankind”,the view of the only swatch of blue in the otherwise blood-vapourising vacuum showed people in a photo that their world is not unlimited. The sense of “It is not endless and infinite”sets an urgency to the climate change question like no other chart or figure does.“If you go on doing this, there will be no more of it, and there is nowhere else to go”.

That is a game-changing paradigm shift.

Such is the change of mindset offered by De Waal in his refreshingly new perspective on the “unselfish gene”.

It was not only a welcome respite from the killer ape narrative, but also a heavyweight lifted from the chest on the burden of being human.

Our Inner Ape is my strongest book recommendation for this year.

Stay in touch throughLinkedInorMedium. Write me a note, it would be awesome to meet you!

Here is my social, I amSruthi Korlakunta.

title: How to keep Running
content:
How to keep Running

Capitalism made my running so fun.

I started running recently. That was 150 kilometers ago, roughly a month and a half. One rainy evening, I wore my year-old gym trainers and took off. I ran exactly 2.4 km with multiple breaks and dragged myself back home on my knees. It was SO HARD.

As a chronic insomniac, the night after the run was an awakening. I slept 9 hours instead of my usual 4. My body twitched in sleep and my muscles were begging to relax. Perhaps that was my greatest motivation to start running.

Some of my deep-rooted issues about self-worth and a sparing indian-middle class mindhave always kept me choosing low-maintenance and low budget hobbies — I try not to buy too much stuff for anything. This isn’t a terrible thingper say,those pennies do add up over time. But as opposed to all other hobbies, when I started to run, I didn’t think about anything, and certainly not all the things I needed for it. Turns out, running isn’t low maintenance at all.

How to keep running

As I ran in about 10 cities in Germany in the past 90+ days, my body remembered that this was not actually my first time running. I remember running in Hyderabad, a city where pedestrians, cows, dogs, lorries, cars, and bikes compete for the same sliver of road. I was about 19 then, overweight and embarrassed.

I remember buying a black and purple running suit about 2 sizes bigger because that was the only size they had in Nike, the only brand name I knew back then. I woke up every day at 5 AM and ran on a road with not too many dogs and when the traffic was low-ish. For about 10 days, I kept on the routine before finally moving on to cycling. I estimated speed and distance for my progress with vague estimates of what a kilometer and an hour might have “felt like”, and what landmarks I’ve reached.

That was my first stretch of “consistent” running. After that, I remember jogging in Bremen with a bunch of fit German boys, huffing and puffing at the back while they looked back at me and smiled politely, waving at me to try and catch up. Lo and behold, I remember giving up in a few weeks and falling back to cycling yet again.

After two more years, I tried running yet again in Stuttgart, a habit that fell by the wayside quickly. Each time, I kept it on for a few weeks and each time, running found its way out of my life.

After all these years (like, 6, 7 ?) I started running again in Ingolstadt, and this time, I could keep it on at least until my pace and distance improved to a level I only could have dreamed of. I have consistently put 150 Kilometers behind me. To an experienced runner this distance might sound like pennies, but to me, it is something my body can’t have ever imagined. What changed?

All You Need For Running

Shoes

I ran the first 100 kilometers in my year-old gym trainers which I also used for walking. The high-impact pounding on my knees on concrete wasn’t merciful to me (and probably the concrete). The Asics GT 2000 10 were an excellent pair of shoes that supported me at the beginning but in their condition, they were giving in, and so were my knees and femur.

I quickly upgraded to a better shoe, more for the fear for my anatomy than to improve my pace. This time, I went all-in and got what they told me were the “best” shoes in the market for my over-pronated gait and beginner butt. I got the Saucony Tempus, a sister model of the one I wanted really — The Asics Gel Kayano 30’s. But I’m glad I chose Saucony’s because the banana-form of my sunshine-yellow boots gave me an added advantage in the pace. The increased metrics because of this kept me going “just another mile” every time I run.

After running a couple of kilometers in my shoes, I felt bad for subjecting the beautiful yellow shade to all the weather impairments that I ran through. I went and got a second, Gore-tex protected pair that will keep me and my Saucony’s safe. Surprisingly, after I came home from the store, I noticed that I picked the same model of shoes I had before — The Asics GT 2000 10. It was cheaper than the newer models (12 was newer) and the most sold shoe among runners. I tried to save a few pennies for my “second” pair while I splurged on the first, hence the old model.

Stats

But ofcourse, all these shoes wouldn’t even keep me running as much as the most important creature in my life right now — The Garmin Vivoactive 5. Getting this fitness tracker not only took my running budget up by a couple of hundred euros, but also my consistency, cadence, pace, consciousness, strength training metrics, hydration and, wait for it, SLEEP, up my many many notches.

I have never been a fan of fitness trackers and smartwatches. Too intrusive and dystopian for my taste. But boy, Garmin changed my mind.It is in fact, the only smart device that gives me back something in exchange for my time and data.The stats offered by the Garmin Connect app, the possibilities to navigate, play music, receive notifications, and set and track goals with a reward system, with NO ADDED SUBSCRIPTION was worth every penny. (I had a Vivoactive 3 before this for a whole year, but it had a battery life of about a day and a half, so the upgrade with 7 days of battery was a needed one.)

Strength Workout

After a few more miles, I noticed how my body was changing. My stomach was a bottomless pit, I could eat a lot more food but I was still burning it up (and staying under the daily limit of calories, we’ll get to that). But my body was developing into a skinny-floopy-blah kind of way. Also, my legs seemed like they had a lot more potential if only they had the right muscle. I went and got some weights to help balance my cardio, so that my body gets the definition and muscle it needs to keep up with the activity. Another may be 100 Eur, but it is also something you shouldn’t do without. Alternatively, get a gym subscription.

I also threw in a home-treadmill for those days that I cannot run outdoors. So yeah, not a cheap hobby, running.

Sleep, Food, and motivation.

The more I got into the scheme of things, the finer I tried to tune myself to get faster (and muscular-er). And the most important thing between you and a good physique is food. I got a kitchen scale and an app that syncs ever so seamlessly with my Garmin device — Myfitnesspal.

You only have to enter your goals and these apps will tell you what to do, and how much of it. That includes calorie consumption, macro tracking, calories spent, amount of water, rest and sleep you need. Garmin also quite accurately assesses the amount and quality of sleep you’re getting and it is a helpful reminder. Myfitnesspal also has a macro range for the amount of sugar and other nutrients you need to sleep enough. This is extremely helpful, especially if you have trouble sleeping.

I also incorporated more protein into my diet and it has been immensely helpful to build muscle and avoid atrophy due to too much weight loss.

Clothing and accessories

Initially I went running in my jogger-pants, and a soft T-shirt. I also remember days when I wore my sleeping T-shirts for a run — soft, cozy and with a large pink bunny on the front. After I could run about 3–4 Kms I realized that heavy clothing would impede my speed. I started to strictly wear breathable fabrics meant for running. I already had a running jacket, leggings and T-shirts from ASICS and Nike, which after all these years of owning them, finally got to do what they were meant for. Also for women, it is mandatory to have an excellent running bra with high support. I’ve always loved how Victoria’s secret sports collection gave ultra-high support while also making you look like a snac ;) but now there are so many brands with good designs, I don’t even know what I own.

The bright running jacket I have has pockets both on the inside and outside (for your phone, tissues for a runny nose, may be ear phones, and that’s all really) and little holes for your thumbs to stick out while your palms can hide and keep from sweating or freezing. Incredibly helpful.

I also have a reflector around my leg for late night runs. (Yes I recorded some in the middle of the night because there were no buses to get where I wanted).

Last but not the least — music. I use the Google pixel buds which really are terrible for sports, but I decided to stop here and not acquire more gadgets. Good music will be a very important factor in going the extra mile. Your legs might be tired but the bangers in your ears will not let you stop or slow down. Get your playlist ready in advance to ensure a smooth run.

And lots and lots of motivation

After all that work of acquiring things, tracking statistics and improving yourself regularly, there is hardly need for external motivation. But I decided to make a post on my social media after every little milestone I reach, like running in a new exotic place, a centenary kilometer milestone or being able to wear the slim fit jeans I’ve grown out of. Do whatever makes you happy and don’t forget to celebrate your achievements. ❤️

title: As a Writer, Cut Your Teeth On the Correct Dopamine
content:
As a Writer, Cut Your Teeth On the Correct Dopamine

What you should really celebrate and what not

There is a story about Relationships that my aunt told me while I was growing up.

Once there was a magic box with a button on it. You could press the button as many times as you want. The first time, you get a cookie. A cookie so delicious, that it beats all other cookies and non-cookies that you ever ate in your lifetime.

After that when you press the button, the outcome is randomized. Either a bird might shit on your head, or you might get a delicious cookie. But more often than not, it’s the bird.

The experience of the cookie is so beautiful and strong that you keep pressing the button for a chance to have the next cookie, regardless of the awful chance of the bird dropping a deuce on you.

You notice at some point, that there are far fewer cookies than birds. But the magic cookies make every pain worth it. You are hooked by choice.

In this story, The box is a relationship. Love is a cookie. The bird is everything unpleasant in the relationship. I cannot help but think that the vocation of writing is not very different from the box. It brings many joyous moments but also leaves you despondent at times.

One might even say it brings more moments of dejection than joy- Because, for every piece that will work, there will be 20 that did not. But the few joyous moments peak so high, that it makes the down-lows worth it for just one go at that piece which you know will work.hooked by choice.

Because, for every piece that will work, there will be 20 that did not. But the few joyous moments peak so high, that it makes the down-lows worth it for just one go at that piece which you know will work.

There is no way out of failure or rejection. Looking for one is like looking for heaven without dying first. What youcando is regulate your mindset as you go through the daunting process of infinite successes and failures to become resilient and get out unbroken.

Learn what to appreciate and what not to obsess over, so that you will remain in the game long enough to win. Get your Dopamine from many sources than a single number!

Time is the biggest investment you will have to make in this endeavor. Here is a list of things you could turn to for motivation, instead ofthat one numberthat does not reflect everything.

Where your Dopamine should come from

Quality of writing

Read your article from a month ago, a year ago, a decade ago. What do you see? How have you grown? Identify what you learned. Note your specific learnings as precisely as possible and concretize these into your future works. Appreciate and repeat what you loved about your writing.

If you don’t acknowledge it, you cannot build on it.

Support from friends, family, and strangers

Maybe the big-success train is yet to arrive for you. But you have already picked up some cheerleaders over the past months. Be it your followers on medium, fellow Instagrammers, support groups on Facebook, friends who read and love your work, supportive partners —Appreciate the human following you already accumulated.

In the book,Man’s search for meaningthe author,a Viennese Psychiatrist Viktor Frankl observes his fellow inmates in a German concentration camp through the process of imprisonment, starvation, trauma, death of fellow inmates, and then liberation.

His groundbreaking theory about the human Psyche is that Humans are motivated to stay alive when andonly whenthey see a purpose to live.Theirpurpose to live. Most inmates who were physically sound succumbed to the camp’s brutality when they lost view of their purpose. Those that had family, partners, children, unfinished work such as books or scientific journals kept themselves alive by looking forward to these experiences in a life after liberation.

They survived by the virtue of their psychological faculties despite their physical damnation longer than those that were relatively physically sound.

Appreciate your purpose and the support you derive from the community.Look forward.From being a latent nobody before you started, you are now an active contributor to the community. Acknowledge this and keep writing for them!

Upping your Research skills

Like every author on the planet, you probably started off by rambling on a paper that was on your mind. Then you went through the process of streamlining it so that it is readable to your audience. You naturally grew into researching similar work on the internet, reading books about your favorite topic, and then came up with insightful new material.This whole process has a name. It is called the Scientific Process!

From a layman-scribbling, you raised the level of your work into a scientific process. According to Author Willian. J. Bernstein in the bookBirth of Plenty,the establishment of exactly this scientific process to develop a theory or an insight is one of the four pillars on which civilization rests. Cheers to you, little scientist!

New technology you learned

You might have started with a Journal or a piece of paper, maybe even a sticky note. You then moved on to various tools on your computer, discovered new apps, new platforms to market your work on, new ways to publish, made contacts with strangers on the internet who are also in the business, platforms that pay you for writing… That is already a long list of achievements. Keep a tab. Pat your back, Then decide on your path to new goals.

Actively setting new goals

Do you want to invest in a book that tells you about the experiences of people who have been there and done that? Do you want to sell Merchandise after you reached a followership that is big enough? Do you want to be a full-time writer and have a platform big enough before you start? Lay these stepping stones slowly but deliberately. Don’t think about the end, think heavily about the means. Think about this week and this month. Incorporate at least 3 new things into your next article. See where that takes you. If it doesn’t work, try again. If you have tried enough times,try something new.

Smaller stuff to check-off on the list

Maybe you did not write that viral article you wanted to. Maybe your book didn’t sell a million copies. Did you try the smaller platforms? Did you try self-publishing your book on the internet to see how it works? Did you try kindle direct publishing? Did you send a piece to your local newspaper?

There are smaller achievable targets in the process that you can focus your time on to generate new sources of motivation for your work. Don’t miss out on these opportunities. They are also a wonderful way to collaborate with your peers and fellow writers.

These are but a few tips from a fellow writer who is typing away from experience and respect for your hard work. There are definitely infinitely many sources for you to be able to see your talent also when it does not seem all too clear to see.

Hope you keep writing and see that you are making a difference already!

title: On Being Charismatic
content:
On Being Charismatic

Do you see these 8 tell-tale signs in yourself?

I always wondered how some people manage to get a deeply devoted followership, just like that. Why the room automatically falls silent when they talk, how their admirers talk of them with utmost reverence and trust. What do they have that others do not? Turns out, it has a name, Charisma — The state of being of somebody that tells you “This person knows what he is talking about, I trust him”.

It is what makes Gurus out of learned men, and leaders out of mere men of power. Mahatma Gandhi, Barack Obama, Jeremy Clarkson, Trevor Noah, Oprah Winfrey are a few names that bring to mind an aura of a charismatic character.

Needless to say, Charisma is an immensely valuable state to develop and it is learnable. I say “state” and not “skill” because it is not a one-time act or a performance for an audience. It is an entire personality backed by wisdom and confidence. A state of equivalence with and focus on one’s surroundings. Like happiness and success, charisma is not something you pursue and attain but a journey that you grow through. Here is a set of characteristics that signify a charismatic character.

Being Decisive

As Humans, we all tend to look up to heroes. We erect statues, carve faces on mountains, make blockbusters out of super-hero movies which we know cannot exist in reality.Theyknow what to do.Theymake the correct decisions.We love it when someone else decides for us. Being able to decide is charisma.

The complexity of the world makes it difficult to make“The best”decisions in the most trivial aspects. New car? Life partner? What to watch on Netflix? What to eat for dinner? “What if we make the wrong choice and end up with not the best possible option!!?”.This unpredictability makes decisiveness attractive.

What Charismatic people do is to inform themselves and decide once and for all. Being able to look one in the eye and say “do it” shows charisma.

The ability to meet anyone at eye-level

Being respectful while maintaining your self-esteem comes naturally with friends and loved ones. Charisma, on the other hand, shows when you are dealing with people that are at a different “level of hierarchy” than you are. We have various constructs of hierarchy inbuilt into our society. Educated and uneducated, Boss and subordinate, Blue-collar and white-collar etc. Whichever bucket you fall into concerning your counterpart, you tend to mentally assume a step on the ladder above or below him. This shows in how you talk or what you say.

Charismatic characters are not insecure about their position on the ladder if they are a step below. They are not arrogant about being a step higher. While talking to a boss, charismatic characters can maintain their self-esteem and be assertive and objective. While talking to a subordinate, they are equally respectful and put the counterpart at ease and give them a sense of being heard and respected without being patronizing.

Being present

Presence is when you are absorbed in the now. Remember those times you stare out of the car window listening to music and lost in thoughts? Presence is the opposite of that. Think about the time when someone is talking right to your face, but you missed the first half of the speech without him noticing? Presence is the opposite of that. It is when you don’t have to recede into your phone every couple of minutes to get a respite from the real world. Or when you are actively involved in the now absorbed in it, trying to make sense of it and gaining insight from it. If you are present, you have a higher chance of being perceived as charismatic.

Willingness to listen and understand

Active listening is the most important skill for a charismatic personality. Nobody knows everything,but everybody knows something.It is enormously important to be able to respect, listen, and filter useful information and make informed decisions. This is what sets apart charismatic characters from the rest.

Charismatic people are secure in their shoes to hear you out without interrupting to look smarter. Listening makes the speaker feel valued and respected, and also arms the listener with information and gains the loyalty of the speaker.

Ability to be stoic in the face of adversity

Social situations are not always pleasant. There is going to be disrespect, insults hurled, emotional extremes, loved ones being left behind and difficult decisions to make. Nobody has made it through life without one or all of these experiences. Charismatic personalities have the grit to remain stoic, trust logic, and pull through to the end.

Charismatic people don’t hurl insult to an insult, plot revenge, or actively hate. They can separate the cause from the person.

Being comfortable with remaining silent

Charismatic people know when it is alright to remain silent. They don’t need to fill the air with words to feel less awkward. They see that silence is as important a part of speech as words.

Silence does not signify weakness or a lack of knowledge. A moment of silence before an answer shows that you are willing to spend time to say something that matters. It shows that you are not blurting out things that make you look good, making your cause secondary.

Silence allows for your listener space to contribute and elaborate. It could be used to emphasize and let sink-in what was just said. They say for a reason that silence is golden.

Self-esteem to not sell one’s self too hard

Charismatic people have the confidence to say more with actions than words. One does not need a thousand words to market one’s self when a few words that matter can say all there is to say. Selling yourself too hard reflects insecurity rather than charisma.

The more words one uses to focus on the cause, the less are needed to elevate the person and personality. The constant need to sell one’s awesomeness raises eyebrows and draws questions. The noise of all the bragging drowns the possibility of listening to the other person’s achievements and learning more about them. It tells the listener “I am superior to you” without offering anything to prove it. Needless to say, it is hard to buy.

Ability to learn and adapt

Last but not least, charisma reflects in the willingness to let go of one’s ego and say “I might have to change my decision” when presented with new information. Learning through experience and logic, letting go of what isn’t valid anymore, and being open to change are difficult processes. But they form the core of being human. A human that hasevolvedfrom a fish that walked onto land andadapted.

New culture, language, opportunity, location, whatever may be the change, charismatic people recognize tradition from fact and can accommodate the change. They can tell the difference between fact, fiction, belief, and theory. When they say I know, they mean theyknow for a fact.They can tell what is subject to change and what is a proven fact.

They can adapt their learning and experience to the new situation, and draw creative solutions to new problems in the new world.

There is a tonne of advice on the internet to “fake it until you make it”, or to “emulate a charismatic character” by imitating the smile, body language, way of talking, etc. of someone else. But charisma is more than a show that you put on.

Focus on improving yourself, read more, learn more, and meet more people unlike yourself, open your mind, and accept them despite your hard held beliefs. This is a more organic and authentic way of developing a charismatic personality.

You can copy someone else, but then, the world will miss out on the original charismatic personality that you have in yourself, and instead be left with a cheap copy.

title: ERP Audit — What an Auditor wants from your Access Management
content:
Cybersecurity, Information Technology

ERP Audit — What an Auditor wants from your Access Management

Enterprise Resource Planning for IT Compliance

It’s that time of the year!

No, not Christmas, the year-end audit. When the rest of the world is shopping for presents, some auditor somewhere is going through your system trails and hoping to go back home to his family before 12.00 AM.

EY, PWC, Deloitte, KPMG for some name-dropping. If you are working for a big company, one of these is most probably your auditor.

So why not do him a favor and give him what he is looking for?

The next time you are building a new system up from scratch or you if you are an auditor crouching upon an unsuspecting company, there are certain things around access management that you want to do. Most certainly, definitely, and without fail.

Keywords:Identity management, IT Compliance, Risk Management, Security

Why should I bother,you ask? The reasons are multitude. For starters,

You don't want this to come back and bite you in your posterior in a year’s time when your company hires an auditor to check your systems.

So that your clients trust that you have a system in place that is not messing with their information.

So that your boss's boss's boss doesn't think about firing you when your company fails the year-end review.

The following article deals withGeneral Computing Controls or IT General Controls,which auditors check for in an ERP system— specifically Access management.

Let’s talk basics. What is Access Management?

Access Management (aka Identity Management) is the organization of who can access what at what capacity (Royally christened as “privileges”).

How does it look in your day-to-day work life?

You cannot, for example, see what that employee gets paid for the same job you do. You cannot log in to your collegue’s HR account, you cannot manage someone else’s time or calendar, and cannot use some functions in your own system. Your boss may or may not be eligible to read your emails, depending on where you live. All this is regulated under access management.

The laws that determine how your access is managed are provided by both your company’s policy and the law of the land in which your company operates.

If you are in the USA, you might be facing the Sarbanes Oxley Law, Health and Information Privacy Law, Gram-Leach-Bliley Law, and what-not laws.

If you are in Europe, you are dealing with GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) and other tedious laws that you don’t want to get entangled in by being in the red.

What does an Auditor Look For?

Incidentally, access management is THE GREATEST risk that an auditor checks for during IT Compliance in an Enterprize Resource Planning system.

Imagine you are responsible for a system that tracks the incoming supply of cafeteria food, the amount of food in the house, who is allowed to buy and supply. As a system administrator, it is your duty to see that:

1. The correct people have access to the correct system

For example, a regular employee should not have access to the pricing system of the cafeteria. He should also not be allowed to change the menus, take home tonnes of food, and record them as “sold.”

When an auditor visits you, he will sample a percentage of users from your system and see if they have all the accesses they need — not too much and not too little.

If you have a couple of thousands of people and access types in your system, and no proper authorization concept for it, consider yourself in trouble.

The right thing to do is divide accesses into roles of hierarchy and decide who needs the power (privileged access) and who just needs the basics.

In this example, cafeteria manager, cafeteria food-seller, clearer-upper, regular employee, etc. Decide what permissions each of these people need.

2. There is a user life-cycle

User life-cycle management is to make sure that not only you have a procedure in place, but it is alive and kicking. If your chef has quit because he is mad at you, his card shouldn’t allow him to come in at night and “make funny” with the food.

You have to prove that there is a secure and legit process in place to add new people, delete users that you don't want looking into your books anymore, and there are no conflicts because of the change of roles and accesses.

3. There is Segregation of Duties

Meaning the same person cannot ask and approve. Most healthy businesses are not a one-man-show. I don’t like to use strong words, but a failure of SoD might smell like fraud.

It is important for the auditor that you have a process in place that ensures, where possible, that there is the “four-eye-principle” where and when necessary.

4. System Configuration Access is Restricted

This rule is meta. Not only should there be system configurations that are ensuring all the above rules, but the configuration itself should be privy to only those people who are supposed to know and do it.

Every change and new configuration should have a predefined and approved authorization process. Every such change should be recorded for audit purposes and regularly monitored.

5. There exists a regular User Access Review

Regular User Access Reviews should be included in the process of every healthy company. It should answer questions such as

Do these people still need access

Are there any fishy users in fishy places

Any users who have expired roles still floating about in the system

6. Change Management goes through a proper funnel

This means, every time you identify a necessary change in the system, you have to make sure that this change is properly:

requested

authorized by someone else

tested

approved

migrated safely into the productive system.

If you are going through this funnel, think back and ensure that SoD in step 3 is ensured.

So that’s about a little something on IT Compliance and Audit rules. Say hello to EY or PwC or whoever comes knocking for your year-end.

If you are interested in articles on Data Science, technology, or social competence, connect with me. Here is myMediumprofile, and here is myLinkedin. See you there!

title: Notes for Self
content:

Python has potential

Topic Idea: Integration into taciturn Europe as a Vivacious Asian.

title: The Most Terrible Story of Today Comes from Arizona
content:
The Most Terrible Story of Today Comes from Arizona

Grandma arrested and threatened with criminal charges for feeding the needy

“I think this is a PR nightmare, but, OK.”—The self-aware but helpless police officer before he arrested a grandma for feeding the needy.

I don’t understand the USA.

Earlier this year, Arizona city arrested a 78-year-old grandma for feeding the homeless in a park. She has been doing this act of service for four years before she was arrested.

The state leveled criminal charges against her and threatened her with prison time, about a thousand dollars in penalties, and a criminal record to go with it.

The city claims that the ordinance passed to make sharing food with the homeless illegal is for “safety and littering” reasons.

“At a city council meeting, the city attorney made it crystal clear. You may host a pizza party in the park for 50 people or a hundred people. Invite friends, invite strangers. You may do it all day every day, so long as your motivation is something other than to help people in need,”

— Norma’s Attorney

I want to throw up just reading that quote.

What is wrong with the USA?

They pretend to be the most progressive of the Western countries and laugh at the rest of the world. Then why do the most terrible stories of state-supported cruelty come from there?

American Media regularly points fingers at Pakistan and China and regularlydoesn’t blamethe Saudis for casual violations of Human rights.How are they different from the people they hate?

Thankfully Grandma Norma doubled down.

She sued the state and said she is never going to stop feeding the homeless. Shedoes not want any money,but seeks justice for her rights that have been violated by the city.

She took her service elsewhere but states that this is a much sadder place where the homeless just lean against a fence and sit down to eat.

I hope Norma wins her suit against the state. I thought this story deserves to be told.

title: Poem — I Want To Be Old
content:
Poem — I Want To Be Old

Like right now.

I cannot wait to seeHow being old would beWhether I will hold up until the endOr would I sooner be free?

I want to know which parts I’d rememberand what never deserved my timeI’d like to see who stuck longerAnd who was never to be mine

I want to know if my old manis standing by my bedsidewatching me as I wake up,thanking heavens I’m alive.

I want to see if in futurethe leaves still fall to the groundOr will there be metal birdsthat don’t ever make a sound

I want to be confusedand let my car decideIf the doctor hears the newsOr if I just need a ride.

I want that alien scientiststear my remains apartI want to be excavated to spaceAnd be misclassified as a pot.

I want to grow old.I want to get to the endIsn’t that the point?to be perfectly dead?

title: The Cardio Gaslight: Why You Can’t Outrun Your Fork And Why the Fitness Industry Hates This Book
content:
The Cardio Gaslight: Why You Can’t Outrun Your Fork And Why the Fitness Industry Hates This Book

Burn: The Misunderstood Science Of Metabolism

Since millennials came of age, gyms get crowdier. A new food achieves star status every day. Protein is getting into everything, including breastmilk and bar soaps. Health and wellness industry is now at an estimated 7.2 trillion dollars.

You probably also spent a few (hundreds of ) dollars into this trillon. Maybe you bought chia seeds and kale, or got the new Asics or Nikes to go on weekly runs, and probably even bought a dumbbell or two for home.

While we are at it, let’s read this book,Burn: The misunderstood Science of Metabolism,for about 10ish dollars and see how much of that spending makes sense. What is actually the state of the art in science that helps you drop those pounds or build those muscles?

Exercise for Weight Loss

When I was at the peak of my madness, I ran extra on the treadmill every time I ate an extra slice of bread or an unplanned dessert. But does that sort of burning off calorie-to-calorie even work?

Well According to Herman Pontzner, no.

Your body is an excellent accountant. It adjusts your calorie expenditure to the total work you are doing. If you run more, climb more, or eat less, it shuts off or slows down some internal body functions to compensate for this loss of energy.

You end up with aConstrained Total Energy Expenditure,which means that your body has a daily budget — Whether you are running a marathon or watching Netflix on the couch, you expend this budget in a day.

Don’t get Mr. Pontzner wrong; he wants you to exercise. People who exercise die less. They live better lives and have a higher quality of life. But for weightloss? Nope. It is as good as scooping water out of a sinking ship with a spoon.

Bottom line: You can NEVER outrun your fork. If you want to lose weight, eat less. That’s about it.

Pontzer studied the Hadza hunter-gatherers in Tanzania. They are incredibly active people who literally hunt lions and trek miles every single day. And yet, they burn the same amount of daily calories as a sedentary office worker in Ohio. Let that sink in while you are at your standing desk.

What is the correct diet?

When it comes to the diet wars, Pontzer basically shuts down the argument by telling everyone they’re fighting over nothing.

Whether you’re team Keto, Paleo, Vegan, or Carnivore, he argues that your body is a masterpiece that has evolved to thrive on almost anything — except the industrial sludge of ultra-processed chemicals.

There is no one specific macro that is a weight-loss villain, a calorie is a calorie, and every successful diet works only because it creates a deficit, not because it “hacked” your insulin or reverted anything in your metabolism.

Well, are we vegetarians or non-vegetarians by nature?

To Pontzer, we are “opportunistic omnivores” by design. We don't care if fuel comes from a steak or a sweet potato. People in different parts of the world have evolved to have different diets based on availability provided by geography.

The Inuit survived for generations on a diet that was nearly 90% meat and fat, with almost zero plants. The tropical tribes get a bulk of their calories from starchy tubers and honey. Then you have desert populations living on milk and blood.

None of these groups suffered from the “diseases of civilization” like obesity or heart disease despite their wild dietary differences. It turns out our bodies don’t care if we are fueling with whale blubber or wild yams. We are evolved to be the world’s most versatile engines, as long as it is not super calorie-dense, highly processed foods, which then do give us the disease of civilization.

Is It Worth the Read?

The book is a reality check. It’s slightly depressing for the cardio-bunnies, but it’s ultimately liberating.

If you’re tired of the biohacking with the cleanse-and-detox culture, this book is a sweet release. Well, it is not a 6-week transformation plan, but it might save you a few thousand dollars in useless supplements and metabolism-boosting gadgets. Read it if you want to understand why your body is so stubbornly good at being a human.

title: 5 Stars For These Netflix Limited Series
content:
5 Stars For These Netflix Limited Series

Edge-of-your-seat material set in opulence and rated in order of awesomeness

If you, like me, are not in the mood for misery, sepia-shade crime dramas or dark and dinghy serious TV, here are some colorful, opulent, bright shows that look amazing and leave you feeling exhilarated.

1. The Decameron

Best actress on the show:Everyone, but especially Saoirse-Monica Jackson.“Fuck yea, signora!”

This is hands-down my personal favourite. A period piece set in Italy during the bubonic plague, The Decameron is a fun-ass (yes, I’m starting to write like this because otherwise, you’ll think this is AI) limited comedy series that just kept getting better.

Everyone in Italy is dying of the plague, and Italy is in shambles. Nobles are rushing to find their ticket out of their declining fortunes and, more importantly, the pestilence. In this setting, a group of miscreants gathers at a villa, each with their own stash of secrets, and the games to claim the villa begin.

The show unfolds like a Theater Drama, with lewd but well-timed and placed comedy and excellent acting. The cast had chemistry to die for, and I’d definitely watch it again.

2. Million Dollar Secret

Best contestant :Sydney Falker“Sydnee is going to hell on scholarship!”

The Million Dollar Secret is a reality drama. A group of 12 people from different walks of life walks into a James Bond — style hotel. They all start equal. Well, all but one. One of them is handed a million dollars in secret, and the group’s objective is to find the millionaire and send them home.

The job of the millionaire is to lie low, more suitably, pin it on a scapegoat, and send them home instead. But there’s a catch: the millionaire gets a secret task, which can make them conspicuous, but penalizes them if they don’t do it.

The watching experience is an absolute riot. There is no player to “root for” because the rules of the game are to cheat and deceive as hard as you can. It is a damned if you will, damned if you don’t situation.

It is interesting to watch different personalities unfold in the face of winning a million dollars or coming out as a deceptive asshole on international TV.

The show currently has one season and has been renewed for the next.

3. Monsters:The Lyle and Erik Menéndez Story

Best actors:Cooper Koch and Nicholas Alexander Chavez“grandma you fuckin moron, I dont need money, I need dimes”

Monsters is a heavily fictionalized true crime story and is adequately spiced up to become a Netflix Drama. From the makers of the Jeffery Dahmer series, which went on to win awards and stir quite a bit of controversy, this is another nail on the head series.

Monsters is the story of two billionaire brothers in their teens and early twenties, who are convicted of murdering their parents in their own living room.

The show goes beyond only recounting what was, or a classic courtroom drama. The interviews with the criminals and their preparation for the courtroom are extremely touching and feel real to the skin. Just like the jury, you will be swining from one extreme to another, when it comes to whether or not they get the electric chair.

4. The Residence

Best actress: Uzo Aduba, but to be fair, the others hardly had a chance.All criminal activity is motive plus opportunity.

I’d have rated this show higher on the list if it had exactly 2 episodes fewer.

The Residence is a Sherlock Holmes-style whodunit, introducing Detective Cordelia Cupp solving a case at the White House. There are about 100 suspects to the murder of the Chief Usher that happened on the third floor during a State Dinner.

If there was one word I’d describe this show with — it was extremelykid-friendly (Except for that one sex incident with the Australian Foreign Minister, does that matter? Idk, I don’t have kids.).I’d go so far as to say, it felt like it was made for 11–20 year olds.

The series is well plotted and exectuted, but drones on at some episodes. Since there are literally a hundred suspects, it is hard to stay focussed on a single character, except Detective Cupp.

I’d rate this is a good watch anyway, the opulent white house set is impressive!

5. Running Point

Best actress on the show:Kate Hudson

While all the other shows on this list require a kind of “commitment” to get through, Running Point is a chill drama in the lines of “Unbreakable Kimmy Schmidt”, which is easy-going. Nothing too bad happens and no one is evil enough to raise your heartbeat. You can easily binge this show, which is highly entertaining, but it doesn’t feel very new.

It follows Kate Hudson playing Isla Gordon, a former party-girl turned girl-boss to a Basketball team-owning company. The plot revolves around Isla’s awkward attempts to prove herself.

Textbook comfort TV — go for it!

title: Moving to Germany
content:
Moving to Germany

In my time-lapse POV

Germany and I

10 full years of Germany.

I learned to pronounce simple English words wrong, I like teeth-crushingly hard bread, I look for bread-balls (brötchen) instead of toastable slices, and I feel an immense sense of guilt while dumping my trash into public trash cans at tram stations without separating for recycling.

I also have a Master’s degree from a GermanUni,worked for three major car-makers, two Automobile Service Providers, and one consultant.

In the 10 years, I also made one very good German friend. My former landlady. Coming from usually stone-cold German disposition, her acknowledgement that I was like her daughter was larger than a compliment.(Hours after she acknowledged me, I embarrassed her by showing up to the Ballet in a Mickey Mouse crop-top, repeatedly yawned, then fell asleep during the show. The rest of the party were dressed in black-tie, sipping champagne.)

The Fatherland

At about the 8-year mark in Germany I was officially accepted into the country with “Einbürgerung” —citizenship.(ngl, this was a cakewalk compared to their Driver’s Licence test)

I am no longer Indian on paper, as if my identity was not confusing enough. Nevertheless, I keep my paper-work and soul in separate boxes, so I am fully German by citizenship and fully confused at soul — a paint brush dipped in brown and then in white without washing it first. I’m not allowed in either paint pots in the current state.

I long to see Germany succeed.

Not because it pays my salary, or builds excellent cars, or because the autobahn has no speed limit. It is because of how Germany thinks.

Die Würde des Menschen ist unantastbar.

— Artikel 1, Grundgesetz

Translation:

Human dignity is inviolable. To respect and protect it shall be the duty of all public authority.

— Article 1, Basic Law

I have seen many an admirable line in constitutions and philosophies across the globe, but never a country that upholds these in their policy with the German level of idealism.

Growing up, values like public welfare, cleanliness, women empowerment stories, anti-casteist sentiments — pretty much every value that the Indian society imminently needs — were taught to me since school.

By the time I was of age to understand the real world, I realized that actual people on the streets did not hold the same values, neither did the government. Going by movies, global political affairs, news, and policies, I could hazard a guess that this is the case also in other major nations across the world.

I was in for a surprise when I came to Germany.They really did respect Human dignity here.

It took me a while to get used to share my opinion, be heard, think for myself — all despite caste, being a woman, being from another country, being of different color, beingnot-very-rich.Hierarchies were not exploited to the degree I was used to. I felt safe in public spaces nearly at all times. Taxes were hefty but roads were safe and water was clean, education and health care was affordable. There were no ignored destitute children all around me with want of care and schooling.

Beyond everything else, people respected each other at a basic level.

Was there no racism and sexism in the country ?— sure there was. Were there no anti social elements ?— sure there were. But it was not my everyday, and I felt supported by the state, the police, authorities who didn’t snub me or demand bribes just to consider me human.

The aftermath of the refugee crises and mass-immigration to Germany, the language and tone used in public media, people showing up to support war-refugees, volunteering, was all too idealistic to be true.

When I felt unfairly treated, I got the support I needed. It all upheld the values that were probably also taught in Germany since school. Even if a few people did not, general policy always did.

For this reason, I want to see Germany continue to do well. I want to see Germany continue to respect its environment, people and values. But I’m afraid that it might all become unaffordable to the country.

The Job Landscape

In the 10 years I have lived here, I have seen the country transform from an entitled Automotive princess to a grandpa struggling to stay relevant in the new economic landscape.

The health of the auto-industry (and chemical manufacturing too, I hear), is nearly directly proportional to how the country is doing. Right from the owners of Porsche Carreras to the little shacks that sell Döner Kebaps, it all trickles down from the mighty car-makers. This is no exaggeration.

I still turn my head and look with awe when I see a matte-back BMW non-chalantly parked in small-town India, but I was far more surprised to see the likes of MG, BYD, Telsa, Hyundai easily shifting German loyalties with their performance in the EV sector. Mind you, German car-loyalty is hard to shift.

In 2015, I remember applying to about 10 internships and being accepted to perhaps 5 of them. Opportunity was literally everywhere and in every industry. Looking for a fulltime job was slightly more difficult than an internship, but reasonable optimism was always on the horizon. In the recent times, I’ve met with students struggling even to find part-time work at Mc Donald’s in metropolitan cities. It has been 5 years since corona, but fresh graduates are still having to learn the the word “Einstellungssperre”(hiring freeze).

Paper Cuts

10 years, and nothing changed regarding unavailable card-payments, paper-mail processes and non-acceptance of email attachments for applications (for real, someone at the city hall literally told me email attachments are not legally accepted). It was such a difficult process to do anything-paper here, that my husband and I just married in Canada instead, and most others go all the way to Denmark.

The toy-town Germany that I got to know feels like a distant memory. When I arrived, I could pay rent, travel, manage food and expenses, all on my student salary working as a research assistant at the university.

Deutsche Bahn has progressively declined in reliability and local transport authorities are seemingly constantly on strike — insufficient compensation against inflation, rising costs of essentials, and fewer jobs on the one hand, and over-worked employees on the other due to employment freezes.

Funny memes on Instagram of people trying to get Mercedes voice-assistant to understand their accented German hint at why.Can German cars hear us at all? and when they do, do they speak English?

Should you move to Germany?

When somebody asks me this question in the current economy, answer remains the same as it was in 2015—

Learn German

Don’t waste your time in part-time blue-collar work as a student, get real student jobs

Do an internship

Apply to jobs close to your work experience and internship/thesis projects

No matter what area of work, learn tools and software that is the state of the art in your industry. If you know them all, learn one more.

But in the current state it might even be difficult despite all this. Should you move to Germany then?

Moving to Germany may not be your classic move-abroad-get-rich-live-happily ever-after scenario. Be prepared to combat a lot of paperwork, look up rules for every little happenstance, buy as many insurances as possible to keep you safe and if you still don’t feel German in 10 years, it’s ok, you never will.

Is that a bad thing though?

The country has a lot to offer and it is not what you think. Be open to be thrown into life in ways you hadn’t imagined and learn to accept moderation. The country is now again a teenager finding itself, and you might be impacted too.

I for one, cannot thank Germany enough.

title: 7 Enthralling Super Short Netflix Shows and Documentaries
content:
7 Enthralling Super Short Netflix Shows and Documentaries

Ta dummmmm

#1. Ghosts of Sugar Land

Ghosts of Sugar Landis a shocking 20-minute documentary about a Black Muslim man who gradually turned radical.Was he really radical, or was he an FBI informant? did he really move to Turkey and join ISIS? or was he merely a government aide?

At its core,Ghosts of Sugar Landexplores the story of a tight-knit group of friends in Sugar Land, Texas, grappling with the radicalization of one of their closest friends. The film offers a perspective on the impact of ideology on individual lives and friendships.

The documentary also addresses broader issues of identity and belonging, particularly for second-generation immigrants. It raises essential questions about cultural assimilation, religious identity, and the struggles of balancing multiple allegiances. Through the personal narratives of the friends, the film tells the stories of challenges faced by young Muslims in contemporary America.

In all, it is a short story of the lives of ordinary individuals caught in extraordinary circumstances. Personal and very real, the documentary is definitely worth a watch.

#2-#5: Wes Anderson's short stories

Wes Anderson is known for his artistic eye with movies such asThe Grand Budapest Hotel.His whimsical visual storytelling is here again, in a series of four short stories — adaptations from the creator ofCharlie and the Chocolate Factory, Roald Dahl. The four stories are each presented as a separate show, under 20 minutes with the exception of one under 40 minutes.

Anderson’s distinct and pastel palette with ornate interiors and early 20th-century costumes are memorable and one-of-a-kind. He renders the scenes from the bygone times with fantasy and romanticism—with all his stories being set in pre-world-war eras. The four new short stories are not in the least what you would have ever seen or expected. The titles:

The Wonderful Story of Henry Sugar(39 minutes)

The Swan(17 minutes)

The Rat Catcher(17 minutes)

Poison(17 minutes)

That is also the order in which I would rate them, from Best to Least Best. The storytelling is unique, and you will enjoy it immensely, especially if you like classic fiction.

#6. The House: Anthology

The Houseis a stop-motion animation series that revolves around bizarre happenings in a house over many generations. Each story in the hour-and-a-half show is about 20 to 40 minutes long and can be watched individually.

I would place this series in the same league as the showLove, Death and Robots, a surreal web of stories with ultra-modern visual storytelling elements that haunt your imagination.

What sets this series apart is its ability to blend the eerie, the whimsical, and the funny. Each episode introduces new characters, each burdened by the house’s curse in peculiar ways.

The first story is about a family that moves into the house unknowing of the fate that the house would inflict on them. The second is about an anthropomorphic architect rat, and the third, an anthropomorphic landlord cat and her annoying tenants.

The narratives are rich with symbolism; it is easy to miss the point of each story if you don’t pay attention. It is one of those series where you might be on Reddit debating the inner meaning for a few days after you watch it.

#7. Camp Confidential

During and after World War II, the United States recruited German scientists, including some who had been Nazi Party members, to work for the U.S. government, military, and intelligence agencies.

This operation aimed to gain scientific knowledge and expertise, especially in areas like rocket technology, which was crucial during the Cold War. Some of these scientists were indeed brought to secret camps and facilities in the U.S.

Camp Confidentialis a documentary based on the recollections of American soldiers who signed up for war against the Nazis, but instead were blindfolded and brought to this secret camp to serve them and woo them with luxuries, to come and work for the USA.

The documentary is a mixture of personal narratives, animated recreations of the past, and real historical footage. It is based on the stories narrated by then-soldiers, who were close to a hundred years old at the time of filming. This mini-documentary (36 minutes) shines new light on war and war crimes, and is a strongly recommended watch.

title: Sattvii — A Small Business Success Story in Germany
content:
Sattvii — A Small Business Success Story in Germany

Don’t wait for new ideas to come, work on the ones you already have.

The ratio of people who spin-off into a self-owned business to those who dream about doing it is notably tiny. The first and the most important question that stops one from getting off the floor is clearly —“What should I do?”.

In fact, as long as you are willing to“Do something about it”, saysViswatez Repakula,a self-made entrepreneur, you don’t have to look far to generate money by making the worlda little more perfect.

Here the story of Viswatez,an Indian immigrant from Anantapuram, Andhra Pradesh to Stuttgart, Germany, anda Project Manager from Porsche AG who spun his little boy’s accident into a successful business of sustainable lunch boxes.

Why

“That was a day I cannot forget. My 18-month old son needed surgery due to a major accident in his playschool. My wife was in pieces. I was trembling”.

That was when Ramya decided she would find a livelihood that will let her be there for her family, day or night.“I will only work where I can give my son the time he needs and deserves”.

It was clear to Tez and Ramya that they had to kick start their next adventure. If there was no place where they could give their little one all their attention while also preparing for his future, they were going to make one themselves.

The couple spent a year researching businesses that will get them their freedom while also permitting them to lead their lives regardless of their location.

Being a first-generation immigrant family in Germany, they knew that there might come a day where they would need to attend to duties back at home.

How the idea was born

Ramya’s nose puckered at the plastic-clad smell of cut fruit in her son’s lunch box. There was no way her son was going to eat the fruit she wouldn't even smell.

Hours of research on amazon showed exorbitant prices and scarce availability, if any at all, for a metal-box with a good design.

No idea is minor if you take action. This was a chance Tez and his wife took to make their self-owned business a reality. They decided to launch a kid-friendly design of a sustainable lunch box as their business venture.

Tez and Ramya worked with professional designers to make a lunch box made out of stainless steel that won't cut into the delicate fingers of their child, which he could open easily and which didn't have choking hazards or parts that snap and cut.

Thus in 2017,Sattviiwas established.

Experiences of owning a small business

The reality of owning a small business is different from the rosy internet stories and get-rich-quick schemes. The legality and bureaucracy of a business are a larger part of reality than the parts one dreams of.

Whatever the commercials or the paid-ads in your LinkedIn inbox might tell you, the IT infrastructure of the real world is abominable.

Banks are reluctant to give new businesses credit until they have established credibility, which is an unfair ask on small businesses.

As a business owner, it is on you to make it work, come hell or high-water.

Tez quickly learned that quality makes people talk. He got his chemical-free products certified in flying colors with the official German certifier-of-everything called TÜV. Only a good reputation can save a business against cheaper competition from overseas.

At the end of the day, it is not all that easy to get your business off the floor, small or big.

“No matter what people say, the toughest step in the whole business is “selling”. The word “seller” (Verkaeufer) in German has a negative connotation. You just have to close your earholes and keep pushing”.

“We can put in an enormous amount of hard work, but if people do not want to spend money on it, we cannot force them to buy. Business needs luck too. This has calmed me down a bit mentally”,says Viswa with a look of acquired wisdom and acceptance.

Regrets and Mistakes

The only thing Tez regrets about his small business is that he did not start at least 10 years sooner. “The sooner you start, the longer you have to recover from all the times you are going to fall.”

Having once been an entrepreneur, Tez will never go back to not being one. Having just one income stream is a dangerous gamble.Don’t just do it for the money, though, apply your prowess, take control!

When it all worked out

In a few months from starting his small business, it grew to a six-figure turnover. It was only further proof that the gap they saw in the market was wide and waiting to be filled.

There were loyal customers who were not only leaving great reviews on amazon but took to marketing the products of Sattvii in local German bio-supermarkets.

The impeccable quality of their products beats their competition by a large margin.“People want the best for their children, and I will deliver it!”

What more can a product-owner ask for?

Advice for newbies

Having established a successful business and made his family’s future secure, Tez can only recommend it with gusto.

“Do it!

No amount of course work can help you understand demand and supply like putting your own money and time can. You will only get better at your day job by moonlighting as an entrepreneur.

Start sooner, think big, go global. Don’t think your village, think the world.

Not even being the CEO of someone else’s business compares to owning your business. If you are afraid of bureaucracy,there is no place on earth where you will escape it, so why not deal with it wherever you are right now!”

Another piece of advice Tez has to young entrepreneurs is to expand their channels. “Don’t just be on Amazon, be on all of them! Explore more market segments, create more brands, if need be. Go wide.”

For all the young entrepreneurs, people on the verge of starting off, people who are just settling into their cozy jobs, people who have long been accustomed to getting a pay-check and nothing else, Tez and Ramya’s story is a nudge and a poke.

Wake up. Do what you always wanted to do. It is never too late to start off and now is as good a time as any!

Note from the author

I met Viswatez through a professional connection on LinkedIn. Tez is a simple yet precise individual who rations his time to make the best of it. Despite everything he manages, his family is the center of his attentions. It is not only his source of inspiration for a new business but also the purpose of it.

Meeting a fellow immigrant who was “making-it” was an inspiring story for me, which I wanted to share with you all.

It was a mind-opening experience, to imagine the story behind every little commercial and profile picture we see on every page.

Thanks, Viswatez and Ramya for sharing your story with me.

For more such features, meet meSruthi Korlakunta.
