title: 5 Nonfiction Books on Animals Every Human Should Read
content:
5 Nonfiction Books on Animals Every Human Should Read

If you knew you are an animal, you would behave differently.

Did you ever read or watch something that made you feel physical tenderness towards another creature?

What if reading about real animal science can make you feel that instead of watching Ratatouille?

Few areas of research are as humanly rewarding as working with animals and trying to understand them. But for most of the 20th century, humankind failed to use this opportunity. It regarded animals as unthinking and unfeeling automatons acting on “mere" instinct, like robots to programming.

How are animals different from us? How are they similar? Here are some excellent reads in ethology, evolutionary biology, and experiences with animals that will change your world.

1. Are We Smart Enough to Know How Smart Animals Are — Frans De Waal

Frans De Waal is a primatologist you want to hug. He writes with the passion of a human being and the objectivity of a scientist.

In AWSETKHSAA, Frans talks about schools of thought in research of animal behavior.

Touching on a multitude of species - among others — chimps, ducks, octopus, wasps, capuchin monkeys, stickleback fish, dogs, and corvids, he explains how the research world has been rigged on the human side.

Back then, the word in the scientific community was that animals are incapable of complex thought. Any association of animals with feelings was struck off as romantic and emotional anthropomorphism.

Turns out that most of the misconceptions are because of inept research methods and not cognitive deficits on the animal’s part.

De Waal challenges this trivializing of animals through his observations throughout the animal kind. Animals are in fact, of the same kind of intelligence as humans, which obviously manifests differently in their world.

After I first read one of his books,Our Inner Ape, I was full of love and admiration for Frans De Waal.

He doesn’t crush magnificent creatures and their existence into mere numbers and patterns. He thinks and feels with them. He shares their pain and joy. He laments the injustice they suffer in the scientific community.

Read this book.

2. What The Dog Saw — New York Times article by Malcolm Gladwell

“What the Dog Saw” first appeared as an article in the New York Times and later in the book of the same title. Author Malcolm Gladwell is one of the most innovative non-fiction writers out there and has been named among Times’ 100 most influential people.

In the article WTDS, Gladwell writes about Ceaser Millan, the dog psychologist from California and the host of “Dog Whisperer” on National Geographic.

Ceaser developed his understanding of dog psychology and pack behavior to train“problem dogs”. He enters the territories of monster dogs and desperate owners and creates order from chaos.

From Gladwell’s article, you will learn how pack behavior demands you to be a leader among dogs. More importantly, it is hard to miss the parallel between dog and human packs as you watch in wonder how Ceaser Milan makes beauties out of beasts.

3. Born Free — Joy Adamson

In 1956, George Adamson brought home a lioness and her two siblings after he accidentally killed their mother. Born Free is the true story of Elsa the lioness. Elsa roamed the Serengeti with Joy and George on top of their Land Rover.

The story drew extraordinary media attention. Elsa became the world’s most famous lioness in the late 50s and 60s. Even Sir David Attenborough visited the couple to watch how a lion accepted two humans as her own pride.

It was later made into a movie by the same name and started many conservation efforts for lions in Kenya.

Born Free is an astonishing story of dignity and forbearance of a wild creature. The book itself is a diary-like chronicle, which might feel repetitive. One should read it at least partially to truly “understand” a lion in the human sense.

4. Our Inner Ape — Frans De Waal

Although I alreadyreviewed Our Inner Ape, this list is incomplete without this Frans De Waal’s masterpiece.

Our Inner Ape talks about chimpanzee and bonobo societies — Two of our closest known species-cousins. It simultaneously throws light on human behavior which is astonishingly similar to that of our close relatives.

The book touches on human politics, group dynamics, alliances, love, sex, and war — right from our primate roots.

It is hard to miss the evolutionary continuity in the behavior of human and ape species. The only difference is that human behavior bears a shroud of morals that our complex meta-cognition has created.

The difference between human and animal cognition is not that of nature but of scale.Talking about philosophy, ethology, and evolution in the same breath, Our inner ape is a brilliant read.

5. The Selfish Gene — Richard Dawkins

In July 2017, theRoyal SocietylistedThe Selfish Geneas the most influential science book of all time. The preface to the second edition reports multiple tearful and shattered readers of the first edition about how the book robbed them of meaning in existence.

Richard Dawkins is loud and clear about every stance he takes — Whether on religion or on contemporary biologists.

Dawkins comes off as one of the few people who really understood Darwinism. In this book, he proposes brilliant explanations in evolutionary biology and game theory for various behaviors in every species (including humans), regarding all of them under the umbrella ofsurvival machines.

He thus obtains a sense of justice to beings and unifies their helplessness into survival tactics that govern them.

The Selfish Gene is a must-read if you want to really shred all non-sense and think about facts. Not philosophy, not god, not ethics, not compassion — but facts of what it meansto be.

title: It's Okay, Ghost, and Stay Home.
content:
It's Okay, Ghost, and Stay Home.

Social life is awesome only if it is meaningful.

I was 10, maybe 11. Whenever we had guests, our home was dressed up the house and made a great show of perfection and happiness.“My kid can this and my TV can that, Oh that new promotion I got, that was nothing”.

We always had guests and visitors. Friends and family were nearly always there.“Having a social life is empowering and healthy”.

After 30 years of cliches, I am forced to look at how I really feel.

Sometimes, social life sucks. Sometimes, adult social life is empty validations and plastic getting along. Sometimes, it's okay to cut down on the additional social calories.

Adult Connections

I moved to a new city nearly every year in the past 6 years. That meant I had to form social connections right from zero very often. Adult connections didn’t happen automatically like in childhood. Childhood friends were from Venus and Mars. Like Tom and Huck, Like Marx and Engels. Adult connections were not that accepting. They came with preset, great expectations.

Many connections I formed had mutual respect and insightful conversation. But oftentimes, I found myself in the middle of one-upping contests and the receiving end of“I am the bigger achiever between the two of us”.

Where there was no ground for such a comparison, there was wealth, caste, and name-dropping. Connecting with my childhood friends after a long time, I felt like there is something about growing up that scares us into the need to validate ourselves against others.

But not every connection has to be like that

title: Pre-process like a Pro — A Must-do list for Data-Engineers
content:
Pre-process like a Pro — A Must-do list for Data-Engineers

If you are somebody that spends most part of your day bent over SQL scripts, Excel Spread-sheets, Python and Dashboards — Hello fellow Data Engineer! I come in peace. I am not here to throw the tip of my nose high up in the air, exhale with force and explain the difference between Data-Science and Data Engineering (I hear that is a trending topic in Academic Circles now). There is work to be done before any science can be done on any data, and Data Engineers are usually the first respondents at the scene.

While a movie (or job description) version of a Data Engineer would be smiling away, firmly shaking hands and closing deals with expensive-looking business partners (with line-graphs pointing upwards in the BG), real-life Data Engineering is mostly toiling away in the coal mine of legacy data and monster-logs to extract that one tiny ounce of what seems like a diamond, but may or may not be one (What’s the canary in YOUR coal mine?).

Whether you deliver clean data for further exploration or whether you offer insights from raw data to make real-life decisions, we have by now realized that a vast majority of businesses don’t have a “Data-Culture” in-place.

I can vouch for the fact that you are not alone and neither is your business. Imagine what happened when they first invented excel - Most people went“Nah, we don’t need them, we don’t have the capacity to teach our employees to type, we’re good on paper”. We all know by now that this won’t last.

To navigate the daily obstacles of data pre-processing, understanding the scene and building initial algorithms, here are some things I have internalized.

1. This will take time. Respect this and Invest in it.

Understand that when it comes to pre-processing, you are not running a sprint before you“Get to the real part”. Pre-processingISmost of the real part. Invest time in this. Make a 100% sure that every data point means exactly what you think it means. If you feed questionable data into your algorithm, you get a questionable basis to make questionable decisions. True story. In Bavarian German this is called :

“Kuddlmuddl nei, Kuddlmuddl naus”

Translation : Garbage in, Garbage out

2. Ask. Ask. Ask

To make doubly sure your numbers mean what they say, ask them all possible questions. Watch out for anomalies. Here is an example list of questions I ask myself:

Are the values in a certain range?

Do they always occur in this range?

Are there garbage values, what do you want to do with them to not lose information?

Should I zoom-in here, should I zoom out of the curve?

Are the numbers always round/always not round (I’d be suspicious to see a nice round series of 100.00 where most others are 45.24, 33.34, 231.73 etc.)

Are there unexpected patterns(Example, everyday at 8.00 AM your meter is reading the same value. Does this mean your sensor has not started yet?)

Why are there recorded entries when everyone was on a holiday? Are these garbage values?

Are some of these numbers references/serial numbers of some kind and don't mean anything?

Which part of the data set is a Code ? Which of these is a number which carries “value” ?(Example : street number 14 is not greater than street 13, but 14 meters is greater than 13 meters. One is a dimension and other is a measure. You don't want to use same kind of logic on both numbers for further processing)

Was everyone recording the data (in case of manual documentation) with the same understanding ?

Are there spaces, other garbage symbols that don't(or do?) make a difference when taken out?

Is the data time-related? Can I use the time parameter, can I take it out?

2. Expand your tool-set

If you have been sticking to excel, expand your tool-set to either R or Python. Automate small jobs like VLOOK-ing it UP or creating joins. When needed, pivot it. Write small but reusable scripts to automate tasks which you know will come up sometime later again. If you are using python, invest time to get a hang of using pandas, seaborn/matplotlib correctly to avoid having to “Stack-overflow” it repeatedly. (If you are not already doing it, “stack-overflow” some things time to time ;) ).

3. Use charts once in a while and go “Hey! What’s going on here?”

If your data has measures and values, plot them at various steps of pre-processing. Make diverse plots of it to get a visual picture of the situation and proceed to pre-process in the direction of what you want to see. Your eyes are the first and very-best algorithm to recognize anomalies and trends. So, do this “Optical-check” before you proceed any further. Plot the easy values right there in excel and larger sets with whichever tools you please (I use Excel, Matplotlib or Tableau).

4. If using Machine Learning, tread carefully

Machine Learning has the allure of being a black-box that spits out magic correct answers. It is not. It is absolutely not. Any algorithm is only as good as the information you feed it.

Where possible, use non-black box solutions

If one class of data predominates the other in your input(Example in classification : you got a single 1 for every 100 0s?),find acceptable methods to stratify or “weight" your labels.

Too many parameters and too little data ? Try Random Forests and SVMs as opposed to Neural Networks. BUT, pre-process the data set to make it easier for the algorithm. The more abstract your input, the less reliable your output.

Lesser data means more pre-processing on your side. Think of it like mashing a banana to feed a baby. The smaller your baby, the more you mash.

Be as specific as possible in the task you assign to your model. Wide range of necessary predictions means more data. In most cases of application oriented, daily-life machine learning, we have neither the kind of data nor the capacity to process it.

DON'T forget to normalize your data to meaningfully fit a range wherever and whenever possible.

You see categories, you one-hot them.

Train your models at different points of time with updated information. Test on multiple test-sets to see if it REALLY generalizes. (Don’t be disappointed if it doesn’t)

5. Respect the “ritual” and Lapp up the meaning

Data-Engineering is.. fun. Don’t be afraid of enjoying the process because it feels like you are far from the goal. Every new insight is a step closer and every theory disproved is a tid-bit of information garnered. Stand your ground and pre-process to perfection. Realize that no finding is trivial and no chart was “ But we knew it already!”. No, not like this you didn’t. Spend more than a moment to look at insights, not just charts. That, is the major role of a Data Engineer.

Pre-process with care. Most other problems that arise later have generic and widely accepted solutions as long as your pre-processing game is on point!

title: Immigrants from India: The 4 Shapes We Bend to Blend-in
content:
Immigrants from India: The 4 Shapes We Bend to Blend-in

Things immigrants do to be accepted

1. The fake name

Robert, he said, was his name.

I met a guy online while looking for a home to rent in Germany.

Robert was his name. After multiple emails and phone calls over weeks, I was surprised to find a man of Chinese origin at his doorstep. He introduces himself as Liang.It wasn’t even difficult to pronounce.

Liang has been in Germany for over 15 years and figured that only fake names get the job done. I know the name-hiding all too well. I shortened my Medium Profile name to be “more memorable to the readers.” Some advice articles on Medium said so.

I know many Sams, Johns, and Addys who arent even Sam, John, or Addy. They were just born into thewrongname.

Netflix contestants

Netflix has a habit of taking care of adequate representation in its cast. Awesome.

In two of my recent favorite shows, “Bullshit!” and “Is this cake?” there were origins in the Indian subcontinent. “Hemu” and “Shank,” the contestants introduced themselves in what were clearly part-hidden or fake names.

I had the same experience with car-sharing drivers, coworkers, and customer service reps. I, for one, don't remember failing to learn any German names, including the wordStreichholzschachtel.

2. The fake accent

When I was working for a corporate firm in India, a popular topic of gossip was the fake accent someone brought back from the USA.

Years down the line, I see how immigrants twist their tongues to blend in.

First, they are shamed for not being understood in a foreign country, and second time when they are back in their home country.

The dawning of the “fake accent” happens sometimes unconsciously, and sometimes because you desperately want to fit in. It ends up failing you in both places- home, and outside.

If you don't try, your Indian accent gets made fun of anyway, so what chance does one have?

The world would be such a wonderful place if all accents were accepted without ridicule.

3. The “I am normal”

I spent a few months in the USA and noticed that Americans have largely accepted people of Indian origins. Comparatively, Germany is just starting to welcome settlers from India.

I love the curiosity of locals when they ask me about the Taj Mahal, Yoga, Air pollution, or elephants. Maybe it only shows that they want to know more about me.

After a while spent answering all that and talking about Chicken Tikka Masala, I miss being seen as “normal.” I order a regular German dish, speak the language, and smile at jokes that wouldn’t be funny in my culture. But I still am “that girl from somewhere else.”

I wish there were also a point where we all becamenormal.

4. The daily/weekly Whatsapp schedule

You go to the gym, clean your house and meet with friends. Immigrants have another mandatory chore on their checklist that they try to work into their schedules. The regular Check-ins with family.

If you see people leaving earlier, blocking their Sunday afternoons, and WhatsApp says that “Your friend is currently on another call”, let them be.

Like Muslims have a Namaz schedule and Christians have a Sunday-church habit, immigrants have a mandatory call home.

That is when mom teaches you how to make your favorite childhood Sunday recipe, and dad tells you about the gossip in your last uncle’s son’s family, and grandparents e-meet their grandkids.

If their telephone is busy on holiday during midday, leave them alone for the next hour or two. They want to go to the game with you, but they have to do this somehow.

If you related to this article, you would perhaps relate to this too:

I am Sruthi Korlakunta. If you have an idea or a story to share,hit me up! I will give your story the voice it deserves.

title: Poem: To Be Truly Free
content:
Poem: To Be Truly Free

I walked like I was freeuntil I looked back.down at my feet, I was held still.

still by the love I gave and took,by need and want,by like and despise.

I wriggled and writhed,to break free,to see what comes next.

When the struggle stopped,I felt free.I only had to see it,then I was free.

Now I could walk,but there was nowhere to go.If I didn’t want it,there was nothing to get.

If there wasn’t love,there was nothing to give and take.With no ambition,there was no hope and plan,No need for perfection.

I know that when I want,I can be free.I only have to picture it,I would be there.

Freedom wasn’t a place to go.It was a dream,to see it once had sufficed.to know that it exists,was the freedom I sought.

title: Choosing Your First Bay Area Bike (or any bike for that matter)
content:
Choosing Your First Bay Area Bike (or any bike for that matter)

Gravel? Road? Cross? Mountain? What is the right bike for you?

I moved to the Bay Area from Berlin.

Wow, the words will never stop carrying weight for me. But ofcourse, every place has its moments. And every place needs its own bike. Here is a thought process, walk with me if you're in the process of bike selection.

Out of the last 7 years, I lived 6 in mountainous Bavaria and one in the Berlin Metropolitan. When I started, I had no idea how to choose a bike until a trusted ally introduced me to the beautiful Mallepuvvu, my Canyon Mountain Bike from Koblenz. Here is a glimpse:

For the first few weeks, it was hard to get used to the sitting posture and how the seat pressed against the buns and left them black, blue, and sore. But soon after discovering the extra joy in mobility that a bike provides, Mallepuvvu had become the first ever “thing” that I had become attached to.

I’ve gotten rid of suitcases, board games, scarves, favourite T-shirts, and all the other beautiful things that I loved. But losing Mallepuvvu was the only thing that really made me cry. And I’m not even sentimental like that.

It was a perfect bike to traverse steep uphills, gravels and paved roads alike. Though it was meant for more adventurous paths, I ended up making it my travel-horse and treated it more like a touring or a gravel bike than the beast it had hidden away in itself.

So when I moved to the Bay Area of San Francisco, I wanted to choose a bike that was made not only for me but also precisely for the adventure these roads can provide.

The type of biking in bay area

I’ve seen a whole bunch of heroes on the bay roads with snappy road bikes and thick-wheeled mountain bikes. When I look at the distances it takes to get anywhere in the USA, and the size of pick-up trucks that can likely run me over in a blind spot, I am kind of apprehensive about paved-biking. This took road bikes out of the question.

How about a mountain bike? One cannot go wrong with this. However, I was looking for a bike with more “real estate” to keep my many mounts for an eventual long-distance ride. (I do dream of riding up North until San-Fran for my first long adventure). The lack of biking paths everywhere, like in Europe, meant far more paved roads. When I do look for bicycling trails around, there are many in the “loose-gravel” area for a decent adventure. A Gravel bike was thus a sweet midpoint. It is like a Swiss Army Knife for Norther California, It can do a lot.

This means lesser traction and stability than a mountain bike on off-roads, but also greater odds that I will keep off the paved roads that I share with drivers who aren’t as used to bikers as in Germany.

We have now reasoned out the kind of biking we want to do, so off to pick a bike!

Budgeting, Finding Brands and Features.

I looked at a few top brands of bikes in the region — Trek, Specialized, Canyon, Liv (The sister-brand of Giant, made for women), and Cervelo. I decided to keep it under $2000, an upgrade from my $1000 Canyon.

However, since my husband offered to make the bike or a part of it a present to me, I budgeted it up to $3000, if my husband wanted to kindly contribute the last $1000. Spoiler alert, I ended up with a bike under $1000.

I still consider myself a beginner after about 2000km, and wanted to try out a good gravel experience before I go the full measure. So I wanted to choose a bike that I can handle more roughly, which rules out a fully carbon frame. This also fits my biking style, which is more endurance and long distance, but not speed and agility. Aluminum and steel frames weigh more, but are more sturdy, hence they were my first choice.

The bike world does seem to tend to electronic gear shifting and hydraulic brakes more than ever. At a $2000 — $3000 price range, these are very achievable features. However, bear in mind that these features break more rarely and cost more to repair when they do, while the classic mechanical brakes and shifting might be more fragile and more easily “fixable”.

The Market

After scouring the market for a good gravel bike, I decided to get a well-maintained used bike (hello unemployment), which gives me more features for a decent starter bike.The Bicycle Bluebookis an excellent place to gauge what a certain model should cost in the second-hand market.

At this point, these were the specific models of gravel I had narrowed down, based on their versatility, prices, and features. I selected them because they are the “entry point” within each brand’s aluminum/steel lineup.

Trek Checkpoint

Specialized Diverge

Canyon Grail

Cannondale Topstone

Giant Revolt/Contend

Marin Nicasio/Gestalt

Liv Devote

Canyon Grizl

Cervelo Aspero

SpanningCervelo Aspero,Giant Revolt, andCannondale Topstone, these are all incredible high-end machines. However, when I visited some local bike shops, where I probably will end-up for a service, I saw that they mostly carried Specialized and Trek. This made it super-easy to try out the gear shifts (which are super different for Shimano and SRAM, the shifts on these bikes I found at the store) and how the bike fits my body.

PS: It is super important to check the bike fit to your body. Though you might like one bike online and the other a little less, you might change your opinion once you try them out.

TrekandSpecializedended up having massive dealer networks. For a beginner who doesn’t want to spend weeks waiting for a package from a warehouse in another state, this kind of customer support is a massive peace-of-mind factor.

Back in Germany, I had made excellent experiences with the Canyon bike. However, I barely needed any service (Which is an amazing thing in itself), and any appointment with the local bikeshop took all summer. In North America, things move more quickly, and Canyon was not known for its quick responses.

CanyonGrailandGrizlwere about the top of my list, but the word on Reddit and in the bike shops talked me out of it.

Craigslist and OfferUp

Of course, there is the downside of buying stolen bikes, so be doubly careful. But if you find a genuine owner with good reviews and a bike with its original receipt and the name of the seller on it, go for it!

Make sure to try out the bike before buying it. Look for the price point on the Bicycle Blue Book for the right asking price. The Bay Area (And honestly, many parts of the world) are full of people who want to try out biking and give up too soon. So a second-hand bike that is less used is always easy to find.

Pro Tip:When buying on these platforms in the Bay, always meet in a public, well-lit place, and go with a friend.

The Winner: The Trek Checkpoint AL 3

I ended up snagging it forunder $1,000, well below my initial budget. It is the perfect entry point gravel bike with an aluminum Frame. I did miss out on the hydraulic brakes, and have a Shimano front and rear gear train which took some getting used to, but I love it now.

It has the endurance geometry I wanted, plenty of “real estate” for my future touring mounts, and a reliable brand with local bike-shop support.

The Bay Area might not have the bike-priority lanes of Berlin, but it does have some amazing gravel paths and coastal rides. And with my Checkpoint finally in the garage, I’m one of the bay area bikers. Whoppie!

title: Dominant Species of 2020 — The Data Scientist
content:
Dominant Species of 2020 — The Data Scientist

A comparitive study of sub-species

The world in 2020 is Dominated by a single kind of Homo Sapiens — The Data Scientist. This species has mercilessly resolved to put most other species out of job with a wishy-washy promise of creating jobs for higher human intellect and to end manual labor once and for all. This piece explores the two major types of Data Scientists that today rule the world.

The major species Data-Scientists comes in two sub-species

The natural habitat of the first is in high-glassy office buildings with ever-green vegetation of NVIDIA processors, strong clusters and unmistakable merchandise featuring proud inuendoes to their job on their various belongings.

Sample Merch Observation 1 found oncars: “My other car drives itself. Does yours?”

Sample Merch Observation 2 found onT-shirts: “Think big, Learn deep, Train fast”

Sample Merch Observation 3 found onDesk:“Totally unsupervised”

They feed mostly on major publications by Geoffrey Hinton and are found to immigrate in large numbers to California, Montreal and may be Tel-Aviv.

The second, more commonly found species sits probably on the lower floors of the same building or other such enclosures throughout the country.

This sub-species tend to attend multiple non-data-related meetings in the day, having to clean-up and dust-off the data they scrape-off the meetings, sometimes also found to live in cooperation with the aforementioned first subspecies.

Most of their day involves figuring out the head and tail of unstructured abomination that is spit out of logging monsters of all kinds — sensors, pre-war Computer systems, logs generated at the will and fancy of a teenage start-up programmer, and the like. They usually do not have the privilage of merchandise.

This species is increasingly being dominated by the invasion of outside professions that encroach on their Data-Science Territory to consume and produce a vast amount of excel-residue.

Species 1 and 2 — A Comparitive Study

Mental existance in time-space

Species 1dominates the sphere of living in tomorrow. Somethings that they conjure up might seem utterly ridiculous to people with less than a million dollars in their bank accounts.

Some examples of what Species 1 might say in all seriousness :

“I am working on a shoe that can also be your best friend. If you charge it for 2 hours everyday the laces might hold for upto 8 hours!”

“What do you think about this LED screen that can be embedded into your wrist just under your skin to show you your favourite Commercials every 10 minutes?”

Species 2lives mostly between yesterday and today. They might still be solving the problems of yesterday because apparently, we know the way to Mars but haven’t figured out how to count distinct occurances of an strange element in excel in under 3 minutes. (A tip to Data-god here while creating species like this : How about we tackle one part of evolution completely before we move on to another, huh? Lets solve excel once and for all and for everyone before we build another rocket)

Some examples of what Species 2 might want to say but is unsure of:

But Boss! this is not enough data to draw a conclusion from. That would be stupid.

Damn! Excel cannot handle this much data. Please re-run the query for a smaller output.

Sh**t, ill try turning it off and on again.

What the Future holds

Both species seem to believe that their profession is the ultimate solution to the worlds data problems and the most noble of them all. Since this kind of egotism has proved fatal in the past to professions such as typists and DVD-rentals and world dominance is but a fleeting moment of blind-joy, the world remains enthusiastic to see the species that will evolve to wipe-out the era of the Data Scientist. Until then, it remains a fact that the earth is dominated by both Species 1 and 2 of Data Scientists atleast until the climate-change meteor hits.
