title: Dear Indian People, Sarcasm is a Terrible Love Language
content:
Dear Indian People, Sarcasm is a Terrible Love Language

and food is not a replacement for love. — A fellow Indian woman

Last week, I met a young Indian couple on a train. Married for 5 years, they think that public display of affection is desperate and cheesy. But they are not very shy when it comes to putting down each other “lovingly and sarcastically” in nearly every sentence.

“I just married her because she was available and I knew her already, as a guy I didn’t have many options”— The story of how they met.“Yeah, I was looking anyway and he was around”.She confirms.

To admit love is taboo in the Indian culture, even if it is to your own spouse. To hold hands or embrace in public is horrifying but hating each other openly is not. I start to wonder if the sarcasm and negativity won’t be internalized over time.

At 13 years old, pigtail-pulling and name-calling were the go-to love language among my peers. The stronger the crush, the more names were called in the classroom. Sadly nearly two decades after all that, the Indian couples I know still seem to have serious problems in expressing love in other forms than sarcasm.

Growing up I have no memories of my parents expressing love to each other. By love, I don't mean physical and public display of affection. I mean taking each other's side, giving each other a compliment, or even appreciating a well-cooked dinner in public.

It is a taboo in our culture, you see. Men who visibly love their wives are“weaklings”and the women who display affection have been“brought up badly and without shame”.Yes, even if it is to your own spouse (who by the way, you married nearly anonymously).

Indian culture has managed to find a socially acceptable replacement to love — Food.

An argument last night and don't want to apologize?cook him something nice.You were an a-hole to her for no reason at all?Just bring her coffee without her asking first.In places where food doesn’t suffice, new kitchen equipment or an expensive gift should cover for not dealing with real issues. Nearly all other forms of love are considered cheesy and too shameful to feel and express.

The result? I see my newlywed peers throwing jibes at each other which grow increasingly mean until we cannot tell a sarcastic joke from an insult. Knowing many older couples who have so internalized sarcasm that nothing positive comes naturally, I feel sorry for these young couples.

I happened to have dated a European man at one point in my life. Negativity escaped his lips only when it was absolutely necessary. He wasn’t ashamed to compliment me in public, hold my hand or express concern for my well-being, all while being watched — by his family and friends, or mine.

Having seen nothing like that through Indian culture, my relationship felt like a dream. I myself still could not get rid of the little jibes that my culture taught me to have at my partner, but I learned the long and hard way how not to do it.

I am not advocating fake displays of affection for the sake of society. But I surely think that validating your spouse even when in the spotlight is a nice gift to your relationship.

It takes strength, courage, and confidence to take the side of your partner all the time, especially when your upbringing tells you to hide all the love. But It is a skill totally worthy of having.

After 35 years of a sarcastic marriage, today when my mother cooks a great meal, all my father can manage is“Could have used more salt, but it is otherwise fine”. It was said so many times, I don’t think either of them thinks it is loving sarcasm anymore.

When I am old and gray, I want to be able to give a compliment without shame. I want to have the strength to say nice things and express love. If you are or have an Indian spouse reading this, I hope it lights a tiny bulb somewhere.

I am Sruthi Korlakunta, read me onMedium, or write me onLinkedIn. If I convinced you to become a member on medium,join here, I’d love to see you and read from you!

title: Literally Everything You Should Know About RAG Pipelines
content:
Literally Everything You Should Know About RAG Pipelines

If you know some Python…

Never heard about it until this month and now, that’s all I hear about. Let’s dive in and clear up the blind spots in this topic — RAG.

Imagine you are working at a large company. You are looking to find a specific company policy on, say, working from home in a different country. Your HR just threw a 200-page document in German at you and said, “Go figure”. (This literally happened to me).

Now you approach Claude and try to figure out what the policy is. Obviously, your company internal information is not on it. So you want to build yourself a solution.

You could certainly paste the docs into the prompt each time, but that gets expensive fast, and many documents are too long to fit in a single context window. You have to scour through a lot of documents, many times because this has happened to you before.

There is a way for the AI to look up the right information on demand, just like a human engineer would search an internal wiki before answering.

That is exactly what Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) solves.

What is RAG

RAG is a pattern where you provide context from a massive database (or a document or a PDF file) to the LLM before you ask a question, so that she can search the right answer for you quickly and efficiently.

The core of RAG are these concepts:

• How to break documents into searchable chunks• How text embeddings turn language into searchable vectors• How to implement search logics• How to combine multiple search strategies for better results

Text Chunking With RAG

Before we can retrieve anything, why not just embed the whole document?

LLM models have a token limit (typically 512 to 8,192 tokens), you will quickly overwhelm your assistant if you throw a 10,000 page document at it. Second, a large chunk containing several topics will produce a blurry embedding that does not clearly represent any single topic.

Ways of chunking

Fixed-size chunking is the simplest approach: split the text every N characters or tokens, with optional overlap so context is not lost at boundaries.

defchunk_fixed(text:str, chunk_size:int=512, overlap:int=64) ->list[str]:chunks = []start =0whilestart <len(text):end = start + chunk_sizechunks.append(text[start:end])start += chunk_size - overlapreturnchunks

You basically say “every n characters is a chunk, but since I dont want the sentence to break off in the middle of a word, add an extra “overlap” of 20 characters so that the meaning is retained.

Here is a visual example:

Sentence and paragraph chunking

A smarter approach respects natural language boundaries. Using a library like spaCy or NLTK, you can split by sentences and then group them into chunks that stay under a token budget without cutting mid-sentence.

import nltk

nltk.download(‘punkt’)

from nltk.tokenize import sent_tokenize

def chunk_by_sentences(text: str, max_tokens: int = 300) -> list[str]:

sentences = sent_tokenize(text)

chunks, current, count = [], [], 0

for sent in sentences:

words = len(sent.split())

if count + words > max_tokens and current:

chunks.append(‘ ‘.join(current))

current, count = [], 0

current.append(sent)

count += words

if current:

chunks.append(‘ ‘.join(current))

return chunks

Recursive character splitting

A popular middle ground — used by LangChain and many production systems — is recursive splitting. It tries to split on paragraphs first, then sentences, then words, working down the hierarchy until chunks are small enough.

The key rule of thumb: aim for chunks of 200–500 tokens with a 10–20% overlap. For structured documents like code or markdown, split on logical boundaries (functions, headers) rather than arbitrary character counts.

Text Embeddings

Once we have our chunks, we need a way to compare them against a user query to find the most relevant ones. This is where embeddings come in.

What is an embedding?

An embedding is a dense numerical vector — an array of floats — that represents the meaning of a piece of text. The key property is that texts with similar meanings will have vectors that are close together in high-dimensional space, even if the words are completely different.

For example, the sentences “How do I reset my password?” and “Steps to change login credentials” will have very similar embeddings, even though they share no words.

Generating embeddings with the Anthropic API

Anthropic’s embedding model makes this straightforward:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def embed(texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]:

response = client.beta.messages.batches.create(

# For embeddings, use the dedicated embeddings endpoint:

)

# Or use voyage-3 via the embeddings API:

result = client.embeddings.create(

model=’voyage-3',

input=texts

)

return [item.embedding for item in result.data]

Storing and searching embeddings

For small datasets, you can store embeddings in memory using NumPy and compute cosine similarity directly. For production, a vector database like Pinecone, Weaviate, or Chroma handles storage and fast approximate nearest-neighbor search.

import numpy as np

def cosine_similarity(a: list[float], b: list[float]) -> float:

a, b = np.array(a), np.array(b)

return float(np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b)))

def search(query_embedding, doc_embeddings, top_k=5):

scores = [cosine_similarity(query_embedding, e) for e in doc_embeddings]

ranked = sorted(enumerate(scores), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

return ranked[:top_k]

The Full RAG Flow

Now that we understand chunking and embeddings individually, let’s see how they fit together into a complete pipeline. There are five stages:

1. Ingest

Load your documents (PDFs, markdown files, HTML pages, database rows) and split them into chunks.

2. Embed

Convert each chunk into an embedding vector and store both the text and the vector in a vector store.

3. Query

At query time, embed the user’s question using the same model.

4. Retrieve

Search the vector store for the chunks whose embeddings are closest to the query embedding. Return the top-k results.

5. Generate

Inject the retrieved chunks into the prompt as context. Ask Claude to answer the question based only on the provided context.

Here is what the generation step looks like in code:

def answer_with_rag(question: str, retrieved_chunks: list[str]) -> str:

context = ‘ — — ‘.join(retrieved_chunks)

message = client.messages.create(

model=’claude-opus-4–20250514',

max_tokens=1024,

messages=[{

‘role’: ‘user’,

‘content’: f’’’Answer the question using only the context below.

If the answer is not in the context, say so.

Context:

{context}

Question: {question}’’’

}]

)

return message.content[0].text

Notice the instruction “if the answer is not in the context, say so.” This is critical — it prevents the model from falling back on training data and hallucinating answers that sound plausible but are wrong for your specific knowledge base.

Implementing the RAG Flow

Let’s put it all together with a minimal but complete implementation:

import anthropic

import numpy as np

from dataclasses import dataclass

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

@dataclass

class Document:

text: str

embedding: list[float]

metadata: dict

class SimpleRAG:

def __init__(self):

self.docs: list[Document] = []

def add(self, chunks: list[str], metadata: list[dict] = None):

embeddings = self._embed(chunks)

for i, (chunk, emb) in enumerate(zip(chunks, embeddings)):

meta = metadata[i] if metadata else {}

self.docs.append(Document(chunk, emb, meta))

def query(self, question: str, top_k: int = 5) -> str:

q_emb = self._embed([question])[0]

scored = sorted(self.docs,

key=lambda d: self._cos_sim(q_emb, d.embedding),

reverse=True)

top_chunks = [d.text for d in scored[:top_k]]

return self._generate(question, top_chunks)

def _embed(self, texts):

resp = client.embeddings.create(model=’voyage-3', input=texts)

return [r.embedding for r in resp.data]

def _cos_sim(self, a, b):

a, b = np.array(a), np.array(b)

return np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))

def _generate(self, question, chunks):

context = ‘ — — ‘.join(chunks)

msg = client.messages.create(

model=’claude-opus-4–20250514', max_tokens=1024,

messages=[{‘role’: ‘user’, ‘content’:

f’Context: {context} Question: {question}’}]

)

return msg.content[0].text

BM25 Lexical Search

Semantic search with embeddings is powerful, but it has a weakness: it can miss exact keyword matches. If a user asks about a specific error code like “ERR_TLS_CERT_ALTNAME_INVALID”, an embedding model might not surface documents that contain that exact string, because the semantic meaning of error codes is not captured in training.

This is where BM25 comes in. BM25 (Best Match 25) is a classical information-retrieval algorithm that scores documents based on term frequency and inverse document frequency — essentially a very smart keyword search.

from rank_bm25 import BM25Okapi

import re

def tokenize(text: str) -> list[str]:

return re.findall(r’w+’, text.lower())

class BM25Index:

def __init__(self, chunks: list[str]):

self.chunks = chunks

tokenized = [tokenize(c) for c in chunks]

self.bm25 = BM25Okapi(tokenized)

def search(self, query: str, top_k: int = 5) -> list[tuple[int, float]]:

tokens = tokenize(query)

scores = self.bm25.get_scores(tokens)

ranked = sorted(enumerate(scores), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

return ranked[:top_k]

Install with: pip install rank-bm25

BM25 excels when users search with precise terminology. Semantic search excels when users express intent in natural language. The real power comes from combining both.

A Multi-Index RAG Pipeline

A multi-index pipeline runs both BM25 and semantic search in parallel, then merges the results. The merge strategy is called Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF), which is a simple algorithm that rewards documents appearing in the top results of both searches.

def reciprocal_rank_fusion(

results_a: list[int],

results_b: list[int],

k: int = 60

) -> list[int]:

scores = {}

for rank, idx in enumerate(results_a):

scores[idx] = scores.get(idx, 0) + 1 / (k + rank + 1)

for rank, idx in enumerate(results_b):

scores[idx] = scores.get(idx, 0) + 1 / (k + rank + 1)

return sorted(scores, key=scores.get, reverse=True)

def hybrid_search(

query: str,

bm25_index: BM25Index,

embeddings: list[list[float]],

chunks: list[str],

top_k: int = 5

) -> list[str]:

# Lexical results

bm25_results = [i for i, _ in bm25_index.search(query, top_k=20)]

# Semantic results

q_emb = embed([query])[0]

sem_scores = [(i, cosine_similarity(q_emb, e)) for i, e in enumerate(embeddings)]

sem_results = [i for i, _ in sorted(sem_scores, key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:20]]

# Fuse

fused = reciprocal_rank_fusion(bm25_results, sem_results)

return [chunks[i] for i in fused[:top_k]]

This hybrid approach consistently outperforms either method alone in benchmarks and is the approach used by production RAG systems at companies like Cohere, Pinecone, and Elasticsearch.

Reranking Results

Even after hybrid search, the top-k results may not be perfectly ordered for the task at hand. A reranker is a second-pass model that takes the query and each retrieved chunk, and produces a more accurate relevance score.

Unlike embedding models (which encode query and document separately), a reranker processes the query and document together, allowing it to reason about their relationship directly. This makes it much more accurate, but also slower — which is why it is only applied to the top-k results after initial retrieval, not to the entire corpus.

import cohere

co = cohere.Client(‘your-api-key’)

def rerank(query: str, chunks: list[str], top_n: int = 5) -> list[str]:

response = co.rerank(

model=’rerank-english-v3.0',

query=query,

documents=chunks,

top_n=top_n

)

return [chunks[r.index] for r in response.results]

You can also implement a lightweight reranker using Claude itself, by asking it to score each chunk’s relevance before generating the final answer. This is slower but requires no extra API dependency:

def claude_rerank(query: str, chunks: list[str]) -> list[str]:

scored = []

for chunk in chunks:

resp = client.messages.create(

model=’claude-haiku-4–5–20251001', max_tokens=10,

messages=[{‘role’: ‘user’, ‘content’:

f’Rate relevance 1–10: Query: {query} Chunk: {chunk[:300]} Score:’}]

)

try:

score = float(resp.content[0].text.strip())

except ValueError:

score = 0

scored.append((score, chunk))

return [c for _, c in sorted(scored, reverse=True)]

Contextual Retrieval

Here is a subtle but important problem with standard RAG: chunks lose context when separated from their source document.

Imagine a company handbook with a chapter on parental leave. One chunk might read: “The policy applies to all full-time employees who have been employed for at least 12 months.” Without context, this chunk could refer to any policy. The retrieval system has no idea it is about parental leave.

Anthropic introduced a technique called Contextual Retrieval to address this. Before embedding a chunk, you prepend a short context summary generated by Claude that situates the chunk within the document.

Generating context with Claude

CONTEXT_PROMPT = ‘’’

<document>

{full_document}

</document>

Here is a chunk from this document:

<chunk>

{chunk}

</chunk>

Write a 1–2 sentence context that situates this chunk within the document.

Be specific. Include the document title, section name, and any key entities.

Output only the context sentences, nothing else.’’’

def add_context(chunk: str, full_document: str) -> str:

resp = client.messages.create(

model=’claude-haiku-4–5–20251001', max_tokens=100,

messages=[{‘role’: ‘user’, ‘content’:

CONTEXT_PROMPT.format(

full_document=full_document[:4000],

chunk=chunk

)}]

)

context = resp.content[0].text.strip()

return f’{context} {chunk}’

Anthropic’s research showed that contextual retrieval reduced retrieval failure rates by up to 67% on some benchmarks. The cost is one extra LLM call per chunk during ingestion — a one-time cost you pay when building the index, not at query time.

Prompt caching for efficiency

Because the full document is included in every context-generation call, you can use Anthropic’s prompt caching feature to cache the document across all chunk requests. This reduces cost and latency dramatically:

resp = client.messages.create(

model=’claude-haiku-4–5–20251001', max_tokens=100,

system=[{

‘type’: ‘text’,

‘text’: full_document,

‘cache_control’: {‘type’: ‘ephemeral’} # Cache the document

}],

messages=[{‘role’: ‘user’, ‘content’: chunk_prompt}]

)

With caching, processing a 100-page document into contextual chunks costs roughly the same as processing it once, rather than once per chunk.

Putting It All Together

A production-grade RAG pipeline combines everything we’ve covered:

• Thoughtful chunking that respects document structure

• Contextual retrieval to embed rich, situated chunks

• Hybrid search combining BM25 and semantic embeddings

• Reciprocal rank fusion to merge the results

• Reranking to get the final ordering right before generation

• A grounded generation prompt that refuses to hallucinate

The beauty of this architecture is that it is modular. You can start with the simplest version (fixed chunks + semantic search) and incrementally add BM25, reranking, and contextual retrieval as you measure and improve retrieval quality.

The Anthropic documentation has excellent resources to continue your journey:

Contextual Retrieval deep dive:https://www.anthropic.com/news/contextual-retrieval

Anthropic API reference:https://docs.anthropic.com

Anthropic Cookbook (code examples):https://github.com/anthropics/anthropic-cookbook

Happy building.

title: Coming Home to India After a Long Time
content:
Coming Home to India After a Long Time

Mom and Dad look slightly older every time they pick me up from the airport. Though I video called on a very regular basis, the real picture still surprises me with every visit.

I am at the bottom of my energy levels after a 13-hour flight in economy class. By the time I land, all I want is to get out of that air travel world in which I was for 20 hours now.

When I see my parents on the other side, I remember that they have been actively tracking every step of my journey through the night. Actually even before I started to pack and actively think about my time here.

Mom has tears in her eyes. But shamelessly, I can’t reciprocate. It hardly feels like an emotional journey after the actual one.

LinkedIn and the media always have me convinced that the world has changed immensely in India, and that Hyderabad is a bustling hub of modernization and development.

So I always return with anticipation. And it always looks exactly the same as the last time.

Within the first day, My parents introduced me to the world of new apps that make life easy. All development seems to be intangible — in food delivery apps, new ways to book your transport and many ways to watch movies. Intangible development is still development. At least it is easier to pay for stuff here than in Germany (cash only).

Within the first few hours of arrival all the sensitive subjects are broached. My potential marriage (or lack thereof), extra pounds on my hips, how my skin is so white now (which is awesome, they likey), most importantly, what I’d like to eat.

My mother is the same. Kind, unreasonably positive, and authentic. She wants to lie beside me and talk. I do too, but after all this time, I am contact-shy. It has been long since I’ve had this much conversation with people who care this much.

Privacy and personal space that the west provides are as addictive as they are damning.Sharing anything, even just information, feels like an ordeal.

So I stick with the easy topics — like how the food on the flight was, how I found the travel and how work was going. Mom doesn’t suspect anything. She’s just happy I’m there at all.

Slowly little things I never noticed missing in Germany delight me.

How milk develops a thick layer of fat when left alone, how lizards feel at home on our walls, how little empty boxes & spice bottles are “saved for the future, just in case”, how people are constantly in other people’s lives like it’s all one big story.

Given enough time, anything gets old. So I decide to wait a little longer and let it grow on me before refreshing my opinion on all of this.

On Day 2, the novelty has waned. I’d like the “esteemed guest" treatment of last night back, please?

My mother is vexed because I wrote a woman’s name first on an invitation card to my party instead of her husband’s (I hardly have a connection to him). I refused to change it. Argument ensued. Ironically, since my big, manly masculine dad sided with me, she accepted the matriarchy of that card.

Out in the world, I find myself asking stupid questions.“Brother, which direction to Ameerpet?”somehow at a glance, they know I’m only partly from here. So they give me a “poor you" look and point to the answers.

At home I start to notice little things my parents do, as if I just met them. My dad likes tiny fist-sized portions of cola bottles andFrootiboxes. I want to tell him it’s bad for the environment to buy many small bottles instead of one big one. But I picture myself soaking in a bubble bath and stop.

This time, I even did not complain about bucket - mug showers, my parents hoarding things that we dont need, or the too many jazzy colours in the living room curtains.

I didn’t complain because home grew more relevant after not having it for this long. I’ve spent a few years feeling “home"less. Now, I’m just happy I have one. And that they welcome me when I’m here.

If you enjoyed this article you might also like these:

Join me on Medium for all you can read and write. It costs 5$ and changed my life!

title: Why Confidence is An Overrated Virtue And What to Pursue Instead
content:
Why Confidence Is an Overrated Virtue and What to Pursue Instead

Tales from a patriarchy

I’d pick an intelligent self-doubter over a confident idiot on any day of the year, any year of my life.

Over the past few years in my tryst with a conventionally “developed” country, I came to notice that confidence, is not that much of a big deal.

Why?

I was born and raised in an overtly patriarchal society. Whatever the man of the house said was the word. Who made more money or had a better qualification didn’t matter.

Things didn’t get done as long as there wasn't a man who could look unreasonably angry and pretend like he knew everything.

If a car was being serviced, my uncle who never owned a driving license in his 50 years of life considered himself qualified to throw in an opinion.

It was “understood” by the natural state of events that the auntie who was a qualified mechanical engineer would bring coffee for the nice repair man and recede into the nearest hole after her service.(Throw on something decent, he is a stranger for god’s sake!).

It is not easy for a girl to count herself as “normal” and “desired” in such circumstances. Until you go elsewhere, that is the only world you know. “Normal” and “fair” are just words to describe the status quo.

If there were women in the same room as men, they talked only to each other and soft enough so that the men needn't have to process the noise. They eventually gave up and found themselves in the “women only” corner of the home.

Unsurprisingly, after I moved to a country where things actually worked, I took to self-esteem like a dog to a dog-pill.

Somehow it was expected that I should be confident at work, overnight. My being such a ‘downer’ was a problem in itself.

Over the past few years in my tryst with a conventionally “developed” country, I came to notice thatconfidence, is not that much of a big deal.

Sure, it is a nice-to-have. But in most sane decisions, countries that have their societies together don’t fuel their economy or their daily lives with just confidence.

Their movies are not testosterone-infused confidence-porn, their offices are not (entirely) propelled on excessive, uncouth, and mad self-esteem.

4 years passed since I was thrown into a culture that expects women to beregular people. So did an avalanche of confusion, devastation, waking up, recuperating, and standing on my feet.

Chasing confidence only made things worse.

Every step taken towards more confidence was a sign that you can’t live with your current self.You should change be be “normal".It was the largest reminder of my helplessness.

I now say what I want, to whom I want, and when I want. Not because I am a confident woman, but because it makes sense. Either I know it does, or I know it doesn't. There is no need for a meta-judge called confidence.

My experiences left me bereft of any respect for sheer confidence.

In places that have public health care, safe roads, and a functional police system, those who get things done, those who “have it all” are not those who blurt out the first things that come to their mouth because“of course they are right!”.

Measuring a country’s standard of living by how relatively safe the women are and feel doesn’t seem to be such a bad idea.

I’d define confidence as the ability to be yourself despite knowing that others are watching.

What is there to not like about confidence?

The campaign for confidence for the sake of itself, thumping your chest for thumping your chest in itself, is no more than a toddler’s exercise in handwriting for handwriting in itself. It has no substance.

It is nice to have, but it doesn’t take you places. Instead, follow your facts, your common sense, and what your personal experience dictates, and confidence will follow.

When it comes to navigation, compasses are better than confidence anyway.

I cannot count how many train-wrecks I had to witness in my 6-year career because confidence was at the helm. If leaders were all fact-based, level-headed, and information-driven instead of confidence-fueled barbarians, we would already be living in the future.

As a fun exercise, who appears more confident, Angela Merkel or Donald Trump?

Merkel comes to the people with her famous “Merkel’s Rhombus”, a sign of openness and calm. Her appeals to the public have a pleading intonation. Trump is the confident one. A man who is not afraid to be himself in the face of the entire world watching.

Confidence puts “the you” in the center of things. Your ego, yourself, your stupidity, your whim, and your fancy. As much as we like to believe in our omnipotence, there is reason in acknowledging that in many cases, we simply do not know.

In the face of a world full of people willing to drive themselves as their prime agenda, it seems important to madly drive yourself ahead. On the contrary, a fair world is built on “the other”. Any healthy organization, team, or family is a place where people listen and care. listento others and carefor others.

The heavily individual success-oriented American culture which floods everyone’s TVs and bookshelves might make this sound counter-intuitive.

There is a mountain of hero-worship around James Bonds, Rockys, and devils who wear Prada, but not enough around the Lebowskis and Alan Turings.

Healthy lives are built in a world where you can accommodate others without destroying yourself and yourself without destroying others. Healthy societies are based on facts and knowledge, and getting things done.

“Do you know?” is a factual question. You don’t need confidence to answer it.

“Are you sure?” is what your confidence asks you.

Even in the largest of organizations established for hundreds of years, absolutely nobody knows anything for a 100% fact. We all do what best we can with the limited information we have and all the missing pieces we assume.

Pick up any book in science, business, technology, or history. The confidence-feigning world we live in rests its foundations on shaky almost-truths and fine-print-disclaimers.

For the most part of human life, that is enough. And then we build on it.

The foundation you need to build upon loose facts is not more confidence but more facts.

Start with establishing facts for yourself. Facts about the world and facts about yourself. What does the world need and what can you give?

What do you want? where do you want to go?, and most importantly, what don’t you want and what is a no-go in your life are all facts.

Set solid boundaries and liquid pathways. Flow where you want to go.

When in doubt, focus on what you can know, measure, check, prove, use, and give. That is a great place to start when all you need is a pinch of confidence.

If you are interested in articles on technology or social competence, connect with me, Sruthi.

Stay in touch throughLinkedInorMedium. Write me a note, it would be awesome to meet you!

title: Thefunnyauslander: An Anonymous Meme-maker’s Way to German TV
content:
Thefunnyauslander: An Anonymous Meme-maker’s Way to German TV

A story of immigration, internet-hate, and influencer success.

As of today, nearly everyone has an internet content venture. Fame, glory, and “the next-level” are on almost everybody’s day-dreams — whether they speak it out loud or not.

thefunnyauslander, an Instagram page by an immigrant in Germany, made an appearance on German TV, a step most people would call making the “next-level”.

The creator wishes to remain anonymous(read on to know why).I will not get into details of his person.

Here is the story ofthefunnyauslander—an Instagram channel making viral memes about immigrant life in Germany. It is the story of spinning your insecurities into humor and then into national TV.

From Instagram to online business to Television

Since its establishment less than a year ago, thefunnyausländer has amassed a followership of 16k Instagrammers. But that is not even the best part.

“Last year at this time, I didn’t even own a page. But soon after I started, my memes got shared thousands of times.

I was thrilled! The fact that people related to my content, and that they laugh at it, gives me a sense of great, great satisfaction.”

Any creator would attest to how much joy followership and audience can bring. Think about the last time you smiled at a “like”.

What came next for TFA was a business out of a hobby and an appearance on Television.

Turning Instagram into “the real deal”

To most immigrants, it always comes down to“Can I turn it into an income stream?”.

This is a fair question. No one would leave home to strange lands if it was not for the prospect of achieving experience or simply, money.

TFA was approached by brands to promote their online shops, given its niche in the German audience and rapid growth.

“It was tempting but I didn’t want to put my brand on the products I was not convinced of. Some were laughable, like furry cushion covers with puppies on them.”

Instead, he decided to build his own brand. Having driven certain memes into existence, It was a very promising avenue.

“I opened an insta-shop. The merchandise started selling in less than an hour! ”

Right now, the insta-shop is picking momentum and TFA has a decent supplement to his day-job.

“I have my groceries for the month covered”, He winks.

“Before I could process this fully, A popular TV-show reached out to me through my insta-dms, asking for ideas. Can you believe that!”.

The TV stint

When he arrived at the sets of a shoot for a show, TFA was confused. He thought he was to deliver written material in the form of ideas. He had under half-hour to perform for Television himself.

His skepticism had a reason.

“A couple of weeks before this, a really large media venture had reached out to me through dm (insta direct messaging) for an interview. It had about 150k followers on Instagram. I went to bed dreaming of how awesome that would be for my page. The next morning they canceled.

To say that I was disappointed would be an understatement.”

With this experience, he was certain that this time too, he would be playing a small part, if any at all. But this time, it came true.

“I still cannot believe it, I tell you. But now that it happened, I see it as one of the opportunities that came my way along with all the wonderful people who support me on my page.”

That was that. Just like that, he could be a TV show he never watched, in a foreign language, for the content he fully wrote himself.

Internet hate

Not unlike everyone who exposes themselves to the internet, Thefunnyauslander also had to deal with hate.

“I like Germany. I like the people, I like the order and safety, and I like that I am allowed free-speech. It is not to be taken for granted in today’s world, I understand that, and I respect it.”

He sees his page as a voice for himself and other immigrants, offering a new perspective. Dealing with hateful comments or messages can be taxing on an individual who started not so long ago.

“Some people take it in good stride, I have very friendly and supportive followers from Germany. Heck, even German TV found it entertaining and not offensive!”

Thefunnyauslander says that he has received threats in the past, images of swastikas in his inbox, and sometimes hate in the comments. To be able to keep going, he decided his conscience was clear, and he did not intend to offend anyone.

“For this reason, I am anonymous. I would like to continue doing what I love, and I know that I mean well.”

What’s Your story in Germany?

TFA came to Germany as a student about 7 years ago. In the beginning, things overwhelmedthefunnyauslander,sometimes delightfully so, at other times, not that much.

“I thought I was weird, that everything else is normal, and I am the one that needs adjustment. Making fun of things or complaining was out of the question because, in my head, I was the odd one.”

I think every immigrant can relate. Behind the brave face that everyone puts up, there is always a slight doubt and insecurity.

He didn’t speak the language when he arrived in Germany. His social skills and confidence, he says, were sub-par. He looks back at all those times and laughs heartily.

“As time progressed, watching the same things happen over and over again, one gets confident in one's own shoes. I trusted myself more. I accepted that my perspective is valid, everybody's is.”

The slow process of overcoming self-doubt is at times hard, but liberating.I can relate.

Plans for the future

Thefunnyauslanderis firmly grounded in the present and would like to continue to sharpen his funny bone. He says he has no plans for the long-term and would like to take things as they come.

“My biggest fear is that I will wake up one day and not have an idea for a good meme”.

He looks genuinely humbled by the experience.

Advice for Influencers

TFA feels new to the whole experience and says he is too new to offer advice.

“There are so many of them (meme-pages)! it takes time to develop devoted followership. In about 9 months, I had about 11k followers. It came slowly in the beginning and then picked up speed. Have patience.”

He says he experimented with brand, niche, and names a lot.

“One of the first mistakes I made was that I didn’t have a niche. But with time, I discovered what sets my observations apart, and now, I have my niche”.

His advice, though he doesn't want to use the word “advice”, seems to be to go niche, be consistent and have patience.

That was the inside story of an immigrant-influencer.

I hope the future has more in store for him and wish him luck, especially with his first year anniversary coming on the 9th of March. Thank you,thefunnyausländerfor sharing your story with us.

The beauty of the internet is that anyone can be a celebrity, as long as they have something to show. I’d like to call it the “democratization of celebrity”. I think it is the greatest gift of the decade.

Good content gets picked up by itself, and consistency pays off. If you are an aspiring influencer, keep at it. Choose your niche and understand what you do best. It will surely work.

I wish the very best tothefunnyauslanderand to you.

For more such features, meet meSruthi Korlakunta.

Stay in touch throughLinkedInorMedium. Write me a note, it would be awesome to meet you!

title: Dear Indian People, Talk to Your Family and Friends.
content:
Dear Indian People, Talk to Your Family and Friends.

They are capable of more empathy than you think.

When I had my first ever heartbreak back in my teens, I remember the struggle of hiding it in our middle-class family home in India where I shared a room with my brother.

It was unreasonably sad and intense like young crushes usually are. But that wasn’t the hardest part. The hardest part was finding a reasonable explanation for my upset face around my mother. It was trying to look alright and participate, while one and only one thing buzzed in my head.

Nearly 30 and life being the b*tch that it is at times, I am sick of hiding. This time I went through it upfront and in full view of my friends and family who I trust. And it was really ok.

Love, failure, and mediocrity are a part of everyone’s life as much as they are taboo in the Indian culture. Until they get to their late 20s, many young adults go through all those life experiences in secret and shame.

This is not without reason. Families themselves have a hard time accepting the objective realities of a situation. Instead of thinking“How can I help my son/daughter/sibling get through this”, they enter“Oh god, life is ruined”mode. Thus starts an eternal downward spiral, leading to more shame and more distress than a situation already brings.

With the rapidly evolving middle class and changing culture, the responsibility to make his or herself understood lies with the young adult. Therapy, personal space, perspective-taking, emotional security are fancy keywords that are not known to the parents of the Indian middle class. And it is not their fault. It is hard to teach your children what you yourself have never experienced.

Instead of cutting them out of the conversation and shaming themselves for having these emotions, young people, especially those who have had the chance to travel and see the world, have a responsibility to bring back home some emotionally healthy practices.

The reason why most families reject new ideas is in part because they don’t get pitched anything confidently, and no one has questioned their decision-making without giving in to emotional black mailing.

When should the change begin? What are the abnormalities that should occur, before which we realize that life is not a template of perfection and that it is okay? Reason with them, talk to them and be vulnerable. It is solely in your hands.

The truth is, with time and perseverance, if you are sure about your choices, your family will understand. If they cannot, they will at least have an honest picture of the person you are. Nothing is more calming to your mind than someone who understands.

So young people, take responsibility and trust your decisions. They will not be perfect. They might not work out the way you want them to. It is not the end of the world. Your family knows that. It is best if they have a real picture of you.

title: Must-have-on-finger-tips Datetime functions for python
content:
Must-have-on-finger-tips Datetime functions for Python

From Python and Pandas

Python offers a whole bunch of functions and libraries for working with DateTime data. I often use:

Python’s datetime

Pandas’s Timestamp

After working with a certain kind of data a while, we end up using some functions more often than the rest. While the choice of the function depends entirely on one’s experience and comfort level, it pays to know your options. If you are working on time series with raw timestamps or some forms of DateTime objects delivered by various systems and devices, this article should brush-up the popular DateTime functions that will come in handy.

1. Strings from Dates and Date Formats

Here we discuss the python.strftime()function and pandasdate_formatoptions in excel and CSV writer of pandas.

Very often, if you save your pandas DataFrame into an excel or a text document, you are unhappy with the yyyy-mm-dd hh:mm:ss default format generated by pandas. This is how I control these outputs.

Let’s work with an example of a data frame with random dates and string codes. Here is the toy dataframe we will use:

start = datetime.datetime(2020,2,1)

end = datetime.datetime(2020,2,10)

dates = pd.date_range(start = start, end = end,freq=’D’).to_list()

codes = [ "".join(choice(ascii_lowercase) for i in range(10)) for jin range(10)]

df = pd.DataFrame(columns=['date','code'])

df.date  = dates

df.code = codes

Obviously, the dates column is pretty informative but not necessarily pretty looking. We don’t want this magnanimous date on our output report, we’re happy with simply knowing the date in the format, say date/month/year.

CSV

If a csv or a text output is your goal, this is pretty easy to change. Just specify the format of output with kwarg date_format in your to_csv function.

df.to_csv(‘C:/location/slash_dates.txt’,date_format=’%M/%D/%Y’)

Excel

When it comes to Excel, you have to be a tad bit careful than that, because excel has a mind of its own when it comes to formatting dates.

Of course, you can just right-click on your format cells and change the format to whatever form you want. But when it comes to slashes, excel is stubborn. It is not one of the format options that excel has to offer (probably because it looks ugly, duh!). And, it is worth not having to interfere with the output in excel each time when python has so much to offer.

python .strftime( ) way:

To achieve the flexibility equivalent to date_format in to_csv, we “trick” excel by converting our datetime object to a string using .strftime( ) and passing it on to excel. (Since we have a Series object, we need a .dt in between to access its values)

df.date = df.date.dt.strftime(“%d/%m/%y”)

ta da!

ta da!

OR,

pandas way:

use the pandas excel writer and specify the format like in to_csv ( and give your sheet a name as an added bonus.)

writer = pd.ExcelWriter(‘C:/location/writer_datetime.xlsx’,date_format=”%D/%M/%Y”)

df.to_excel(writer, sheet_name=’xl_slashes’)

writer.save()

ta da!

2. Date from string

Here we discuss the.strptime( )andto_datetime( )functions, basically the sister functions to what we did earlier.

From the above steps, You now converted your datetime64 object with a set of Timestamp properties into a string series. (Pandas data type is “object” for a string or mixed series)

Before .strftime():

After .strftime():

For some reason, you want your dates back. Say you want to get what month the date is in or what calender week it falls in. You are better off using the inbuilt DateTime functions than parsing a string that looks like datetime.

For this, we use the alter-ego of .strftime( ), which is.strptime( )or the pandasto_datetime( )functions, if that’s what you like better.

pandas’ to_datetime( ) way:

Note the format and corresponding short forms of what they mean. Say your date string looks different, like this column:

Did you note that %B represents expansion of the month?

Let’s convert this date string to a DateTime object to get the calendar week from this.

df['date_month'] = pd.to_datetime(df.date_month, format=’%d-%B-%y’)

Now that we have the datetime object back, let's get calendar weeks using the pandas datetime function .week

df['calender_week'] = df.date_month.dt.week

Python’s strptime( ) way:

Pandas use of Timestamp.strptime( ) is deprecated and is replaced as seen above with a very effective to_datetime( ) which can be applied to an entire series using the accessor .dt.

However, if you have individual entities of timestamps that need to be formatted outside of a series, .strptime( ) is an effective python function.

import datetime

date_string = “2 March, 2020”

dttime_obj = datetime.datetime.strptime(date_string, “%d %B, %Y”)

The isocalendar() method of datetime is a tuple containing calender week at position 1.

dttime_obj.isocalendar()[1]

Those were some useful DateTime object functions in and outside of Pandas in Python. Hope this helps!

title: Why Is Everybody Terrified of My Legs?
content:
Why Is Everybody Terrified of My Legs?

The story of the infamous Facebook leg-photo and the person attached to them.

I updated my profile picture on Facebook after a year and a half.

As if I gave the world a mass-panic-attack, I got phone calls, WhatsApp messages, and instant dm’s from friends and well-wishers.(Spoiler alert, you will see the picture at the end of the article).

“Change your profile pic immediately!”.

Maybe I watch so much Netflix that I have become desensitized to looking at legs. Or maybe, I’ve walked around so much that I started to assume legs are just a muscular part of my body, which by the way also look good.

Whatever be the reason, I was genuinely shocked to see how much furor a full-frontal of legs can cause when posted to a Facebook profile picture of a regular,non-Beyoncetype girl.

A week ago, when someone said legs, I would have thought “KFC”. Or“Man! I should finally do those squats I've been putting off.”But now, when I hear “legs”, I tilt my legs towards the dark underside of the chair a little and possibly find a blanket to cover them up. Even if I’m wearing flared jeans.

I told myself I don't care, butman, do I care!

Leg-trospection

In all honesty, I thought it was a really beautiful picture.

I looked happy(albeit with a fake smile, but whatever.)with outstretched legs in the background trying to propel me higher on the swing. The legs were protected at the top by a pair of short pants (Some would say hot pants).

And in the foreground, a big grin on my face almost too close to the camera, making my face appear a little more round and curved than it is.

A little bit like this awesome guy down here, but with a pair of human legs at the back.

After having pondered over (and, I won’t lie, admired) the picture for a while I decided in an act of blatant defiance to let my picture be.

I was feeling, again, I won't lie — extremely uncomfortable with my decision to let it be. Now that they have sexualized my otherwise just nice legs for me, I just cannot unsee it. (Ew, the thought gives me creepy-crawlies, heebie-jeebies).

A few weeks later…

Now, the infamous legs-on-the-swings were up for about 3 weeks. Ironically, I was online much less often, lest there be, god forbid — another leg-related altercation.

In the three weeks, I posted a series of articles and perhaps gave a talk or two on metal health. I also duly and transparently noted in my talks and writes that I counted myself a survivor of ill mental health.

I made friends and intellectuals who related to my stories and also made their appreciation of my work public. Liked, shared, subscribed, quoted, and commented.

All for which, by the way, I am extremely thankful.

In the course of the many nice people I met, I made a special friend and a mentor, with whom I have started to have great conversations.

My mentor passingly mentioned my leg picture and how he “empathizes” with them.

His version of the legs was that I, suffering from the self-image issues I had previously mentioned, commoditize myself by posting a picture of these pair of products aka legs for attention. Also, in this story, I was doing it very subconsciously, so I cannot possibly have chosen it.

**As a sidebar to the reader, I was in excellent mental health condition as of the day of the above story. In fact, I’ve never been better. Decisions are easy, I love everyone around me and I am extremely grateful for everything in my life.

A formal medical mental health summary report would just say— MMMMuah!**

In the second leg of the race...

Another very leggy photo that has since been removed came up on myAbout me story on Medium. (The story has still not been completelylegsterminted, but that’s me trying to look like I don’t care).

In the first few minutes of publication, in what I first mistook to be fan-mail, was friendly advice on de-legging the story. This gentle reminder, I really couldn't stand up against.Fool me once, shame on you; fool me twice, something is wrong with me — right?

So I hurriedly deleted the picture and reposted my story, quickly checked the statistics, and noted that only 12 followers have seen them.

That Phone Call

You see, I’m somebody who, when my legs fail me, I will fight on my knees.

So I decided to take it up with at least one of my well-wishers to make it clear to him that sometimes, legs are just legs. Which one I called, really doesn’t matter to this story. But I called one with metal prep and determined to reason the devil out of that discussion.

I pulled up my sleeve, read some research papers, and went prepared for nothing short of a TED talk. In retrospect, it seems like a little much.

Unloading the freshly noted research points from the sticky note on my table, I went —

“You know, man, different cultures sexualize and stigmatize different parts of the body.

Induced need for modesty and consequences of guilt and horror shame women in different societies to identify with their body-selves more than real-self. ”

This part, I directly quoted from apaper, but I didn't tell him that.

“Also, the Chinese used to sexualize and cover-up feet, and in South India, it is the navel… In Germany, it is apparently the legs, but that is an idea from your head, not the objective reality. I don't want society’s approval, that is not why I posted this photo. It is because I am indifferent to its disapproval. That’s my point.

Aha!! snap, snap!”

To be fair, I thought of none of that while posting the picture. I was just gleaming about how the picture made me look nicer, especially with that filter which made everything look like somebody poured vanilla-coffee all over it some weeks ago.

Leg-islature of freeing the legs

The whole ordeal was like walking on bricks of Leg’os.(I promise that is the second-last leg pun).

I had to stand up to a bunch of internet and externet people, most of whom I really like, and prove that I don’t will them to stare at my legs.

The horror of imagining that the picture was up for judgment by my thousand something Facebook friends when the first requests for change were pouring in, was something I just had to live with.

Not only do I not want people to measure me by the number of leg pixels in the picture, but I also want the people I hang with to know me and talk to me, and not make sweeping assumptions about my intentions.

After all, whatever be their opinion on female legs, I like them genuinely (both females and legs). If I was to end every relation over opinion on political, social, leg, or family issues, I’d really doubt that anyone would talk to anyone in the world.

I still could not convince anyone that sometimes, legs are just legs.Leg them be. (The last pun, like I promised, also like I promised, here is the famed leg picture) .

Fin.

For more such features, meet meSruthi Korlakunta.
