title: Manifesting it
content:
Manifesting it

A Probably Funny Poem

They say that if we want it enough it’ll come true. So here’s a list. Universe, Do your thing, Manifest me these things, please:

A lottery that’ll set me free,

A puppy that’ll grow into a beast,

45% returns on every saved penny,

If I ever am in a viral video,

By choice or force,

Let it be shown that I’m quite saintly,

I’m not good at public apology.

Give me an idea for a booming business,

With large profits and no tax reduction,

So that I can stop going to the gym and get a liposuction.

Give me trust. Help me find Jesus,

Or Jehovah or Allah or Zeus,

Let him show up this morning,

And hand me my life’s purpose.

Give me all the benefits,

And zero responsibility,

Of a job, a business, a dog, a kitchen, and a car,

Come on, do it now, we’ve already come this far.

Give me love with only green flags,

that doesn't end on a sinking ship,

And let me die peacefully in my sleep,

A few days before his final leaf.

title: What if God Showed Up and Gave you Your Purpose?
content:
What if God Showed Up and Gave you Your Purpose?

To the nihilist in me: You’d burn the envelope.

Dear nihilist in me,

Yesterday was a good day. Admit it, it felt good to see people. It felt good to be a part of something. It felt good to see someone smile and dance.It is all a meaningless waste of time to fill life until you die,you might say.

Say later this week, God showed up at your doorstep in a polo t-shirt, white canvas shoes, and a big smile and handed you your bigger purpose in a manila envelope.

Being who you are, you ask him for proof that he is god, he exists, and that you aren’t dreaming. So he hands you a second envelope with a mathematical formula that proved that god exists, and how and where to look for him.

He can walk on water, annihilate the world with a flood or fire, responds to wishes made against small sums of donations to churches, temples, or other, and so on. All that is true, and here is the proof, he says, andPoof!gone.

You stand there, with undebatable information about your bigger purpose staring you in your face.

In truth, if such a thing as purpose existed, it could be anything on a scale of utterly disappointing to madly exciting. The odds, simply mathematically, are somewhere in between.

Now since you identify as a nihilist, you might be convinced that your birth is an accident that was inflicted upon you without permission, that you will die the same way too, and that everything in between is utter nonsense.

Imagine, to humor me, that god willed you into existence and wanted something important from you.

Your purpose, according to god, isn’t just to become bacteria fodder when you die. It is to be there for your friends and family, serve your employer faithfully, find a partner, make babies, and everything else that you don’t think highly about because it should be “meaningless”.

God’s envelope said,“Your purpose is to give your best to the life you are living. You will be handed an evaluation at the gates of heaven when you are finished. If you don’t do well, you will be handed it in your mouth while being cooked in hell’s kitchen.”

What would you do? More importantly,how would you feel?

Your excuse for pessimism was that the “objective truth” of the universe wants nothing from you. That you can fail disastrously and you can leave people hanging if you “don’t feel like it”. You can run from your problems because they are a result of your existential crisis which is natural to beings with consciousness.

All that is no longer true.

Are you excited about the prospect of a meaningful life with friends and family which you will soak in to the fullest?

..Or, will you buckle, realizing that the free will inside you doesn’t want any of that, wants to be free from purpose so that you can walk in any direction you want and change it as often as you wish?

If the latter is true, which it probably is, it is proof enough that nihilism is a concept YOU invented to fit YOUR will. You want the world to be meaningless. You want to believe that believers are stupid, that people who chase after goals are in some way, unintelligent compared to the mighty you.

Think about the Honeypot ants (image above), whose purpose is to hang to the ceiling with honey in their bellies for the colony until they drop dead. Their stomach is a pot and their legs stick to the ceiling. Their entire life’s purpose is to be a hanging jar.

That is how terrifying a clear purpose looks like. And it might even actually exist for you, as clearly as if you were a honeypot ant.

The next time you equate nihilism to pessimism, choose to not show up and to not try, scorn at happy believers, quell your excitement, remember that it is your choice, not the objective truth.

You have no purpose because you choose not to have one. If god handed you your purpose, you’d burn the envelope.

Best,

The part of me that is tired of my inner nihilist.

I am Sruthi. I write on medium on tech and life experiences, dotted with some short stories. Join medium with my referral link, let’s create together! It costs you 5$ a month and changed my life for the better.

title: The Most Valuable Feedback I Received
content:
The Most Valuable Feedback I Received

A minimalistic idea that is worth it’s few seconds

My reaction to feedback on my content has always been at either of these extremes:

My content was terrible, this feedback doesn’t even cover it.

My content was awesome, this person has no idea what he just heard/read.

About 50 articles, 10 talks, many feedbacks, adrenalin hits, reliefs, and disappointments later, I think back to one point of feedback I received in my first toastmasters evening, which stays with me till date. I tried to ignore it (I ignored it), I tried to tell myself that the listener can not always get what he likes, he just has to like what he gets. But turns out, in both speech and writing, it is an extremely valuable tip to have. (ThanksDeepak Nagaraj)

Going back to my articles on medium, toastmaster's speeches or anything that I communicate with my colleagues in a span of 30-minute meetings, this single feedback tip made my words more valuable for their count. I see it clearly reflected in the stats of my articles or even the response rate to my emails. It is this —Minimize the number of ideas in a piece of content.

Why keep your ideas to a minimum

As verbal creatures, us content creators love words, tonnes of them, And ideas, everything seems connected and important. But every content creator should bear in mind that the listener, as opposed to the speaker has spent a minimal amount of time thinking about the same topic. He has, as he is reading/listening to the piece only a fleeting fraction of a second to grasp the same thought which the author formulated over a period anywhere between some minutes to a lifetime.

Every listener/reader has allocated anywhere between some seconds to some minutes to a certain thought he or she encounters on the internet or in a toastmasters meeting without actively searching for it.

There is an upper limit to his or her mental capacity to grasp the idea behind that content. If you use these few minutes to overload your reader with 10 ideas that you think are allthe most important thingsyou want to say, you might lose his attention altogether. Instead, if you focus your words and energies on conveying one single thought and explain it in all it’s clarity. If you cannot find that one important thing,you may stretch your ideas and condense them to three but more is a no-no.

Examples for minimalistic literature

Trains of thought are fast, you have to stop at a station to let strangers in. That is exactly what you do when you stop at an idea and take your listeners into your train of thought.

A prime example for such minimalistic writing is author Malcolm Gladwell. Gladwell’s best selling books such as Outliers, David and Goliath convert extremely simple and intuitive ideas, spread over a very relaxed 300 pages. Take David and Goliath for example. The central idea of this is this:

There most definitely is a strength in every weaknesses.

Malcolm Gladwell cites examples and evidence from famous personalities and pop culture throughout the book, in various fields (From the top of my mind, IKEA’s unlikeable CEO) until the thought is embedded in your grey cells until the day you die. He is, and rightfully so, a constant best selling author on New York Times lists.

The moment one brings up the name of a book Gladwell wrote, it is very difficult to not be able to summarize what it was about. You cannot fail if you try, even after years of reading it! Think about Talking to strangers, Tipping point, David and Goliath, Outliers. The title of the book is the summary and the book is the supporting statement to the beautiful one or two-word summary-title.

I hope this was a valuable addition to your magic bag of writing or speaking skills. To reiterate, before you go on a writing spree, limit your ideas between one and three. ;)

title: What I learnt From Mindless Arguments: Cheatcodes to Win-Win
content:
What I learned From Mindless Arguments: Cheatcodes to Win-Win

Boss doesn’t give you a raise? Burned that bridge?

I recently had an altercation with a friend about an opinion I found hard to digest.

It probably wasn’t the smartest decision I made. After all, one’s perspective is a result of what side of the argument brain-washed you first.

Needless to say, I unwittingly burned that bridge at the end of the argument. This flustered me more than a little, because “I was rational”, in my head. I then evaluated every argument I had. Turns out unsurprisingly, there is a pattern to how these end.In most cases, as rational as we consider ourselves, emotion is the only gasoline that drives an argument.

Consequently, I consulted a bunch of literature to see what I was doing wrong (and right). Some smart people and great thinkers have figured this out for the rest of us already. There is a whole of material out there. My take-aways from this :

Minds are malleable but will resist the change unless it’s their idea (Or they believe it is).

People can be as nice to you as you are to them despite major differences in opinion.

Arguments don’t have to be a heated self-defeating emotional mess.

Here is what experts say about winning arguments

1. Stay Focused on “The Thing”

Ask yourself:What do you wish to achieve with this argument.Is it a feeling? (Moral correctness? Sense of validation? Correcting the “wronged”?), or is it a very specificthing? (A cooperative partner? A raise? A clean home?). Recognize that feelings (as opposed to things and actions) need more work on your side internally before taking it up with another person.

The second kind of goals though are worth working towards. All through your discussion,stay focused onThe thing.Don’t lose sight of your goal because you are emotionally distracted. This is undoubtedlyRule 1of convincing.

2. Brute force always fails

No matter how ridiculous is the argument, how unfair or how unacceptable to you as a person, DO NOT BRUTE FORCE a convincing gig. That’s the opposite of how to succeed. “But that’s stupid”, “You can’t do that”, “That’s wrong” are all conversation killers. Stay away from outright declarations of ridicule. Calm down, breathe, and practice your poker face.

3. Kill the “Whys”. Ask “Hows” and “Whats”

As FBI Director Christoph Voss says in his bookNever Split the Difference, “Why” is in many cases inherently accusatory. Try to stay on a factual basis and make a genuine effort in understanding your counter-part with explorative questions such as What, Where, When, and How. Their questions also give the listener a sense of control without resistance. It also puts you at an advantage of assimilating more and more information about the situation, based on which you can rationally build your arguments.

4. Watch your tone

Your tone is a perfect mirror for the roller coaster your emotion is on. Loudness sends a message of aggression and puts your counterpart in a fight or flight mode and makes him consequently leave the room. Are you talking hurriedly and explaining yourself repeatedly? Your interviewer smells something fishy. Practice your Polite-Office-Secretary voice. The Calmness of your voice will also spread to your rationale and arguments.

6. Mirror and voice the other person’s perspective

Occasionally, say literally what the other person just said, or the keywords in what he said. Then let yourmirrorsit in the room for a second. This however is a part of listening and not talking.

While you present your arguments, formulate them while acknowledging and repeating their perspective in your argument. Such an acknowledgment gives the listener a sense of being understood. Take care to see that you don’t “I” too much. It should be aboutthemin their ears.

Him:I am annoyed and confused. What is going on?

You:Annoyed and confused?

Him:Yes, I did everything right but you seem not to recognize my work at all!

You:It seems like you feel unappreciated although you did everything on your part.

This kind of “Mirror” and emotional label diffuses the situation.

Even using only one or two of these tricks seems to calm down a situation to work towards a goal that everyone in the room can live with. I strongly recommend trying it out yourself, if for nothing else but the sake of experiment, in case the future holds an upcoming argument for you. Diffuse with care!

title: How to Prevent Injury While Running
content:
How to Prevent Injury While Running

Start and never stop with these tips on safe running

Running is a very addictive sport. It is as engrossing as it is painful and time-consuming. I started running about 90 days ago and have some 270km under my belt. In this time and distance, I figured that if you want to keep running, the hardest parts are not the runs but the rest days.

It is easy to get lost in numbers and keep aiming for better mileage, better pace, or cadence. Here is one thing that will keep you from getting to the numbers you want — Your body. Turns out, calcium and muscle are great but not at all like their Gen-Z cousins — metal and silicone. If you want your body to run with you, you must learn to listen to it.

Here are some tips to help you run and recover so that you can stay on your goals and plan your distances and speed better. Under no circumstance run all 7 days a week.

1. Listen to your body (but not too much)

As an absolute beginner, running can make you want to gouge your arms and legs out of your ball-and-socket joints and hit the bed ASAP. But only until you discover that you can run your 5k or 10k entirely without stopping. When you see that you have a healthy pace and you have built up your stamina, by all means, go at your favorite speed.

But, the day after and maybe also another, and another — if your shins, joints, or other parts of your body hurt, don’t fiercely chase your miles and speed. Give it a rest. For your next run, your body should feel like it is starting fresh — no residual shin pain, no aching muscles, and no painful stride. Rest days are as important as your running days because your muscles need about 24–48 hours to recover based on your experience. If it is your joints or other bones that hurt, see a doctor.

2. Slow down

It might be tempting to run your best speed at every session. But after a night of, say, a hangover, a tough hike, a day at the swimming pool or the gym, your body might not yet be ready for the run. If you feel like you have recovered, but the body isn’t yet at its best form, run a slow one. Walk if needed. Or run-walk-run. Don’t push yourself over the edge at every run.

3. Eat

You might be running as a means to achieve your goal weight. If that sounds like you, you might be restricting calories aside from doing just your cardio (aka run). For smaller distances like 5k, you don’t need to alter your food intake dramatically. Don’t over-restrict calories such that your run starts with fatigue. Eat a reasonable portion of carb-rich food a few hours or the night before the run and start fresh.

4. Take your minerals

Adults have a prescribed requirement of Magnesium and Calcium to help recovery. Make sure you get the required dosage for your age, sex, and activity level to aid your recovery. My gym trainer told me that while Magnesium helps recovery while Calcium helps your body absorb magnesium. Take care to see that your diet has an adequate amount of these or consult a doctor for a good supplement.

5. Train your muscles too (Strength training)

A good core is helpful for a good run. Alongside just running on the road, use a couple of your non-running days to train your core and leg muscles so that they can handle the load of your high-impact running.

6. Get good shoes

You might be tempted to run in your old shoes or the shoes you always used to walk. But running needs good protection for your shins and knees. Make sure to get a good pair of running shoes with good shock absorption so that you can run safely.

7. Sleep

After you begin running, you will see quite clearly what sleep does to your body. It’s really like magic. You won’t wake up with the same body you slept in. It will feel new and refreshed, ready for a new run. But this works only as long as you get a good amount of good-quality sleep. So sleep well and wake up rested for a good run.

8. Have a few 100% rest days.

Some days are running days while others are for other forms of activity like Gym or swimming. It is important to have days of no sport at all — Days of full recovery. Consider piling up your gym and run days so that you fully rest on one day. Take at least a day a week completely off.

9. Stretch

Just before and just after your run, don’t forget to stretch. Spend about 10–13 seconds on each stretch pose and stay in position until your body is all oiled and geared up for the upcoming run. Stretch all parts of your body and not just the legs. If needed, you could walk for about 5 to 10 minutes before setting off on your run. Do this also AFTER your run and not just before.

Happy recovery and Happy running!

title: Fixing a Broken Ubuntu GUI
content:
Fixing a Broken Ubuntu GUI

Lessons learned from bad driver Installations

I switch between Windows and Flavors of Ubuntu a lot. I recently noticed that I haven’t used my Kubuntu PC in a while during which it gathered dust. I decided to bring it up to speed. Having had enough of Kubuntu, I switched toUbuntu 20.04, which wasn’t all so much a pain to install, you know… the GUI support, and all.

The trouble came afterward when the installation was suuuuper slow. In the process of fixing these errors, I went through the all-too-familiar NVIDIA, light-dm issues that I had to go through a couple of times as a Ubuntu newbie.

I decided to document it once and for all, for everybody suffering from new installations of Ubuntu like me.

SLOO…OOOWWWW Ubuntu?

This is probably because Ubuntu defaults to the XOrg drivers with a new Installation. You can see that in your Software updates application under “Additional Drivers”. If Xorg is selected (The third option in the screenshot), it’s probably not the best. (Used up the majority of CPU in my case).

In order to fix this, you now either have to:

Install Proprietary drivers of your graphics card (Mine was NVIDIA GeForce GT)

Find the latest stable version of drivers for your graphics card and install them

(You will know you have to do 2 when you try 1 and it fails(!)

If 1 worked for you — awesome, do a

sudo reboot

and you are good to go!

If your driver Installation failed and GUI broke down

If your GUI hung up for some reason, or the process didn’t finish successfully, switch back to Xorg and reinstalling stable drivers. If you don’t do this, your GUI gets all messed up on reboot.

In my case, the reboot failed. It took me to a blinking cursor on a blank screen like so:

In order to get out of this abyss, press ctrl + F1 (Or F2,3,4,5,6,7) until one of those works.

ctrl + F1

you will enter a regular command-line interface from there. Log in with your username and password. Then, execute these:

sudo apt-get purge lightdmsudo apt-get updatesudo apt-get install lightdmdpkg-reconfigure lightdmsudo reboot

This is because Ubuntu’s display is managed by lightdm package, which failed. You are purging it and reinstalling it to fix these issues.

When you reconfigure lightdm, you will be asked if you want to stay with lightdm or change to GDM3. I stayed with lightdm because I was more familiar (And a lot of ppl on the internet complained about gdm as a manager with NVIDIA drivers)

At this point, I was overjoyed because everything seemed to work and I came to my sign-in screen.

But while I typed my credentials, my computer decided to just keep asking me for my credentials again and again. I WAS STUCK IN A LOGIN LOOP!!

Note: If your dpkg reconfigure fails in this line,

dpkg-reconfigure lightdm

run this and you should be fine:

sudo dpkg --configure -a

The login loop and the way out

If you cannot log in to your account but the GUI is back, you have to fix your drivers from the command line (you broke them in step 1!!!)

So now, Press Ctrl+F3 to get back control over your command line. (with Alt+arrow keys, you can switch between screens: Login, command line, etc.). Login with your credentials.

Ctrl+F3

There are 2 possible sources of failure here:

.Xauthority permissions are messed up

NVIDIA drivers are messed up

In our case, we know it’s the drivers. If you want to check your Xauthority permissions, do this:

ls -lA

Now look for the line:

-rw — — — — 1 user user 79 Sep 3 19:56 .Xauthority

If instead of where your username has to be, you see:

-rw — — — — 1 root root 79 Sep 3 19:56 .Xauthority

You have to:

chown username:username .Xauthority

And you have your authority and you can log in!

If that wasn’t the problem in the first place, reinstall the drivers:

sudo add-apt-repository ppa:graphics-drivers/ppasudo apt update

Now look for what drivers are available:

I used the third-party free recommended driver. Install it (replace the lastyour-Nvidia-driver-herewith the actual name of it from the above result):

sudo apt install your-nvidia-driver-here

If this worked, not only is your GUI back but also you have better drivers for your graphics card hardware!

do a:

sudo reboot

This should do it!

My computer now works like a good regular computer and doesn’t take forever to startup. If you have had a similar problem, hope this helps!

title: Review: Salaar — Yet Another Caricature Telugu Movie.
content:
Review: Salaar — Yet Another Caricature Telugu Movie.

Why does it have to be like this?

I watched the newest Telugu Blockbuster Salaar in the Theater nearest to me in Hyderabad. That was only because I am too stingy to leave at halftime after paying that ticket price.

At the heart of it, Salaar is an ambitious plot. Think of it as a Bahubali meets Game of Thrones meets a random zombie apocalypse. Add in some context-less human rights abuse and rape so that the hero has something to do and someone to save.

If the whole point of the movie was to get some extremely stylish stills of Prabhas without actually revealing much of his face, then it has achieved the target. But it needn’t have been a movie, just an elaborate photo shoot with agonizing screams and cacophonous background music thrown in.

Centered around a fictional town called Khansaar, Salaar wants to tell a story of a heavily militarized feud for the throne spread across generations. However, what emerges from this attempt is a tangled mess that feels like the story was “made up as we go”.

The most annoying aspect of the film is certainly its unchanging, dreary color palette. From start to finish, the screen is doused in a sea of darkness that fails to capture the changing times or eras, a little like the lead Prabhas’ previous movie Adipurush.What are they trying to hide?

The performances by the cast are as lifeless as the palette itself. While Shruthi Hasan is a mere prop, The rest of the actors come off as forced and artificial. There’s absolutely no depth in their portrayals, leaving the audience disconnected from the characters’ plights. I was indifferent, unable to empathize with any of the characters’ journeys or emotions. Prabhas himself has all of 10 dialogues throughout the movie, mostly one-word sentences.

Jagapathi Babu, who deserved more screentime is the only saving grace of the otherwise dull acting. He carried his role with poise and gravity. The few minutes post-half-time when he enters the screen, are the only bearable moments in the movie if you aren't a Prabhas fan-boy.

The first half of the movie is almost disconnected from the meaty second half. It is an overly long commercial of what is to come, without actually having any part in the plot. I was left grasping at straws, attempting to piece together the fragmented narrative, ultimately leading to confusion and frustration rather than intrigue.

Where the film truly falters is in its gratuitous depiction of gore. The excessive violence lacks any meaningful context or emotional buildup, resulting in shock value without substance. The relentless bloodshed feels more like a desperate attempt to shock the audience rather than serve the story, leaving a taste of gratuitousness that lingers throughout the film.

The costumes and the stylish set-up of the movie are certainly eye-catching. But at times, this was too much, and without explanation. Characters sport inexplicable and distracting makeup choices that detract from their performances. A lot of times, a lot of coal has been rubbed in people's faces for no reason but visual drama.

Salaar was a painful watch. In principle, the epic tale it aims to portray is certainly a story worth telling, but director Neel has not told it, certainly not in this movie. Though it is being touted as a blockbuster, it is a very forgettable piece of cinema.

title: The truth about “making it”
content:
The Truth About “Making It”

“He worked hard,he made it and he deserves it!” — Sure, seems like a logical statement to make about a celebrity. When I heard someone say this to me one time, since I was the subject who made and deserved the“it”,it was actually strange to hear the words. Did I “make the IT”?

Then it struck me, Most of us grow up with the simplistic worldview offered by the anomaly-loving media :

“Nothing is impossible if you work hard”, “If you are unsuccessful, it is completely and entirely your fault”, “If you have a million dollars, you ARE THE BETTER man”, “winning is THE GOAL of life” “Working to the point of breaking is noble”.

Then it all made sense, the need to “market yourself” in every conversation, the proud 13 hour workdays despite the burn out, the need for generous use of superlatives in business, the “Everybody gets a trophy” upbringing that is today so popular — All those times when you are the larger subject of a task you do than the task itself.

The bone I have to pick is neither with the ambition towin and make it “big”nor with claims of being self-made. It is with the false impressions that are so generously fed by the media, based on which these goals are set. It is JUST NOT TRUE that you can entirely break out of your social, economic and cultural zones and be who ever you want to be. The fact that I have a better income and can afford more than my grandparents did has a lot to do with what happened with the Indian economy in the past 30 years than with how awesome my 6th grade scores were. In most cases, the best one can be is a representative statistic that lifts the bar graph by a nanometer. Of course, you can also tell the people the truth about anomalies, how they happen all the time and every generation has a hundred in billion anomalies that “make it”. But it needs to be worded more truthfully.

“Be good at what you do, because it gives you joy and motivation; Earn money, if that’s your thing, because of course it can be exchanged for goods and services”. This won’t sell, because it doesn’t have enough scandal in it. A guy that went to school, gets moderately good grades, gets a job he likes OK and explores life” obviously makes for a very boring movie and news article, but it is still a good way of living, ask the non-millionaires that are 99.1% of the population. Think about the side-effects of also glorifying the reality of life: Conversations with everyone will be less competitive, you can visit your relatives without having to boast or hear about most recent wins, That annoying person at work who wants to be the “Employee of the month” will tone down a bit and everyone will love their job a little more and be a little less over-worked.

Such a worldview is not to demotivate the winners and “go-getters”, but to make the world a place where more such moderate wins can happen to a larger number of people. However, it is important and urgent to consider the theory that “rags to riches” stories are not the norm but anomalies. Acknowledge, alongside your own efforts, everything else that contributed to your way up the ladder. Try and give others around you the same generosity you received when you messed up but didn’t suffer because someone had your back. Because no matter what they do, they may be limited by their state government, family, circumstances or a million other things that they have absolutely no control over.

title: 7 addictive features that tell users that apps don’t care
content:
7 addictive features that tell users that apps don’t care

Time to rethink them to show some respect.

On the face of it, every social media business has only our best interests in mind. They all want us to be rich, famous, and mentally healthy. Every now and then, we also get a survey about how you are feeling about the app, complete with smileys, custom videos, and balloon emojis.

Is that enough though? Here are some addictive features which hint that the app-makers don’t really care. There must definitely be ways of rethinking these features to not harm the user.

Email notifications without content

Why do the apps care enough to tell you via email that you have a notification, but not enough to give you the information without you having to click?

A great example is LinkedIn. “You have a message from the most interesting person in the world” — Says LinkedIn in your email inbox, social media section.

But it is impossible to know what they wrote without opening the app or the webpage first. The result — You end up spending another 10 minutes scrolling at things you don’t need to see, where you would have spent only a couple of seconds otherwise.

The apps know that your email is the center for all your curated and important information from all sources (If you are digitally clean, that is).

They leverage this fact to slide into your mailboxes and yet keep you scrolling.

Autoscroll on Insta explore

Ah, Instagram! my old friend.

Gone are those days when Instagram was the go-to app because it did not flood your newsfeed with racist comments your uncle left on that hairdresser’s page.

As if Instagram doesn’t have enough content flying through the web like shots from a machine gun, they introduced an auto-scroll in the explore pool which automatically takes you to the next video content under microseconds. You get literally 0 seconds of recuperation time at the end of a video.

That was to be your last video but now you are invested. Another hour's sleep down the drain.

Finding the delete/sign out button should be easier

For you to sign out of any of these apps, it is not a matter of seconds. In fact, it is not even worth your time. Because extremely unintuitively, the signout button is buried under settings, below the signature that looks like it means “This is the end of this menu”.

If it is your first time signing out, your mind will definitely be de-incentivized the next time you want to look for it. You will decide to just live with the app, whether you want it or not.

I want to uninstall not deactivate

With so much thought put into ease of installation and set-up, the option to uninstall is left alone to fend for itself. With my Android phone, I cannot uninstall Instagram or Facebook if I want to.

The best I can do is deactivate. Why though? Because you want your junkie baby to come back home in the evening. You know her, right Mr. Zuckerberg?.

I can understand that some essential functions such as telephone cannot be permanently uninstalled from a phone, but why is it alright for apps in entertainment to be impossible to uninstall?

Why should I retain the shell of a reminder that the Facebook icon becomes after being disabled?

Screentime should be automatic

After googling to find out under which of the 100 unintuitive permutations of settings I can find a setting that limits my screentime, I used the Instagram screen-time limitation function once.

It popped an alarm after 15 minutes, which was my set screentime. I slept well that day.

The next, however, I swiped it away (I don't remember doing this, it is possible that the alarm never showed up, but I give insta the benefit of the doubt). And Instagram let it be.

If my alarm clock did this kind of shoddy work, I’d be unemployed by now.

When they do make efforts to de-addict you, the apps should also mean it. Half-assing features to look like they care is not enough.

Pro-tip, Here is how you set insta screentime: Menu>Activity>Time tab>Set daily reminder

Notifications of old notifications

Finally something Medium is also guilty of. Apps like Medium and Linkedin take care to see that you have a flashing red circle or a green circle with a number inside it. Whether it is true or not.

If a notification was seen 11 hours ago, you don’t have to come back to the app to find it alive again, they know you have already seen it! So dear apps, please stop conditioning us like Pavlovian puppies!

In case I do have a notification that is fresh, Medium shows me that I have 5 because the last four notifications in the past 16 hours are technically “fresh” right? Wrong. Nobody ever got happier on finding that they were duped into tapping on a notification. Please stop doing this.

Gamification of my daily-life is a NO

Ok, here comes an unpopular opinion.

I like stats.

Having said that, I don’t need to see every tiny change in everything every morning. Once a week is more than enough frequency to update stats — on any platform. Actually, an option to choose update frequency would be awesome.

This is especially true of profile visits on Linkedin. What should one do with this information of profile visits on a daily basis? Tell me once a week, please.

It would be hard for me to let go of, but that way, I can focus on being creative and worry less about the numbers every living second of my day.

I can really get behind the Medium Weekly stats email report in this regard. Perfectly sums up what it should do.

If you are interested in articles on technology or social competence, connect with me,Sruthi Korlakunta.

Stay in touch throughLinkedInorMedium. Write me a note, it would be awesome to meet you!

title: I Built a Pro App in 60 Seconds: Use Google AI Studio to Vibe Code and Learn
content:
I Built a Pro App in 60 Seconds: Use Google AI Studio to Vibe Code and Learn

Imagine you have a high-stakes test coming up (ehm, I actually do), or none of the productivity apps are cutting it for you. Not everyone can build their own apps for every little thing, right?right?

WRONG!

Ten years ago, this would have cost you may be$15,000 (?)and three months of development. Last year, you probably went on ChatGpt and copy-pasted the code.

Yesterday, It took me two prompts in Google AI Studio.

My husband didn’t want to pay $10/month for a premium journaling app, so he prompted AI Studio to create a simple React app that saves his entries tolocalStorageand uses Gemini to give him a journal that he wants. It cost him nothing. It took him ten minutes. Here is how you do it, follow along.

AI Studio vs. Chat GPT and the costs involved

Google AI Studio is not a chatbot; Its your personal code-engineer. It’s a full-stack development environment that runs on Gemini 3 Flash. I didn’t just ask for a code to build my app, but for an app with a specific “Duolingo” style experience to pass my DMV. The AI doesn’t just give you some code to copy and paste into your environment, but creates an entire environment where it can run. Here is the system architecture I got :

index.tsx&App.tsx: The brains and layout of the app.

geminiService.ts: A dedicated service to fetch dynamic questions.

ReviewMode.tsx: A custom logic path for retaking missed questions.

When you sign up for the Google Cloud Free Trial, you receive $300 in credits valid for 91 days. That is about enough to get you started.

But why would they give you this “free lunch?”

Google is currently aggressive about winning the AI war. They are competing with AWS and Azure. By giving you $300, Google is trying to remove your barrier to entry, and who knows, your app might scale to professional and generate them business, instead of for their competition.

What does $300 actually get you?

The Gemini 3 Flash model (which I used for this DMV app) is optimized for high-volume, low-cost tasks.

$ 300 means technically 3 billion tokens ($0.10 per million input tokens, the price of Flash). That’s enough to build thousands of apps. There is no hard limit on how many “apps” you can generate. You are only limited by your Daily Quota.

Under the Free Tier, you can send upto 1500 requests a day, and 15 per minute. Even if I do exhaust my credits, this DMV app will cost me less than $2.00 per month for hosting and API calls.

Learning From Fully Written Code

With AI Studio, it is easy to look behind the curtains and learn how your code works, so that you can then look in the code and refine it specifically, maybe even with the help of Gemini.

The specific tech stack used here was:

React:React breaks your app into “Components” (like a button, a header, or a quiz question) so they can be reused.

Vite:It’s a tool that takes all your files and serves them to your browser at lightning speed.

Tailwind CSS:It’s a library of “shorthand” styles that tells the app exactly what colors, fonts, and spacing to use without needing a separate design file.

Theservices/geminiService.tsfile handles the "phone call" to Google’s AI. When you ask for a new set of DMV questions, this file sends the request and brings the answer back to your app.

You’ll notice most files end in ts or tsx.

.ts(TypeScript): Used for logic. This is for files that calculate things (like your "Probability of Passing" score) but don't show anything on the screen.

.tsx(TypeScript XML): Used for visuals. Any file that contains a button, a text box, or a layout ends in.tsx

Just look under the hood and get Gemini to explain the details to you!

Taking Your App “Home”

If you ever want to move your app out of Google AI Studio, you can download these files as a zip. To run them on your own computer, you would just need a free tool called VS Code and a small program called Node.js.

But for most personal apps, staying right inside the “Build” mode is the easiest way to keep experimenting for free.

title: Did ChatGPT Just Make Medium Obsolete?
content:
Did ChatGPT Just Make Medium Obsolete?

ChatGPT, a large language model developed by OpenAI, has the potential to revolutionize the way we create and consume written content on the internet.

But did it just kill all writers?

One platform that could potentially be impacted by this technology is Medium.com.

On the one hand, ChatGPT’s ability to generate human-like text could make it a valuable tool for writers and content creators on Medium.com. With ChatGPT, writers could quickly generate high-quality content, saving them time and effort. Additionally, ChatGPT’s natural language understanding abilities could help writers create more compelling and engaging content for their readers.

On the other hand, the rise of ChatGPT could also pose a threat to Medium.com’s business model. As more and more content is generated by AI, the value of human-written content may decrease.

The ability of ChatGPT to quickly generate large amounts of content could lead to an oversupply of content on the platform, making it more difficult for individual writers to stand out. What chance do you have against an AI that can tell (well..) nearly original stories that take you a day in under a minute?

But, Medium may be able to adapt…

Despite these potential challenges, Medium.com has the potential to adapt and evolve in response to the rise of ChatGPT. The platform could focus on highlighting and promoting the work of human writers, or it could incorporate ChatGPT into its own publishing tools to give writers an edge. How Medium can differentiate between AI and real writers is yet to be seen.

There are also additional Paraphrasing AI tools on the internet that can make it impossible to recognize text produced by ChatGPT.

Overall, it is uncertain whether ChatGPT will make Medium.com obsolete, but it is clear that the technology has the potential to significantly impact the way we create and consume written content. Medium.com and similar platforms will have to adapt to this change if they hope to remain relevant in the future.

Courtesy, Chat GPT ;)

title: Your Most Honest Piece of Writing
content:
Your Most Honest Piece of Writing

The story of this and every article

I have been writing for years now. In a book. It was my own sweet world where I was the best. (Of course, I was. I was the only person in it). Then came from nowhere the courage (or the naivety) to go public. Facebook, no, LinkedIn, no no, Medium!

The thing with a long blank sheet of medium, instead of the “What’s on your mind” bar on Facebook, is that you are quietly encouraged to say more than you are willing to.

As if that is not enough, there are detailed statistics on how awesome or awful or mediocre you are in the pool of millions. Imagine! Imagine pouring your heart out on the bright blue screen in the dark with wide eyes. And 100 million active readers saying “meh”!

There have been ups and downs. It ebbed and flowed. First, I laughed at the 2-cent revenue. Then I was delighted at 48-cent turnover! Then it rolled off into the 10s and gradually into 100s. I felt like Hemmingway. All social media platforms fell apart for me. The writing was my creation. A piece of my heart. Everything else was a facade.

I looked away from all the badly performing stuff, I saved some white space to write solely for myself and some dear friends. Like some toddler and his family admiring his expensively framed-kindergarten crayon disaster. I read my good articles again and again, with an almost narcissistic joy.

I wrote, I told myself that didn’t bother about the stats. But I still checked them. This couldn’t go on forever. It is not going to help to turn my head and look sideways at my stats while pretending to not look.

I cannot write for myselfandexpect everyone to like it at the same time. Just like I wouldn’t walk into a restaurant and eat the chef’s favorite matchstick soup, delicious as he might think it is. I made my choice. I want to be read. I want to be heard.

The sheer admitting of this “want” took courage. As if I had challenged the biggest and vilest of the school bullies to a duel — in plain sight! as if hoped to win, despite my small stature, in a situation that would, in a sitcom, play out to give the viewers an ironic comic relief.

As if an invisible “they” are watching me, rubbing their fists, waiting to revel at me fail. But what’s a life where you haven’t clenched your jaw and ground your teeth. If the most primal of my urges — to fight, has to be lived, what better way than to do it on paper?

Armed with newly found realization, a good long look in the mirror, and awareness of my willingness to fail, I want to write what I would be proud of having written when I look back down the line. There, I said it.

title: Why It is Important to Drop the Ball Once in a While
content:
Ladies, Learn to Drop the ball

Why women should learn to be imperfect

Have you ever walked into a meeting 10 minutes too late and felt the eyes on you, a slight look of contempt a the corner of your condescending boss’s mouth?

Have you ever missed a thick line on just one side of your excel cell, and discovered it too late, and silently screamed “aaaaaaaaaarg” ?

If you are the boss-woman who never has a hair amiss, whose every Powerpoint presentation is just a step away from permanent enlightenment, you might want to sit back down and ruminate over this article for a while.

Unfortunately for a lot of us every school course, every exam, and lecture focuses on making you a perfect human. There are hardly any places to learn how to come to terms with the forthcoming avalanche of imperfection that is life.

No, your trains are not going to arrive on time, no your math is not going to solve real-life problems, no you are not going to spell everything always correct (and neither is your newspaper).

Being spectacularly unprepared for the realities of life, you will then enter a work relationship with people with even less tolerance for others’ mistakes. Ergo, YOU happen. Entering rooms with doubtful eyes and stuttering through phone calls and going red at lines on excel sheets.

This mass-unpreparedness for dealing with imperfection is what makes it a precious skill. Stutter too much, people double-check your work just because it was you. Apologize too much, you’re out of the question for a promotion. Apologize too little, you’re an as*hole.

It’s not attending a meeting too late that is going to put you in a bad light — It is what you do afterward. Did you just completely lose it because “you made a bad impression”? or did you show acceptance for yourself and your ability to have stuff under control?

It is safe to assume at this point that what you want is not just a promotion, higher pay, or a bigger car. We all know it is deeper than that. Is it the need to be admired? is it to be the alpha in the room? or is it just to reassure yourself that you are in fact quite a sharp person (as opposed to what that middle school teacher screamed at your face about how you will never make it in life). Once you are faced with a “proof” of your perceived inadequacy that you so dread, it knocks you right out of your element.

Hear me out before you are terrified of having to face this over-humanizing of your super-human goals. None of the above things arebad, per se. Ifinsecurityis your Gas, who am I to say a Porsche cant run on it?

Step one to learn to be imperfect is to know your fuel.

Why are you doing what you're doing? You think a trained psychologist can tell you better, but no. You can tell you better. The simplest explanations are usually correct.

Identify your simple explanation and run with it. If you identify that you were made to feel inadequate because “you ran like a girl” and “counted like a girl” and “punched like a girl”, acknowledge this feeling of inadequacy. It may or may not go away, but the next time it walks in, you can say “Why, Good morning!” and go on with life.

Take care not to transfer the blame to society and others. Yes, they gave you an imperfect world to grow up in, but they had the same. Acknowledge your ability to grow beyond it.

Drop the ball, then pick it up with grace. Everybody has done it. I’m not asking you to screw up on purpose here. But once you do, realize that it is as normal as missing a comma in a sentence. Apologize, only if needed not if you felt like your heels are too loud, correct the correctable andmove on.

You just read another post from In Fitness And In Health:a health and fitness community dedicated to sharing knowledge, lessons, and suggestions to living happier, healthier lives.

title: Which Claude Model to Use?
content:
Which Claude Model to Use?

Between Opus, Sonnet and Haiku

tl;dr— Default to Sonnet. Use Haiku when you’re processing at scale and don’t need deep reasoning. Reach for Opus when the stakes are genuinely high. That’s it. Go build something.— — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — -

Let me save you the 45 minutes of reading documentation.

Anthropic’s Claude family has three main models: Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku. Everyone keeps asking which one to use, and the answer is, annoyingly , it depends. Here are the details.

Opus: The Big Brain You Rarely Need

Opus is the most capable model. It is slow, it is expensive, and imo, for most things, it is overkill.

Where it is worth the money: complex architectural planning, deep research documents, financial modeling, legal analysis, and such deep-thinking, multi-layered tasks.

Things where being wrong costs you real money or real time. If you’re asking Claude to review a 200-page contract or debug a distributed system design, Opus is the way to go.

The catch: the latency is noticeable, and the price per token is the highest in the family. So unless the task genuinely demands the best brain in the room, dont touch this.

Sonnet: Just Use This One

Sonnet is genuinely the right answer for about 80% of what you’ll throw at an LLM.

Interestingly, Claude 3.5 Sonnet actuallyoutperformsthe older Opus on many benchmarks. Faster, cheaper, and smarter than the previous generation’s top model.

Content pipelines, data analysis, complex automation workflows, code review, and copywriting — Sonnet handles this kind of work well. If you’re building something professional and you want it to feel smart without watching your API bill climb, go Sonnet.

Haiku: Fast, Cheap, and Perfectly Good for What It’s For

Haiku is built for volume and speed. Think near-instant responses on simple, repeated tasks.

Customer support bots that answer the same ten questions in different words, Content moderation, Pulling structured data out of messy text, Categorizing things, Translating. Anything where you need thousands of quick, accurate responses and complex reasoning would be wasted energy.

Haiku does not do nuance. It is not trying to. That’s fine — most tasks don’t need nuance, they need throughput.

One Trick Smart Developers Use

Claude recommends using a cascading approach: run Haiku first on incoming requests, and only escalate to Sonnet or Opus if the task flags as complex. You get the speed and cost efficiency of Haiku for 70% of your traffic, and the intelligence of a better model when it actually matters.

It’s the kind of thing that seems obvious in retrospect but nobody tells you until you’re already staring at an unexpectedly large invoice.

title: Earphones Under $200: What should you pick?
content:
Earphones Under $200: What should you pick?

Comparing earphones between 100 Eur. and 200 Eur — A review and an earphone rabbit hole journey.

At the beginning of the summer of 2023, I brought mySony WX-1000-XM3 Bin-ears to the workshop for repair.

I bought them 1.5 years ago during the 2021 Black Friday Sale for 100€ (down from then 169€) and LOVED them ever since. Even today, they cost 159€ in the market. It was an excellent buy for an unbeatable price.

As I handed them in for a potential repair, something told me this was goodbye. And so it was. After 2 weeks, I was told they were unrepairable.

Thus began my search for perfect earphones for the upcoming beautiful Bavarian Summer. It was going to be flowers, blue skies, Alps and excellent music!

What should I pick next?

This was an important decision. I want the best price-quality balance. I wanted True Wireless, Active Noise Cancelling earphones. I was most keen on looks and a comfortable fit. I didn’t begin the search with excellent sound quality in mind.

A quick optical scan showed me JBL Endurance Peak 3 has the most practical design among the sport style in-ears. I have memories of my Sony’s slipping out when I jogged, so I wanted to give the behind-the-ear hook design a shot. I picked them up for about 100 €.

JBL Endurance Peak 3

In the beginning, I loved them. The ear hook really stuck! But it did come with the drawback that the music wasn’t as in-ear-y as I thought it would be. I remember trying a friend’s AirPods once. The experience was out-of-the-world level of immersive.

Slowly the JBL voiceovers started to annoy me. Every notification is read out clearly and loud. Every time I change a setting or turn off ambient noise, JBL tells me. No beep-beep boop-boop, but a lady reading out every notification interrupting my music all the time!

What’s more, when I turned off the ambient sound it said “Ambient Sound Control Off". It drove me crazy. Because “sound control off" means ambient sound was ON. Not OFF. “Ambient Sound off" was the right way to say it. I also got into a Twitter argument with JBL on this.

Though the hook was practical for sports, casually using them with long curly hair was a messy tangle.

Eventually, I returned the JBL Endurance Peak 3s. What next?

The Search: Airpods, Huawei Free buds, and Galaxy Buds

I decided to spend another 50€ this time and look for good earphones with better sound. Trying Airpods Pro spoilt me.

I could of course get the AirPods themselves, but it didn’t appeal to me to shell out nearly 300€ a pair, especially when I had no other i-devices. Plus, according to reviews, they were not holding well during sports. Replacement buds in case of repair were 90€ each (!). Buying an older, cheaper generation while longing for the shiny new ones in the glass window felt like a “no". No apple for me now, no thanks.

Huawei Free Buds Pro 2

The consultant in the shop told me that his Huawei Freebuds at 160 Eur. were as good as, maybe even better, than AirPods. This was a near-perfect find if they were as good as he promised.

I really would have gone home with them, but I didn't — They were all sold out. I didn't want to order online and wait. So I let it be. I wanted to be able to return them (in case I don't like them) without going to the post office (it is a hassle here in Germany), and I wanted to take a pair home with me right then.

Sony WF-1000XM4

As I looked on, I checked out the ultra-sexy-looking Sony WF-1000XM4s. I found these to be undoubtedly the most good-looking earphones in the lot. Sony really has the best aesthetics.

When I looked through the videos of the Sony WF-1000XM4, I found that like my old Sonys, these were a bit on the bulkier side. They protrude quite a bit from the ears and are a bit bigger than I like. Plus, I already did have Sony for quite a while, with an extremely similar design, so why not something else?

Also sold out was Bose Quiet Comfort, another brand I had my eyes on. But they are rather old-fashioned in their looks and are slightly over the 200 Eur. Mark.

It came down to this — Pixel Buds Pro or Galaxy Buds 2 Pro

All things considered, two models were readily available, had good design and given that I have a Google Pixel phone, very enticing.

The first was the Pixel Buds Pro (170 Eur.) and the other was, Galaxy Buds 2 Pro (175 Eur.)

I picked up the Pixel Buds Pro, to try and see if it meets the mark.

With the PBs, google definitely did the design right. My fog-colored buds look incredibly sleek and elegant in my ears. Unlike EVERY OTHER earphone model on this or any other list, I hardly feel like I have them on. They look quite modern and simple.

They are super light and feel like nothing in the ears. Their sound does not disappoint. Noise cancellation is excellent, and the sound quality too. The soft click magnetic closing of the Pixel Buds case is just…satisfying. They nailed the shape of the case too. It feels awesome in my hands and slides into my smallest pockets.

But, the curious heart wants to try. I decided I want to test the Galaxy Buds too, before I settle down. I borrowed a pair from a friend.

The one thing that they did an excellent job of, was the fit. The first few times, I needed a mirror to see if my Pixel Buds are sitting right. When I offered them to a friend, he had worn them funny too. Pixel Buds aren’t super intuitive in their fit. But the Galaxy Buds are!

The sound quality felt different, but not better or worse than the Pixel buds. I guess Pixel Buds, like photos on a pixel phone, are true to “natural” tones while Samsung makes certain areas of the sound louder(like they do with their photos?)

All said and done, I still liked how Pixel Buds look and sound so amazing. The Samsung case, though compact and cute, was not comparable to the awesome egg-shaped Google case.

Galaxy buds are a tad bit bulkier and feel like they are too large for my ear. They remind me of the time when my mother used to shove cotton balls into my ears during Indian winters. Plus, the local electronics market had a different price for every color of the Galaxy Buds, which was, well, weird. The black ones were marked down.

In all, I am sticking with my Pixel Buds Pro.

After using them for a month now, I also put them in right, and they feel awesome. I still wish they fit a little more tightly, for example when I take the e-scooter on the bumpy European stone roads.

I am, in the meanwhile, not only happy with them but quite love them. They make me want to walk more and longer and feel super cute in my palms.

Looks like I am going to associate summer with the Pixel Buds in the future!

title: What Makes a Good Dashboarding Tool
content:
What Makes a Good Dashboarding Tool

Use this article to get your employer to pay for a Dashboarding solution, and not stick to excel.

TLDR: Here is a Table of Contents for this article. Feel free to jump to the right section. For full impact, feel free to begin at the beginning.

Table of Contents:−Why not excel?−Choosing a Dashboard Provider−−1. Your dashboards should Customize and scale−−2. Your dashboards should be Intelligent, interactive, and easy to use.−−3. Dashboards should be usable also by employees who are not data experts.−−4. You should be able to Extract, Load, and Transform (ELT) data from multiple channels.

Dashboards are literally the only way to turn vast amounts of data into revenue. If your business is still on excel, it is time to buckle up and pay for a good Dashboarding tool subscription.

Excel dashboards and charts tend to have much smaller databases than most modern solutions. Refreshing data to the newest available data is cumbersome and limited with excel. The number of charts available in excel is not large enough, and the graphics are still from the 90s.

Most Modern Dashboarding solutions relieve you from these problems. Here is an article hoping to convince you to move to a dashboarding solution from excel without throwing in any names.

What are dashboards? Why not excel?

Dashboards are a condensed representation of data. No, excel charts are not “good enough”.

When you convert your data into an intuitive graphic, you can Identify trends and anomalies, forecast future demands, look at sum total of parameters (for example hours worked or sales made), and compare them to values of previous years or months.

Dashboards are the most in-demand business intelligence software solutions. Today, they are not limited to decision-makers and executives.

If you build good dashboards, your insights will be available to employees at all levels, harnessing their capacity to the fullest and improving your company’s productivity. If you are stuck with excel, good luck to you and your company.

Choosing a Dashboard Provider

1. Your dashboards should Customize and scale

When you choose a dashboarding solution, the most important parameters to consider are its reach, longevity, and scalability.

Your dashboard should be able to integrate into your business’sexisting appsand in-house screens.

It should be scalable in enormous magnitudes. You can publish them to thousands of users without affecting the speed and quality, be it employees or customers.

You can limit the type of access each employee or customer has to your dashboard by allowing everyone to receive only the information they need.

2. Your dashboards should be Intelligent

Modern dashboard solutions also include insights powered by Artificial intelligence. I am yet to find a good AI integration in dashboards that actually works but I’m certain that it will change very soon.

With AI, your dashboard should help you predict the future based on data you collect from previous records. Mere showing the current results may not be enough.

You should be able to perform what-if analyses by constructing scenarios and planning in advance based on simulated outcomes.

Intelligent Dashboards also provide in-built anomaly detection, which would enable one to spot, reason, and react to anomalies faster than ever before.

3. Dashboards should be usable also by employees who are not data experts.

In the traditional method of obtaining business intelligence, companies rely on teams of experts who analyze the data over many weeks and deliver useful insights. A good dashboarding tool should put these people out of business.

Once built, intelligent dashboards should make themselves easy to understand.

If you can integrate Natural Language Querying into your dashboard and ask questions like you would perform a google search or ask an expert, using nothing but plain English, it is future-safe.

4. You should be able to Extract, Load, and Transform (ELT) data from multiple channels.

In an ideal dashboarding tool, you should be able to load and save data in a matter of a few minutes.

That is a super quick view of everything your dashboarding solution must be able to do. time to move to a good dashboarding solution from Excel.

title: People always talk when things are not all right.
content:
People always talk when things are not all right. I think there should also be days when people talk when things ARE going well.

Of course it *should be* obvious that men and women are equal, that a boss cares for his employees’ welfare, or that you should be there for your family.

But *obvious* is an idea, a result of a deep sense of entitlement, a hope for the distant future.

For now, we should make sure to leave an explicit word of thanks when things are doing just fine.

Thanks to my employer for keeping it together during the pandemic. Thanks to my boss for asking me if everything is fine. Thanks to my mother for reading my blog posts and giving me feedback. Thanks to my friends for calling me and thinking about me.

Little things make up most of my life. Thanks

title: Swim or Run? What is the better Cardio?
content:
Swim or Run? What is the better Cardio?

Swimming and running are two popular forms of cardio exercise that offer a range of benefits for overall health and fitness. I love both (tbh, I like swimming way more). On the occasion of many ambitious New Years resolutions, let's compare!

Both running and swimming are great ways to get your heart rate up and improve cardiovascular endurance, but there are some key differences between the two.

Impact on the body

One of the main differences between swimming and running is the impact on the body. Running is a weight-bearing activity, which means that it puts pressure on the joints and bones. This can be beneficial for building bone density and strength, but it also means that running can be harder on the body.

Swimming, on the other hand, is a low-impact exercise that is less likely to cause injury. Which is my fav reason to swim.

Another difference between swimming and running is the muscles that are used. Running primarily works the legs, while swimming works the entire body. Because swimming involves the use of the arms, shoulders, back, and core muscles, it can be a more well-rounded workout.

Swimming is more intense.

Running is mostly done on land (and air), and swimming is done in water. Water provides natural resistance, which can make swimming a more intense workout. Additionally, the buoyancy of the water can also help to alleviate pressure on the joints.

Swimming burns more

When it comes to calorie burning, swimming can burn slightly more calories than running, depending on the intensity and duration of the workout. However, running can be more effective for weight loss, as it is a higher-impact activity that can help to boost metabolism and burn more calories overall.

Swimming and running also offer different mental benefits. Running can be a great stress reliever, as the rhythmic motion and outdoor environment can help to clear the mind and promote relaxation. Swimming, on the other hand, requires focus and concentration, which can help to improve mental focus and clarity.

But running is easy.

Running is relatively easy to do, as all you need is a pair of running shoes and a place to run. On the other hand, swimming requires access to a pool, which may not be as easily accessible for everyone.

Swimming and running are both great forms of cardio exercise that offer a range of benefits for overall health and fitness. However, they do have some key differences, such as the impact on the body, the muscles used, the environment, and the mental benefits.

If you are looking for a low-impact, full-body workout that is easy on the joints, swimming may be the best option. If you are looking for a higher-impact workout that is great for weight loss and building bone density, running may be the way to go.

title: Why I Won’t Write My Memoir Anymore
content:
Why I Won’t Write My Memoir Anymore

A surprising price to pay for good mental health.

The drama

In a weird twist, life was so much cooler when I was regularly sad.

Everything was dramatic. Everyday was full of action because I actively dodged everything that came at me. Nothing “just happened.” It all “happened to me”.

Back when life was a Shakespearean tragedy, I lacked purpose but I hoped to find it soon.“Maybe after my first job”, “Maybe after I marry”, “maybe when I move to a different part of the country”. Purpose of lifewas just around the corner, I only had to look hard enough.

Had I written a memoir back then, it would have made a bestseller. To tell a gut-wrenching, heartwarming action-packed story you have to believe it. You cannot make that up, not entirely. I am half certain that JK Rowling tried to run herself into a pillar.

I had bucket loads of empathy. Nothing anyone did was bad enough in my eyes.“Poor wife-beater must have suffered some blows when he was ten. That poor child!”Empathy makes for great stories too. Look at the bible. It is literally the world’s most sold book ever.

The movie’s over

Maybe overnight, over a week, or a month. I can’t be sure, but calm settled in. It was over. I wasn't sad anymore.

Things straightened themselves out. There was no “end boss” to clear every day before I moved on to the next level. The princess was not in another castle.

I opened the doors to the world, sure. But not like a puppy waiting for its man. I felt more like the automatic door at the supermarket which doesn’t care if it is a man or a mindless piece of plastic that came into its field.

I didn't care. Depression was over.

I remember one day I was riding my bike to go somewhere I absolutely didn’t want to. I would have literally traded a hundred dollars to stay home that day.

Then something strange happened. Tears flowed out of my face but there was no sadness in them. IknewI didn’t like it, my body was literally pushing saltwater through my eyes in the hope to escape. But I kind of didn’tfeelanything.

What I saw, I wouldn’t call it light at the end of the tunnel, but a bright neon sign highlighting the nonsense and meaninglessness attached to everything.

Out of the cinemas into the world without a memoir

People scream angrily at each other. A friend has a burn-out at work. Someone is deeply unhappy with the way things are. I didn’t get any of that.

Maybe, I discovered my set of ethics and drew a line around my duties to the world. Maybe, I justwasfor a while, without evaluating whether it is good. That was it. Everything beyond was really hard to care about.

What brought me peace was the utter complexity of everything and its clear unknowability. You just can’t know everything.Actually, you cannot knowanything!The most perplexing was an absence of theWhy.There is no good answer.

Somewhere down that road, I started to write. The more I wrote, the more I read. The more I read, the less I knew, the less there was to say. I searched for a memoir in me, there was none. Things are as they are, what is there to write about?

Though it killed my dramatic memoir, not caring has its perks. Decisions become at leastmostlylogical, ergo easier.Knowingbecomes your poison,But why?Because why theHnot.

I have nobody to blame. I don’t have a deep tragedy in my perspective. Things are as they are, and I am ok with that.

I amSruthi Korlakunta, I write here onMedium.

In my day job, I am a Data Analyst for Audi. During and after work, I vigorously explore the world and my mind for the truth. I’d be happy to connect!

title: Pretty
content:
Poem: Unpretty

When I go out with hair on my arms, ill-matched socks, and a dress one size too small, I feel eyeballs that wish I were gone.

I stutter, I stammer — not for the lack of ideas or words to say. For the lack of acceptance, a road on which my ideas can walk.

When I am the perfect size and shape, with freshly bought clothes and a skirt that just fits, they love me and want to have me like I was a sock with a picture sewn on it.

I don't stutter, I don't stammer. I say more words that are only partially me.

I feel like a farce.Is this really me? Isn't the truth always less than perfect and not dressed up?

It is all me. I don’t see it, but it really is all me.

Why comes joy from a thing of beauty? Shouldn’t it be a thing of truth?
