title: Facade
content:
Facade

One moment you feel stronger than ever before. In another, speculation topples the tower of logic that holds your thought, deed, and self.

Like winter breeze behind an avalanche, imaginary chances crumble your reality. Is this normal or are you just a facade?

There is a now. Stable, concrete, real as it can be. Alive in all its truth and nakedness — and yet, hard to believe.

The slippery now. Threatens to fall and splash into its components, never to be recognized as whole again. It might never be this beautiful again.

If only you had the magic combination of choices to keep your now forever whole. Before you make the frantic run for the correct combination, the now is gone. As if it has never been.

The tomorrow, however, the mirage of a fair maiden, Tantalizingly concrete, deceivingly controllable, looking like everything it is not.

Tomorrow is a game of chance, rigged against you like a cruel claw machine. So that you never win the prize of now.

To live unwillingly in the now, watch choices pass by, reaching out and settling down, wondering if this time you will reach for the correct tomorrow, or if you should even reach, is the evil sport of being alive.

title: Password Protect All your Excels At Once with this Easy VBA Script
content:
Password Protect All your Excels At Once with this Easy VBA Script

Here is a no-nonsense guide to do what is in the title. I tried multiple things with win32 in python and openpyxl workbook protect. They both wouldn't work.

Win32 gives me an error because it fails to recognize files in the path. openpyxl does not protect the entire workbook. A VBA Quickfix byJustin Hamptonworked for me. (Turns out he didn't write it either, but took it from another guy).

Here it is :

Open Excel outside of where your files-to-be-protected are located.

Open a new VBA Module and paste the following script.

Change the path in the following script to the location of your files to be protected:

PublicSubaddPassword()DimFSOAsObjectDimfolderAsObject, subfolderAsObjectDimwbAsObject'create a FileSystemObjectSetFSO = CreateObject("Scripting.FileSystemObject")'update the path where the files are saved belowfolderPath ="Paste\Your\Path\Here\"Setfolder = FSO.GetFolder(folderPath)WithApplication.DisplayAlerts =False.ScreenUpdating =False.EnableEvents =False.AskToUpdateLinks =FalseEndWithForEachwbInfolder.Files'it works on formats "xls", "xlsx" and "xlsm"IfRight(wb.Name,3) ="xls"OrRight(wb.Name,4) ="xlsx"OrRight(wb.Name,4) ="xlsm"ThenSetmasterWB = Workbooks.Open(wb)'update "yourpassword" to the password you would like to use, belowActiveWorkbook.SaveAs Filename:=Application.ActiveWorkbook.FullName, Password:="yourpassword"ActiveWorkbook.CloseTrueEndIfNextForEachsubfolderInfolder.SubFoldersForEachwbInsubfolder.FilesIfRight(wb.Name,3) ="xls"OrRight(wb.Name,4) ="xlsx"OrRight(wb.Name,4) ="xlsm"ThenSetmasterWB = Workbooks.Open(wb)'update "yourpassword" to the password you would like to use, belowActiveWorkbook.SaveAs Filename:=Application.ActiveWorkbook.FullName, Password:="yourpassword"ActiveWorkbook.CloseTrueEndIfNextNextWithApplication.DisplayAlerts =True.ScreenUpdating =True.EnableEvents =True.AskToUpdateLinks =TrueEndWithEndSub

3. Run the script and wait until all files are protected.

4. Files are protected.

ta-da! Thank you Justin!

title: Python StdOuts: Don’t Print, Log!
content:
Tips and Tricks

Python StdOuts: Don’t Print, Log!

A pythonic way to display status messages

Often on Python, especially as a beginner, you might print( ) a variable in order to see what is happening in your program. It is possible if you rely on too many print statements throughout your program you will face the nightmare of having to comment them all out towards the end. A much more pythonic way to see what the program is doing is logging. You can then limit your prints to command line outputs that the end-user wants to see.

Why log?

Logging is a comfortable tool to see the status of the program in the development phase. It is ideal if:

You want to differentiate between debug output and program output

You don’t want irrelevant stdout in your final product

You would like to disable all stdout after development and test

You want to save your program execution history to a file with meta information like time and more detailed debug information.

How to log?

You log in python using the logging module.

import logging

The logging module provides some default states of logging various status messages in your program. The default levels areDEBUG,INFO,WARNING,ERROR, andCRITICAL.

If you execute the following program:

import loggingimport ossavepath = ‘path/to/save/at/’

if not os.path.exists(savepath):logging.warning(‘Warning! The savepath provided doesn\’t exist!''Saving at current directory ‘)savepath = os.getcwd()logging.info(‘savepath reset at %s’,str(savepath))

else:logging.info('Savepath provided is correct,''saving at %s’,str(savepath))

The path in the example Doesn't exist. You will see the following output:

WARNING:root:warning! The savepath provided doesn’t exist!Saving at current directory

The new current save path information, that is logging.info is not displayed.

This is because the default level of severity of output is “warning”. The order of severity is as follows:

DEBUG

INFO

WARNING(This is default)

ERROR

CRITICAL

If you want to see outputs of a lower severity level, you have to explicitly set them in the logging configuration. Start a new interpreter after this setting, else it won’t work.

logging.basicConfig(level = logging.DEBUG)

Now, everything will be printed out, starting from level debug.

output:

WARNING:root:warning! The savepath provided doesn’t exist! Saving at current directory

INFO:root:savepath reset at path/to/current/directory

That is awesome! But for a decent log, we perhaps want to see more information and make it available in a separate file. This can be set using the format and filename in the configuration. Add the desired configuration at the beginning of your file:

logging.basicConfig(filename=’logfilename.log’,level =logging.DEBUG,format=’%(asctime)s %(message)s’,datefmt=’%d/%m/%Y %I:%M:%S %p’)

This logs your information to logfilename.log along with timestamps.

13/06/2020 05:14:47 PM warning! The savepath provided doesn’t exist!Saving at current directory13/06/2020 05:14:47 PM savepath reset at current/working/directory

The next time you run the file, logs get appended to the textfile, so that you have a historic record of your logs in one place. You can also change this and create a new text file each time by adding this to your logging.basicConfig

filemode='w'

You can disable all logging centrally by adding the following to your script, without having to comment each of the messages out:

logger = logging.getLogger()logger.disabled = True

Alternatively, you can choose to change the level of output needed to be written to the log file to only enable warning and critical error messages to the end user.

That was a primer to logging on python. Logging offers much more flexibility than described above (Handlers, filters, changing the default log levels, etc) But this should be enough to start using basic logging and do away with print statements.

title: The story of a monkey
content:
50 Words

The story of a monkey

But mostly about a coconut

A monkey saw coconut.

The monkey wanted coconut.

The monkey struggled and took coconut.

Once he had the coconut, monkey didn’t know what to do with it.

So monkey destroyed the coconut and then found out that it used to be sweet inside.

Monkey = UsCoconut = Democracy

title: Will Your Marriage Work?
content:
Will Your Marriage Work?

and is love good enough?

I’m exactly the kind of person who lies awake at 2 am wondering if my husband were bankrupt and sick, would I stick with him. Is love enough?

Last night, I followed this Pandora’s rabbit hole of snakes thought-experiment. Exhibit A : Once, my friend and I were waiting for a bus for about 30 minutes. A minute before the bus arrived, she realized she had to head back to the house to fetch something. Obviously, the bus arrived. I had 3 seconds to decide if A. I could get on it and let her take the next one or B. Wait for her and stay out in snow for another half hour.

That day haunts me to the end of times. She took the next bus and came to me an hour later of course, but the look on her face as I was waving to her from the bus window — An embarrassment to my soul that will stick with me for 7 lives.

Like all mortals I play out scenarios in my head. In this lala land, I always do the right thing and emerge a hero. Real life often puts me in a tough spot and gives me split seconds to decide. Only yesterday, I cheated on an eye-test. In retrospect I could have hardly predicted my own behavior if I knew what was coming.

My soliloquy so far probably wasn’t promising, especially if the reader is my husband. But there’s more.

Specimen B: I once had this supervisor at work who treated me kindly, let me carry out my research despite piling evidence they were going to a digital landfill. She also helped me out with novel methods of thinking through this and helped me survive what could have been a disastrous waste of a semester. Brace yourself for embarrassment #2.

On my last day at the facility I went to say good bye to her. At this point I was brimming with admiration for the lady and had as good as sworn my allegiance like Brienne of Tarth. I said goodbye to her, and lingered a moment too long with the handshake and nearly a tear fell from the corner of my eye. Though it was actually a relieved-researcher tear, to the onlooker it was anything but. The onlooker happened to be her mortified husband who, I’m pretty certain thought I fell in love with his wife. I could read the embarrassment on her face like it was plain text.

So I AM capable of genuine emotion. But when the genuine emotion is out in the cold for an hour, there is a good chance that it starts taking the bus alone.

Oh my God, this isn’t good news.

Yet. this isn’t good news YET.

Every time my husband holds me and tells me how much he loves and values me, I feel a sense of fear that I might not hold out my end of the bargain when the time comes. And then I remember, he needs something healthy to eat for lunch tomorrow and I have time to prepare a healthy lunch. So I put these thought experiments to a side and go do it.

Do I do it for love? No.May be?but it makes me happy if he does something healthy. Kind of like, I’m a relay race runner with a loose shoe and a sprained ankle, but if he is fast enough, we both win. It is a bit selfish, but it also takes an inherent trust that I cannot just manufacture for anybody and didn’t “choose” to have for him. At least, it’s selfish and still brings us both forward.

Or maybe it’s much easier to focus on his cholesterol levels than my own deep-seated fears about the future. Idk.

When I hear snide comments implied at him or his kin I want to defend him. Love? No.Maybe?because it makes me feel like a hero for doing the right thing (another selfish reason), or maybe, I finally feel like I have a family that I built with my hands and by choice and now it’s mine to defend (wait, that was another selfish reason that has nothing to do with him).

Whichever combinations of loyalty, competitive spirit, selfishness, or just spite against the rest of the world will come to my rescue when the judgement day comes, I do not know at the moment. If my husband were to be bankrupt and sick, I’m rolling the dice with a good chance I will be selfish and stick with him. As it appears, none of the things we do are calculated and premeditated anyway. The silver lining is of course that we are all selfish, and good things come to selfish people who keep their teams.

title: The Hero’s Journey: A Storytelling Format Making Viral Plots Since Star Wars
content:
The Hero’s Journey: A Storytelling Format Making Viral Plots Since Star Wars

Created by Narratologist Joseph Campbell

A good story keeps us sane. The story we tell of our life is nothing but a coherent story formed in hindsight. We have an inherent need to relate effects with causes. Stories are children of this need.

The Science of Story Telling: The Hero’s Journey

Given its immense importance in society, storytelling has evolved into an art and science over the years.What makes some stories stand apart and shine? Why do we get the end of books and movies?

The field of study that deals with these questions is called Narratology. Multiple theories exist about the best way to tell stories. Almost all of them provide a backbone your stories. While following the skeleton, the onus on the writer is to put the muscle and meat on the Narrative using:

Characters

Emotions

Relatability

Explaining Cause and Effect relation while conveying a message

One such attempt at formalizing the art of storytelling is The Hero’s Journey.It is a theory popularized by Joseph Campbell in the bookThe Hero with a Thousand Faces, influenced heavily by the teachings of Carl Jung.

Joseph Campbell described the skeleton of a narrative of a hero as follows:

A hero ventures forth from the world of common day into a region of supernatural wonder: fabulous forces are there encountered and a decisive victory is won: the hero comes back from this mysterious adventure with the power to bestow boons on his fellow man.

The Hero’s Journey is the Narrative form that influenced the Star Wars saga by George Lucas. According to this theory, every story, movie, or book takes the protagonist through 7 major phases. (The original Campbell structure has 17 stages):

Everyday Life

Call to Adventure

Accepting the Call

Challenges/Action

Death and Resurrection

Transformation and Atonement

Return

For a perfect blend of all elements, Campbell suggests the following timeline.

The first 10% of a good story comprises of the “is” state of life of the Hero. That is, if your story is of 100 minutes, the first 10 minutes shows the everyday life of the protagonist.

That is when something out of ordinary happens, a call to adventure. The Hero decides to answer this call to adventure wittingly or unwittingly.

At a little over 20% of the time or length of the story is when the meaty part begins — The various challenges your hero faces and adventures he experiences on the way.

At half-time is where the Hero hits the abyss— Be it emotional, physical, or otherwise.

The second half of the story is the saga of death and resurrection, emerging from the abyss. This goes on until about the three-quarter point of the story. This is the phase where a nugget of wisdom or a major realization changes the hero and raises him from the nadir.

At about 75% of the story timeline, Victory nods and the hero lives.

Then appears a transformed Hero, stronger than before, having lived through it all. Here begins the journey back, relaxing the emotional high. The end is a refreshing look at the “new and transformed” normal.

Picture any of your favorite stories and note this structure in them. Here is an example, The ClassicMemoirs of a Geisha (Spoilers ahead).

Applying the science of Storytelling to the book Memoirs of Geisha

Everyday Life:A poor little Chiyo lives in an idyllic Japanese village

Call to Adventure:Her Mother dies. She thinks she is going to be “adopted” by a rich factory manager and is delighted.

Accepting the Call:Instead,she is transported to Tokyo where she realizes she has been sold to a Geisha House,Okiya.

Challenges/Action:She is mistreated, and realizes that her sister has been sold to a brothel. She makes multiple attempts at escaping.

Death and Resurrection:Her attempts to escape injure her. she is condemned to be a servant girl to a vile woman instead of becoming a coveted Geisha. A chance meeting with the Chairman and an act of kindness causes her to fall in love and she is determined to become a Geisha. A surprising chance emerges.

Transformation and Atonement:In years, she becomes the most successful Geisha Tokyo has seen and redeems her self-esteem.

Return:She finds love with the chairman and in light of World War II, Emigrates to the United States to continue to live with the chairman as a free woman.

Regardless of which movie or plot you pick, the odds are that it sticks to this or a similar backbone. This version of Campbell is called the Mystic structure. You can mold, adapt, or incept these phases of the hero’s journey to create interesting plots and patterns of storytelling.

If you are interested in the original Hero’s journey Archetype, I suggest Campbell’s bookThe Hero with a Thousand Faces.

It is absolutely sensible to begin your plot with this backbone in mind. Writers are not mere artists, they are scientists. Defined and proven backbones like this support excellent storytelling. I hope to see you soon with amodern structure of storytellingthat is making viral shows on Netflix. Until then, happy writing!

title: Indian Mantra — Food for soul
content:
Indian Mantra — Food for soul

Your body is a temple, and the food you eat makes up the temple you live in. Ancient Indian wisdom tells us that the body is balanced by seven energy-centers, in the form of energy disks or wheels, called Chakras. Each chakra is responsible for a different aspect of your physical and mental wellbeing. When the seven Chakras are balanced, your soul is in balance. These are the Chakras depicted in our Logo.

At Indian Mantra, we offer food not just for your body, but your soul.

The Chakras

The root chakra, or Muladhara located at the base of your spine, is responsible for your sense of security and stability.The sacral chakra, or Svadhisthana, is a chakra for your creative energy.

The solar plexus chakra, or Manipura, stands for your confidence and self esteem. The heart chakra, or Anahata, stands for your ability to love and show compassion.

The throat chakra, or Vishuddha, is for your ability to articulate and communicate. The third eye chakra, or Ajna, is located between your eyes. It is responsible for your imagination and intuition.

The crown chakra, or Sahasrara, is located at the top of your head. It stands for your role in the universe and life’s greater purpose.

Indian Mantra’s goal is to bring harmony to your body and soul through our food. The spices we use in the Indian kitchen are said to have healing properties and find medicinal use in ancient Indian medicine called Ayurveda. At Indian Mantra, we use authentic recipes and source our spices right from their places of origin.

title: How to Write a Great Joke
content:
How to Write a Great Joke

The anatomy of a joke, as told by the best comedians

Look closely at stand-up comics of the day. Do you observe some recurring themes, patterns, and a sense of it right before a punch line brings it home? That is because you have a subconscious patter-recognizing machine that ships with being human.

In this piece, I tried to find these joke-telling patterns.

I am going to dissect the jokes of Jerry Seinfeld, Bill Burr, George Carlin, Louis CK, Jimmy Carr, Ricky Gervais, Taylor Tomlinson, and Karunesh Talwar and see repeatable trends in comedy.

The Skeleton of a Joke

Most jokes follow this skeletal structure — Set-up, detail, and punchline.

There are definitely other forms of jokes that go beyond this structure, but stand-up comics in English tend to stick to this basic skeleton.

The Set-up: a primer

The set-up is the premise on which the joke is told. Itsets upthe listener to anticipate something. An unusual fact, an incident, made up or real, or an observation.

Here is an example set-up of a joke from Louis CK:

You know what’s amazing to me, you can name your kid anything you want.

This is an observation with a slight extreme. The irony in the statement sets you up for what’s coming.

Detail: lay the trap

The detail is where you can add your details and escalate your story. While the setup is a crisp one-line statement, details can be fluid but must be immediately relevant.Notice the free use of suggestive superlatives like at least and literally.

“There are NO laws (around naming a kid). There should be atleast a couple of laws. None. You can literally name your kid anything. You can name your kid a name without vowels if you like — PNSNDLTN.”

Punchline: pull the trigger

A punchline reverses the expectations of the set-up or escalates it to an extreme.

“I’d like to name my kid a whole phrase. I’ll call him Ladies and Gentlemen. This is my son, Ladies and Gentlemen.”

— Louis CK

Some Joke Tools

If the set-up, punchline duo is like the dough-base to a pizza, the tools are the sauce and the seasoning.

Let’s explore some popular tools and how some genius comedians use them.

Self-deprecation

Self-deprecation endears the teller to the audience. It is a sign that says “Hey, I don’t take myself seriously either”, and not taking one’s self seriously is a serious requirement as a comedian.

Jimmy Carr makes fun of his own “donkey-like” laughter, Louis CK and Ricky Gervais of their own “fatness”, Bill Burr of his inability to be emotionally vulnerable, Taylor Tomlinson of her inability to be in relationships, Amy Schumer about her appearance, John Oliver of his bad haircuts and frog-like spectacles.

Self-deprecation is a sign of dealing with your insecurity and a way to show that you are self-aware. Rightly done, It is more a sign of intelligence than of low-self esteem.

A sentence or two of self-deprecation at the right moment makes you a love magnet. Like salt, too much isn't good.

PS: If you are a go-to person of a certain skill, don’t deprecate this skill. that’s suicide. For example, as a writer don’t say “despite my horrible grammar…”

Puns and word-play

Puns and word-play are known as the “lowest form of jokes”. I beg to disagree.

They are only as good as the writer. Jimmy Carr for one, keeps his audience on their toes for entires shows delivering just puns and wordplay.

Here is an example of well-done word-play:

There is a thin line between obsessive and compulsive. It is called a hyphen.

— Jimmy Carr

Personification

Personification is when you assign human-like characteristics to animals or inanimate objects, it puts a hilarious perspective on them.

Jerry Seinfeld has mastered this art in style. This is one of his jokes where he personifies horses in a race:

The horse knows that the jockey is in a hurry, but he is at the finish line thinking (while gasping for breath) “What was the point of all that? We were right here, that was the longest possible route we could take! if we just stayed here, we would have been first!

— Jerry Seinfeld

Analogy

Analogy is similar to personification, but instead of attributing human characteristics, humor arises from attributing characteristics of unrelated scenarios.

Take the example of Taylor Tomlinson’s analogy of your 20’s to a lake that is about to freeze:

Your 20’s are an opportunity to fish out trash out of the lake before it freezes over. Codependency? Daddy issues? Better get all that out of there because WINTER IS COMING!

— Taylor Tomlinson

The best part of these jokes is that they are already sitting there in the universe, you only have to see it with your funny spectacles.

Breaking expectation

If the setup is equivalent to flashing an indicator light towards the right on your car, breaking expectation is what happens when you then take a sharp left.

I once encountered a homeless man who asked me to spare a rupee. He said “If you give me a rupee, God will bless you with 10 thousand”.

I said to him “clearly, you need the 10 thousand more than I do. I’ll gladly take your money”

— Karunesh Talwar

Political incorrectness

Political Correctness is not natural to human nature. We are rather weaned to it over years of adulthood. Breaking this convention throws a metaphorical switch in your head which turns on the laugh bulb.

A lot of modern comedy is based on political incorrectness. It made many careers.

Politically incorrect does not automatically mean offensive. But take care to see that the more politically incorrect your joke, the funnier it needs to be.

To avoid burning bridges, No examples here. My favorite comedian who owns political incorrectness is Varun Grover (Sorry, he only does jokes in Hindi).

Irony

The literary element of Irony is analogous to sarcasm. It has a powerful sense of saying without saying. For example, saying “Wonderful!” where you mean “That’s awful”.

There are many inexplicable daily life scenarios that we let slip because “it is the convention”. This is a great source of thoughtful ironic humor to tap into. Here is an example of irony.

Where did the fear of germs come from? Americans panic easily, so now everybody is running around scrubbing this and spraying that, and over cooking their food trying to avoid contact with Germs. In prison, before they give you a lethal injection, they swab your arm with alcohol. Well, wouldn’t want you to get an infection!

— George Carlin

Pump up the literary devices

Literary devices like allegory, alliteration, diction, euphemism, rhyming, can pump up the interest in your work.

George Carlin is a great example of making literary masterpieces out of his monologues.

Take a look at this example from his iconic environment rant:

Leave nature alone! Haven’t we done enough? We are so self-important. Everybody has to save something now:Save the Bees, save the trees, save the whales, save those snails![Note the Alliteration, Anaphora and Rhyming]

The planet has been through a lot worse than us. Through earth quakes, volcanoes, plate-techtonics, continental drifts, solar flares, sunspots, magnetic storms, the magnetic reversal of the poles, hundreds of thousands of years of bombardment by comets and asteroids and meteors, world wide floods, tidal waves, world wide fires, erosion, cosmic rays, recurring ice ages — and we think, that some plastic bags and some aluminum cans are going to make a difference!? The planet is doing fine, we are f*cked. [Notice the emphasis that is created by listing out many examples without pause]

— George Carlin

This is by no means an exhaustive list. There are, for example, vocal elements and pauses that play a major role in spoken comedy as opposed to writing. I have friends who can leave you in splits in about 3 words.

Cultural aspects, pet peeves, stereotypes, childhood memories, memes… There is an endless list of possible ways to make people laugh. I hope to see you with yet anothernearly comprehensivelist soon.

Stay in touch throughLinkedInorMedium. Write me a note, it would be awesome to meet you!

Here is my social, I amSruthi Korlakunta.

title: 21 Titles People Will Definitely Want to Read About
content:
21 Titles People Will Definitely Want to Read About

Tired of writing about COVID, race, AI, writing advice, self-help, and Donald Trump?

The writing market seems to be saturated with already written topics. There comes a point where you want to frantically swipe left on the app-window that can't seem to stop talking about COVID, race, AI, writing advice, self-help, or Donald Trump. Here are some off-beat topics that people would want to know more about. With enough research, insight, and excellent writing, these topics have a huge potential to draw an enthusiastic audience. I’d personally love to read well-argued and fact-backed articles on them.

1. Businesses to go extinct by 2025

2. Inventions that we desperately need but don’t have

3. A School of philosophy that explains millennial behavior

4. A smarter alternative to reading Terms and Conditions

5. Inventions that are on the verge of becoming daily-technology in 2021

6. Most Important experiments in Behavioral Psychology in modern times

7. The education system is outdated. We need these core changes NOW.

8. What they did with all my stolen data so far

9. The current-day Isaac Newton

10. What everyone is talking about in these industries: Automobile, Consulting, Medicine, Chemical, and Agriculture

11. We wanted flying cars in 2020, we got 180 characters. What if Social Media was not THE technology of the day?

12. What a world where women and men are really equal looks like

13. A better economic system than capitalism that works and doesn’t kill

14. How the Computer changes high-level language to Machine language

15. Travelling is killing the planet, stop glorifying it

16. Ground-breaking discoveries and inventions in 2020 that are not COVID related

17. The most significant experiments in the education system so far

18. Natural Experients in psychology that changed the way we see the world

19. Apps that are heavily underrated

20. Philosophies about the meaning of life — From BC to 2020

21. Lessons to teach your kid before it is too late

These are not topics that I find on my newsfeed every day or even once in a blue moon. They are full of potential and promise. I hope to read well-researched articles on at least some of these soon!

title: Buck, Summer, and the Love that lasted a Winter
content:
Buck, Summer, and the Love that lasted a Winter

Like every day for many years, Buck woke up in his massive wooden bed, dressed himself in his wooden-framed mirror, picked up the keys on his wooden table, and went to work in his little blue car.

The old car was comically small for his imposing frame, but the little steering wheel in his massive hands reminded one that Buck was a gently giant. It was easy to picture Buck holding a child’s teddy bear or shedding tears sometimes in solitude. Buck was adorable.

Day after day, he went to work and didn’t look up from his desk until 5 PM. At 5, he clicked his laptop shut and walked to his car with a cigarette between his fingers, which he lit up just as he was about to drive. Buck didn’t hate his dull life, but he didn’t love it either. He lived like things are already the way they were meant to be. There was no need for change. Ever.

On this particular day, something was new. On his desk was an envelope.

“Dear Mr. Buck Wanderwaal,

You have been assigned an apprentice. She shall remain under your guidance for the next 6 months and shall be trained in basic skills to qualify as a technical assistant.

Please see this as a binding request. It is in the best interest of all parties involved.

Thanks in advance,

Tim Habbadock,

Head of the department.”

“Ugh!” He groaned to himself and looked to the corner of his computer screen. She would have arrived an hour ago. Buck thumped his fist on the desk in mild protest and proceeded to the office reception.

It was easy to spot the new girl dressed in colors far too bright for this office. She sat there swinging her legs, waiting to be received and shown around.

“Hello. You must be Ms. Hal.”

Buck didn’t even try to smile. “Please follow me, I’ll show you to your desk.”

Summer Hal attempted a glorious smile at her new boss but Buck turned away too quickly. She followed him quietly to her desk, where a long list of starting  instructions was laid on her table by the Head of the Department.

“Read them. Let’s meet after lunch.” Buck said, leaving her at her desk. Summer eyed the squirt of ketchup that had left a stain on his shirt. It must have been at least a day old. She found it oddly endearing.

“Yes sir, Mr. Wanderwaal. See you after lunch."She was certain he didn’t like her.

In the weeks that followed, Summer felt alone in the otherwise full office. Buck only shoved reams of paper and scores of emails at her with little or no instruction.

She dutifully scoured through google.com’s third, and even 4th pages to understand what she had to do, and managed to do a sloppy business if at all.

She already knew that her poorly done work would irritate Buck. So when he showed his disappointment and very audible criticism in everyone’s earshot, Summer wasn’t surprised. She pressed her desk and anxiously waited for the ordeal to be over.

In a couple of weeks, Summer dreaded each day that she had to go to work. Every morning, she woke up with a groan and boarded the bus to work with a barely concealed unwillingness — even the bus driver was unhappy to greet her in the morning.

That night she had decided, she would do her best,  even if Buck didn't guide her or care what she did.He wasn’t a bad, guy, just a weirdo,she told herself, making an excuse for him. Perhaps he was a misfit, just like she was at school.

The smileys on the office chat were new and interesting. Buck didn’t know what to make of them. He’d tried to be professional and showed no soft corner to the young girl under his training. He had no idea how to deal with women, this was the only one that seems to talk to him with any interest.

title: Pick up or Drop? — Flash Reviews for Non-Fiction
content:
Pick up or Drop? — Flash Reviews for Non-Fiction

Selections from 13 nonfiction books I read this year.

1. The Bonobo and the Atheist — Frans De Waal, Pick up

Genre:Zoology, PhilosophyQuite often, I turn to philosophy for answers. Surprisingly, I found more convincing arguments in biology than philosophy.

In the book Bonobo and Atheist, the atheist primatologist Frans De Waal makes a strong case for the idea of religion. He presents well-rounded explanations for the existance of religion, despite himself being a non-believer.

Written with great empathy to the believer, the book is a refreshing take on religion and belief.

Pick it up. Now

2. Astrophysics for People in a Hurry — Neil deGrasse Tyson, Drop

Genre:Science, AstronomyAstronomer-turned-TV star Neil deGrasse Tyson put together this book in a hurry. As the title suggests, the book seems to be one astronomical fact after another dumped all together in a rush.

The pro to picking up this book is up-to-date information on astronomy. Sadly, the writing lacks story-telling and chronology. If you only have a little time this year, save this book for another day and pick up Carl Sagan’s Cosmos instead (Also on this list).

Drop this.

3. Them: Adventures with Extremists — Jon Ronson, Pick up

Genre:Investigative JournalismJon Ronson’s slightly self-deprecating British humor makes me laugh out loud. Eccentric people seem to catch his attention like a ball of yarn catches a cat’s.

In his books, Jon deals with quirky and at times terrifying subjects — the US military, cybercriminals, church-healers, and psychopaths.

In the book Them, Jon follows potentially dangerous people — Neo nazis, radical Islamists, and believers of the secret society that rules the world from a room. His adventures are enriching, telling of the world, and outright hilarious. He bent over backward to make this book happen, one can tell.

Pick it up.

4. Born Free — Joy Adamson, Drop

Genre:Biography, unusual experiences, Animals.The story of Elsa the tame-lioness became a worldwide sensation in the 50s and 60s. Born Free is the story of Joy and George Adamson who adopted this lion cub and raised her in the Serengeti until she had her own cubs.

Though fascinating and one-in-a-million experience, the book by Joy reads like a diary (which it is) and can get repetitive at times. If you pick up this book anyway, (which is not a bad idea) you will probably skip a huge wad of pages.

Drop it for now, maybe later.

5. Kitchen Confidential — Anthony Bourdain, Pick up

Genre:BiographyThis is an entertaining biography of a star chef and a sneak-preview into the backstage of American restaurants. Nothing has been left out. All secrets laid bare.

Anthony Bourdain wrote this book in a conversational tone. He speaks with humility about his larger-than-life experiences and the murky scenes and rowdy lives of line cooks. With added restaurant facts and knife-using tips, the book is a fun read.

Pick it up.

6. Inner Engineering — Sadguru Jaggi Vasudev, Drop

Genre:Yoga, Biography, Philosophy.The book is a transcript of the YouTube sermons of Sadguru Jaggi Vasudev. It could have been used to explain complexity in Sadguru’s philosophy because more can be done in a book than in a speech. Alas, one is left wondering.

The first 30% of the book is an autobiography, and the rest is a mixture of life advice and Yoga. The book claims that Yoga can only be practiced but not explained, and ideas on philosophy are the same as those on Youtube. Feel free to pick up something else.

7. Our Inner Ape — Frans De Waak, Pick up

Genre:Human Behavior, BiologyIf I had to select one book from this list that has been the most enriching to my life, it would be this one. Also on Yuval Noah Harari’s list of favorites, this book brought a major paradigm shift in my understanding of human behavior.

Writing about Chimpanzees, Bonobos, and other ape species, Frans De Waal explains different aspects of Human behavior including love, war, sex, power, and group dynamic in a way never seen before. No wonder he was named among the Times’ most influential people back in his day.

Pick it up.

8. Guns, Germs, and Steel —Jared Diamond Pick up

Genre:History, AnthropologyThis book gives you a good explanation to questions such as:

Why the world is the way it is? Why are white countries so rich? Why did some colonize while others were colonized?

Covering major milestones in the development of human civilization, Jared Diamond tries to give non-racist explanations to the imbalance in the world.

Pick it up. If not today, then soon.

9. Malcolm X — Alex Haley, Pick up

Genre:BiographyIt is uncomfortable to say anything supportive of a religious extremist or a racial extremist. Malcolm X might change that for you. This biography written by Alex Haley tells Malcolm’s side of the story.

Malcolm is charismatic, eloquent, and not afraid to fight. He understood the predicament of the black man in society. He chose the road of radical Islam to change that.

This book might make you all the more ambivalent about Malcolm X. Who knows, you might also empathize why the black man chose to not trust the white man after centuries of atrocity and why he felt safer with a gun.

10. Meditations —Marcus Aurelius, Pick up

Genre:Stoic PhilosophyThe book is a healthy respite from all those self-help books that read like rude football coaches. It is a delightful and light primer into the stoic philosophy.

I am not bought on the idea of stoicism itself, but the book has certain very sensible suggestions for a way of life, straight from the last benevolent Roman emperor Marcus Aurelius.

Pick it up for some light philosophy.

11. The Selfish Gene —Richard Dawkins, Pick up

Genre:Evolutionary Biology, PhilosophyThe Selfish Gene is one of the most important books in biology for non-experts.

It is a no-nonsense book of facts of what it meansto be. Reducing all creatures both human and animal, into the word “survival machine” the book suggests game theory solutions to why we are the way we are. Why we love, why we cheat, why we hate and why we jump into deep waters to save a stranger.

It is a must-read in a lifetime.TheRoyal Societyscience book prize listed it as the most influential science book of all time.

Pick up.

12. A Walk in the Woods — Bill Bryson, Pick up

Genre:Travel, HumorBill Bryson’s wry account of hiking the Appalachian Trail with his unprepared friend is hilarious. Though he did not make the planned 2000 something miles on foot, he left this hilarious gem after 200 miles. This book was named CNN’s funniest travelogues ever written.

Alongside his trail, Bryson talks about ecology, changes in American forests over years, and idiots who try to hike the Appalachian. The book is also a nice piece of research.

Pick it up.

13. Cosmos — Carl Sagan, Pick up

Genre:AstronomyRating:Pick up

Carl Sagan, besides being an astronomer is also an enthusiastic storyteller. Although published first in 1980, Cosmos needs few revisions to date.

The book talks about the history of astronomy in great detail. Beginning at 1B.C. the story of discovery that the earth is round, to the journeys of the Voyager and Viking past the orbits of Saturn, to the possibilities that future astronomy brings, the book has EVERYTHING.

Although you might end up feeling really, really petty and insignificant in the massive universe, it is a good thing.

Pick it up.

Stay in touch throughLinkedInorMedium. Write me a note, it would be awesome to meet you!

Here is my social, I amSruthi Korlakunta.

title: Five Non-Selfhelp and Nonfiction Books That You Will Binge
content:
Five Non-Selfhelp and Nonfiction Books That You Will Binge

And will keep in your mind until you die.

Ifyou are anything like me you have your doubts about the self-help genre and don’t have much of an eye for fiction either. Once in years like Haley’s Comet comes a good self-help book through.

I think there is enough objective motivation out there that doesn’t need to explicitly scream in your face like your football coach. A good teacher, a person that experienced things you didn’t, a good observation or even a good story can up your spirits and give you a more truth-based perspective to stay positive.

Here are some books in these sections you might want to pick up.

Outliers — Observational Motivation

ThisMalcolm Gladwellclassic tells you what you already had a hunch about, but puts it into convincing words with water-tight proofs.

Outliersshows that chance happenings influence your success as much as your abilities, if not less. For example, your year of birth, physical attributes, social and cultural influences, and a whole world out there that you don’t choose to play a huge role in who you are.

A sneak-peek into the book is how airlines could minimize their air crashes by identifying the least suspected culprit —miscommunication between the ever aggressive New York air traffic controllers and the overly polite Asian pilots.

This book will push you to identify the correct sources of any problem without making assumptions and jumping to immature conclusions.

Educated — Experiences that will wake you up

Educated is an outstanding Autobiography. It is the account of a Cambridge Alumni,Tara Westoverwho recounts her childhood in vivid detail. Growing up in an “End-of-the-world” obsessed family that practically lived in a bunker, most of her childhood was spent manufacturing ammunition and canned food for the D-day.

Medication, education, safety, everything that is “public” in the modern world was oblivious to her. She endures physical and emotional abuse from her family, but doesn’t give them blame.

Her objective perspective of this harrowing tale leaves you with a great deal of endearing love and respect for her personality. Her cluelessness of suddenly entering the “normal-world” right up to the events that led her to graduate from Cambridge, offers a refreshing hope in humanity.

The book is also a refreshing literary respite. It has the tone of a curious child to whom all of it was normal and an adult who comes to terms with things very matter-of-factly, though her world turned upside down.

Read it and thank me later.

Design of everyday things — Every engineer should read this

This text-book-format book byDon Norman, an Ex-Chief Design Officer at Apple is a good place to begin understanding product design.

If you ever held a job you know what it is like to create a product for somebody. For your boss, for your coworker, for your employees, or a customer. The product could be a presentation, an actual commodity, or perhaps even a new coffee machine for your department.

Norman discusses human-Centric design, how self-blame culture condones bad design, how security is often ignored or over-designed, and the importance of including environmental concerns in design with examples from his work-life.

He talks about products as simple as washing machines, air conditioners, baking ovens, and coffee mugs to IT environments. The book is a delight.

The next time you create something, it will cause you to say “ I could make that better”.

The Glass Castle — Biography of a Nomadic childhood

Another extraordinary Biography is ofJeanette Walls, a successful journalist with MSNBC and The New Yorker, with an unusual childhood.

Not everyone makes it from an on-the-run lifestyle — running from cops, violent neighbors, snakes in the desert, and government authorities — to life in Long Island New York.

Such was the childhood of Jeanette walls whose parents, at the time of her writing the book chose to live homelessly and hunt in Public Garbage cans for dinner.

The extremely jarring situations in her life and yet the successful progeny the family made make you wonder if there is only a handful of things to teach your children and learn yourself.

Born a Crime — Your favorite comedian has a story to tell

The last book in this series is also a biography. I am a major fan of the talk-show host,Trevor Noah. It is hard to tell whether it was before or after reading his biography.

This very talented young man grew up during the apartheid times in South Africa to an African mother and German father. As he was born, it was illegal to be an inter-race child. Hence the title — Born a crime.

Trevor was raised by a mother who doesn’t give a flip about the law or the rules, or the fact that he is now famous. The book recounts how various social restrictions as a colored child lead him to be the versatile speaker and people-person he now is.

It sometimes makes you wonder how one could come to terms with what happened with themandmake great quality humor out of the story. If that guy doesn’t complain, maybe we can complain less.

title: What Can Be Said Can Be Said Clearly
content:
What Can Be Said Can Be Said Clearly

Language Lessons for the layman from Wittgenstein

This week I chanced upon a baffling theory, located precariously at the mid of logic, language, and philosophy. I was endlessly intrigued by this school of thought. Without riddling this post with jargon, I’ll just call this the “Wittgenstein’s school of thought” (WST) here on, after the swiss philosopher who came up with this theory. With this article, I want to convey one simple idea that can be implemented in the reader’s daily life, which derives from Wittgenstein’s school of thought. The reader should of course bear in mind that the purpose, course, and the bottom line of Wittgenstein’s philosophy are far more complex than the ideas present here. If the WST is analogous to a persian carpet, consider this article an unintentionally free-floating lint ball.

Wittgenstein is considered by many as the greatest philosopher of the 20th century. Ironically, his thesis proves that logically speaking, philosophy is a meaningless field of study.

Some Language Lessons from Wittgenstein

What can be said can be said clearly

The next time you are at work and you try to explain a concept to a group of people that have not worked on it, remember, throwing more jargon and abbreviations into it is a sign of a logic deficit. If you know what you are talking about, it can be made clear as crystal. There is little or no room in work-related environments for statements that are not fact-based. When they are fact-based they are capable of being broken down to atomic facts that can be universally understood. When you spot someone who makes a presentation with subject-specific lingo, you should smell obfuscation, not intelligence.

Identify which “Communication Game” you are playing

Language is like a game where we paint pictures in each other’s heads. Often the source of pain and emotional discomfort in the world is the feeling of being misunderstood. What adds to this discomfort is one’s inability to speak clearly about an issue because of societal taboos and other mental blocks. As a result of this discomfort, words, which enable one to form pictures of facts are blurred and defeat the very purpose for which they are uttered.

The purpose of communication is usually either:

Exchange of facts Or

Seeking or giving comfort

Consider the following example of a familiar topic of interest in a romantic relationship.

She:You are not interested in talking to me. You don’t love me anymore.

He:But we have been talking for the past hour!

She:You are always at work and barely spare time in the evenings for me.

He:But we were in the town all weekend and talked all day, how can you claim that we don’t have enough communication, that is simply not true!

In the above conversation between a couple, If they are playing a picture game, When she claims that her partner is not interested in her she is not playing the fact game but a game of comfort, whereas the man interprets the purpose of communication as a fact game and goes on to state supporting facts to his theory. This leads to miscommunication and misunderstanding, thus embittering everyone involved. Identifying the “game” the other is playing is an essential part of communication.

Wittgenstein’s philosophy of no-philosophy

In this section, I will talk about some interesting aspects of the Wittgenstein's philosophy which eventually leads to proving that philosophy is nothing but a linguistic therapy to clear the blurry aspects of language.

What can be said can be said clearly

The purpose of language is to unambiguously describe logical thought. For example, If I say “The apple is on the table”, it is an unambiguous description of reality. Like a numeral is a representation of a “number”, which is an abstract concept, language is a “representation” of facts.

All philosophical problems result from trying to articulate things that are not meant to be articulated with language

Imagine the ancient Greek when decimals were not yet invented. There was no such thing as a “half”, there were only whole numbers. The field of mathematics was as of then unprepared to handle problems such as the circumference of a circle, which never seemed to be “full” numbers. Such is the nature of human language when it comes to the expression of the intangible. It is not built for this purpose.

Talking about propositions of natural science, such as things, positions, states and such “facts” is made possible through the exchange of mental images, by the means of language. Since they are “facts” they are logically consistent and can be represented by language. Communication thus is like a game where the listener guesses the picture wanting to be painted by the speaker.

When it comes to interpretations of states of affairs, such as emotions, the good and the bad, will or want, Human language is a tool that is not equipped to paint accurate pictures. So questions such as “Is this apple good?” are redundant and meaningless, since such a sentence does not use the very aspects of language which give it it’s meaning. The word “good” derives from what people “like”, which cannot be understood by all people in the same way. The logic of language is defeated.

The end of Philosophy is that philosophical questions are not to be answered through language and inquiry but by existing in the present

Since abstract concepts are not accurately represented by language, questions about meaning, existence, and value are not aimed at language but living itself. Getting one’s self entangled in these questions is a result of unclear thinking, and searching in the wrong place — Language. Philosophy is therefore not a search for meaning, but the untangling of linguistic inconsistencies that form its problems. It’s nature is purely therapeutic and the science itself should consider itself more with language than with the core of existence itself. Most of the philosophical questions are to be answered by living life as it is in the present. In Wittgenstein’s own words,

“The truth “Shows” itself. It is not said or even expressed in thought. What can be said can be said clearly. and whereof we cannot speak, we must remain silent”

title: Song — The Song of Sisyphus
content:
Song — The Song of Sisyphus

A country song on the meaning of life

I really wanted to write a song, but I have never been much of a singer or a music player. So I decided to write it to one of those tunes that tickles me.

This song was written in the tune and chords of Family Guy’s“Have you ever put butter on pop tarts”. Feel free to sing it that way. Link at the end.

All my life I have been waiting,to see something trueAll my life I’ve been waiting,to find out what is new

I talked to many people,by the mighty church steeple,I tried to go to college,for what they call knowledge,I even traveled the world.

I thought that there is meaningto find it would be freeingbut I still felt like a little girl

I don’t want to live to hundred,or end the world’s hatred,I don’t wanna cure poverty

I don't wanna be famous,I don't even want your kindness,I just wanna be free

All my life I have been waiting,to see something newAll my life I’ve been waiting,but there’s no such thing as truth.

The original song is here, which by the way — HILARIOUS.

title: Alfred
content:
Short Story: Alfred

and how he saved my work, purpose, and sanity.

Every country I set foot in told me that fear is my right. That as a lone young woman, fear is the logical response to most things. So when a frail old man stumbled out of the taxi and hit the ground I did not jump to help. I crouched in fear.

The stillness of the German villageHeimsheimjumps at outsiders. Especially if you come from a bustling country with a billion people, like me. Impeccable homes stand quietly, the lights in the windows not matching the absence of sounds.

Alfred rudely interrupted my daydreams by crashing face-down onto the road 100 meters from me. I stopped and tried to camouflage. Then I saw that he needed help.

“Ugh! If I had just got off work 3 minutes sooner, I could have just walked on and finished my presentation in peace!”

Having started in my full-time job 5 years ago, I was just grasping the permanence of the hamster wheel. Ambition burned hot when I first set foot in this country. Now I wondered if a bigger job title, and putting down the downpayment on my dream home would get me closer to myself or farther away.

“Why I am doing any of this?”The question floated to the top of my mind no matter how much I drowned it in work. My family was far away in India, friends were mostly on Instagram.Why do we emigrate?For whom?

“Wait, am I unhappy!?”That would be just arrogant. If there was a German dream for immigrants, I was living it.

I scrolled aimlessly on my phone at the hospital lounge, waiting to check in on my good deed from last night.

“Do you feel better?”,I asked Alfred when I saw him. They had put his arm in a cast. He had been drunk and couldn’t remember how he got there.

“Fit as a horse”, he smiled.

Bless public healthcare! The poor guy seemed like a nice fellow.I talked to him for a while and called him a taxi home. I went with him, just in case.

From then on, I visited Alfred everyday at his home on my way back from work. It had become a ritual. He was alone, 68, and unemployed. He had the most honest smile I’ve ever seen.

At first, I was being polite, asking about his health. But day after day, I grew attached to Alfred. His witty comebacks reminded me of my mom.I wonder who is listening to her anecdotes back at home.

It was hard to watch him readily submit to every form of alcohol he could afford with the welfare he received from the state. Part of me wondered what I would be doing with my life if I were Alfred — without friends or family, without the need for a sane face at work.

The evenings with Alfred gave a sense of meaning to my days. 8 hours a day, I made my employer his money. But for one hour at Alfred’s, I felt like I really mattered.

Summer came, I was one rung higher up at the work ladder. With new responsibilities, I worked overtime nearly every day. My visits to Alfred became less frequent. I could not answer his drunken calls late in the evenings.

Three months had passed and I hadn’t seen him. It then struck me that he hasn’t called in a week. This was unusual. I was worried. And guilty.

Fearing the worst, I drove the familiar road to his home. I hoped to find him on the porch that bright summer evening, sulking because I hadn’t called back. As I rolled closer to his gate, his frail figure did not emerge as I imagined it would. I felt a chill pass down my chest.

I peered inside the window and saw him huddled under many blankets with the TV running. “Alfred!” I called out twice. I saw him move, but he did not respond. He was clearly drunk out of his depth.

In his time, Alfred was a door-to-door salesman. Gradually, the world had become too fast for him to catch up. Friends and family moved on, old lovers passed.

Alfred stopped trying and found solace the only way he could. He kept his doors open to strangers like me, both to enter and leave as they liked.

After more than an hour of prodding and begging, he let me in. He hadn’t eaten or drunk anything that wasn’t alcohol, perhaps in many days.

I whipped up a quick dinner and forced him to eat a few bites. I got him to drink some water and sat down by his bed. Alfred did not die but he came really close.

“Why do you do this!?”.I was exasperated.

“Little lady, I have seen a lot of life till today. Since I was mostly drunk, I saw most of it twice, isn’t that great!”,came his usual witty comeback. I giggled.

That moment, I was immensely grateful that I could see him and his honest smile again. I promised myself that I’d keep it alive every day I could.

He did not reproach me for not coming. He didn’t ask why. I doubt that he knows how I felt when I imagined the worst. I wish he knew.

“Don’t you have work to do?”,He asked, only superficially interested.

“I do. Please get some rest”.

That day on, I noticed that my mind did not struggle to find meaning.

I noticed that people look forward to more than their screens at work. That they like to notice others and be noticed. I saw how after work, people called their loved ones from the car though they were headed home to them anyway.

I noticed that Alfred did not drink himself unconscious anymore.

PerhapsI found mywhy, perhaps I made it up.For a moment, I felt less alone in Germany.

title: The Price Immigrants Pay
content:
The Price Immigrants Pay

Leaving your country for a good life is not fun.

In one gut-wrenching moment in the documentary “Cuba with a Cameraman” a four-year-old girl in New York gets to go to see her grandmother in Cuba for the first time in her life. The girl’s mother breaks down in tears of joy on reuniting with her mother.

They were Cuban exiles whom Fidel Castro allowed returning to the country for a visit.

I am a free woman.

Unlike a Cuban exile, I can go home whenever I want. But there is a catch. I can’t.

When I see my German friends visit home for Christmas, easter, weekend, and laundry, I shudder a little inside. Maybeenoughis far more often than I think.

How often is a visit to your aging parents often enough? Once a year for a week? twice a year for a week? thrice?

When I hear colleagues talk about their six visiting grandchildren, I think about my parents alone at home with most young of the family and extended family overseas. They have no doors open for young Ideas.Doesn’t that make you age faster?

Last night, I explained to my mother how to log in to her Gmail for a half-hour over WhatsApp video. It was exasperating. I don’t want to be the monster who only calls once a day to be rude.

Every time I sit down with evening music, drive through green mountains, curl up with a coffee and a book, I think “My mother would have liked to be here”. No matter how often I make a vacation home, I can’t give her this.

“Who is responsible for this?”

Nobody with reasonable working conditions, human rights, and safety would want to leave home for good.

However, a slight critic against either the ruling or the opposing political party of India would not make me any friends. Indians are very politically sensitive, you see.

I cannot talk about how the same jobs in India would give me laughably less time for private life, about how the pay and human respect need to be negotiated by the government with every International firm that sets up shop for peanuts.

I cannot complain that I don’t feel safe traveling home at 2 AM from work. I cannot even utter “Women don’t get treated the same way back home”. I had to cut off a male friend who thought meeting women at eye level was asking for“too much respect”.

I am tied.

I have friends in the USA who haven’t been home in 5 years, lest a catch in the not-yet-H1B-visa will never allow them to come back.“Just another year,”she said to her half-conscious dad while neighbors and kin rushed him to the emergency for a cardiac arrest.

Germany is different. My parents can visit me for 3 months on a visitor visa.

3 years ago, My parents visited my brother and me. Affordable homes in Germany are big enough for a human and a hamster. A big feat in a small home. We shared a 40 square-meter space with no rooms for a month and a half. But my parents were happy.

The joy was real and costly. My parents didn’t speak German or understandable English. Every time my father lost his way in the big city, my mother got an anxiety attack. They couldn’t remember the names of the streets or numbers on the buses to places they didn’t recognize.

It was not going to be sustainable.

The last I went home was before the pandemic.

I saw my mother. She was in the Intensive Care Unit. I got there in time to post on social media for blood donors.

I remember how her eyes lit up when she recognized my brother. She was heavily sedated, drifting in and out of consciousness. She didn’t know she’d live. We didn’t know she’d live.

Had it unfolded any other way, I would have kicked myself every waking moment.

I took everything from her. My being, my good, my bad, my hobbies and everything else that makes me human. I didn’t give her back a single day. I watched her retire from 40-year employment from the pictures and videos of her speeches on the Whatsapp family group.

I am not a Cuban refugee. I have all my papers, I have my vacation days and I have a good employer. And yet, Immigration is hard.

I am afraid of calling home, I might hear the sadness in my mother’s voice. I cannot bear to video call, her eyes don’t hide anything. I cannot tell a friend back home, I am a selfish person who chose to be away.

Immigration is hard.

If you enjoyed this story, you’ll perhaps also like:

Write with me on Medium with thislink. It costs 5$ a month and changed my life. ☺

title: What makes for conversational cringe-fest and how to avoid doing it
content:
What makes for conversational cringe-fest and how to avoid doing it

3 anecdotes that demonstrate conversation killers

Close friends might accept you for how you are. No matter what your tastes in food, people, music, or movies, they will let you be.

Such a friendship only develops with time. No matter how much you “click” in the first meeting — real, deep, and honest friendships form only over years. There is no way around that long road.

As kids, conversation meant simply hurling the first thing that came to mind, and thankfully, it was accepted. Adulthood is harder. What kills off a budding friendship in adulthood is bad conversation.

Regardless of whether you want to make new friends or just make good decent acquaintances, here are conversation killers you are better off avoiding.

The Food Overdose

I lived in a shared apartment with 5 others.

People moved in and out of our apartment constantly as they finished their internships or exchange semesters. In the course of my two years in that apartment, I lived with a health freak. Let’s call him John.

In the first week, John was very enthusiastic about enlightening us about his eating habits. We listened with wonder because he really did seem to have an impressive regimen.

Week 2. John is still talking about nothing but HIS food habits.

Week 3. John is scorning at us for ordering pizza on game night. He brought his own food to the table. Which we understood since he had his regimen going on. The conversation “accidentally” drifted off to how we are causing ourselves cancer.

Regardless of what anyone is talking about, John works his early-morning water routine, his late evening snack-factoid, his avocado-aficionado into the conversation.

“Hey what’s up, John!”

“Oh, you mean the bio-organic, gene technology-free bread that I am holding over my head? You should really stop buying canned beans. I see you stopped working out, I already have my 2 hours of Yoga behind me.”

By the time I moved out, John made sure we knew everything about his food and can habits. John doesn't have my new address.

The condescending one

I moved into my new apartment recently.

Thanks to the pandemic, I can only have a few people over at a time. I invited my friends one or two at a time, depending on what the law allowed.

Sometimes for dinner, sometimes for a game evening. In all, I had a brilliant time with my friends, except when some of them used the conversation to mildly condescend.

My friend, who walked into the kitchen seconds after I finished cooking casually remarked “Oh, I see you didn’t clean the kitchen yet, that’s ok, I’m flexible”.

All this when I was preparing for people to come over. After 8 hours of work. Because I liked them.

If you are visiting and you can’t say something nice, don’t say anything.

The competitive one

So this happened to me.

I was returning from a party with a group of friends. Annie, my friend in the driver’s seat, said “Wow, that was awesome! I didn’t party like that even in college!”.

Rosa, also in the car goes “No? My college life was awesome! I was constantly at parties. I had so much fun, this party was SO boring in comparison.”

The car fell silent.

Whatever we were going to say about that awesome party, we didn’t say it. Annie wasn’t expecting competition, she was trying to be cheerful and make small-talk.

The competitive tone Rosa had chosen made us worried that we would be one-upping instead of just exchanging experiences. After a long awkward silence, Annie chose something else to talk about.

Don’t get competitive. Listen and hi-five. Don’t one-up.

Dealing with all that — Your role

For a long time, it bothered me heavily to deal with a conversation that is “not ok” in my opinion. One long look at myself suggested that everybody has their quirks.

Lying, bullying, bad-mouthing being a no-go, I think it is possible to continue to maintain healthy relationships also after killed conversations. Just because someone made an error, doesn’t mean they are evil. To be fair, we are all equally evil.

There are situations out there that would make you behave the same way you are being “behaved to”. Trust me, there are. When you encounter a conversation that pushes all your wrong buttons, try not to react immediately.

It is hard. After all, we are human. Flex your tolerance and try to move past one conversation at a time.

In order to have a fulfilling and low-stress (it is never going to be no-stress) social life, it is important to be able to “let go”.

title: My Experiments With Kindness
content:
My Experiments With Kindness

Why I thought kindness was a good idea

I was a mean 12-year-old. I thought that being inarticulate, overtly religious, not being the smartest in the class, and not having many talents made people lesser beings.

I easily defended the “strong” and saw the “weak” as a non-threat and never took them seriously.

Growing up, I realized that this is the behavior that was exactly wrong with the world.

Wars, climate change, Water shortage — Name a problem, I am certain that it can be sorted by cooperative people — only if this behavior did intervene.

If you are biased, an idea carries only as much weight as the person saying it. The person saying it is buried under so many layers of colored cellophane — His place of birth, postal code, color, sex, symbols on their body, language... As a result, productive ideas are shot down. Conversely, the ideas with the same colored wrappers as you are welcomed too soon.

This felt illogical. Wrong even. Because one does not really “choose” his beliefs as much as he thinks he does. The reason you pray in a church is that you were born in a family which did this. You love watching sports because all other boys seem to be doing the same. If you were born in Saudi, the odds are that you are in support of Sharia law.

Why we are kind

The ability to ignore this bias and accommodating another individual is for me, kindness. Turns out it is really difficult. This is why kindness is considered a rare virtue. If a lion supports a lion in the kill, it is an act of nature, but if he supports a crocodile, it is an act of miraculous kindness. Cellophane called species has been ignored.

Kindness is unnatural probably because we as a species are genetically predisposed to loving ourselves the most and putting ourselves first. For Survival.

It makes sense too — If you put someone else first, and sacrifice yourself, you will die before supporting a single other soul.  Selfishness is natural. Kindness is a subsidiary quality of selfishness; The reason you develop kindness is so that you get your group’s support, thereby adding a buffer to your survival chances.

What remains of these dregs of evolutionary kindness is altruism.Being kind felt good because evolution programmed that into me but being altruistic is getting high on that kindness-kick. Altruism is remains of evolutionary kindness showing up as symptoms.

Evolutionary kindness is important. It certainly seems to get more things done in the larger world. If I am not kind to my fellow man, he will be unkind to me and the project we have together will get lesser excellence. Less innovation means fewer chances of survival.

For example, If you are the best climate change activist on the planet, but you are uncouth to your fellow humans who eat meat, they are not going to be vegetarian for you though they would LOVE to make a difference. And climate change does not get done by one person.

Kindness is a must. Even logically.

My Experiments with Kindness

As an experiment, I tried being kinder than normal for a year. I consciously tried to suppress what vileness seemed to come out of me. This was easy because I had people who were unreasonably kind to me for a few years then.

I noticed that kindness doesn’t come naturally. It needs practice, like your body at the gym. It gets easier, it will never be finished and perfect, but it needs constant practice.

It failed.

At the end of the year, after “tolerating” everything, I felt like I was going nowhere, it felt unsafe, unfair, and stupid. It felt difficult to turn the other cheek when I perceived what I thought were unprovoked attacks, regardless of how I treated the other.

“You have no talent”, “ Do this for me, or else, you are a bad person”, “You are unimportant”, “I am better than you because I have habits by the book”— All the little ways people try to assure themselves that they are good, while you are not.

I launched into a rant in my head about how people will continue to be rude, discouraging, and hateful if you don’t cause harm first.

They will blemish your reputation with common friends, they will hope you were unsuccessful, they will slide back-handed complements into conversations, they will throw a stone and run away, and they will tell you that“You are not everything you think you are”.

Kindness wasn’t helping at all.

Why I think this happened

I think this is because people only truly want “real” superiority over their surroundings to be at peace.

They want to know that they are “better” than the people around them. Kindness requires you to down your defenses. If they don’t see a clearly defined defense, people almost subconsciously attack.

I think it makes no sense to be kind for kindness's sake.

People sense the respect, love, and admiration that you have for them, whether or not you show gestures of it. When you don't genuinely have some, it is futile to muster them for an act. This core respect for humankind is transparent, even if you don't want it that way.

For now, I have no conclusive reason or evidence in support of actual kindness or against it.

The alternative road is to stick with people you truly respect. Find people who have similar goals and interests as you. Value people for what you actually see, and not what you hope to see.

If you cannot turn a blind eye to the atrocity you see, take the correct side and stick with it with conviction.

Don’t just be nice. Be true.

title: And The Most Hare-Brained Scheme Ever Hatched Award Goes to…
content:

Umm. ok then.
