title: Must-check notes
content:
Must-check notes

Catchy title — Keep it simple

Introduction — Get to teh point and tell them what you are going to tell

Must have subheadings, paras should be 5 to 6 lines max.

Link other ppl as well

End with a Question

People scroll to the bottom, go back to the top and then read the entire thing. So keep the conclusion meaning full and summary.

Ljnking others are awesome. They are very flattered, do this more.

title: Books to Make Your Holidays Even more Memorable
content:
Books to Make Your Holidays Even more Memorable

In the summer holidays of my 7th Standard, I remember this picture vividly.A Ruskin Bond’s Short Storieswas face-down beside me, while I laughed at my cousin in the antique living room of my mother’s ancestral home.

The stories in that book reminded me of a carefree and carelessly dressed me on that holiday, sneaking in a page of reading before lunch or before bed.

Books enhance memories — like smell, taste, and music that remind you of that place and time.

Here is a list of books for light but highly engaging reading on a holiday, to make your time even more memorable. They are personal, funny and ironic.

Rank 5:

Shit my Dad Says: Justin Halpert

Shit my dad says is a perfect flight or beach read. What started as a twitter page became a bestseller.

“You are not a cigar person. Well, the first reason that jumps out at me is that you are holding it like you are Jerking off a mouse". —his dad

The book is basically a collection of shit his dad says put into tiny chunks of unrelated paragraphs. It’s delightful and bite-sized.

Rank 4

Men who stare at Goats: Jon Ronson

For years I’ve only been able to read non-fiction. The real world is simply too entertaining, we don’t need no fiction.

That’s because writers like Jon Ronson make reality enjoyable. A Journalist forThe Guardian, In Men who stare at Goats, Ronson investigates hippie psychics in the American military.

Generals with flower garlands trying to kill goats in the next room by thinking hard about them, people who actually succeed paralysing hamsters by staring at them — this book is a wild ride.

Rank 3:

Short stories — Someone Who Loves You in All Your Damaged Glory: Raphael Bob Waksberg

If you have seen the Netflix Series “Bojack Horseman”, you know Raphael. He’s the creator, the brain behind the horse.

I went on a quest to find everything else he has ever written because Raphael understands the “condition" of being human. (Yes, I think existence it’s a disease). This is the only book I found, other than the story of Bojack’s formation. It describes itself as a “Tragicomedy".

This book is the collection of super wierd short stories. The first Story for example, is entirely a text printed on a can of cashews.

Irony, Humor, Satire. Raphael is the boss of this genre that he created with himself. This book of short stories is his first. It is human. Actually it is also dog, cashew can (And more).

Rank 2:

Short stories — Interpreter of Maladies : Jhumpa Lahiri

Jhumpa Lahiris’s debut book won her the Pulitzer prize for fiction and the Hemingway Pen award.

This book captures India in the most intricate detail I’ve so far come across. I’ve often come across books written on India by NRI authors that have a tone of condescension and exasperation. Jhumpa Lahiri is a refreshing neutral vantage point. Herself an American woman of Indian origin, her stories are extremely accurate, put in the simplest language. I became an immediate fan.

Rank 1:

This is Going to Hurt: Secret Diaries of a Junior Doctor Adam Kay

This is by far the funniest non-fiction book I’ve read.

With people stuffing Thanks Giving Turkeys up their snatches, and quips like “I’ve seen a lot of penises in my Career in urology, but this was the worst one yet", Adam Kay makes this hilarious book from his experiences as a doctor in the NHS in the UK.

Like in the title, the stories are as funny as they are hurtful (quite literally). This is by far the funniest book I’ve read.

Hope you enjoy your holiday reads. These books are as memorable as it gets!

Join me on Mediumfor all you can read and write. It costs 5$ and changed my life.

title: The A-B-C of AI Fluency
content:
The A-B-C of AI Fluency

Beyond Prompt Engineering: Follow this framework to make AI your first language.

If you use it ineffectively, AI might become your new glorified spell-checker. But do it right, it’s the foundation for your new future. Here is an introduction to fluency in AI, a summary of the course I took from Anthropic Academy. Glad to share that it goes beyond some prompt tricks.

Let’s look at AI in the three major ways we put it to use:

1. When you know what to do and want someone to do it :AI Automation

2. When you don’t know what to do, but have an idea:AI Augmentation

3. When you want AI to completely take over a role independently:AI Agent

While Automation is very helpful, you will supercharge your AI career if you become an expert in Augmentation and AI Agents.

The Framework

The Anthropic Academy has identified 4 pillars of AI fluency:

1.Delegation:

Deciding where you should take over, and where should AI come in. This needs an understanding of your goals and the expertise needed to achieve them.

2.Description:

Instead of asking AI to “solve this for me”, “make me a picture”, make it a habit to patiently explain to AI what you want, the steps it needs to take to get to it, context, tone, and style involved in achieving your desired result. Think of these three key components of Description:

Product Description:Clearly defining what you want the AI to createProcess Description:Guiding how the AI approaches your requestPerformance Description:Defining how you want the AI to behave during your collaboration.

3. Discernment:

This is the most important part of your “vibing with AI”. Check if the facts are accurate, if the reasoning makes sense, and if the output is for the intended audience. Refine the ideas generated by AI.

Just like for description, there are three key steps to discernment:

Product Discernment:Evaluating the quality of AI outputsProcess Discernment:Assessing how the AI approached the taskPerformance Discernment:Evaluating how the AI behaved during the interaction itself

4.Diligence:

How are you ensuring fairness while using AI to control biases, verifying accuracy, and maintaining transparency? Diligence would let you confidently say, “This is my work, this is where AI has helped,” and sign it off.

Scaling Laws

If you haven’t directly worked with AI development, it is crucial to understand why, in 2026, AI has become this big, and not before.

The current AI boom is thanks to three fundamental pillars —1. Algorithms: Neural Networks (around for decades) and Transformers (These were developed in 2017)

2. Data: Widely available today like never before. This is the raw material to AI.

3. Computational Power: Hardware like GPUs and TPUs over many clusters of computer networks is a fairly recent development.

As these factors contributed to the growth of AI Models, they began to develop capabilities that no one had built or predicted. That is the world we live in today. AI that thinks and adapts.

Prompt Engineering

Prompt engineering is the practice of designing effective instructions for AI systems, combining familiar human communication principles with AI-specific considerations.

Anthropic defines these foundational techniques for prompt engineering: giving context, showing examples of desired outputs, specifying constraints, breaking complex tasks into steps, asking the AI to think first, and defining the AI’s role or tone.

Six foundational prompting techniques:

Coming to the most talked-about skill on LinkedIn this year — how exactly to prompt-engineer like a professional?

Here are the state-of-the-art guidelines from the Antropic course:

1. Give context: Be specific about what you want, why you want it, and the relevant background2. Show examples: Demonstrate the output style or format you’re looking for3. Specify constraints: Clearly define format, length, and other output requirementsBreak complex tasks into steps: Guide the AI through multi-step reasoning4. Ask the AI to think first: Give space for the AI to work through its process5. Define the AI’s role or tone: Specify how you want the AI to communicate

What does this mean for you?Here’s a conclusion you can paste in:

What Does This All Mean For You?

AI fluency isn’t about knowing every tool on the market or being the person in the room who can rattle off the latest model releases. It’s about having a framework in your head that helps you make smart decisions every single time you sit down to work with AI.

The 4 D’s — Delegate, Describe, Discern, Diligence — are deceptively simple. Don’t skip straight to typing into a chatbox without doing any of the four. If you see someone complaining that “AI isn’t that useful.” That’s not an AI problem. That’s a fluency problem.

You don’t need a CS degree or a background in machine learning to be fluent. You need curiosity, a little patience, and the willingness to treat AI like a collaborator rather than a vending machine.

We’re at an early enough stage where getting fluent now puts you genuinely ahead. That window won’t stay open forever.

Ready to build this for real?

Anthropic Academy’s AI Fluency course is exactly where I’d send anyone who wants to go beyond the surface. It’s free, it’s practical, and it covers the kind of foundational thinking that makes everything else click. Go take it —

👉Anthropic Academy — AI Fluency Course

title: The Starting Over Fantasy
content:
The Starting Over Fantasy

Annie’s core memory was of her and her best friend planning a runaway when they were seven. They talked about all the ways they’d hide their tracks, how long they’d have before their parents realized, and how they’d live in a cave in a forest far away from where their parents and teachers could find them. They made inventory lists of people they’d invite and trust with information about their whereabouts. They even made a detailed packing list including ropes to hang their clothes after washing them at a forest stream.

At 30, alone at home in a sleepy village in Germany, Annie was on Homefinder.com, looking up the average rent in Melbourne, Australia, daydreaming of starting over yet again. She had moved cities 6 times since she left her home in Malabar, India, but she had yet to find a place where she could feel at home. Her friend who planned the grand escape with her as a child was now a mother of 3 kids, refusing to step out of the home except to the mommy’s meetups and family gatherings. While Annie dreamed of a life in Australia surfing the waves on weekends and finding odd jobs throughout the week to sustain herself, at her age, life had become real. Annie knew deep down that if she couldn’t find a home in Bremen, Düsseldorf, Berlin, Leonberg, Karlsruhe, or Munich, she probably wouldn’t find one in Australia either. It wasn’t a lack of websites or jobs that stopped her, it was her deep sense of being a stranger no matter where.

Tired and beaten up by the futility of surfing, Annie logged out and looked at her phone expectantly. There was no familiar notification symbol for WhatsApp, and no one had called or dm-ed her on Instagram. Robotically she scrolled through every social media app she had on her phone. Another couple married, another friend was celebrating her child’s 5th birthday, a childhood teacher who relentlessly posted awkward selfies to her Facebook. Frustrated by the plasticity of it all, she closed all the apps and retired to bed.

The clock ticked on, while Annie lay there unaffected by passing time. It was 03:00 AM, but sleep wouldn’t take pity on her. Her recently ex-boyfriend would now be on his way to India, to be wedded in a few weeks to a beautiful, younger, richer bride of a higher caste than her. The thought made her want to throw up, but she would endure. It has been a few months already.

„Ding!“ the WhatsApp notification gave her another reason to pick up her phone. It was morning in India, and her mother was active in the family group. „Good morning Lovely family ❤ ❤. “ Annie ignored the painful cheerfulness that her mother displayed. She wondered how she endured a life that Annie passionately planned and escaped.

Chapter 2

All her life, Annie felt trapped in her childhood home, unable to get anything right, unable to ever dress to her family’s satisfaction, to get grades at school to their satisfaction, to be as smart, intelligent, well-kept, or bright as everyone else around her. Nothing was enough. When she moved to Germany, all that had changed.

Germany had been kind to her. Her grades were good, and job opportunities came knocking after she graduated in Informatics. The immense castles, spotless roads, polite men, and the never-ending lust for travel hit her like a fresh wave of life, and she surfed through her twenties. Memories from home were like a distant mirage. She could start new and she did. Life was great until she settled down. Slowly, the job grew on her, and the endless list of chores, taxes, and paperwork became the focus of her life. While in India, she had frantically escaped from piercing scrutiny and judgment, now, after 10 years, she missed being noticed at all. When the only person she was attached to walked out the door for a better city, better job, and a better woman, that straw broke the camel’s fragile back.

It has now been 6 months since she ate or slept like a normal person. Showing up at work was a chore, friendships seemed plastic, and she couldn’t bear to be around forced smiles and laughter. She had no family or close circle of people around her in this little sleepy village and the Germans didn’t exactly open their arms and friend-circles to take her in. It was taboo in the Indian community to be without a man at 30 and the German community barely cared for what was in their neighbor’s life. Going home was no option, the thought of submitting to her difficult father seemed dreadful to Annie — and once again, after all these years, Annie was stuck.

It occurred to her that this wasn’t the first time she was alone. She had moved from job to job, relationship to relationship, country to country. The sense of belonging and acceptance she craved to feel was always just around the corner but never quite there.

The sun crept up from among the buildings and through her large glass window. Thankful that she could stop trying to sleep, Annie got up from bed, getting ready for a job she dreaded. „For whom?“ she thought as she brushed her teeth for probably the 1000th time in the same mirror, in the same lonely apartment.

It was still 5 AM. Too soon to work, too late to fall asleep again. Annie took a long look in the mirror “Maybe he’d have stayed if I wasn’t so fat.” She knew very well that this wasn’t the reason. Reluctantly she walked up to the treadmill in her living room, untouched since she bought it half a year ago. She turned on the TV and lazily walked on the machine. She tuned into a travel vlog on YouTube, a story from Australia, as she longingly pictured herself making the great escape.

“Alrighty mates! Welcome back to my channel! Today, I’m taking you on an epic adventure into the legendary Australian Outback. Right now we are watching the sunrise over Uluru, this legendary ancient monolith in the most beautiful red hue I’ve seen in my life…”

She turned up the speed as she watched, perhaps there is something to look forward to in life after all. 6 Kilometers per hour, then 7, then 9… Soon she was running as fast as she could, as if trying to escape her living room. She could no longer hear the TV amidst the din of her machine. Tears started to flow down her cheeks, and she didn’t wipe them off. She held on to the arms of her treadmill but didn’t stop. 12 kmh, 14 kmh…

All of a sudden, the TV went off, and the room faded into a purple glow. Annie could neither see anything around her nor hear. She could only feel her legs in motion, refusing to stop. An undying, prolonged beep as if from a broken computer program, engulfed her surroundings. Annie felt herself fall backward into an abyss.

The sun pierced her eyelids in a reddish glow. Annie was now conscious and dazed. She felt her arms pressing against what felt like gravel. Her body ached like it had just been in the washing machine. She opened her eyes to a clear blue sky and scorching heat. Slowly she hoisted herself up to a sitting position, confused as to why she didn’t see her blue-gray blanket or the usual dull yellow walls of her living room. Around her was a vast expanse of what seemed like a desert. A scorpion approaching quickly made her jump and tumble to the side.Where the h** am I?

While she dusted herself off and looked around, miles away, she saw it.Am I dreaming?!The silhouette was unmistakable. She must have seen it a thousand times in the last few months. The Uluru. She was either dreaming or she just actually woke up in Australia!

title: Inside The Head of an Anxious Jane
content:
Inside The Head of an Anxious Jane

A year ago, I was looking up from rock bottom. Thanks to the wonderful people I met on my way, I am better than ever. There is a way out of anxiety. You are not alone, no one is alone. As proof, here is a transcript of my every day from back then.

This means nothingIt’s not even a real successIt’s not your doing, you're just luckyYou would never have been without that happy coincidence

“Something is going to explode"Like a self-taught astrologerspeaking with experience“it always does after things are right”

Why am I so happy?Maybe I left the stove on-My home is surely burnt to the ground when I get homeThings can’t be this perfect.

Why is this workingI didn't fix it yet.Did I do it right the first time?That can’t be.

Maybe he just wants to trick meNobody is this nice.Maybe everything I achieve.is because I am a woman.

Is it ok to be this person?Are other people different?Can I imagine a world where I am someone else?Why is everyone else so “normal”

Will they still hang with meIf I “come out” as me?I’ll just smile,better safe than sorry.

You just read another post from In Fitness And In Health:a health and fitness community dedicated to sharing knowledge, lessons, and suggestions to living happier, healthier lives.

title: The 2021 Poem
content:
The 2021 Poem

You know how although a part of you knowsthat it is just a new YYYY,another feels hopeful like you're watching title creditson an IMDB 9.5?

May you always look forward and expect the best,whether in life or in a soap,May no anti-climax ever dim your hope.

You know how in DecemberJanuary feels dreadful, cold, and far away?But in January, January feels like a new chance,Although nothing really changed?

May your days be full of manufactured happinessMay there always be pleasant surprises

You know, how you can call anyone any day,But new year’s feel like a special excuse.to send gifs and emojis and display love,because today, you can let loose?

May you always give and be loving,And may it always cost you nothing.

May you go out more than the year before,If you can’t, may you find love for the indoors,may you believe everyone who gives you compliments,may your veggies last longer in the warm seasons,May you discover a new problem to worry aboutThe pandemic and global warming are no more around,May you never find out about anything bad said about you,May you dream of success and also want to make it true.May you be brazenly foolish because of confidence and not because of fearand most of all,May you have a happy new year!

title: A Guide to Freelance Journalism
content:
A Guide to Freelance Journalism

According to the London School of Journalism

Writers see the world through their eyes, journalists through the eyes of others.

I recently came across a guide published by the London School of Journalism on getting started with professional Freelance writing. I thought I already knew enough to be a freelance writer but some lessons from this textbook really lit up my day. Most of the lessons from this article are specific to the field of writing for journalism. But the format of thinking and the “formula” for developing a piece of work can be applied to other forms of writing as well.

Here are some condensed notes on what professional journalists say about Freelance writing. This article is divided into the following sub-sections.

Why you write (and should write if you want to get paid)

For whom you write

Profiling your readers and editors

Pitching

How not to write

Why do you write?

You can tell yourself that you want to write for passion. Because you care about the art form, because for writing itself. You might find it deplorable to write for money, like some sort of cheating on your art.

Say what you want, unless someone is willing to pay for what you write, it is of little consequence.

And just like in any other business, the customer is always right. Writing might have many causes, but the largest of them is to be read.

Difficult as it is to admit, it is the job of the writer to make himself be read, liked, and paid. It is all in a package. Not one of the above three conditions suffices to make a writer, you need all three of them.

Where do you get started, if you want to be read, liked, and paid? Here you go.

Understand what you like to write about

Broadly speaking, there are three types of writing in journalism. News reporting, feature writing, and sport. As you read these three titles, you already know what your area of love is.

If you are a writer on Medium, I assume that you are at leastkind-ofinto feature writing. Each of these three goals asks for a different style of writing.

Feature writing can either be about a vast array of topics, or in a specific niche. Each of these two styles come with their own pros and cons.

If you are a generic writer, such as a writer of fiction, you have a large audience base. There is clearly a lot of market for fiction and fantasy. At the same time, it comes with a lot of competition to make your voice heard.

On the other hand, if you are a writer of niche topics of esoteric interests, you have a much smaller but specific market. Competition is relatively low, but of higher quality. Understand your market and your competition.

Understand what the readers want

If you are going to be either a mainstream journalist, or a feature writer on niche topics, don't limit yourself to reporting stories from your own back-yard. there is one thing you have to bear in mind more than anything else:

Journalism is about people.

It is not about you, it is not about your personal experiences, it is not about your grandmother and it is not about what your boss thinks. It is an objective, impartial piece of research work and it needs to be written like that.

You have to work on keeping your work about the interviews, facts and figures, balance and impartiality. Your job is to identify the sources and put perspectives on it. The story on the other hand, tells itself.

Understand what your editor wants

If you are looking to be paid, you want to write for a major commercial publication or a media outlet. Every newspaper, publication, TV channel, or radio broadcast has its own voice. It has its own style, called the “house-style”. This includes design, language, content, and perspective.

Each source of information has its own target demographic to which the content and style are tailored.

Do some research and understand what the publication wants.

Are their readers rich, young, middle-aged, unemployed?

If your pitch and your sample article hit all the right spots, it is difficult for the editor to not ask you to shut up and take his money.

Where is your market?

A freelancer by definition is paid for exactly the amount of work done for the employer. He is not bound by the employer per hour, neither is he exclusive. For a freelance journalist, the internet is the oyster. A lot of what you read on the internet, starting from Buzzfeed and pop-sugar to Huffington post, might be written by freelance journalists.

It is a part of your job as a freelance writer to hunt for the correct market and not just shoot blinds and hope there will be a hit.

Google for newspapers, glossy magazines, and other small and big publications in an online media directory if you want to get your work published.

Then find out what the subscription costs, get some editions as a sample, get a feel for the target reader base. Look for the sponsors, do you see any big names? If it is an expensive publication, expect that the readership also differs from that of a publication for pennies.

At the beginning, especially target those publications which are small and have a dedicated space for your niche topic of writing. It is not the worst idea to look overseas in English speaking countries to find willing editors who publish pieces from abroad.

Choose a publication that you read, know, and relate with instead of directly aiming for the big fish whose inboxes are probably full. This will give you leverage when you apply bigger and also some experience as a novice writer. Don’t ever send a pitch without knowing what the publication habits of the publisher are. Else you are just waiting for an automated reject.

Prepare your pitch

Take extreme care of how your email package looks. Take care to see that your article has the same tone and tenor which the publication usually assumes. Include pictures where necessary. Keep it under the word limit.

Be professional and keep your reporting accurate and entertaining.

Brownie points if your article is timely and focuses on current affairs with a new perspective.

The most important task is to be persistent. Keep sending in your pitches and well-timed articles on time. A large part of being a journalist is to be timely and to produce quick publish-ready wonders in small amounts of time. Here are some things you can check right off the bat:

Keep your formatting simple and don't go overboard with colors and fonts.

Keep it in size 12 pt. on MS.Word and in a common font like Calibri.

Don't have awkward spacing gaps.

Include your contact information on the top right and number the papers. Write “end” at the end and “more…” on other papers.

Every paper should contain your catchline in under 10 words on the top right. (eg. Women’s team brings home Gold).

Check-in in advance what formats of documents and image resolutions are required by your publisher.

Ask for a publication style guide if possible, here is an example of theGuardian-style guide. Feel free to use it for your draft if you don't know the style of the house.

Thus you will remove all fore-seeable hassles.

If you have issues with grammar or some doubts about the use of a certain comma or a certain word, don't be shy to go back to the basics once in a while. Mistakes are not uncommon among the most experienced journalists. Get tools like Grammarly, if you can.

How not to write

If the reader has to read any, ANY, sentence twice, it is a bad article. If there are loose ends and open questions, it is a bad article. If there is a logical flaw, or if the reader has to look away for a minute to see if there is logic to the flow, it is a bad article.

That said, the newspaper, as opposed to a work of science has the need to be entertaining. Don't dot it with savant jargon, don't show off your skills and knowledge, explain like to a child.

Be clear, edit until the removal of one more word makes the article meaningless. Most of all,

Be entertaining.

There was a time in the past where reading was reserved for an elite few. Those days are now gone. Writing must be understood by everybody. keep it simple, clear, and unambiguous. If someone reads and gives you feedback that they didn't understand a sentence or a part of the story, take it seriously.

Those were some basics to get started with freelance writing, journalism in particular. Getting published is realistic. Like any other job, there is a demand for good and new ideas. The internet is a place that never runs out of information and you one of those people keeping it alive. So don't be afraid to pursue your freelance writing dream.

If you are interested in literature, technology, or social competence, connect with me. Here is myMediumprofile, and here is myLinkedin.

title: 10 Super Useful Tips For Readable Python Script
content:
10 Super Useful Tips For Readable Python Script

Python tips for beginners

Python is known for its readability and simplicity. More and more users are taking to python as it slowly replaces traditional analytics tools because of the ease and flexibility in data manipulation it offers.

However, as you write more complex code, it’s easy to fall into bad habits that can make your code harder to read and understand. Here are 10 things to keep in mind when cleaning your Python code:

1. Use meaningful variable names

Avoid using single-letter variable names or generic names like “temp” or “list”. Instead, use meaningful names that clearly describe the purpose of the variable.

2. Keep lines short

Limit the length of your lines to around 79 characters. This makes it easier to read your code on smaller screens and helps prevent horizontal scrolling.

3. Use white space:

Use blank lines and indentation to separate different sections of your code and make it easier to read.

4. Use docstrings

Use docstrings to document your code and explain what different functions or classes do.

5. Use comments

Use comments to explain any non-obvious parts of your code or to provide context for what you’re doing.

6. Use consistent naming conventions

Use consistent naming conventions for variables, functions, and classes. This makes it easier to read and understand your code.

7. Use the correct data types

Use the correct data types for your variables. For example, use a dictionary instead of a list when working with key-value pairs.

8. Keep functions short

Keep your functions short and focused. Avoid having a single function that does too many things.

9. Use modules and packages

Make modules and packages out of your code to organize your code and make it easier to reuse.

10. Use version control

Use version control to keep track of your code and make it easier to roll back to an earlier version if needed.

By following these tips, you can make your Python code more readable, maintainable, and efficient.

Remember, taking the time to clean your code will make it easier to work with in the long run and will also make it easier for other people to understand and use your code.

title: As a Business Analyst, Here Are Your Next Career Options.
content:
As a Business Analyst, Here Are Your Next Career Options.

and how to get there.

So far I’ve mostly only worked for big firms. I was at Deloitte, KPMG, Bosch, Volkswagen, and currently at Audi.

In almost all of those places, I performed Data science and/or Analysis of some sort. I was first an IT Risk consultant, then a Machine Learning Research student, then a supply chain data analyst, and now a Sales Planning Analyst.

The world of analytics is brilliant and endless. Though your work may be fun, you might feel stuck at the bottom of the ladder. Because, as important as it is to have data, the non-technical people dealing with your work cant RESPECT for the process that creates this data. They just want to run with the results and you might end up feeling taken for granted.

So how do you go up the ladder? You either make more money or take a higher job role or both.

Here are some ideas I’ve been toying with.

1. Certify

The most sure-shot way to go somewhere is to equip yourself with the right tools. If you already use some visualizing tools, certify yourself in them in your free time. This shouldn't come at extra time cost since you already know the tools. It improves your employability for higher pay.

In addition to these tools, learn new tools for your next steps.

As a Data Analyst, you have good prospects in:

Data security and governance

Data Engineering, and Data Science.

Forecasting and predictive modeling using ML.

Project Management in Data Sciences.

If you want to feel “growth” in your position actually and psychologically, it really pays to stop analyzing and start managing. Learn how to delegate, coordinate and convince people. Try roles in project management as a scrum master or a product owner. For this, qualify yourself in a suitable Project Management role in your domain like:

Agile Project Management.Here is a course I took.

SaFe certification (Scaled Agile Framework)

PMP (If you are already a project manager of some kind)

2. Go back to School

If you have the opportunity to take a sabbatical, consider using it to get a degree in your specific field of expertise. Now that you have experienced the industry you can learn more consciously.

Get legitimate, full-time, degrees in your field. While choosing a course, don’t be tempted and stray too far from the path you have already made. Build upon it.

3. Apply for a higher role or a new Industry

Once you qualified yourself in your topic of choice start applying to roles in that area. Look for a mix of your tasks + the new role so that the switch is not too sudden and too much.

Keep your current job, and don’t quit emotionally. In the field of Data analytics, opportunities are plenty.

Your move can either be

To a new industry

To a new location

To a higher pay

A better job role.

Since every industry needs analysts, it is a brilliant opportunity for you to experience more in your field. You could consider research, automobile, aviation, medicine — you name it! You are wanted everywhere!

As a heads up, a career in consulting is not my first choice. I have always enjoyed working more for companies that make or sell their own products as opposed to those trying to sell services to these companies. You could make such companies your concrete next-step goal.

I am Sruthi, a Sales Analyst at Audi. For content on career, books and analytics, follow me here on medium. Create with me with the following referral link, it’ll make us both happy ;)

title: The Skin-care Scam
content:
The Skin-care Scam

Forget glass-skin. Do what actually matters.

I’ve been obsessed with skincare for as long as I can remember. Recently I jumped on the product-based skin-care bandwagon expecting miraculousglass-skinresults.

Spoiler Alert, I’ve never had worse skin in my life.

My mother was the original skincare queen — she was an expert in all this before the internet. Her method was simple: grape juice for brightening, tomato pulp for exfoliation, milk-cream and coconut oil for moisture. No preservatives, no fancy serums, just things from the kitchen. It always worked.

I followed in her footsteps until I got sucked into the world of products — it was hard to resist the science-y words and I’m paying in more than one ways.

The beginning of the AHA/BHA Disaster

It started when I first heard about AHA and BHA. Reddit swore by Chemical exfoliation. “It’ll give you glass skin,” “It’ll unclog your pores, fade dark spots, change your life”.

Umm, okay. Maybe what got me here won’t take me further? Maybe I need more?

Sure it briefly changed my life — For all of one day, I was glowing. White heads and black heads of all sorts came off.Wow!

But within a week after that, my face was red, sensitive, and I had about five new red pimples. I never had so many at once, not even as a teen.

Reddit told me that I had Over-Exfoliated, now I had to be doubly careful about everything. Oh but there’s a cure for this too, of course — more product.

Enter Niacinamide and Salicylic Acid

“They’ll calm the inflammation,” “They’ll fix your barrier,” “They’ll give you glass skin”.

Big surprise, I bought them all.

And because I have a tiny bathroom, half-used serums, creams, and cleansers now keep falling off different ledges for the lack of space. Oh, did I mention my depleated savings and worse skin?

Sigh.

The Makeup Mistake

About the same time as skin care, I started experimenting with make up. The boost it gave my look wasso friggin awesome. I loved the pink look blush gave me, the way skin tint made me glow like I was a source of light.

When the pimples showed, I tried just a little foundation to even things out. Some concealer for the breakouts. Best colour match, bestwork-look, I got sucked into yet another world.

Another big mistake.

Every product, no matter how “non-comedogenic” the label claimed, made my skin bumpier and drier. It looked awesome through the day, but internally, it just added another layer of irritation and damage.

My skin clearly didn’t need more products. It needed to breathe.

Infinite Tiny Bumps

About 4 weeks into this experiment, small bumps appeared on my face — not acne, not irritation, just… bumps. No matter what I tried, they wouldn’t leave.

I was angry with every bottle of product in my bathroom. If they weren’t that expensive, I’d have thrown it all in trash without a second thought.

But here we are.

Sigh again.

DIY Revival

By now I wanted my smooth skin back no matter what. I’ll live with my pimple scars and all, but please god, give me my normal skin back!

I ditched the products and returned to fruit masks, vegetable pastes, and kitchen remedies. While they made my skin feel nice, they weren’t undoing the damage the products had caused.

Perhaps the DIY stuff had some preservatives in them?

Maybe that’s why they weren’t working the same way they did when my mom made them fresh. So I decided only to use fresh fruit and veggies, and plant oils.

Coconut Oil

I kid you not. This is a real story.

My mom used coconut oil for everything skin related. Even itching and texture.

And guess what? It worked! And OVER NIGHT, I had an improvement. Since it is not easy to identify in photos I got no proof.

My skin, which had been freaking out for months, finally calmed down. The glow came back. The tiny bumps? Slowly fading. Nothing fancy, no active ingredients, no multi-step rituals — just a single ingredient I always knew.

The Skin-Care Scam: What Really Works

Skincare is a billion-dollar industry that thrives on convincing you that your skin is a problem to fix. Chuck that.

Remember this

Not all trends are meant for your skin. AHA and BHA may work for some, but they wrecked mine, might wreck yours too.

Skincare doesn’t have to be expensive to be effective. In fact over-doing causes more damage than help.

DIY isn’t always perfect either. Fresh, simple ingredients? Amazing. But homemade concoctions with hidden preservatives? Nope.

The simplest solutions are usually the best. Coconut oil was in my house all along, and it turned out to be the answer.

I won’t pretend I’ve sworn off all store-bought products — I still love a good moisturizer. But now, I balance it with what actually works for my skin, not just what the internet tells me to try.

Be careful what you let on your skin. Most of it is just snake oil.

title: Many ways of Pivoting with Pandas
content:
Many ways of Pivoting with Pandas

Data exploration hacks for Pythoneers

Pandas offers an endless number of data exploration tools that are intuitive to use and are worth having at the tip of your fingers. This article explores one such tool,pivoting. On multiple occasions, data is obtained in its raw state as a linear tabular/unpivoted/stacked form as shown under:

This is a very convenient form of data to work with, especially when dealing with visualization tools like Tableau, Power BI, Qlik, and the like. Most of the interpretations or pivots such as “orders per customer/date/product” are inbuilt to these tools.

A pivoted form of such a table makes visualization with tools or further processing with pandas tedious. However, there are scenarios where it might be necessary to look at the raw data in a tabular form, where cross-tables or pivots are the most preferable and intuitive forms of presentation for the human eye. This is also a preferred format to extract the data from the source since it condenses the data into fewer lines and avoids repetitive entries. Here is an example of the above data pivoted with excel(Gesamtergebnis means Grand Total, Pardon my German):

I will compare various forms of pivoting with pandas in this article. Pandas offers the following functions to pivot data:crosstab, pivot, pivot_table, and groupby. For this example, you only need the following libraries:

import pandas as pd

Pivoting with Crosstab

Crosstab is the most intuitive and easy way of pivoting with pandas. It automatically counts the number of occurrences of the column value for the corresponding row. You could use the aggregation function (aggfunc) to specify a different aggregation to fill in this pivot.

I now want to see how much total quantity of each product was ordered by each customer (by his customer ID):

orders_db = pd.read_excel(‘path/to/orders/orders_database.xlsx’)

crosstab_pivot = pd.crosstab(orders_db.CustomerID,orders_db.Product,values=orders_db.Quantity,aggfunc=’sum’,margins=True,margins_name=’Total_Qtty’).fillna(0)

Crosstab is intuitive in how it works. Since we wanted to see the sum of “Quantity” in the pivot table result, we passed this column to values and aggregation function as ‘sum’. The kwargs ‘margin’ and ‘margin_name’ specify if we want the output to contain a grand total column analogous to excel.

In crosstab, if you do not specify the values argument, it returns the frequency of occurrence of the column value:

crosstab_pivot = pd.crosstab(orders_db.CustomerID,orders_db.Product,margins=True,margins_name=’Total_Qtty’).fillna(0)

If you want to group more than one column/rows to the pivot, you only have to pass the columns as a list like this:

crosstab_pivot = pd.crosstab([orders_db.CustomerID,orders_db.Date,orders_db.Address],[orders_db.Product],values = orders_db.Quantity,aggfunc=’sum’,margins=True,margins_name=’TotalQtty.’).fillna(0)

pd.set_option('max_columns',100)

print(crosstab_pivot)

with a keyword argument called “normalize,” you can choose to express all your values as a percent of the total. Include normalize= “index”/ “columns”/ “all” to normalize your values to the total. Here is the output for normalization across columns:

Pivoting with pivot

pandas offers a pretty basic pivot function that can only be used if the index-column combinations are unique. It does not make any aggregations on the value column nor does it simply return a count like crosstab. Take the same example as above:

The Customer IDPRSDNTordered the sameProduct Atwice with different order numbers. Since the pivot function does not perform aggregations, it does not know what to fill for “value” in that column.

pivot_db =  orders_db.pivot(index = ‘Customer ID’,columns = ‘Product’,values=’Quantity’)

This would work for a unique combination of index+column like in this example:

pivot_db = orders_db[orders_db.Name==’Holmes’].pivot(index =‘Customer ID’,columns = ‘Product’,values=’Quantity’)

When we have such ambiguous entries, which is most of the time the case in real-world applications, we use the more flexible pivot_table function which allows for aggregations.

Pivoting with pivot_table

The Pivot table function is similar to a crosstab

pd.pivot_table(orders_db, values=’Quantity’,index=[‘Customer ID’,’Address’],columns=’Product’,aggfunc=[‘sum’,len,’mean’],margins=True,fill_value=0)

you could also pass multiple aggregation functions to return a set of columns for each aggregation function. Here [sum, len, mean] have been passed, len meaning the count of entries. You can also extend the same to multiple values by passing a list analogous to the index list.

Whether you use pandas crosstab or a pivot_table is a matter of choice. Note that you don’t need your data to be in a data frame for crosstab. You can crosstab also arrays, series, etc.

Pivoting with Groupby

Groupby is a very handy pandas function that you should often use. Let’s check out how wegroupbyto pivot. We want to get the sum of the quantities ordered by the customer per product:

Method 1 :

orders_db.groupby(['Customer ID','Address','Product'])['Quantity'].sum()

Method 2:

groupedby_db = orders_db.groupby([‘CustomerID’,’Address’,’Product’]).agg({‘Quantity’:’sum’})

In this method, you can specify how you want to aggregate each column. (Example, If you had another column called ‘weight’ for which you want mean, you could specify that as:

orders_db.groupby([‘CustomerID’,’Address’,’Product’]).agg({‘Quantity’:’sum’, ‘weight’:’mean’})

You could also apply filters to your group with the handy filter function of groupby for ease. Say you want to see only customers and their addresses when they ordered at least 4 or more products:

big_orders = orders_db.groupby([‘CustomerID’,’Product’]).filter(lambda x:x[‘Quantity’].sum()>3)

The filter takes a lambda function argument which specifies the function that a specific column aggregate should satisfy.

That’s about basic pivoting with pandas. See you later with some more pandas hacks!

title: Don’t Be This Guy
content:
Don’t Be This Guy

Checking your narcissism before someone else does

Here is a bunch of things, which if you find in yourself, you might want to consciously correct it, for the benefit of your personal life and relationships.

It is a paradox — a narcissist finds nothing wrong with him or herself and a liar might be so delusional as to believe his or her own lie to some extent. Don’t see the word narcissism as a mean and negative personality trait. See it is an illness that needs a cure.

1. Don’t: Lie about verifiable facts

Everybody lies. Most mentally healthy people tell white lies. Like “my dog ate my homework” or “ I was hung up in traffic” or that “ I can’t come because I’m under the weather”.

If you find yourself making up facts or situations that did not happen way too often for no specific end, you might be ill. Trust me the listener knows by your second lie that it's a pattern.

You might feel compelled to do this to make yourself look good. Don’t. Everybody can tell.

If you are making up facts that somewhere in the corner of your mind ring false, don’t say them. This might be hard at first, but trust me, it ruins trust and relationships faster than it builds your image up.

2. Don’t: Always have negative pavlovian responses

There is constructive advice, and then are reflexive reactions when you find yourselfobviously superior.

If you hear “I love eating Chinese noodles with Jam”, try stopping yourself from saying “Really? Yuck!”. It is disrespectful. Don’t wonder aloud “If his car has always been this small”. Don’t visit one at home and say “This is small, how do you manage!”.

Leave them alone.

If you find yourself quick to jump at something harmless and personal only to turn it negative, stop. It takes conscious effort. Try replacing it with silence instead, you don't have to be overly positive just for the sake of it.

3. Don’t: Be constantly under-impressed

Not everyone has to be your cheerleader or give you constructive feedback. However, if you find yourself constantly under-impressed with other’s achievements, evaluate yourself.

It is hard to feel good about competition, especially if you are not on top. But it is also beautiful to have a company that helps you grow. It is never a contest.

You don’t have to bake a cake every time he or she takes a successful sh*t. But if you consciously refrain from congratulating him or her on achievements they see as very important, reevaluate yourself.

4. Don’t: Treat every conversation like a competition

If you have the need to top your friend’s weekend trip with a better weekend story, his success at work with an equal or bigger success story, his breakfast with a healthier breakfast story, don't. This connection is not going to end well for both of you.

You are just sabotaging their little joys in life and making them appear insignificant. If someone is happy about an achievement only to be told “Yeah? That’s nothing, I already did that as a baby, thrice”, it doesn’t help him grow. It doesn’t help you grow as a person.

Jealousy, being at a bad spot in life, feeling of inadequacy might all lead one to lie about your success stories. But remember, you are already good at something, no one is looking down on you and you are already loved and wanted.

Don’t: Take credit for “not your ideas”

If you find yourself acknowledging a compliment where your contribution has been minimal, stop in your steps, and be honest.

If you conspicuously do not bring up the one who really worked for the said achievement, or plagiarise, remember that this is going to be found out sooner than you think.

Lying shows in your eyes and actions more clearly than you imagine, like a cat closing its eyes while drinking stolen milk. Everybody sees it but the cat.

Be a good coworker or friend and grow honestly.

All of us fall short somewhere. But there is hope. Try and don’t do it!

title: New Year or a Starting Over Fantasy?
content:
New Year or a Starting Over Fantasy?

or am I just getting older?

Aweek before the year turns new, I turn a year older. Every year I look forward to going “forward in life” without dramatically changing much. “Incremental changes”, I think — Wake up sooner, run longer and faster, earn a little more, travel to atleast one new place, do a teeny bit better to my family and friends.

But by the end of every year , the drama is unmistakable. A completely different movie begins.

“Ajeeb Daastan hain yeh, Kahaan shuru kahaan khatam”…plays the radio at the back while I write this. sigh. No, I’m tired. Give me normal.

Last year, it was the end of a relationship. The year before that, a new job role, the year before that the beginning of a relationship that ended last year. lol (?).

Each time I turn a page to write the next chapter, the entire book changes — The genre, setting of the era, location, weather, characters and even myself. It feels like I begin the year as a somebody and end it as somebody else.

After 33 of these new years, I’ve not yet learnt to let it be. There is no such thing as “normal”. Look out of the window and absorb what you see, becase next year, the view might change.

Does it scare me? I can’t tell anymore if normal or new scares me more. This year, I restarted in Berlin, consorting with people I’ve never met before as if they were my best friends, solving problems at work like I’ve always known the BMW factory floor since 1942.

My new-year’s resolution — I want to hold on to my reality and never let it go. If it runs away, I will chase it. I will change nothing. I will do everyday exactly like I would have done it last year. May be constant is the new change I need.

Just like everyone was saying it last year — Thanks for coming to my ted talk. ** insert another cliche ** ,over and out.

title: Thanks For Making The Second Edition of Bayern Baithak a Huge Success!
content:
Thanks For Making The Second Edition of Bayern Baithak a Huge Success!

Here is how you can be a part of the next meet-up.

After two editions of Bayern Baithak with an audience of 100, another stage is coming up.

In this newsletter, find out more about the founders Deepika and Sivani and how you can take your art to the next level.

About the Founders: Deepika and Sivani

Deepika Panchamukhi, Bharatnatyam dancer.

Deepika has been practicing Bharatnatyam since she was 6. She graduated with a Bachelor's in Performing Arts from the University of Mumbai. Along with being a principal dancer of the Sankhya Dance Company, she trains, curates, and choreographs aspiring dancers professionally.

Deepika is keen on developing a form of Bharatanatyam that suits both traditional and modern dance movements. She founded her institute ‘ārohaṇa’ in Munich and teaches kids and adults since 2020.

She co-founded projects like Varnam Salon Deutschland and Bayern Baithak to provide platforms to artists in Germany. You can find out more about her atwww.deepikapanchamukhi.comDeepika Potdar Panchamukhi - YouTube

Sivani Saraswatula, classical singer and Bharatnatyam dancer.

Sivani is a singer in multiple genres, Indian classical being her focus. She is also a playback singer for movies in Telugu and Kannada languages.

In 2015, Sivani won the Telugu reality singing competition “Paduta Teeyaga” judged and hosted byPadma VibhushanLate Shri S.P. Balasubramanyam. With 20 aired seasons, the show has been viewed tens of millions of times. It remains the most popular Telugu reality show to date.

Alongside singing, she is also a trained Bharatanatyam dancer with experience of nearly a decade. Sivani does music therapy sessions in her instituteSamathva,a space for Indian classical music & dance in Germanywheresheteaches dance and music.

As an active volunteer withthe Isha foundation, Sivanihas performed for audiences of millions spread over 25 nations. Shehas been a part of the ‘Save Soil’ global campaign driven by Sadhguru Jaggi Vasudev for the cause of soil regeneration.

See more of Sivani’s work onYoutube — Sivani Saraswatula

Why Bayern Baithak?

With Bayern Baithak, Sivani and Deepika want to bridge the gap between the artist & the audience.

“You will enjoy the show, but also understand the art form and share our wonder.

We want to keep the classical arts alive. We want to collaborate with artists and present the Indian classical arts to the world.”

Who can come?

“If you are an enthusiast, this is for you.If you find classical art boring, this is for you too!Anybody can come! This is for more than just professional artists. You won’t be disappointed😉”

Where you can find us.

Instagram.@Bayernbaithak

Do we take more performers?

If you are a trained classical dance or music performer, send them a dm onInstagram. Deepika and Sivani would love to have you on board.

That is Awesome! When is the next meet-up?

April 2023. In Nürnberg.

Details of the date and location will be published on ourMedium pageandInstagram.

Stay posted and see you there!

Subscribe to our newsletter here and stay connected
