title: Life hacks for real-life drama
content:
Life hacks for real-life drama

Navigating the emotional struggle of every day

Who doesn’t deal with Drama on a daily basis? There is always someone out there to convince, cajole, or just plain talk to without really wanting to. Unpleasant as these situations might be, there are but a reminder of the human self that you cannot help but take to work and everywhere else, as much as you’d have liked to be square as a robot. Here are some drama-handling that made me a little more rehearsed for it all.

Oops! when you did it again

If you are sorry, guilty or feel like you have not done enough where you had to, it’s best to say it once and follow it up with actions rather than repeating your apologies or elucidating excuses.

This is not to say that you have to carry your guilt around owe reparations for the rest of your life or that you should be blind to your own faults. However, if you really mean your guilt, piling more words of self-incrimination just makes it harder for you to lift the weight off your chest.

Instead, take opportunities to prove your apology in action. If the deed is done and irreversible, understand that you’re human, apologize with grace and move on.

2. Haters are not going to hate

If you have this “vibe” from someone that they dislike you, this trick will help you get over it. Pretend as if they do not. Better still; pretend as if they LOVE having you around. Ask your college if he wants to get coffee in the break or share a video you find interesting. Offer your roommate some food or even tell her about your day casually.

Assuming your surroundings do not want you doesn’t make your job easier or you friendlier. If you can hold your act long enough, people will come around. They probably did not dislike you in the first place. If it doesn’t help, at least you did your part.

3. People are machines

In dealing with people, it helps many times to imagine them as an input-output relation than a complex, unfathomable mess. After all, that’s who we all are, complex machines but nevertheless responding to metaphoric buttons — love, hate, disappointment, success and the like.

The next time someone pushes you to an uncomfortable corner, think about what you can do to push the right button (but of course, not just for the heck of pushing it, you have to mean it). At the root everyone else wants pretty much the same as you do — to be respected, recognized, appreciated, cared about, loved, trusted, fed, complemented and that — not downplaying the bottom line here — Honestly.

4. Be the bigger person

Infinitely many petty unsavory things happen every day that on a particularly bad day simply throw you off the rails. A row with your boss, a friend that keeps trying to one-up you in every conversation, a stray hair lock that just won’t sit down with the rest.

The most natural response is to defend yourself, tell someone away, convince yourself that this person isn’t for you or that its obviously not your fault. It may help the situation if you can “be the bigger person” and forego the obvious edge of “winning” you have with the other person, and talk things out, even if it sounds like a compromise on your side.

This of course is not to say that you have to patronize the other person, just like complements and pushing buttons, it has to be honest if it has to work. It is much easier if you don’t see it as a competition where only one of you is the winner and has a +1 on “drama score”. Imagine a world where no one is keeping score and both of you have better things to do anyway.

title: My Movie of the Month — American Beauty
content:
My Movie of the Month — American Beauty

I watchedAmerican Beautyas a child, but watching it again in your 30s is something else.

Directed by Sam Mendes and written by Alan Ball,American Beauty(1999) is a timeless classic. The film explores a mediocre suburban life and what lies beneath the facade of the normalcy of an ordinary middle-class family.

Kevin Spacey portrays Lester Burnham, a middle-aged man undergoing a midlife crisis, who becomes infatuated with his daughter’s teenage friend, Mena Suvari. His wife, on the other hand, is a materialistic and ambitious real estate agent desperate to maintain her image of success and control. Spacey’s portrayal of Lester’s transformation from a disillusioned office worker to a liberated individual seeking genuine happiness is compelling.

The iconic scene of rose petals floating above Angela Hayes (Lester’s teenage crush) perfectly catches the film’s theme of finding beauty in unexpected places, even within the midst of life’s chaos. Adding to this is the eery effect of beauty in destruction — The scenes with Lester’s daughter’s psycho boyfriend, who finds beauty in misfortune, death, and decay.

The narrative dissects the seemingly perfect suburban life, revealing the cracks beneath the surface. Themes of alienation, identity crisis, and the pursuit of happiness force you to confront uncomfortable truths about your own life and desires, and what you do despite them.

American Beautyis a thought-provoking critique of the middle-class culture, challenging societal norms and conventions. It explores themes of sexuality, materialism, and the search for purpose, all set against a picturesque yet suffocating suburban neighborhood.

The film certainly earned its widespread critical acclaim; its five Academy Awards include Best Picture, Best Director for Mendes, and Best Actor for Spacey.

American Beautyresonated with me more than the first time I watched it as a child. In the lines of the bookLolita,this movie is not as much about sex as you would think it is. You will unexpectedly reflect on your own complexities of the human experience. It certainly is a must-(re)watch, offering a deeply insightful and unforgettable cinematic and intellectual journey.

title: Short Story: A Life Not Lived
content:
Short Story: A Life Not Lived

A bird landed on Jon’s head. But for the first time, he was not angry. He was motionless, while the pigeon perched on the edge of his breakfast plate and pecked at his dry bread.

Jon lived a life he heartfully hated. There was nothing wrong with it, he just thought he deserved better. His kids were too dumb for his taste, his dead father was a loser, his wife was not smart or beautiful as he deserved, and his home was not classy as those of his rich coworkers’ homes.

Jon only chose battles he could win. He loved the fear his booming voice instilled. He loved to watch kids tremble when he arrived home.

Jon’s wife had a name, but no one would remember it. So why mention it anyway.

Like all good wives (her mother taught her), she gave her husband slavery and respect. Not once in their three decades of marriage did she question why she has to put up with this life.

If Jon doubted his own strength for a moment, he would bellow at her and watch her tremble until he felt stronger. She stayed and took it like a good Christian wife.

She was his punching bag on the bad days and his housemaid on the good days. When Jon made bad financial decisions, she would take up jobs and schedule them so as to not inconvenience Jon at his evening beer.

If she had a life before her marriage to Jon, she did not remember it. At some point, her slavery was easier than thinking about it.

In his 30s, Jon was sure he could make life happier for himself, and if they obeyed, for everyone else.

In his 40s, he was afraid that his misfortune was permanent. He might after all, never be rich and successful, with the perfect woman and kids who would make him proud.

In his 50s, Jon was sure that this was it, that people around him ruined his good life by being mediocre, ugly, and stupid.

At 60, he had given up. His disgust over things around him was permanent, it was too late to turn back and make new ways.

It was 15 years ago that his wife passed. In the weeks following her death, Jon was angry about how his shoes were unpolished and there was no breakfast on the table when he woke up.How could she inconvenience him, dying like that?

His thoughts did not linger. He quickly forgot all about her as he sunk himself in his phone and wrote angry comments on youtube videos in terrible grammar. By the time he looked up from his phone, it was past lunchtime.

He ate the remaining pieces of bread he had left from breakfast and went back to his couch for the sports channel. It was a routine he was used to for years now. But that day, something strange happened.

“Bring me a cold beer will ya!”Jon Hollered into an empty kitchen as if someone was listening. He had never done this before.

Soon after the words left his mouth, Jon felt cold and hollow.Was he losing his mind?

He picked up his phone, mostly to hear his own voice. He did not know this, but Jon felt the need for a story he could tell, a narrative he could call his own.

He called his son, his useless son who remained a convenience store clerk all his life.

To his surprise, somebody answered.“Guess your job lets you have a loads time to answer calls eh?”.

“Sorry, who is this?”

Perhaps it was his son, or perhaps his son did not have the same phone number. He was not going to honor this person with a question. He hung up.

Emma’s nephew had turned the volume all the way up.

By the time she came home from the supermarket, she could hear YouTube belting out tunes to the entire apartment. She ran up the stairs to prevent another ugly altercation with her angry neighbor.

She rushed in, turned the TV off, and stood still to hear the footsteps of an angry man approaching her door. But none came.

Days after that, no one knocked when her nephew dropped a glass vase to the floor in the middle of the night, when he invited his friends over for PlayStation, even when she vacuumed the apartment late in the evening.

Slowly the silence of the neighbor grew suspicious. When the quiet turned into a stench, she was certain.

Emma cried like a baby when she saw the dead man lifted through his window with a fire truck.

No one showed up to claim him. No one wanted ownership of his apartment or any of his belongings. They were all removed by the state one by one.

She wondered what his first name was, whether he lived and died a happy man. She cursed herself for not having tried harder,after all, he was a lonely old man.

Jon did not know, that after his passing, he would linger and watch.

He wished he didn’t have to see his rotting corpse plagued by pigeons and flies.

He wished someone came and at least cursed at his corpse. He wished he let the kid from the neighborhood in. The sight of Emma crying in the corner felt strange and beautiful to him.

Was this what love would feel like?If it looked this beautiful in its weakest form,what would it be like, to feel it for the people he knew,he wondered before disappearing forever.

title: Forbidden Love
content:
Forbidden Love

I lay, in love, a blanket over my thoughts

cozy under my loneliness, eyes closed

I think about your smiling face

No one can find out you are in there

But you are.

The warmth of your honest smile

brings a sharp tear of joy

that stops at the lid where the world cannot see it,

Because nobody may find out.

You are in there,

where I am allowed to hold you close.

Far from everything that will take you from me,

You are safe and mine.

title: 2 Month Review of being a New “Housewife.”
content:
2 Month Review of being a New “Housewife.”

Is it the insta tradwife-dream, or would I rather be employed?

I am a 30-something woman. I haven’t been unemployed since I graduated 12 years ago, except for 5 months when I moved to Germany and didn’t fully discover student employment yet.

Now in my early 30s (no kids), I am blogging away at 11 AM on a Tuesday, and the paycheck stopped coming in 2 months ago. When my mom asked me how I feel being a “housewife,” I instinctively snapped at her. It made me angry. But honestly, is it that bad, though?

I have all the time to spend at the Gym, work on my favourite new AI projects, catch up with friends and family who have the time, and, ofcourse, blog. This newfound freedom with no concrete finish line is exciting yet scary. It is not something I ever thought I’d have in my life. I thoroughly enjoyed it, until about now, when the fomo is creeping in.

The 1,2,3 of “Housewifing”

An enjoyable housewife's life needs 3 ingredients — (1) A non-jerk husband (who is employed, hopefully), (2) a very automated home, and (3) a decent degree of purchasing power.

My husband makes (1) very easy. He brings me flowers every week and never complains about my average home-keeping skills. He eats his meals the way they are cooked and not the way he’d ideally want them. He even tries to take over the kitchen and times our eating out schedules so that I don’t feel like I’ve been “cooking too much”. Once, I even caught him preparing a little dance at the door with flowers in his hands. “Can’t complain” is an understatement. If husbands were a movie, he’d be nominated for an Oscar in several categories and win in at least 3.

About (2), living with dishwashers, robot cleaners, and moppers does make life very easy. The thing about being a housewife isn’t that you MUST do all the cleaning, but you’re at home so much, and it begins to bother you if it's messy. Also, you end up having more time on hand than someone who goes away to work 9 hours a day. So yes, it does fall in your hands 99% of the time. Since most of the cleaning can be timed with an app, I mostly just load and unload the dishwasher and the washing machine. It’s work, but ok, I can’t complain.

Now (3), what is a “good amount of” purchasing power? I have enough savings to, say, buy a good eyelash curler or a foundation shade that’s a really good match. I also allow myself an occasional bicycle accessory or a good Matcha latte.

It’s always on the back of my mind that I need my savings to last as long as possible, open-ended. I always need enough money in the bank for a long-distance plane ticket and to cover rent for a few months. It sounds grim, but that’s what “emergency fund” means. I can swear by this — ladies, you need an emergency fund, employed or not.

Compare that to when I lived in Berlin and had a great salary — I went to all-day saunas every month, floated in therapeutic saline pools whenever I felt like it, got myself some good soulhouse-massages, and took a flight or train away whenever the scenery got boring. I freely swiped my card for a friend when we got a drink, and told off an intrusive family member if I felt like they were overstepping boundaries. That kind of purchasing power is more than “a good amount.” It is priceless.

Time vs. Freedom vs. Dignity

Being a housewife is great because it buys you time. But is it greater than being able to give? to yourself or others? No matter how much you are supported by your husband, it is difficult to buy yourself stuff you cannot always justify. And buying yourself stuff that you cannot justify is the ultimate sign of power and comfort.

And of course, most Indian women need to “prove themselves” financiallyand more, to be “allowed” to make their own decisions by family (not just the husband). That power to walk away and mind my business is definitely priceless.

The brightside — being a housewife is great because before you find your next job, your husband will (might) stand by you and you can have a worry-free job-search journey. And no other species in this world except a housewife has a worry-free job-search phase.

In this POV, it is a win-win ;)

title: A Solo Traveler’s Packing List For Bicycle Touring in Europe
content:
A Solo Traveler’s Packing List For Bicycle Touring in Europe

For about 7 days, stuff that fits in your bike pack.

I went on my first ever solo bike-packing last week, about 330 Km over 4 days. I started in Trento, Italy, went south, and then east, topped off with a mesmerizing end at Venice. An experience I’d never forget until the day I die.

This was my second ever 300+ KM bike tour, and the first one solo.

As someone who has no clue where life is headed, and a half-year-long turning thirty crisis, this bike trip was cleansing to the mind.

What’d I do differently if I did it again? Hear a lesser amount of music on the road and stayed with my silence more comfortably. The fun-est parts were where my earphones died out.

If you are planning a bike-packing trip on your own, here is a packing list I took with me. I did not camp or make my own food. I had the luxury of hostels, hotels, and Airbnb on the way (whichever one was the cheapest).

The packing list

For you to be completely comfortable and fearless in a foreign country, traveling as a lone woman (or a man…), Here are the things that I packed. I’ll also tell you what I’d leave out and what I wish I had packed more of.

Safety

Things to do for safe travels:

Keep an easily accessible note (Say in your mobile-phone holder on your handle) with your emergency contacts, their phone numbers, and emails in case you should get in trouble.

Carry a big, fat power bank (Very important.). A backup of it too if you can.

Take care to see that you don't run out of internet. Get a good data plan.

Write down the list of your hotels, accommodations, and their telephone numbers and addresses in case your phone should die or get lost.

Set up the emergency dial on your phone ( Something that can easily call the police or ambulance for help. Most android phones have this option for SOS)

Keep some cash in your breast pockets. Set up cashless payments in case your wallet should go awol.

All important things that you will need to get home — your passport, visa, cash, and credit cards, should be ON YOUR PERSON, not on the bike back.

If you are riding alongside cars, keep to a side and don’t make sudden-jerky movements or stop suddenly.

If you have noise-canceling headphones — Keep the ambient sound ON! you don’t want to let cars sneak up on you too late.

Gear for safety

Helmet, duh.

Gloves — If you hit the ground, you will probably fall palms-first.

A first aid kit.

Good and strong headlights, with additional batteries.

Pepper spray. Just in case. Keep it handy. (I did not take it, but I should have.)

In your bike pack

To wear

Cycling clothes — Depending on your chances to do laundry, take a pair every 2 days. If you plan well, you get away with having 2 or 3 pairs over the course of your biking.

Leisure clothes: Something light for when you are done for the day and just want to explore the city. 2 pairs for 7 days. It is REALLY important to travel light. Less volume is the key.

Something to sleep in. (These can be really thin cotton that doesn't take up much space.)

A towel. (Not the fancy ones you take to a spa, but a really compact sports towel that is large enough for the road, shower, and the beach. I got mine at decathlon, it folds into a very tiny cube.)

Rain jacket (Also something that can fold into a tiny cube)

A scarf to protect you from all the weather elements should you need it.Here is an example of a good cycling scarf.

Arm warmers (If it is late spring or early fall, it might get cold.).Here is an example.

I did not take this, but you might need a thin jacket for the chilly winds depending on where you are going.

Extra socks, a pair a day, unless you can wash.

Cycling goggles. You will need them against sun, insects, and dust. Get a good pair.

Underwear, a pair a day, unless you wash.

Swimming clothes.

Miscellaneous for you

An insect repellant. I took it on multiple cycle trips in Germany, Austria, and Italy but I never used it. It is still a good thing to have. If you are traveling with a group, only one of you could take it.

A good sunscreen. VERY IMPORTANT, especially in European summers.

A plastic bag for all the smelly, sweaty things until you get to wash.

A culture kit — With toothpaste, brush, soap, lip balm, and a comb.

I took my kindle. (But I never really read it. I was busy exploring.)

Gear for your bike

Some chain-oil

An extra tube, in case your tire should bust. But also, learn how to fix it first.

On your bike’s body

Get a portable air pump that you can leave on your bike.

A bike multi tool kit that you can also leave on your bike’s body.

Water. Carry some on your bike, and if possible more in your luggage or backpack.

Reflectors and lights.

A phone holder with enough room for a power bank.

Nutrition and Health

Carry enough water. Never underestimate how much you need. I took 3 liters. Always had more than I could drink, and thankfully so.

Magnesium and vitamin tablets. They help regenerate quickly.

Protein bars for those tough hills and snack breaks.

Any medication you might be on.

This list got me through Italian summer of 31 degrees and no shade for 330 Kilometers over 4 days. My travel route was :

Trento — Verona — Garda Lake — Vicenza-Venice.

I used the fifth day to chill in Venice and did not cycle at all. I was glad to have some regular but peppy clothes for the last day.

I’ll update the planning information and more about the bike trip itself in future blog posts. Happy bike trip if you are planning one!

I am Sruthi Korlakunta, read me onMedium, or write me onLinkedInIf you have an idea or a story to share, hit me up! I have 880+ followers and over 220k reads and counting.

If I convinced you to become a member on medium,join herewith my referral link, let’s create together!

title: Flash Fiction: Reviving a Dead Life
content:
Flash Fiction: Reviving a Dead Life

The story of vexation with life and recovering purpose, temporarily.

Inai couldn’t tell if she loved her husband. She didn’t hate him either. Life just “was” and she accepted it without ever wondering if that is what she wanted. To want something was a skill she never learned.

Her dad said she’d study Math. Her teacher said she should graduate in commerce. Her mother said she should be married. Her husband said she should work because the money is good. Her boss told her what to do at work.

If somebody literally held strings to all her limbs and moved her along the way, her life wouldn’t be very different from what it already was.

For 38 years, Inai didn't feel the futility of life. The fake feeling of progress she got from moving from kindergarten to school, from school to graduation, and from graduation to employment and marital bond kept her afloat. Then, the stagnation started to grow on her like moss on a dead stone.

That evening after work, Inai gathered her belongings and left for home. She drove through the first three traffic lights. At the third, she broke down into a pile of tears.

The homeless man passed out on the sidewalk made her envious. She thought about her checklist of homely duties and twisted her face in disgust.

Teeth clenched, she pulled at her steering wheel in anguish as if trying to pluck it out. Realizing that she was free to scream, she shrieked and cried heartfully, oblivious to the traffic outside her closed windows, and she was oblivious to the world outside.

Inai did not take that next left in the direction of home. She drove instead in the direction away from the city and to a riverside that she had never seen before. She got off the car and sat on a wet stone.

Her husband would still be at work. If he was home, she wondered if he missed her, or if he felt relieved as she did at that moment.

Inai thought passionately about her absence from her daily life. She imagined that world where she had disappeared, without notice and smiled.

She pictured herself driving in a new direction, one that did not exist in her town. It was fulfilling to have everything under her control, her every move made only by her will.

She would live in the mountains. No, not in that plastic country of Burger Kings and Mc Donalds. She would find a place farther away.

A couple of hours passed. Inai was perched on a rock by the side of the river. It was closer to town than Inai thought. Or hoped.

She stood up in a jolt when the booming voice laughed in the woods behind her. She didn’t see a face to whom that voice belonged, but she knew from the carelessness in that laugh that it was a man too strong for her self. At best, she’d be embarrassed. She didn’t want to imagine what would happen in her town where bad news regularly made headlines.

Inadvertently, she crouched behind the rock she was sitting on. She heard some bottles break into shambles, voices get louder. Realization dawned about how dark it really was and how far away she was from civilization.

Momentarily dropping her aspiration to be free for ever, she made a stealthy exit in the direction of her car.

Inai felt safer in the woods, in her car. Could she sleep there until morning? As the night got colder and darker, she shifted and turned in her seat, trying to fall asleep.

Again, she woke up startled. It was only the wind, but she thought she heard footsteps. A few moments later, a spider landed on her windshield. Another jolt, another jump scare. Could she really survive the world outside?

By morning, she was sleep deprived and scared. The idea of everyone waiting for her back at home and work made her feel uneasy. The thrill of last night and the hope for an anonymous future was starting to dwindle.

She longed for a bed and the lock on her door.Was anonymity easier to achieve behind closed doors and in the comfort of her home?

Without considering it twice, Inai drove home.

title: I Didn’t Believe That Depression Has an End
content:
I Didn’t Believe That Depression Has an End

Yet here I am. Alive, well, and human.

I was a good student at school. I don't have a “break” in my career to date. I always had decent work and a well-paying job. When I was in school, I thought graduating with good grades will make me happy. After that, I thought a good college will make me happy, and then a good job was a carrot I dangled for myself to keep going. Another country, Another degree, another job, another carrot.

I “passed” all my life tests. I have great grades from school through master's and a job that most people dream of. But why wasn't I feeling great? I was good at my job, I was in the best shape of my life. Why wasn’t I happy?

One day at work, all my fears and disappointments culminated, I ran from a meeting and locked the door behind myself in the hotel room I was put up at. I was struck with a panic attack.

Now I look back at that day 2 years ago in my life, and I smile. I don't know that girl anymore.

If I had known, not known but “known” these things back then, I wouldn't have put myself through that pain. Here are my hard-learned lessons.

Tell yourself the truth and don't be ashamed.

To everything you do, there is a why. You know this. You don't admit it to yourself but you very much know it.

I told myself I want to be a star, but the truth was I wanted to be sure“I’m okay, I’m not awful, I'm helping”.

I wanted to have friends, not because I loved them, but I was afraid of being unloved. I put myself in situations which made me wildly uncomfortable and pretended. All because I was scared and ashamed of how the truth would sound.

Because of childhood bullying and some rough scenarios I had to face up to in the past, I was extremely sensitive to what people think about me. I NEEDED to be liked. I was ashamed of this need.

If I had known, that needs are not causes for shame, but knowing them is like knowing the manual to the machine that is your mind, I would have paid more attention.

There is nothing wrong with wanting to be loved or wanting to help. Don’t find excuses, acknowledge and fulfill your needs. The more actively you pursue them, the less important they get. Clarity will emerge.

Accept the worst that can happen

Fears. They are everywhere. “What if I don't, what if I can’t, what if they laugh at me?”.For me, this pressure came from a peer group.

I come from an environment where achievement is valued very highly and underachievement is explicitly ridiculed. Every time a different kid than myself was the best, I died a little inside.

I wanted my family and friends to be nothing but proud of me but this day never seemed to be near. It was an imaginary light at the end of a very dark tunnel.

Then, I left the country and came very far from the culture that came with it. Distance in space put distance in my mind. As an adult, my biggest fear was that If I fail at all my attempts,theywill laugh at me.

I was tired of being afraid. So, more out of chance than will, I decided that I was too tired to care.

I was willing, in fact, I was wishing to see the worst happen. I reveled in the thought of losing my job, becoming homeless, and not having enough money to pay rent. Ironically, to want to live like that needed much more courage than to show up to a Job respected by society. Because my fear is not homelessness but society.

Willingness to fail was my freedom from“what will they think about me!”I, as a person, will survive anything like a boss. My “worst” is actually just someone else’s worst.

I started inadvertently to think about all those people who would still love me. It is very difficult to fail in that sense because too many people love me to let that happen to me. I then really saw that light at the end of the tunnel. I saw love in others instead of fear from them.

Be the bigger person

Memories of childhood ridicule were very hard to get over. In my eyes as a grown-up woman, these things looked much smaller on the outside. But from the inside, it was as if I was still a little girl.

I looked up my bullies on Facebook. I wanted to get closure. I wrote up one of them. Turned out, he had no memory of bullying me.

It was shocking to know that this nightmare that kept me awake forever was someone else’s speck of dust.

At first, I was angry. I tried writing some angry notes to this person on Facebook. But as I typed those words, I felt how ridiculously enlarged my fear of the past was. How I was looking at this tiny speck through a microscope and letting it shape my entire adulthood. I decided to be the bigger person.

The moment I started a conversation it got boring. I wasn't interested. It was a speck of dust for me too. I was over it.

I took the responsibility of pacifying myself off the table and kept it for myself. I was more comfortable being in charge.

Read

A big savior of life for me was discovering various schools of philosophy.

I read many books of nonfiction and philosophy. The opened doors to me which a movie or another human could not have opened. Some of these books were purely objective, such as Malcolm Gladwell's “Outliers” or Jon Ronson’s “Lost at Sea”. But they seem to talk to you between the lines.

Some were about Medicine, Life, Neurology, and Geriatrics. Like Atul Gawande’s “Being Mortal” or Paul Kalanithi's “When Breath Becomes Air”. There were books of philosophy like “Man’s search for meaning” and David Beckers' “Denial of Death”.

I became a leech for information. I derived pleasure from reading that nothing else ever gave me. It felt like it had answers to all of life’s questions without really having to put it in plain words.

I laughed, I cried, I celebrated, and I was glad to be alive. I still am.

title: How to Make the Best of Your Music
content:
How to Make the Best of Your Music

Act I

Scene I

Hunger, check. Recipe, check. Ingredients, check.But I’m not yet ready — not without music!

How can I cook in silence?Ineedto fill my ears.

Ineedsomething to distract my brain while my muscle memory is put on auto-pilot in the kitchen.

Scene II

Long drive. I can probably manage with just enough gas,but how can I without setting up my playlist!Almost frantically, I set up music before my navigation. Again,

Ineedsomething to distract my brain while my muscle memory is put on auto-pilot in the car.

Scenes III, IV, V, and VI

Shower, the same story.

Boring repetitive tasks at work. No problem.

Long walk? Sound on.

Gym? I get out 30 minutes earlier if I didn't bring my music with me.

Anything but silence,please.

The only time I am not listening to anything is when I am consuming another form of content.

A quick sideways glance showed me that I am not alone. My flatmates, friends, parents, everyone seem to have their choice of escape from reality.

Great. We successfully Matrixed ourselves by 2020.

Act II

B street.

A story from two years ago. Sven and I jogged together every morning. I always showed up with my super-awesome Bose in-ear sport headset and a workout playlist ready to go. But Sven just showed up in his shoes.

I never understood how one could just jog without music for hours together.

When Sven wanted to say something, he could temporarily have my attention when I unplugged one ear and “accommodated” a comment.

“Why don’t you bring music too?”, I asked him with genuine curiosity.

“Running is like meditation for me,” he said.“It is a whole other experience if you are invested in it with all your senses. I like to feel my muscles tense and feel the wind. I like to be present for it all”.

Act II, Now

Three years after that day, yesterday I noticed that I compulsively wanted to be doing multiple things at a time. I actively for a second task, few minutes into starting the first one. While doing it all, I wanted to be entertained.

It felt no more natural to be going through the day without being entertained in some manner. Sven came back to my mind. A new meaning of his words rang in my ears.

My music-with-everything habit was either a symptom or a part of the cause for this phenomenon.

Act III

An addict or a connoisseur?

Another friend of mine, Jamey, is a devout lover of music. I wouldn't have noticed this if my “habit” of listening to music was not bothering me.

I’ve never seen him play music during work or in the kitchen, but every evening when I visit him, I find him on the couch exploring Spotify for something new.

Jamey listens like he means it. With a devoted ear, and with respect, trying to understand what is being sung about. He looks up the artists, tails similar work, and develops a taste.

It was difficult not to notice our evidently and wildly different forms of attachment to the same form of art.

Why does my habit feel like it makes me progressively more chained every day, while his seems to make him freer?

My playlist grows slowly, by maybe a track every month if at all. Jamey treats his music like a food or wine lover treats his palette.

He explores, delights himself with discoveries. He is not easily married to familiar tracks with dogmatic adherence. While I use music as a distraction and an excuse to get away from reality, Jamey seems to be welcoming new music into his reality.

He focuses when he listens to music. I use it to do the opposite.

I wonder how many of us are tricked into believing that mere occupying our ears is actually a good habit.

Are all music lovers connoisseurs? Or are some of us just addicts?

Epiphany — But, isn’t music wonderful?

Not just our personal habits, but the music industry itself pushes its own agenda which might determine how (un)healthy the music we are listening to can be.

Like in every other industry, the music industry’s main agenda is making money.

You are subsequently fed products, money, and unnecessarily exhilarating music that has the sole purpose of sticking in your ears and makes you turn up the volume until your ears hurt and your emotional stability shakes.

Popular themes like sex, ageing, attitude towards the opposite sexes, love, attitude towards money, which are found dime-a-dozen in modern music are often misrepresented. Under the masquerade of being a “life-giving” art form that is music, the ill-effects are swept under the carpet.

Well, that is the price we pay for a free world. The onus is now on the ears of the listener to choose an attitude towards music that is healthy both to the ears and the mind.

Act IV

My experiments with staying in the real

We all listen to music for various and different reasons.

Music for me, above all, is nostalgia. It is associated with the land I grew up in, which no longer surrounds me. A reminder of the good times I had back home, with familiar faces and languages.

At times, it is a guilty pleasure, because some local tracks are famously misogynistic but fail to stop ringing in your ears. Even musically appalling tracks stick for the nostalgia-brownie-points.

To get out of this rut, the past 8 weeks, I decided to give silence a try. I tried these:

I cut off loud and distracting music for myself, except for the Zumba dancing workout sessions.

At work, I turned off music 100%. I noticed that my colleagues want to talk to me more often like this.

While traveling, I’d switched to the radio to keep it new and fresh, like a stream of cold, fresh, flowing water. Sometimes I turn that off too.

When I had company and there had to be music to complete the “togetherness”, I let the other’s playlist play.

When there were people and people wanted the music to play, I turned down the volume significantly, so voices were always more important.

I actively seek and discover new music. When I do, I don't do anything else.

I expanded to new genres. Actively, purposefully and intentionally.

In the beginning, the feeling of loss was unmistakable. But as of now, I definitely feel better.

I have always been kidding myself about how music is not the cause of my mild but chronic ear pain, but I already see an improvement in the past two months.

Of course, I still listen to music, but I have a generally changed and open attitude towards what I put in my ears.

I also like the music I listen to more, now that it is not a never-ending buzz in my ears.

Week 9 update: I absolutely do not miss the never-silent air. I feel more present. Atleast, when I scan my head, there is no empty track playing all the time.

Some guilty pleasures are hard to let go of. Especially when you are up against the army of corporate firms with exclusive research and science on how to get you to be an addict.

Music is no exception. For years, social media has been a target for criticism on how it generates addicts but the music industry has successfully escaped this botched reputation.

As consumers and some of us now addicts, we know for a fact that there is something in there that is not in our best interests. We want to enjoy our music. We want to grow beyond the lists youtube confines us to.

We want to become free with music, not enslaved.

I hope some of you will look at your relationship with this life-giving art form and decide if it is helping you or hurting you. If it is hurting you, time to make amends!

title: Immigration and Loneliness — Cut From The Same Cloth
content:
Immigration and Loneliness — Cut From The Same Cloth

Are we looking at the cure all wrong?

ALL of human misery comes from being inadequately loved, and loving inadequately. I’m convinced.

The fear of being alone or actually being alone and unsupported are a reason for a lot of our emotional baggage.

I’m not just talking of people who are actually alone in an empty apartment with no family or friends, but also those surrounded with family and friends and still find themselves chronically lonely in thought and belonging.

Even while in prison, the worst form of punishment is solitary confinement. When faced with the prospect of solitary confinement, the deprivation of the open sky, the lack of autonomy over one’s time, and the absence of freedom to choose a meal become secondary concerns. Misery renders itself bearable with company.

Immigration in the Metropolitan

I moved to Berlin last year. Starting over from scratch.

During my time here, I met other people dealing with various challenges that come with immigrating. Learning a new language, finding a new hobby, a new job, a friend’s circle, someone who genuinely cares about the mundane highs and lows of your day, or even a person to start a family with. All these are roads that you go alone when you start over, not to mention in a new country that doesn’t speak your language.

Some have it better and some worse. The conventionally attractive, financially stable, and the fairer sex might have it slightly better than the others, but by no means completely free from the problem.

I’ve also met people doing this alone for so long, that they have stopped trying to find anything.

I cannot tell for sure which of the two kinds of people have found their peace. I would guess  neither.

Instagram, Marriage, and other Solutions

We oscillate between desiring a respite from groups and yearning for acceptance of a trusted community. While we don’t necessarily favor one over the other, the true value lies in having the option — the ability to distance ourselves without being vilified, and the opportunity to maintain connections.

The idea of an inconsistent community is terrifying. That is also probably the reason why most people “settle down” into marriages. Like some wise man once said — “We all want a witness to our lives”. Whether that solves the problem is a different can of worms.

Popularity and clout is just one side, a relatively smaller side of social media. The truth is, we want to be perceived, often in good light, or in other cases, in any light. Hence the loudness of Instagram and the like.

Here’s some pictures from my party last weekendis the adult version of“Mom look! no hands!”

What Enlightenment?

Anyone who is convinced that a permanent isolation is bliss, i think is still on an incomplete journey. Even The Buddha came back to civilization to tell everyone what he figured after years of meditation.Why? If loneliness was ok, and expectation was misery, why come back?

There is a lot we can fight against, but I doubt that any amount of enlightenment can help in fight our primary biological make-up. Can we meditate hard to never needing to breathe oxygen — like plants? Can we miraculously stop flinching when we accidentally touch fire?

The need for human contact belongs to that very primal department. You need it because you are programmed to need it. There are no short cuts. Unfortunately, as much as you are programmed to want it, the universe is not laid out for you get it. On the contrary.

A friend once told me this story. As a little boy, he would collect diverse bugs and trap them under an upturned glass tumbler. He would then watch the bugs kill each other, eat each other or just die. In the end, he would take the last bug alive out of the glass and stomp it to death.

That is about how the universe is laid out for us as well. We die trying to survive, or live better, and eventually life catches up. And that’s that.

You could have fulfilling human contact or you could die trying. The fulfillment aspect of connection is also much like a donkey and a carrot. To a fortunate few, the sense of contentment is inbuilt. To others, it is eternal damnation.

title: There is no true love and most other things you want don’t exist either
content:
There is no true love and most other things you want don’t exist either

True love, they said. One for all, they said. God is watching, they said. If you work hard, you will get there, they said. The truths they told, are countable on finger-tips. The lies, geez!

Parents, teachers, books, religion, bosses, colleagues, friends, family, strangers — no one ever tried. Illusion after illusion, they made up my fragile world. Probably yours too.

Happiness is something you can’t just have.This was the motto they drilled into your head. Obey, follow, request, beg, plead, manipulate, askthempermission to let you have it. Or trick them into giving it to you.

This is everyone’s song. Everybody’s tale. Every day’s mission.

Youneedlove to be secure. Youneeda family to hold you when you fall. Of course, you can only have it if you obey and comply.

True love is unconditional,the same people told me. Talking only to the love I give. not the one I get. The one you can have is marinated in condition. And conditioning.

Go to school, get a degree they said, it will make you smart they said. They didn’t tell me that my degree was wet in the mouth of a rabid dog, waiting for the kill, looking out to destroy the non-believing, non-complying, scum that asks questions and refuses to hate the religion it hates, refuses to bury the homo’s and chain the heteros, the scum of the wrong sex.

They said I should find a love like theirs. A love so vast, it occupies all of the corners it is pushed into by everybody around. To remain together despite all the adversity, the only adversity, which is a loathing of each other. Because “god forbid the neighbours know that I hate you!”.

They lead by example how to laugh when strangers knock, to hide those prints on each other's throat with fake smiles. But the neighbours know, they too, are here only for respite from each other. Because we are both good families that “keep it together” despite the odds.

How lucky are the kids!

title: How and Why to Exploit Literary Devices in Your Writing
content:
How and Why to Exploit Literary Devices in Your Writing

With examples from pop-culture

Literary Devices are elements that are used to enhance the perception of your writing and create the intended effect on your reader. Whether you want to give your writing an artistic flare, an emotion, emphasis, or flow, literary devices are what you are looking for. I’ll lay some keywords on the table so that you are reminded of some examples from high school English classes: Allegory, Allusion, Alliteration (you see what I did there? I alliterated), Juxtaposition, Exposition, Oxymoron (Yep, I assonated), Diction, Personification, Repetition (I rhymed)…

Literary Devices are elements that are used to enhance the perception of your writing and create the intended effect on your reader.

In this article, I am going to quote Eminem, Liam Neeson, Charles Dickens, The fight club, Justin Halpert, Leo Tolstoy, and others to convince you to actively interject your writing with literary devices.

Take a look at the following sentences you probably never heard of:

Things were ok, some were good, some very, very, bad…

He tried to avoid looking at her but he couldn’t resist himself. He eventually saw her, because obviously, he was going to.

Who am I talking to even?! If you don’t let my daughter go, I will track you down and kill you!

Now look at the same information delivered with literary devices, you most definitely heard of these before:

“It was the best of the times, it was the worst of the times”— Charles Dickens, A Tale of two cities

“He stepped down, trying not to look long at her, as if she were the sun, yet he saw her, like the sun, even without looking.” — Leo Tolstoy, Anna Karenina

“I don’t know who you are. I don’t know what you want. If you let my daughter go now that’ll be the end of it. I will not look for you, I will not pursue you, but if you don’t, I will look for you, I will find you and I will kill you.” — Liam Neeson, Taken

The goosebumps on your hand, my friend, are but a ripple of what literary devices can do to a text. The next time you are in a meeting at work, at a eulogy, a wedding toast, or anywhere where there is some stillness and you get to listen, observe how you feel when different things are being said. Observe, the day after, what made you feel good and what you remember from the experience. What was insipid? What was powerful? You will very likely notice that words intended to create a human connection, or emotional aspects of a speech tend to stay in your memory easier and longer.

One way of creating this effect is to inject some literary devices into your speech or writing. If you think it is cheesy to force these into writing, you’re probably right. The first rule for using any literary devices is this:When in doubt, leave it out.But if you think that they do not belong in modern writing, hold my beer and be prepared to be amazed. While it is impossible to list out all possible literary devices in a blog post, I will do justice to that little white space I have on this page, and cite five examples.

Assonance

“Light the fire in the dark night”.

It looks like a regular statement, but sounds good for some reason doesn’t it? That’s my favorite part of assonance. When you look closely the above sentence has a phonetic /aɪ/ that repeats itself almost clandestinely, making it interesting. Think about Rap Music. What makes it so special? You’re right, Assonance it is!

Oh, there goes Rabbit, he chokedHe’s somad, but he won’t give up that easy, noHe won’t have it, he knows his whole back’s tothese ropesIt don’t matter, he’s dope. — Eminem

Assonance also helps set a mood.If you observe the first sentence in this section, it creates an aura of a calm night with soft fire without actually using those words or any of those adjectives for that matter.

Epigraphs and anaphora

Epigraphs and Anaphora create emphasis. What if I told you emphasis is the most important element of emotional speech? What if I told you, there is a way to create it? What if I told you I know how? What if I told you, now you know!

The repeated use of what-if in the above statement is an example of Anaphora. When such repetition occurs at the end of a sentence it is called an epigraph. They are used to create an emotional effect or emphasis in a speech or writing. The examples cited above, from Dickens’Tale of two citiesand Liam Neeson’sTakenare both classic examples of anaphora. Here’s an Epigraph for you from Fight club:

“The first rule of Fight Club is: You do not talk about Fight Club.”

Allusion

Allusions derive usually from Pop culture and famous references. An Allusion uses a popular example to refer to an unrelated current event without really saying what exactly is being alluded to.

For example, If I say “my friend pulled a Brutus on me”, you know immediately that he put a metaphorical knife in my back, and whatever he did, it wasn’t good. The best way to get better at allusions is to be well-read.

Here is another example from the Hilarious book“Shit my dad says” by Justin Halpert:

Dad: “does it hurt?”

Me : “No, it’s just, I don’t know, Wierd”

Dad: “Thank you for that detailed description, Ernest F*cking Hemingway!”

Anachronism

An anachronism is a strong tool in comedy and satire. The popular German Movie “Look who is back!” shows Adolf Hitler come back to life in 21st century Berlin. The movie opens with him being utterly confused about the lack of respect for him on the streets and the lack of manners of people in general. This is an example of anachronism-based humor.It highlights contrasts from two different times by forcing them to co-exist. (Juxtaposition is a similar literary device that puts not necessarily time-related heavy contrasts beside each other.)

That’s about five examples for the magic of literary devices. In short, they make your writing memorable. They differentiate reading a newspaper from reading a book, conveying information from making a statement. Humans are emotional beings. No matter how rationally you formulate your argument, the way to one’s heart and everlasting memory is by inciting emotion. Use literary devices to your advantage if you are not already doing it consciously. It is a sure-shot way to enhance your writing!

title: The Next Thing That Will Knock You Out of Balance
content:
The Next Thing That Will Knock You Out of Balance

Is your mental health prepared for it?

After a major phase of minor enlightenment, I felt for about 2 years like nothing can bring me down anymore. I had figured it out — life has no meaning and there is no reason strong enough to be ever depressed about. Shit happens, cleans itself, and happens again. That is just the way things are.

It wasn’t like my years were perfect. Imperfect relationships, sometimes a sense of imperfection in work life, feeling of needing to be more — sometimes with my body and sometimes with my brain. I had all of these. Yet, I had accepted that it will be alright, and if it won't, that's ok too.

Each time when something went downhill, I had a minor episode of “Ah Fish!” and then pretty quickly came to terms with reality. Each time I came to terms with something, I wondered if my good mental health was here to stay or if there was a cause that is going to drop from the sky and knock me out.

Not even the strongest of us can withstand all the adversities life casually drops in our path and keeps walking its way. It pays to work your acceptance muscle.

Work that acceptance muscle

Right now, as I write this, I am not at the highest of my spirits. But I’m on my way back up (Ergo working that acceptance muscle).

In fact, I have been through a major episode of sadness that I didn’t know I was capable of feeling. A physical aversion to my surroundings and happenings made me freeze in my skin and refuse food for days. The kind of sadness that you can feel in your body and under your skin, the coldness in your chest, and emptiness in your heart.

What happened is really not important, because years down the road, something else is going to happen, and it will feel like this all over again. Years before this, things like these have been happening to people, and worse.

At first, I suffered in silence. And then, I suffered with my family. I recruited friends. Well-wishers. Strangers on the internet. All in the hope of feeling a teeny bit better. To feel like I have help, that there is an objective perspective to this whole thing that I am unable to see. Luckily for me, there was. there always is.

There always is an objective perspective to stuff that you cannot see from your place. Talk to people.

The world is a more kind and more friendly place than you believe in your low moments. Go looking for that kindness.

Does it really help to talk to people?

Life often tricks you into believing that you are the worst person alive. Its own private joke. Because there have always happened worse things.

When you talk to others, the right people, you get a glimpse of perspective. That everyone has skeletons in their closets. The musts and shoulds and woulds of the world are all made up, imperfect standards set by the world because everyone is trying to do their best in their own way.

Will it get better?

We live in a world where mountains turn to sand and goop turns into people after a billion years. Layer by layer. Year after year. Day after day. There is no better. There is no perfect life. Layers happen and disappear over time. You are no different.

Things happen to you, around you. You watch, accumulating those layers. You might start off as a shiny new kid in kindergarten wanting to be the president. But the layers that you gather might tell a different story. Don’t hate that story. It is much more exciting than the one you have on mind.

Give in to your wants and needs but not enough to perish if you don't have them. There is no “better life” than the one you have. Just a bunch of happenstance. Learn to live with them.

Work that acceptance muscle.

I am Sruthi Korlakunta, read me onMedium, or write me onLinkedIn. I like hearing from people. yes, you too!

Accurate
