title: Times When Bojack and Rick Gave You Existential Crisis
content:
Times When Bojack and Rick Gave You Existential Crisis

From Netflix’s own Bojack Horseman and Rick and Morty

Few TV shows are as brilliant and real as Netflix’s Own Bojack Horseman and Rick and Morty.

Bojack Horseman brought to life the universe hidden in the microcosm of the human psyche. Never was the clash between human and perfect, duty and self, society and individual, work and life, been depicted more beautifully. Depressed or not, one cannot escape the extreme human-ness of the characters.

Rick and Morty did the opposite. Rick Sanchez stands for nothing and no one. The Cartoon series is an epitome of intelligent nihilism. There is no good or bad, just what you want and don’t. Acknowledging your nothingness in the universe might make your existence an intolerable pain or an immense pleasure.

Here are 5 moments from both masterpieces of TV, the best in my living 28 years, which made me grow body hair and then stood them up.

Rick Sachez on Nihilism

“When you know nothing matters, the universe is yours. And I’ve never met a universe that was into it. The universe is basically an animal, it grazes on the ordinary. It creates infinite idiots just to eat them.”

Rick Sanchez about purpose of life

Robot (whizzing to life):What is my purpose

Rick:To pass the butter

Robot (passes butter and asks again, impatient):What is my purpose

Rick: (Annoyed):You pass the butter!

Robot:Oh-My-God.(Depressed slouch)

Rick:Yeah, welcome to the club pal!

Rick Sachez about Love

“Listen, Morty, I hate to break it to you, but what people call “love” is just a chemical reaction that compels animals to breed. It hits hard, Morty, then it slowly fades, leaving you stranded in a failing marriage. I did it. Your parents are gonna do it. Break the cycle, Morty. Rise above. Focus on science.”

Rick Sachez and Jerry Smith about traditions

Jerry:Traditionally, Science Fairs are a father-son thing

Rick:Well, Scientifically, traditions are an idiot thing.

Beth and Rick Sachez about self

Beth:Dad, I am out of excuses to not be who I am. So, Who am I? What do I Do?

Rick Sanchez:My advice, take off. Put a saddle on your universe, let it kick itself out.

Beth:I can’t Do that! The kids, Jerry, My job! and as much as I hate to admit, ABC’s The Bachelor!

Rick:I can make a clone of you. A perfect instance of you, an exact copy of you in every way. It’ll love and provide for the kids and do your job and consume broadcast network reality TV on the same allegedly ironic levels as you. You could be gone a day, a week, or the rest of your life with zero consequences. The moment you decide to come back, I flip a switch and the clone’s job is done.

Beth:If nothing matters, why would you do that for me?

Rick:Maybe you matter so little that I like you, maybe it makes you matter. maybe I love you, maybe it’s about your mother. Don’t jump a gift shark in the mouth.

Beth:I don’t know if can do it.

Rick:Then stay, and luxuriate in a life that you finally you know you have chosen. My secret bonus is that no matter what you choose, you are finally going to chill the fuck out.

Bojack Horseman

Bojack Horseman About Closure

“Closure is a made-up thing invented by Steven Spielberg to sell movie tickets. It’s like true love in the Münich Olympics. It doesn’t exist in the real world. The only thing to do now is to keep living forward.”

Herb Kazaz About saying “Here is the thing about…”

“Aaargh! I hate it when people say “Here is the thing…”. Just SAY the THING! You don’t need to introduce the concept that there’s going to be a thing!”

Bojack about finding a partner who completes you

“Nobody completes anybody. That’s not a thing. If you are lucky enough to find someone who you can halfway tolerate, you sink your nails in and don’t let go. No matter what.

Settle, because otherwise, you’re going to get older, harder, and more alone. And you are going to do everything to fill that hole with friends, career, meaningless sex, but the hole doesn’t get filled.”

Bojack and Hollyhock about Depression

Hollyhock:That voice, that tells you you are worthless and stupid and ugly…

Bojack:(With recognition) Yeah?

Hollyhock:It goes away, right?

Bojack:Pauses.

Bojack:(Lying) Yeah…

Mr. Peanutbutter about existence

“The universe is a cruel uncaring void. The key to being happy isn’t to search for meaning. It is just to keep yourself busy with unimportant nonsense, and eventually, you will be dead.”

title: Planning your First Bike Packing Trip
content:
Planning your First Bike Packing Trip in Europe

September is on the horizon, the best time to bike-pack in Europe!

With summer frequently touching 40 degrees, September provides the optimal weather conditions to do multi-day, long-distance rides. Alongside the weather, the multiple well-defined, well-equipped, bike paths in southern Europe make optimal destinations for a lifetime of memories.

Whether you choose the German-Austrian flat roads along the Danube, the colorful vistas of southern Tyrol, French roads along the Seine, or Mediterranean roads in Spain — Any of these is a great place to start bike-packing even as a beginner.

If you have already ventured out of your town for a 4 to 5-hour bike ride, here is how to take it to the next step.

Planning your Bike Tour

The best place to start is by choosing a road and how to get there.

Depending on your location, it might be easier to drive to a nearby long-distance cycle path in your car with your bike in tow.

Alternatively, there are inter-country train connections equipped with facilities to transport bicycles and the relevant gear.Remember to book your bike spot on the train along with your regular ticket — In the high-demand season, last-minute deals might be hard to find.

Another option is to transport your bike with long-distance buses, which usually also carry about 5 bikes per ride on their bike trailers.

If all else seems cumbersome, most cities popular with bikers are also equipped with bike rentals where you can choose from a variety of bikes that you can return after your bike-packing tour.

Travel within your bike trip

It is important to inform yourself in advance about taking public transport with a bike in your destination country.

Many remote places in Europe are poorly connected with buses and trains and sometimes don’t allow bikes on board.

Find out which large cities/villages exist along your road, just in case. You might need shelter or help from weather conditions, due to injury/bike repair or just to relax for a day if you feel like it.

Mark in advance the populated buckets on your tour and where you could possibly take public transport if the need arises.

Download the local apps for public transport schedules andcarry some cash with you!

Planning your Stay

While planning your stay, take care to see that you don’t over-estimate your daily distances.

Based on your experience level, path difficulty, and weather, plan reasonably your kilometers every day and look for accommodations in the cities that lie within these distances from your starting point each day.

Once you have an idea of your “safe limits”, a good place to start is to look for bike hotels on the way which offer service in case you should need it.Some bike hotels also offer a luggage transport service that brings your luggage to the next station in case you choose not to carry all of it on your bike pack.

If bike hotels are not as frequent as you like, you can look for hotels, hostels, or Airbnb based on your budget. If you are feeling really adventurous, you might consider carrying a tent and a sleeping bag on your bike and sleeping in camping places on the way.

Packing for your ride

Here is an extensive list of dos and don'ts along with a beginner’s packing list for your first bike-packing trip.

The key to packing for a bike trip is volume. Your bike will carry the weight (depending on the stability of the carriage on your bike). It is your job to fit everything in the mini-volume at the back of your seat.

In general, you will need:

1. Your documents: Passport, visa and other papers, some cash.Keep these on your body.

2. Clothes: Cycling clothes, Leisure clothes and Something to sleep in, Swimming clothes, and a towel.

3. A rain jacket.

4. A scarf.

5. Arm warmers.

6. A thin jacket for the wind.

7. Socks, a pair a day, unless you can wash.

8. Cycling goggles.

9. Underwear, a pair a day, unless you wash.

10. Enough water. (About 2–3 liters)

11. Protein bars for snack breaks.

12. Any medication you might be on.

13. Phone holder with enough room for a power bank, and a power bank

14. An airpump, some chain-oil, a multi-tool, and an extra tube.

15. Insect repellant.

16. Sunscreen.

17. A plastic bag for things until you get to wash them.

18. A culture kit — With toothpaste, brush, soap, lip balm, and a comb.

19. A book to read and one to write, and a pen.

20. A first-aid kit.

Going Home

Book your travel back from your destination in advance.August and September are usually peak-travel times for bikers. If you don't rush your bike might not find a place on the transport.

If you are traveling with the train, take a bike harness to fasten your bike to prevent it from falling.

Those were some tips for planning and packing for your bike ride as a beginner. I’ll keep you posted on the exact roads on my bucket list, ranging from a few 10s to over 1000 Kilometers. Watch this space or subscribe to my articles. Happy riding!

I am Sruthi Korlakunta, read me onMedium, or write me onLinkedInIf you have an idea or a story to share, hit me up! I have 880+ followers, 220k reads, and counting.

If I convinced you to become a member on medium join me with my referral link, and let’s create together!

title: Being Immigrant
content:
Being Immigrant

This is not a rant. This is not a complaint.

Realization,you’re finally alone. No matter who you meet and no matter the love or hate you find, it's not what you know. It's not home.

Liberationone moment,you burden no one’s thoughts, take up no one’s heart and mind, finally a world to conquer!

— The next, what now? Like a candle out of its candelabrum, free to stand alone. But how, why?

Emptiness,like clutching at air before losing your footing. At one instance you stand, at another, you may topple.

title: How to Avoid Carpal-Tunnel and Still Be a Prolific Writer
content:
How to Avoid Carpal-Tunnel and Still Be a Prolific Writer

Keeping your arms safe

If you have not already looked it up, Carpal-Tunnel is that feeling of weakness in your hand that makes anything and everything too heavy to hold. You might have observed it after repeatedly refreshing your stats, checking notifications on various social media for an extended period of time, such as a couple of weeks.

If you are a writer in 2020, I assume you mostly live digitally. Emails, text editing apps, socializing with fellow writers and potential readers — Hardly anything left for offline professional life. If you can already tell from how your fingers feel, poor things probably need a break. Here are some tips to help you keep writing while your fingers rest.

1. Get a vertical Mouse

The natural position of rest for your arm is thumbs-up. When you are using a regular mouse, your palm rests in an unusual position where your palm is parallel to the ground. This strains your Forearm ‘Carpal tunnel’.

Try it out, rest the side of your palm completely on the surface you are typing, and feel the difference. Doesn’t it feel like heaven all of a sudden? A vertical mouse helps your palms rest in their ‘natural state’ and prevent you from moving around the mouse only with your wrist. I totally recommend it for comfortable long-term typing.

2. Use the dictate function

The dictate function is very underrated. It may not feel perfect the first time around, but it is perfectly suitable for drafts. Together with editing apps like Grammarly, dictate function makes writing drafts very easy. You can dictate your draft into an app and then fine-tune it with finger typing.

Here is an example of the dictate function with Microsoft Word on android. Note that you have to say the punctuation out explicitly:

Word also offers the dictate function with office 365. Your PC also most probably has this option hidden somewhere. Google for it and put it to use. If you find dictating your first draft inconvenient, write it on a good old piece of paper and read it out for your computer, saving your palms and forearm in the process.

3. Get a Squeeze ball

Squeeze balls not only take the stress out of the day but also the strain out of your forearm. The studies about it helping to completely alleviate the Carpal tunnel are a mixed bag. Personally, It helped me quite a bit to finger-chew my stress ball while I lay in bed and read.

4. While you are at the gym, think about your fingers and wrist too

It is easy to forget the little muscles while at the gym, but it seriously helps to train your finger and wrist muscles along with your bigger muscles if you are at the gym, or even at home. Most of these finger-exercises do not need heavy equipment or hours of time, so I recommend to incorporate it into your daily activity. Since I am not a trained professional, I won't tell you which exercises help, but there is enough help on Youtube for training your fingers.

There is not much talk about there for writers to protect themselves from Employment hazards. However, the hazards writers face with too much screen time is as much if not worse as for Software Engineers. Think about your fingers and keep writing!

title: Monkeys Can be your Therapist
content:
Monkeys Can be your Therapist

No, it is not because of the dopamine boost that you get from looking at cute baby monkeys.

No, it is also not by owning a pet Capuchin. For heaven’s sake, don’t ever keep a monkey pet! They are social creatures with higher need for brain actvitiy than you can afford to give them.

I spent a large part of the last  2 years learning about monkeys from books on primatology and evolution. That experience was… therapeutic.

Childhood trauma.

Relationship issues.

Work pressure.

Meeting expectations that you set for yourself or those set by peers, family and collegues.

Existential crises.

title: How to Travel Switzerland on a Budget and a Camping Checklist
content:
How to Travel Switzerland on a Budget and a Camping Checklist

From my recent travels to this marvelous heaven on earth.

The advice I will give is most uncomplicated if you are already in Europe. But, I met people from the USA, Australia, China, and the most remote parts of the world doing the same thing while I was there. So it is not impossible to do if you're from somewhere else.

Interlaken and Grindelwald

Interlaken is hands down, the most beautiful city I’ve seen in my life. I traveled to Grindelwald, a small village close to Interlaken, and stayed there for a week. This place was as close as I could get to heaven on earth.

I couldn’t believe what I was seeing. The grass was literally greener than anywhere else I can remember, the water was a pristine turquoise blue. Numerous waterfalls along the road, bright green pastures with grazing cows, and mountain peaks that paint the sky with a stunning view — it was an experience for a lifetime.

While I looked for accommodation and travel options to get there, a quick search told me this was going to make quite a dent in my bank account.

Switzerland is the third most expensive country in the world. For comparison, a coffee costs about 3.80 Eur. in Germany in a nice cafe in Bavaria. In Switzerland, the cheapest coffee at a cafe was over 6 Eur. But this “double price” is only if you get lucky. A bottle of Hugo costs about 2 Eur. in Germany. This was 9 Eur. in a supermarket in Switzerland. Accommodation costs for two in a Swiss chalet range from about 2500 Eur. to over 3500 Euros a week at the top of the season!

Can you really enjoy a holiday while you're constantly worried about breaking the bank?

So I found a hack. Camping!

Camping in the wild — Checklist

Grindelwald has a lot to offer for nature lovers. Hiking trails, paragliding, trekking and mountain climbing, bicycling, canyon swinging, skiing — whatever outdoor action you seek, you can find it here.

It is also home to Europe's tallest mountain peak — the Jungfraujoch. There are several smaller (but not really that much smaller) peaks close by that are equally pleasing to the eye.

If you are on an adventure mission in this region, you’ll love camping.

It is ano-nonsense, only-essentials,way to travel that will also keep your budget in check. If you are a comfort-seeker, there are ways to make your camping cozier and more luxurious so that you get the best out of this region. The easiest way to camp is if you drive down with your things in tow.

What if you are traveling from afar? Principally, you can fit a one or two-person tent in your luggage easily. But if you don’t have a lot of space in your luggage, you can buy your tent and other gear right here in Switzerland (or in neighboring countries in Europe) at a Decathlon.

You will also need these essentials to make your camping comfy:

1. A camping stove and fuel.

2. Your hiking gear

3. Some essential storable foods (like pasta, instant noodles, pesto, etc.) You can always buy them at a supermarket but you'll be saving A LOT if you bring some food yourself. Trust me, Switzerland is that expensive.

4. A good light for your indoors and a torch light

5. Camping utensils that are fire resistant.

6. A little pillow and a sleeping bag. (If you have space in your car, you can carry more stuff to make your camp more cozy. I took a nice blanket and an air mattress)

7. A set of playing cards, uno, or something to read or entertain yourself in any other way.

8. I took an electric water cooker and instant coffee. I love coffee in the mornings.

9. House slippers, outdoor shoes.

10. Comfy clothes, city clothes, swimming clothes, sleeping clothes, and outdoor-adventure clothes.

11. Plastic bags for your used clothes and trash bags.

12. Chargers, travel adapters, and other equipment for your electronics.

13. Bluetooth speakers are an added luxury. (Don't disturb people with loud music).

14. Power bank

15. Socks, towels, and paper towels.

16. Toiletries (soap, shampoo, toothbrush, paste, etc.)

17. Weather-appropriate clothing (rain jackets, cold jackets, umbrellas, etc.)

18. Your documents (Passport, visa etc.)

19. If you have a bike attachment to your car, take your bicycle. Highly recommended, since renting is expensive.

20. If you are driving from outside of Switzerland, the Swiss Vignette for your car (like a road tax) will cost you 42 euros and is valid through the year.

If you have space in your luggage, don’t get lazy on any of these things. You most definitely don’t want to buy everything there. It will quickly get very expensive.

Most importantly though, learn how to set up a tent.

Despite doing all of the above, my holiday to Switzerland was not that cheap. I would have even carried some more stuff since I had the space in my car, that way, I could have avoided some extra costs.

Note that despite all of this, the picturesque train rides, Funiculars to the mountain peaks (which you must not skip, they're AWESOME memories for a lifetime) will not be cheap, and will be the highest chunk of your costs.

Things to do in Grindelwald and Interlaken

If you are an outdoor adventure expert, you will find your crowd there very easily. But if you are a “normal” traveler like me, here is a little help.

1. Hike up to mountain peaks :

There are numerous mountain peaks in Grindelwald that range from an elevation gain of 200 to over 1800 meters.

Choose your difficulty level and get hiking! I did theFirst mountainpeak (that's the name of the peak) and I highly recommend it. You can also do the Pfingstegg, Größe / Kleine Scheidegg peaks, Bachsee loop, and Kleine Scheidegg to Eigergletscher loop (You can get there with a mountain train). Take the train to at least Kleine Scheidegg and see the Top of Europe from there. This is a train ride you don't want to miss.

2. Visit nature sites and waterfalls

Go to Lauterbrunnen Falls, The glacier Canyon in Grindelwald, the First Cliffwalk, or the hundreds of viewpoints. (You can also find great hiker trails on the Komoot app).

3. Drive or ride to Interlaken and enjoy the city, Lake Thurn and Brienz. Visit the Harder Kulm.

Interlaken is nestled between lakes Thurn and Brienz. It is a spectacular city that will remain in your memory forever. Go shopping for Victorinox knives, and Swiss watches or just take a walk in the marketplace. Visiting turquoise lakes Thurn and Brienz is definitely worth the time.

If you have less time and want to do only one thing, Go to Harder Kulm. That’s the highest point in the city. You will see the whole Interlaken in an Ariel view.

4. Go to Iseltwald

For the best experience of Brienz Lake, visit Iseltwald, a little village on the lakeside. Take a boat ride or just bask in the views.

5. Cycle down from Grindelwald to Interlaken or Lauterbrunnen

I promise you, you will thank me for this. Best if you can take your bicycle with you. Else you can rent it in Grindelwald.

Visit other beautiful places nearby

At the end of our holiday, we drove down to Lake Maggiore to experience Italian summer after the mountainous Grindelwald. You can also choose to visit other beautiful places like Lucerne which isn't very far from Grindelwald.

You can also consider the mountain Matterhorn (village Zermatt) which is about a half a day drive from Grindelwald. This is the famous Toblerone mountain we know from the chocolate’s cover image. Since the weather forecast wasn’t perfect, we skipped Zermatt and headed down to Italy instead.

Have a nice camping trip! and don’t forget to comment if you have questions.

title: 5 Signs That You Are a Good Researcher
content:
Research,Science

5 Signs That You Are a Good Researcher

Do you have the guts to speak the insignificant truth?

There are researchers who love their work, and there are those who only love the glory that comes with it.

Ironically, you can only do one of these two things truthfully. Most of the time, the scientific process brings mildly satisfying results, if any at all, and only once in a century, there comes a groundbreaking revelation that is capable of turning heads and breaking hearts.

If you are a researcher, the odds are that you are helping the world massively, but it probably feels like your research is just a tiny drop in the ocean. This is absolutely normal.

The “Ugg”ly truth

To say something as simple as “Hence we conclude that your Uggs wear out half a millimeter over 150 km”needs the poor researcher to wear those Uggs and walk for thousands of kilometers and measure the thickness possibly every 10km. Not to mention the years he spends walking around with a notebook on all possible terrains to be able to generalize every word in that sentence.

Even then, he is only sure of this particular result with a man his weight and walking style. So he calls in 50 college students, pays them 12 bucks each, and walks them around with uggs and kilometer-trackers after taking their height weight and getting them to walk on a treadmill to classify walking style.

Sigh. Such is science.

Diamonds in the Rough

In the scientific community, it is not uncommon to find blasphemous deviants to the truth and process willing to conclude that “Uggs might cause cancer” just because that paper has a better chance of being read. (No, Uggs don't give you cancer. Actually, I don't know if they do).

Financial expert William J Bernstein rightly identifies a genuine scientific process as one of the central pillars for civilization to exist(Birth of plenty).In Order to keep civilization on its road to progress, it is necessary to weed out the deviants. Here is a beginners checklist to see if you are a good researcher:

1. The Disclaimer

Do you see that line in a research paper? It says :

“The current results may vary when performed under different circumstances. Further research may be needed to generalize the results and establish the conclusions.”

That disclaimer is NOT a legal obligation. It is a heart-felt truth that makes the researcher think even in his sleep, “I hope people don't jump to conclusions based on my paper.” Be this guy. Disclaim loud and clear.

2. You swept nothing under the carpet

Did you perform an experiment 20 times, but you got the expected results only thrice? So you titled your paper saying “Verified thrice” and silently swept 17 attempts under the carpet? Don’t be this guy. All 20 of your attempts need to see daylight. The 17 falses bring science forward as much as the 3 trues.

3. You err in the direction of less glory

Do you have an ambiguous discovery, that in one sentence, looks like you discovered the proof for the existence of god and in another means that you wasted 8 years of your life?

Don’t choose to say, “The results arevery close to being almoststatistically significant that there exists a God that cares about you.”

Say instead, “It could not be shown with this experiment with a significant probability that there exists a God. More research and replication is needed to establish the result.” (Note that there is a difference between it could not be shown that there is God, and there is no God).

4. Your results can be replicated

That brings us to replication. If a research result is claiming something to be true, it should be possible to replicate it. It is painful to spend time doing what has been done and to say,“What was true last year is still true.”Even more so when you have to replicate it multiple times.

The future of civilizational credibility of science rests on being able to replicate. Dust those replicated experiments, publish them and affirm replicability.

5. You are terrified of ambiguous language

Significance of a result should mean to you that “The experiment would rarely fail to deliver the expected results.”Not that it“Has at least once delivered the expected results.”If you see language in a paper that does not establish case 1 above, you should have your doubts. If you are writing a paper yourself, you should make black and white conclusions only when you establish case 1. If your result belongs to case 2, you should make sure that you publish them with clear disambiguation as to what you really intend. Say, don't imply.

If you are interested in Data Science, technology, or social competence, connect with me. Here is myMediumprofile, and here is myLinkedin.

title: Shut up and take my money: 7 Products & Services in my Hall of Fame
content:
Shut up and take my money: 7 Products & Services in my Hall of Fame

They improved my life exponentially and are worth every penny.

Let’s cut to the chase. Here are 7 devices that brought my life on track and automated the hardest parts of discipline. They remove the obstacles to your growth and make your life easier. 100/100 would recommend if you plan to buy any of these products.

1. Garmin Vivoactive 5: 200$, Changed how I look and feel, made fitness a mind game.

I had never run consistently in my life before Garmin. I had never lifted weights. I was gifted a Garmin by a friend once. It took me a long time to get used to wearing it.

But one fine day, I started running, and I never stopped since. The detailed metrics, coaching plans, and the many badges to collect made me obsessed with improving my running game. I stopped realizing that running is primarily a fitness activity, and just looked forward to doing it for fun. Garmin did that.

A year later, I upgraded to the ultra-thin, lightweight Garmin vivoactive 5 from an older vivoactive 3. This really supercharged my entire fitness routine. That year (2024, I think), Garmin also introduced strength training in its coaching plans.

No more confusion in what to do at the gym, Garmin had my plan, my one-rep maximums, rest times between sets, everything curated according to my goal (4 times a week — Squat Day, Deadlift day, Bench-press day etc.), all were ready and set on my watch for every 6 week plan, with my progress being tracked without me bothering.

I can certainly say that Garmin is the only reason my training is on track — No notebooks, remembering sets and reps, no wondering if I already trained glutes this week. Its all done for me, including detailed metrics in the afterburn.

2. Trackball Mouse, Logitech: 40$ Bliss on my wrists, Bye Bye Carpal Tunnel.

My biggest learning after years of school and work experience:You grow the most with the skills you develop in your free time, while chasing your curiosity.That’s how my Medium account now has over 230 articles; that’s how I can program Python. Skills I built in my free time, with love.

The best way to automate this is to make a working station at home that makes you want to go back to it. Back upright, big monitors, but most of all, a mouse that doesn’t make me want to put my hands down to rest every 20 mins.

The first time I tried andreviewed these mice 3 years ago, I wasn’t yet accustomed to the trackball mouse. I have since bought one more and gifted a couple more of these trackball mice since I love it so much.

A trackball mouse keeps your wrist in its natural resting position through out. only your thumb moves, while your entire arm rests gracefully on the table, using minimum movement and avoiding any awkward placements. It’s a great addition to your desk!

3. SoundCore Aerofit Pro (Bone-conduction earphones): $100, saved me from chronic ear pain, no thanks to AirPods and Pixelbuds.

4 years ago, I was terrified I would lose my hearing by mid-thirties. Ear pain was chronic, but I needed music for workouts. Every time I thought that the pain went away, one session of workout with music, and it was back again. Now in my mid-thirties, all my friends with high-end noise cancelling earphones have the same complaint.

All that changed when I randomly tried the soundcore aerofit pro earphones. Theyre a bone-conduction earphone set that don’t block your ears and don’t cancel noise. They deliver high-quality music without even touching your earholes.

Since I started using the Soundcore Aerofit Pro, my ears have thanked me profusely, many times. I have since also upgraded to the Aerofit 2, but nope, not even remotely as good as the Aerofit pro, the older version.

Button controls on the buds, battery lasts forever, ear pain disappears, and they give an amazing snug fit. 100% would buy again, and all under $100!

4. McFit Fitness Studio (Germany): All you can Lift for 20 Eur? Sign me up.

If you live in Europe, you are not going to stay in any one country for more than a couple of months before wanderlust gets to you, and you want to travel.

I’ve been to McFit Fitness studios in multiple cities in Germany — Bremen, Stuttgart, Ingolstadt, Berlin, Munich, Wolfsburg, Braunschweig, Karlsruhe (on the outskirts somewhere), Graz (Austria), and Salzburg (Austria). In Berlin alone, I was in at least 4 different McFit studios, based on where I wanted to explore that weekend.

Many times I have heard people complain about how McFit is always full. But it also has, hands down, the best deals in town, and a network reach that another chain cannot replicate.

I used to be a proud McFit girl, and would recommend you sign up too. It’s 20 Euros and guarantees you an hour or more of activitiy 365 days a year in all whethers and all cities. You should only want it.

5. Kindle Paperwhite: Unlimited knowledge for the price of 5 hardcovers.

You order a book, it gets delivered in a day or two, and you store it away for later… and it sits there, untouched. And one day on the train, you wish you had packed that book even if it felt heavy.

Kindle makes all those problems go away. It is paper-thin, and you have your entire library in your backpack all the time. Want another book? It takes a second. Voila! Think it, read it.

You can acquire as many books as you want without worrying about the clutter and the dust they will gather. It always weighs the same, and it is always there for you, on your phone, on your computer, or on your Kindle device to read in your bathtub. the thin feel on the Kindle as opposed to a 600 page book tricks you into reading more.

In my opinion, this is the only way to keep reading in your adulthood with any consistency.

6. Refurbished Dyson Supersonic Nural: Finally, I don't have to choose between sleeping with Wet Hair or going to work with frizz

Adult women have a unique problem. Their thick, long, beautiful hair. It is a damned if you do, damned if you don’t situation. Keep it and suffer hours of care and thousands of products and constantly changing care-science behind the products, or chop it off and have an easy life and miss your tresses.

Heat can really tame curly hair. Or any hair. But it brings permanent damage with it. For the fear of ruining my beautiful hair, I never used a hair dryer on it for many years. I slept with wet hair, fell sick from it, and went to work with wet hair due to the lack of time, and had damp hair for many consecutive days.

Dyson solved all that. It dries my hair in its curly form without damaging it. The new one in the market was about $500, and no matter how good the promise, it was just not worth it for a hair dryer. (I’m a cost-conscious consumer, the pink tax doesn’t work on me.) So I went on and got an officially refurbished Dyson Supersonic nural for about 230$ from Walmart. And yes. It has changed my life, and my relationship with my hair. I dry it all I want without the guilt, and sleep with a blissfully dry head.

7. Costco Membership, USA

I don’t see Costco membership as a shopping subscription, but more like a life hack for the fiscally conscious. While the upfront fee might look like a lot, the way you are forced to shop at Costco, it will pay back by February.

I love the Kirkland Signature name, because it gives me quality for 30% less than the brand names. Other than the bulk snacks, it’s so easy to meal-prep with Costco quantities, easy to stay in shape if you just shop consciously, once a month or every three weeks. A quick comparison of ANY product in Costco with your garden variety grocery store tells you that even with the massive quantities that you buy, you come out so much cheaper at the end of the year.

It has a spectrum of products and a generous return policy, not to mention affordable snacking for after-shopping tiredness. Costco forever!

That was a list of my bests, I look forward to more advice from you. What are some products and services that changed your life as you know it? Tell me in the comments.

title: Prose Poetry — The Past and I
content:
Prose Poetry — The Past and I

I march on, thinking of my past as “I”. I hold on to it, like it means everything.

Memories, family, home. A little bit of past as if it is a real place somewhere far away. As if I could take a bus and go, I just have to tell my boss and set an auto-reply at work. Then, I can go.

I will meet my friends, it will be just like old days. I will sit at the porch with my mom, we will talk about that movie. As if all this will happen again.

I send a message, I like a photo. I write“sorry I missed your wedding",as if everything we have is not just the past. It has been 13 years. It could be 30 more.

A real look into morrow…

Work, dinner, I have to fix the plumbing. Where are all the people I was sure I’d see again?

Why was I?

title: Toastmasters is Something You Should Definitely Have Heard About
content:
Toastmasters is Something You Should Definitely Have Heard About

If you haven’t already, now is a good time.

It’s a funny name -Toastmasters. What does it even mean? Does it have anything to do with breakfast? Is it a Game-show likeWho’s the next millionairebut all questions are about different kinds of toasts? The questions are all valid. To not keep the suspense brimming — It is an international public speaking club. What then is all the “Toast” about?!

The “Toast” in toastmasters refers to the raising of glasses before a special event (Like a wedding) where you drink to the honor of someone or something. Sometimes it is as simple as “To our friendship!” and sometimes it is a speech serenaded byclink-clink-clinkon the brim of a chic glass of champagne. That’s where the name Toastmasters comes in. It prepares you for occasions in life where you are required to say something sensible to a listening audience and keep them interested while uttering adequate sense and emotion to the occasion.

If questions have already cropped up in your mind about whether the club is for a target group that mesmerizes an audience for an election ballot, or sells theatre tickets — well that’s not wrong but it is more than that. The goal of Toastmasters culture is to give the “common man” an environment to speak on a dais and grow as a speaker — both in small and intimate circles like home and family; and in large circles- like work and may be, an audience of a million as an enormous holographic presenter that is taking over the world ;).

The goal of Toastmasters culture is to give the “common man” an environment to speak on a dais and grow as a speaker

There are places you learn to paint, to dance, to do math and to program computers. Why then, is it conveniently ignored, to give a man a stage to learn to speak? Given that the mouth is one of the most widely used organs in modern professional and personal life, isn’t it imperative that one cultivates the skill of speech in an organized and loving environment?

I for one, badly need a stage to fail, get back up, be mentored, go back out there and speak, speak more, and speak a lot. Makes my life outside of the stage much easier.

How do you find these people? Well lucky for you, it is an international organization. So If you live in a decent-sized city, and you google this stuff (or go to toastmasters.org) you will likely find a handful of clubs that will be happy to have you. If you live in Ingolstadt (Bavaria, Germany), and you are looking for a club in the local language (which is German with a tinge of Bavarian accent) You’ve struck gold because I personally know the club there and here is how you get in touch:

Lucky for me, I love the spotlight and easily found myself a toastmasters club and started my speaking journey. If you are shy or would rather gladly swallow a knife than talk, this is probably your 911 call before that fright costs you a promotion at work.

Find yourself a club and start talking. If you make just one speech this year, it is more than 0 speeches you made last year. Good luck and have fun talking!

I am Sruthi Korlakunta, living in Ingolstadt since 2019. I work full-time for Audi as a Data Analyst.

If you moved here recently, Welcome to Ingolstadt. Feel free to get in touch onLinkedIn. If you want to be a part of Medium,join here.

If you are new to the city, check outhttps://ingolstadtexpats.com/for some great contacts in the city. It is a blog run by and for expats in the town and has some great content to get started

title: Sunday Neurosis: Content Consumption Excess and Existential Vacuum
content:
Sunday Neurosis: Content Consumption Excess and Existential Vacuum

From Viktor Frankl’s epic creation: Man’s Search for Meaning

Ever wondered why you have this urge to click on the “Next Episode” button so hard, though you don’t really want to? Even though your eyes tire before your mind?

Can you “enjoy” your day off, not thinking about anything, not doing your taxes, not having planned a day out with your friends, not having invited anyone, not watching Netflix, not practicing a hobby, and not cleaning up?

A couple of weeks ago I started to worry about this need to occupy myself with hobbies, writing, fitness, and whatnot. “Why? What if I don’t ?”. That was when this book “Man’s Search for Meaning”, by Viktor Frankl, a Holocaust survivor, came to my rescue. This book explains a lot about having to binge-watch than it appears at first sight.

Frankl’s philosophy

From his experiences in concentration camps of Auschwitz and Dachau for 4 years, Frankl observed that the inmates with a perceived purpose, such as prospect of reuniting with their child or their beloved after the war, or to finish their lifetime dreams such as their doctoral research, survived the camp by clinging on to every atomic chance for survival they could get.

This was despite their physical frailties compared to those who had the faculties to muster the muscle strength to outlast the atrocities imposed on them by the SS.

Inmates with a perceived purpose have survived the camp clinging on to every atomic chance for survival they could get

Intellectual and philosophical strength, according to him, played as important a role in survival as physical if not more.

The prisoners came to be able to tell when an inmate was about to die in the coming week. They reacted to nothing, not threats and persuasion by fellow inmates, or blows by the camp guards. Theygave upand traded their last morsels of bread with “luxury” goods like cigarettes, which was their way of “enjoying” their last minutes, knowing they mentally decided to die.

Another example of thissense of purposesaving a life, I found in the bookNightby Elie Wiesel. It is one of the most potent Holocaust survivor’s memoirs I have ever read.

In this book, Wiesel talks about Moishe the beadle. He is taken to a concentration camp and manages to escape after feigning death while digging a mass grave. He comes back home to Transylvania to warn the unsuspecting jews at home about the atrocities of the SS, of killing factories and mass graves and to plead them to immediately leave town before being taken.

This gave him a sense of purpose — to save his people — which in turn gave him the strength to crawl his way back though he was already nearly a corpse himself.

When his town’s people do not believe that his stories are true and refuse to evacuate, Moshie loses his sense of purpose and dies.

This alternate theory that life needs a sense of purpose eased my mind. Frankl’s school of philosophy states that whether there is or isn’t an objective meaning to life itself, what keeps a man sane is the sense of purpose he creates for himself — Something he looks forward to in order to wake up hopefully and purposefully into another day.

Existential Vacuum

For the most part of history, people needed to occupy themselves with instinct. They needed to watch out for a bear, look for food or possibly run from something dangerous. Only since a couple of thousand years is this not the case.

21st Century Automation and organized society leave little or no need for your instinct to survive through life. This leaves one with a lot of time and need to make decisions that are not instinctive but learned.

This is a lot of intellectual pressure. So people tend to either of two things — Conformism (Doing what most others do) or Totalitarianism (Doing what one power-figure tells you to do). Both these tendencies leave you with a sense of loss of control and meaninglessness of life. this meaninglessness is what is called “Existential Vacuum”.

Sunday Neurosis and Content Consumption

A direct consequence of the existential vacuum is boredom or distress, two emotional extremes. More and more automation, the betterment of societies and organized planning have resulted in plenty of leisure time, which is a phenomenon new to the 21st century.

This causes what is called “Sunday Neurosis”, the sense of void felt at the end of a “purposeful” work-week, or more immensely after retirement. People then turn to various methods to fill this void. Such as Will to power (or Earning more money) or the will to pleasure (addiction to pornography, addictive Content consumption).

What is the solution?

Frankl ties everything together with responsibility. Responsibility is, according to him the maxim that allows you to exercise your sense of purpose. “live your life as if you have already lived it once and are doing it for a second time”, he says.

The solution is to be as aware as possible of what, to whom you are responsible. Meaning of life is a larger question everyone has to answer to himself.

A therapist might help you figure out what your sense of purpose might be, but asking a therapist for the purpose of your life according to Frankl would be like asking a chess player for the best move in the world.

The heavy task of defining and finding meaning for life rests on the shoulder of the owner of the life being lived.

Thanks for reading Writers Guild — APennamepublication

Share your stories onManyStories.comto reach more readers.Auto-tweet your stories on repeatwith Signal to increase engagement.

title: Statistics and It’s Love-Hate Relationship with Science
content:
Statistics and It’s Love-Hate Relationship with Science

Statistical significance, null hypothesis, and illusions

Why does the toothpaste commercial want you to know that 9 out of 10 dentists approve their product?

This article addresses statistical significance, a popular convention in modern empirical science and statistics.

Although numbers form the heart of statistics, only when an interpreter converts the problems of math to usable formulations in language, it can be put to any real use. This makes statistics a double-edged sword. On the one hand, numbers have a concrete meaning. One slip of interpretation, they turn your world upside down.

If you are a business intelligence expert, you definitely have experienced it at work. Absolute numbers tell you less than percentages and percentages tell you less than the percentages compared to last year.

The numbers don’t change, but the story you tell with them does every time you see a new perspective.

In a way, you can be glad because numbers can be automated but interpretations as of now, need human finesse. But human finesse is associated with a multitude of cognitive biases[Thinking Fast and Slow, Daniel Kanheman].Though heavily mathematically equipped, we are not wired to be mathematical creatures. we as of now don’t know how to feel 38% happy with something[Fooled by randomness, Naseem Nicolas Taleb]. We are either happy or sad.

The problem with Empirical Sciences

Empirical sciences and statistics have a love-hate relation with each other. Empirical scientists are eager to achieve the magic threshold of statistical significance to prove their propositions. Statistics on the other hand is a reluctant participant in establishing cause and effect.

For instance, statistics can measure a correlation of 100% between your toilet breaks and the number of goals scored against your favorite team. But it is merely an unwitting participant in your conclusion to never pee again.

While empirical scientists are eager to prove and disprove theories and toss them into black and white piles, only the white pile grows larger each day. The “disproves” don’t get a paper to their name because it is unexciting to read a paper titled “The color of your car is irrelevant to your probability of getting cancer”.

On the other hand, it is very exciting to publish a statistical significance report on how every time Miley Cyrus drops a new single, the Himalayan tectonic plate moves a centimeter closer to Eurasia. (This is not a fact)

Statistical significance and the null hypothesis

What is “Statistically Significant”?

If you are testing the impact of a cause on an effect by observation, a statistically significant proof is when there is substantial evidence from observations that the observed “effect” is not by mere chance.

Say you are testing a drug for causing happiness. You want to claim that the drug is effective. So you will start by saying “Assume that the drug has no impact on happiness whatsoever”. And then you will go on to prove that this is not correct.

For you to claim that your drug is effective, you have to prove that

There were enough number of days where you were happy after taking the drug

There are enough days you were NOT happy when you did not take the drug.

The “enough” number is the statistical significance threshold. If the opposite is true, that is if the drug actually has no bearing on your happiness, it established thenull hypothesis.

How do we measure statistical significance?

The value measured to prove statistical significance is called the p-value. It is the probability value of the observed results, assuming that the null hypothesis is true. The threshold accepted in the research community for a statistically significant result is P ≤ 0.05.

Is it a god-given number found in nature? Is it an observed value, like acceleration due to gravity =9.8 m/s²?, likethe meaning of life = 42? (Hitchhiker’s Guide to the galaxy, cheers if you got the reference). This value, like many things in statistics, wasestablished, I assume, per intuition by F.A Fischer in the early twentieth century.

Let’s see how it works:

Consider the following excellent example by Jordan Ellenberg. for you to check for both conditions 1 and 2 above for your drug, you take 100 bunnies and test their happiness after giving 50 of them the drug and 50 of them a placebo. But it is not enough to show that the 50 drug-bunnies are happy. You should also show that the non-drug bunnies are relatively unhappy.

Imagine that out of 50non-drugbunnies, in 5 days, 45 are happy. the probability of finding a happy bunny if the drug would have no effect at all after 5 days (as is the case in the non-drug bunnies), is therefore 45/50 = 90%. Now look in your drug-bunny lot. You see that all bunnies are happy.

The probability that a bunny is happy when one does nothing is 90% of the total, as observed from the placebo lot.

The probability that a second bunny is also happy is 90% of the remaining 90%, 0.9*0.9 = 0.81

The probability that the next bunny is also happy is 90% of the remaining, 0.9*0.9*0.9, and so on

With each new happy bunny, the probability that the next bunny is happy falls. The probability, therefore, of all bunnies being happy is (0.9) multiplied to itself 50 times, 0.005, which is one in 200 times. The observed number of 50 out of 50 happy bunnies is, therefore, an event of slim probability under the assumption of the null hypothesis. Thus you statistically proved that your drug has an impact. The tinier your p-value, the happier you can be.

Can you trust statistical significance?

The above example is more than convincing. If your results are that glaringly significant, maybe it means that they are correct. Then what is all the noise about?

Charles Darwin himself mused that if his observations about evolution are a mere chance, It would be a wonder that so many observations in the natural world fit his theory.

Unfortunately, this convention of meeting the statistical significance threshold has caused a furor in many scientific communities. Statistical significance is only as powerful an instrument as the data set it measures.

If a bigger dataset or a larger demographic or a different area is chosen, it is very likely that the significance results differ vastly. At the same time, It is very unlikely that some cases like drug tests will even reveal a null hypothesis. The odds that the null hypothesis is exactly found are very less when you massively alter your body chemistry with a drug.

In the words of Jordan Ellenberg, it is more likely that something will cause or prevent cancer than do nothing! this extreme unlikelihood of the null hypothesis leaves many loopholes for desperate researchers to botch results using clever language.

As a result, it is technically scientific to splash tomatoes against a wall and tell the future with the patterns they make because you had it correct about 100 times. The 1000 tomato-splashers who don’t get it right don’t write a paper. If a thousand tomato splashers try splashing tomatoes a thousand times, it is not a wonder if one gets the future right 100 times by mere chance.

Does it mean that all scientists false-report?

So are we to believe that scientists and statisticians are unfaithful to their science? Is there no true scientific inquiry?

To be fair, science and philosophy share one common pitfall. Both the scientist and the philosopher believe that they are in pursuit of the truth. But it is very easy to stray from the path and end up pursuing evidence for what one already believes is the truth without realizing it.

Add to it the pressure of having to publish for your employer, publish for your sponsor, find evidence for something (anything!), the stringent course of time experiments need for validating, and the possibility that the research is outdated in the market after the n years it takes to do it. Truth is by no means a trivial pursuit.

As a reader, it is essential to know that one has to take results and their meaning with a pinch of salt. Accept that hardly anything is black-and-white. The more parameters involved in coming to a conclusion, the better. But the more parameters involved, the more complex is the question and its less it’s ability to be answered by observation alone. It is also a great practice to look for the repeatability of the results. Did 10 other researchers find the same thing? did 10 researchers all come from the same demographic and time-period? Such are the questions you should ask to make your interpretation of results more meaningful.

title: 5 Songs with Genius Lyrics Not About Love or Relationships
content:
5 Songs with Genius Lyrics Not About Love or Relationships

Because life has so much more on offer.

I have nothing against love or romance. Everything we care about is just proof that we are not just futile little robots running around until our time is up. Love and romance too, are among them.

When I was younger, the prospect of finding love seemed to be all-consuming. Poets, philosophers, movies, and music did nothing to change this.

As I am turning 30, I see that love (romantic or otherwise) is just another thing you feel. Like joy, wonder, anger, dejection, or a million other things which have not been named, love is just there, not sitting on any high throne.

Consequently, I have respect for music that talks about diverse themes. Here is a beautiful playlist of non-love songs whose text is mesmerizing.

1. Ninety Miles — Emisunshine

Emisunshine is a 16-year-old Tennessee singer-songwriter. She rose to popularity when a video of her singingBlue Yodelat a Flee market received a million views.

In her 2017 AlbumRagged Dreams, Emisunshine wrote a song called Ninety Miles, singing from the perspective of her Autistic friend. The song moved me to tears.

The lyrics seem to come from a mind far beyond Emi’s age. Her grainy voice fills your head with the loneliness of a misfit. The song embodies the passive struggle in a child’s mind, painted in the simplest words.

Ninety miles is the first song in years that had my respect and attention from the very first word. It is on the top of my list.

2. The Show — Lenka

I came acrossThe Showthrough Brad Pitt’s 2011 movieMoney Ball. The song was like a splash of cold water on the face.

Simple, unassuming, indifferent to the vast meaninglessness of the world, it talks about a girl who has figured out that life is a joke being played for us and we all got tickets. We are there tojust enjoy the show.

The show seems like a country song because of its storytelling, acoustic guitar, and folk undertones, but it also has some klezmer and Balkan music aura to it.

The show is a refreshingly brilliant perspective on life at large with a touch of childish innocence.

3. 500 Miles

If you have a love for the road, if you are an “ Into the wild” type, if you leave home in search of a bigger life, If you are just anywhere in the world with a bit of home in your heart, then 500 miles is you.

It was difficult for me to figure out the origins of the song. My favorite version is that of Justin Timberlake, posted here. The original version, according toWikipedia, is from The Proclaimers in 1988, but there are sources that say otherwise.

The text of the song is minimalistic yet resourceful for the vivid pictures it paints.

If you missed the train I’m onYou will know that I am goneYou can hear the whistle blow a hundred miles

500 Miles is a beautiful song. It is hope for starting anew, a call to adventure, andhumanfor thinking of home.

4. El Condor Pasa (If I could) — Simon and Garfunkel

El Condor Pasa is a melody originally composed in Peru. It has since been used, written to, and sung thousands of times in hundreds of versions. Mine isIf I could, the cover by Simon and Garfunkel.

The song is in the spirit of freedom. Freedom from the rut of life and from the artificial world.

Away, I’d rather sail awayLike a swan that’s here and goneA man gets tied up to the groundHe gives the world its saddest sound

Its strongest lines sound eerie and mysterious in the hands of Simon and Garfunkel. The simplistic affirmations like “I’d rather be a forest than a street” make the song congenial and give it a child-like friendliness.

It rightly features in the Reese Witherspoon adventure “Wild”, the version posted here.

5. Space Oddity — David Bowie

Ground control to Major Tom

The iconic starting lines of Space Oddity were inspired by another Genius, Stanley Kubrik’sSpace Odessey. It is the story of a fictional astronaut Major Tom in space.

5 days after the song made its debut, Neil Armstrong stepped on the moon for the first time. In 2013, 44 years after its first recording, Canadian astronaut Chris Hadfield played the song on the International Space Station, making the first video in space.

The song is also a great story. If you seek adventure and enigma in life, but you're sick of stories of miracles and religion, Space Oddity is a refreshing respite.

title: I’m glad it all worked out for you, Aravind.
content:
I’m glad it all worked out for you, Aravind.

I worked at an MNC in India before I moved out. The difference between treatment of local and offshore employees of the same job title was palpable, both in terms of pay and respect.

I am sure there is progress. But it still makes me wonder why so many young Indians emigrate towards the west and not the otherway round

(of course there are people coming to india from the west, but I’m guessing not because of “better opportunities and better air”.)

There certainly are ways to find a good life in India, but undeniably, there are cultural nuances that suit individual liberty better in the west.

You might be of a different opinion, and your opinion may be right for you too. But the fact remains that emigration is a hard option many are still actively choosing for a reason, like you did when you left to the USA.

title: A Key Quality You Need to Keep Your Dignity
content:
A Key Quality You Need to Keep Your Dignity

Self-Effacement makes people respect and trust you subconsciously.

There is one key skill I noticed in the most powerful manager I worked with in my career. Though I was not very happy with the nature of my job, I stayed on only to learn from this charismatic genius.

The skill is called Self-effacement, the quality of not claiming attention for oneself.

How Self-Effacement Looks

I was a freelance analyst with this boss (who I shall not name) who “ran the show”. After working with him for a year I realized that there was no decision in the office without him moving the pawns. He wasn’t even the business owner. He made key insights available to upper management and caused every major push inside this organization. He had no friends or enemies.

What surprised me the most about this person was, how long it took for me to realize that he was that important.

What surprised me the most about this person was, how long it took for me to realize that he was that important.

Then I realized, this was by design. Heintendedto stay that way and actively made me believe that he was a “normal” person. This made my affinity to him grow and easy for the employees to approach him.

He followed the principle of “show, don’t tell”. It made me trust his judgment easily. This principle has an immense impact on the listener because humans don’t like to be told, they like to interpret.

They believe the conclusions they draw for themselves instead of those which others hand them.

Self-Effacement in Journalism

I noticed that all the people I love to watch on TV like Jimmy Kimmel, Trevor Noah, and Jimmy Fallon are extremely self-effacing. They are awesome without telling us that they are awesome.

I also noticed another comedian who I also love, John Oliver, is actually not that self-effacing. He often draws attention to himself by making himself the butt of the joke. Though it is intended only to be fake-modest, those are the cringiest moments for me on the (otherwise brilliant) show Last Week Tonight.

Rosemary Horstmann, in her book Writing for Radio (A & C Black), explains:

‘The reporter has to use his skill with words and set a scene and bring it to life for the listener and then self-efface himself by eliciting as much of the story as possible from the key people involved.”

The more stage given to story, the more stage given to the OTHER person in the room, the more it enhances your own importance and role. The most important people usually don’t draw attention to themselves because they are confident in their shoes. This automatically translates to “Classy” behavior.

Conclusion: Self efface.

Here is a tip,

Suppress the urge to make yourself the hero of the show. Listen, give thoughts and ideas the respect they deserve, and give credit and respect where it is due. Don’t be fake.

Put your “self” at the bottom of that ladder, but not because you are ashamed and scared.

This is a sure-fire way to win respect and credibility.

I am Sru. If you have an idea or a story to share,hit me up! I will give your story the voice it deserves.

title: Python Clean Project Structure —  Everything You Need
content:
Python Clean Project Structure —  Everything You Need

Clean Project Structuring is vital as clean code

Don't underscore module names. If you must create submodules, best separate them into subfolders and access them with the dot operator.

When possible make namespaces visible

Get a virtual environment for all your dependencies

Do all your pip installs inside this venv or use the version of python you like. You can deactivate and jump to a different env whenever you want

3. get requirement specifiers using pip freeze

4. Get a version control system : GIT or something

5. Directory structure needs to be taken care of:

modules are .py files

a package is a folder with __init__.py and other python modules

There should be a test and documentation folder

6. Get a distribution system up and running : distutils with python

or setup tools (that comes with venv package)

7. Take care of delivering updated versions. use the __version__ attribute in your __init__.py file eachtime you ship a new package and argument called version in setup.py or use the git package versioneer to autoupdate all changes

Modules should have short, all-lowercase names. Underscores can be used in the module name if it improves readability. Python packages should also have short, all-lowercase names, although the use of underscores is discouraged.

We’ll get to what exactly modules and packages are in a moment, but for now, understand thatmodules are named by filenames, andpackages are named by their directory name.

In other words,filenames should be all lowercase, with underscores if that improves readability.Similarly,directory names should be all lowercase, without underscores if at all avoidable. To put that another way…

Just as Code Style, API Design, and Automation are essential for a healthy development cycle

Any directory with an__init__.pyfile is considered a Python package. The different modules in the package are imported in a similar manner as plain modules, but with a special behavior for the__init__.pyfile, which is used to gather all package-wide definitions.

It is possible to simulate the more standard behavior by using a special syntax of the import statement:from modu import *. This is generally considered bad practice.Usingimport *makes code harder to read and makes dependencies less compartmentalized.

Usingfrom modu import funcis a way to pinpoint the function you want to import and put it in the local namespace. While much less harmful thanimport *because it shows explicitly what is imported in the local namespace, its only advantage over a simplerimport moduis that it will save a little typing.

title: Note to God
content:
Note to God

I believe in you.

I believe in you.

I marvel how you created so many things which are this blessed and this much in pain all at the same time.

I know that I'm not the only one. I've seen better, I've seen worse. I've seen everything I needed to see, to believe that you, God, do not infact exist.

And yet, there are days where my completely undeserving fortune calls everything to question. You blessed (or cursed) me with the power of saying "why me?" at the most blessed moments, instead of at the cursed ones.

In retrospect the dots look connected. But you and I both know, god, that there are no dots to connect. They do not make a story, though I see it. Not any better than bunnies and bears that I see in cloud formations.

But yet you show me these shapes, and I eat them up. They're so good, I even believe them to be true.But it's not this magic that makes me believe in you. It is not my immense fortune, or the illusion of, that makes me say "wow, there really is God"

It is fear.

Not fear of burning hell, not the Satan or the consequences of not complying to the scripture. But the fear that every thing may infact have no sense or meaning, and the fear that you and your plan do not exist.

I lived long enough without that fear. I noticed that right and wrong doing aren’t by the scripture. I saw that cause and effect are not like you said in the book, or the sermon, or by karma. So I do not fear that you will be mad at me for doing wrong. Your anger has nothing to do with what people do or do not.

This fear stems from all the alternate fears that are real, not imagined. That the misfortune that you plagued me with will infact leave me alone while craving love and camaraderie through that ever present loneliness.

If I do not believe in you, I am presented with the very real fear of being too small for a world that is too big, too fast, too random and too lonely. No matter who I meet, how far I run, and where I go, I am left with that loneliness and it will not go away. It is with this fear that I embrace the idea that you may exist.

I tried running away, literally. I tried sleeping on the foothills of mountains being entirely self sufficient, not needing a single other soul to fulfill me. I biked, worked, tried to give without asking for anything in return, I did everything they said would fulfill me. But around all that is a loud and terrifying loneliness that is way too justified and way too real.

No matter how much my heart yearns to trust and believe, I find myself alone and like a child that wakes up in darkness, I have an unreasonable fear of what I don't know, don't have and can't have. So I tried a thought experiment today.

I believed that you exist. That I need not run away, or run towards anything. I could picture you there, and me writing these notes to you. I pictured that you were actually reading them and understanding them like a human would. And that you are just there. It was immensely comforting. Because no body can take you away. You know why? Because you don't really exist, you cannot be taken away.

So thank you God, for always being there for me, though you do not exist. Please give me the strength to continue to look into nothing and believe that there is something — you. Thank you for everything and thank you for nothing.

title: Dealing with Dislike — Stoicism Didn’t Help
content:
Dealing with Dislike — Stoicism Didn’t Help

Blame, block, or suffer?

I learnt a trick over the past two years.

If I can pretend like something doesn’t exist long enough, it will eventually go away!

It seemed like a brilliant idea.

I was having a hard time with my flat-mate who seemed to love to turn everything into a contest. Like this:

“I buy only all natural-eggs, what do you do for health?”**scorns at me**

“I have 300 over-hours at work, how many do you have?”**Actually waits patiently for me to answer**

“I bought a house in my hometown because I save so hard, that you buy all the toilet paper”**Umm, okay now**

Every. Single. Conversation.

I decided ignoring his existence long enough will make this annoyance go away. And it did go away. Only, it did not give me a sense of “done and dusted”.

I felt likeThe unbreakable Kimmy Schmidtwho used to close her eyes, put her hands up and jump up and down screaming“I’m not here! I’m not here! I’m not here!”until she believes it.

Hilarious and it works. But it is not right. Why?

In a corner of my head, I felt bad for this small-town boy who felt the need to constantly feel superior and needed all of us to confirm it.

I felt like I was one of those bullies in middle school who didn't want to play with little me.

He couldn’t really help it any more than I can help thinking he was ridiculous.

By ignoring him, I was actually not passively protecting myself, but actively hurting his self-esteem, which was not even the tenth of my intention.

All I wanted was peace. Now I didn’t have it.

Stoicism failed me

I tinkered with the idea of “ignore or accept” quite a bit. Ignoring was definitely working — very selfishly speaking.

There are more things around that you cannot deal with than those you can.

Just because one of those“undealable-with”things waltzed into your personal space, doesn't mean you suddenly love it. Or that you are capable of accepting it over-night.

My Plan A to deal with the whole thing was the stoic philosophy of Markus Aurelius that I found inRyan Holiday’sBlog post. Until I realized that Markus Aurelius wanted to be God. I didn’t want to be God. I wanted to be comfortable with my human shittniess. Iknowit is not going anywhere.

The human being is inherently flawed, forever changing and forever hating whatis. Because “good” changes. Priorities change. There is no end to this tinkering with the “best”.

Markus Aurelius set at a “best” rational standard defined by the logos (Nature), and “The Gods”.

There are two reasons why this doesn’t work.

First, none of us is rational. Not me, not my roommate, not you, and not even Ev Williams.

Second, Rationality is the largest dogma I know of. “This is logical, so it is true” makes for wonderful science and hateful people. Because logic itself, is shrouded in prior knowledge, and we don't know everything there is to be known. We never will.

I love logic. If I want to see a rocket take off, I’d follow the logic. But people are not rockets, people are animals.

It is only after I already “believe” that there is a good and a bad outcome, and logic leads me to what is good.

So Markus Aurelius’ philosophy was not without its setbacks. If he was still around, I’d send him a strongly worded letter about rationality.

Coming back to being human

By now, I know that I am going to dislike things forever, I am going to find faults forever, I am going to introspect and I am going to repeat. None of the things, for which stoicism has acceptance, by the way.

All the while you try to be “the bigger man” as defined by Markus, you are going to “admit” that you are better. That the other will suffer in the consequences of their own shittiness and you have to “continue to” be righteous.

You are going to have to ignore many things and keep moving, pretending to be a stone-pokemon, toughening up your shell because you are perfect. or.

OR?

Or you find acceptance. Assume, for a second, that this idea is a freely-floating dandelion about a few inches from your forehead. You don’t necessarily have to jump up, catch it, and make it yours. You don’t have to love it but you also don’t have to hate where it came from. You can acknowledge your ignorance and its existence.

The dandelion is already in the air. That is a fact.

Why not just look at it and let it go? Why not accept that it is there, look at how it makes you feel, and as a rule, let nothing come out of your mouth that assumes superiority?

But you are going to fail. You are going to say dishonorable things about other people who you deem illogical.

Accept this too,because what else are you going to do?

To be honest, I have not found a way to talk to my falt-mate without puckering my brow and finding the “least resistance path” to the kitchen and bathroom to minimize encounters. I did not find acceptance for his ideas. I don’t think I ever will.

But what I did find acceptance for, was my inability to deal with this, without assuming moral superiority. I will never be able to buy into his ideas. However, humanizing my inability has been extremely helpful.

I don’t want to cause harm, but I did. Now, I am going to live with it.

If it comes back again, I still don’t know how to deal with the problem. I will perhaps still keep away, but this time, I’ll acknowledgemyinability to understand,not his.

I’ll acknowledge that I am going to be imperfect tonnes of times to come. And that this person, is an image of my own shittiness.

To be honest, it did not change anything outside. It did not change what the other person did or felt about me. But it did help how I felt about “the other” and about myself.

Try it. Go on and be shitty and carry it on your shoulders before rubbing it on someone else’s face.
