title: A Series of Good Bosses Changed My Entire Life
content:
A Series of Good Bosses Changed My Entire Life

If you see bosses like this, stay longer.

In my career of 10 years, I’ve had many bosses. Three of them changed my life for good.

No, not by paying me exorbitant amounts of money, but by being excellent at their job, yet so kind that I couldn’t believe my eyes and luck. Leaders are important. And I don't mean this lightly. Good leaders make good families, communities, cities, and even countries. It all trickles down.

Growing up, I only had leaders whose only goal was to be a leader desperately. It didn’t matter what made everyone in the team happy, what was more productive, or what achieved the best results. It was their word or none at all.

The funny part is, until I met a good boss, I did not know the meaning of a bad one. Now, my brain automatically disposes of the comments of a bad boss and lets me focus on the right thing. It is easy to tell a bad leader.

How you can tell a bad leader:

They are easily “hurt” by your disobedience.

Every discussion you make is a defense against a “No” that comes automatically from the leader.

They win, no matter what. When push comes to shove, they leave or let go of people than finding a solution.

They don’t care if someone fails, even on their own team.

They spend more time being the boss than understanding the goals and working towards them.

One doesn’t have to be an employee to be a boss. Even families, groups of 2, group of friends, and even a collection of strangers (like in a mob) has a leader. It is biological, automatic, and even natural to look for someone to follow.

Being a follower doesn't mean you are a loser. Being a leader doesn’t mean you have to win all the time.

Here is a story of three bosses I worked for. I understood the true meaning of leadership only when I met these people. It changed my perspective on the world and made me want to be a better employee, and most importantly, a better person.

Boss 1 — I’ll Call him S

S was my boss when I was a student. Fresh from India, having just arrived in a new country, I was still lamb-like at work. I had just started learning Python (because he told me it would help), and straight into the world of deep learning.

I was under the impression that work is everything in life. I showed up during my sick days, never took holidays, and never said “No”.

S let me be. He trusted me with all the equipment I needed to work. He enquired about

Boss 2 — I’ll call her M

Boss 3 — I’ll call him H

title: I am Sick of Environmental Issues —  Fight Me
content:
I am Sick of Environmental Issues — Fight Me

The unfair standards of the Western world

Dear Wealthy Countries of the West,

You said that I should bring my own cloth bag to the supermarket. So I did. Then you bombed two entire countries this year with ultra-modern fighter jets and missile tech. (Did you power those with renewable electricity or fossil fuels?)

I grew up in India. (No, I’m not whining, I’m telling you, FYI!) All my life, my family of four shared ONE motorcycle, sometimes, all of us were on it at the same time. We showered in water portioned in plastic buckets and heated over a stove, so yes, now I shower longer than you. We bought our vegetables in cloth bags (not because it was the hip thing to do) and ate meat once a week at best, sometimes less. We rarely bought items in plastic packaging (except for the fancy Frito Lays chips you sent us). We had one set of glass containers for all our life and we filled our peanuts, lentils, spices, and snacks in those. We represented an average family in the 1990s and the early 2000s, and yet, our land is drowning in plastic now.

Why do we have all that plastic, you ask? It is all yours. It is USA’s, Canada’s, Germany’s, and wherever else in the world that preaches and does not practice because they can afford it.

So people like me wandered off. In search of cleaner land, cleaner air, more money, more resources, and taps that ran water all day.Now that we have made it, you want us to stop.

You want us to stop buying plastic,but that’s all you sell.Unlike locals with generational wealth and a huge family network, every piece of stuff I own, I have bought with money I made with my own two hands. My meager resources.

Every last object in my household, from a ballpoint pen and clothing to the ultra fancy Alexa devices you sold me, I paid for with money I made since I turned 23. If a lot of that is plastic, it is because you made better options unaffordable to people like me.

Why on earth does a bamboo basket cost 25 Eur in this world, but a plastic basket 3 Eur? Why do you let down people who are genuinely trying?I’ll tell you why, it's because your entire plan is to dump them back at my home.

Every time I go to the supermarket for a bottle of chili, I get a new glass and a new plastic top. Every time I want green peas, they come in an icky plastic bag that has to rot in my freezer before it is sent to MY home for Christmas dumping. I don’t want them! I don’t want any of this plastic in my household. I don’t want to fill your yellow recycle bags. Just because Germany is extremely good at recycling does not mean that you have to keep using plastic each time you want a spoon of peas.

Asking people to stop using plastic bags is the most ridiculous thing in the world.

No matter what we do, we silly individuals are only pleasing our own souls by taking out that little cloth bag. The scale of the problem and the solution is larger than we can ever comprehend or solve.

It should not be up to my volition to save the world, it should be YOUR policy!

If you really have the intention to make it better, make trains cheaper (it costs me less than one-third of the train to make the journey with a car, also your train systems are a joke.)

The moment I can afford a car, you want to make it electric and unaffordable. And what electric solutions do you provide for people just beginning to get wings, like myself? e-scooters (another massive environmental disaster).

Everything is getting cheaper because the East can make it for you. Everything is becoming plastic because the East can dump it for you. Emission standards are a joke because the worst type gets sold to us and to Africa.

This is not how you pretend to solve environmental concerns. Unless you make a serious attempt, stop asking me to shower shorter, take the bus, bring a cloth bag, and not put my computer on standby. You have lost the right.

title: Immigration: Why Moving Back to India is so Hard
content:
Immigration: Why Moving Back to India is so Hard

It’s been eight years since I moved to Germany. I’m officially German now. As time passes I am beginning to realize the possibility that I might need to move there for my elderly parents, knowing it isn’t going to be one bit easy

I look around for examples of people who have moved back. I only know those who talk about it. Few have the courage, means, and most importantly, both.

Why does anybody want to move back?

The shortest answer is family.

Many rest easy telling themselves that it is ok, pragmatic even, to choose your life over your parent’s emotional needs.

Not visiting your parents often is an unsavory guilt also in the west, let alone in a culture as closely-knit as India. Once you move somewhere longer than about 3 hours of flying distance, you are definitely absent from your parents. Technology can do a lot in bridging this gap, but not everything and not enough.

The second reason to move back is that the country needs you.

The feeling thatif all the potential CEOs and innovators leave, what happens to India then?Will we always produce cheap clothing and provide customer support as a nation?

Suppose you never moved back after experiencing the good parts of other cultures. How will the changeable in the country change only by adopting ideas shallowly depicted on the media as “progressive”?

This is not to condescend to the country and its culture. It is merely a fact that meeting people from over the world adds value to your life and the perspective you bring to your country.

Future or present?

Choosing between a developed and a developing world feels like choosing between your children (or the children you will have) and your parents.

The life the children could have, the opportunities, and access to an international environment which is easier to find in the developed world.

It is easy to overlook the present in which your parents are eagerly waiting for those two weeks a year when you visit.

The rush of life around immigrants and their sky-high aspirations make one believe that it is “okay” that you should choose your future, not your past.

What if they do move back?

If young parents choose to move back to India, their kids will have a bigger family and a network of people. They will continue contacts that have formed, in some cases, over many decades or even generations.

In contrast, most immigrants live as individual entities in the west with friends who come and go. Somewhere deep down, they are “nobody" but respected taxpayers and breadwinners. Their economic turnover mostly defines them and their purpose.

Besides, if the parents do move back, the odds that their children will leave for the more-developed west eventually looms. It has to be either themselves or their parents who suffer from the separation of a child from a parent.

…Or don't?

If the parents choose not to move back, their kids — the first-generation Indian Germans (or Indian-anybody’s) will be the first ones to build long-term connections in the new land.

Such connections are only possible by growing up in a place and knowing it like your territory. They form only by watching a friend become a full person from scratch.

On top of having the luxury of “mentally belonging," children of immigrants have the opportunity — An opportunity for which the parents immigrated in the first place. It is for them is no longer a golden deer to be chased but a life taken for granted.

Career and Competition in India

India is hands-down a country full of brilliant young people with diverse skill sets, especially in the IT sector. India’s work ethic also fosters a world with constant (healthy and unhealthy) competition, which might make for a stressful workplace if you are unlucky.

European culture offers a more balanced outlook on work-life. Also, other developed countries outside of Europe have standards in place to protect their workers against racism, sexism, ageism, mobbing, and multiple other “-isms." These standards are often overlooked in a vast country like India, where a strict hierarchy is the Gold-Standard.

A smaller number of applicants for job positions in the west also makes your role more valuable to your company than if you were easily “replaceable" as an employee.

It is hard to turn your back on a life where peace at work is easy to find. You should not always have to run for your money. Work deserves respect where it is due.

Convenience and health in India vs. the West

I was never a sporty person growing up. I grew up being on the heavier side of the scale. I remember constantly innovating ways to work out, and they all ended up in a gym or another studio room.

Physical activity out in the open was nearly impossible in Hyderabad, my city of 10 million, with no cycle paths or roads to run.

This is a luxury I shamelessly enjoy having in Germany. I like that I can hike in a forest a 10-minute drive from home. Staying fit isn’t even difficult, I only have to choose it. I miss this in India. I want a good life also outside of a gated community.

I watched Hyderabad grow less and less green every year since I was born, hurting me. I feel helpless against this change. But it also pains me that my parents have no choice but to live with it.

Knowing what I know now, the only solution to this seems to “be rich." Unfortunately, it is not an option all of us are born with.

I have the luxury of having independent parents who are highly educated and can find their way without their children needing to lead the way. I imagine the decision is harder for those who must be there for their parents AND need the money.

Moving back is a hard decision. Moving forward is equally brutal.

If you related with this article, you will perhaps relate to this too:

I am Sruthi Korlakunta. If you have an idea or a story to share,hit me up! I will give your story the voice it deserves.

Join medium with my referral link for all you can read and write. It costs 5$ a month and changed my life for the better.

title: 5 Books That Will Make You Smarter Immediately
content:
5 Books That Will Make You Smarter Immediately

Improve the level of your thinking and conversation with these nonfiction science books.

Sometimes, the smarter thing to do is to shut up.

You must have seen people around you fall apart arguing which religion is better, what is nature or nurture, and what is good or bad.

Here is a band of books where you can find your concrete, state-of-the-art, fuzzy answers to difficult questions on the subjects of religion, cognition, morality, human psychology, and behavior.

The following books are all works of award-winning scientists. They are not opinions, and when opinions are stated, they are not stated as facts.

1. The Selfish Gene, Richard Dawkins

The selfish gene has been hailed as the most influential science book of all time by the Royal Society of London.

The Selfish Gene takes a pan-genetic view of animal behavior (both human and non-human) and explains the world in terms of Game theory.

Cheating on your spouse? skewed sex ratios? why in some species do males sing and dance while in others it is the females? why some people are deceptive and some honest? why are some people altruistic and some people selfish?

Dawkins answers all the above questions by treating them merely as strategies in a game and assigning value scores to each action. He doesn't judge or comment on these actions, just explains them. The book is one of the most objective perspectives available on the entire world at large.

2. Behave by Robert Sapolsky

Behave is the most scientific book in the lot, and also the hardest to read. While the rest of the books in this series have been written in a noob-friendly tone without jargon, Sapolsky offers no consideration for the average reader.

Behave writes on different factors that influence human behavior from microseconds before the behavior occurs to a billion years before the creature evolved.

Every outcome in the book is backed by experiments in neuroscience, ethology, psychology, and statistics. Sometimes they wired up brains to see what’s inside, sometimes they looked at what the subjects did, and sometimes they asked them. There are all kinds of scientific testing involved.

After reading this book there were times when I could no longer perceive my happiness and sadness as my honest emotions.Instead, they felt like uncontrollable reactions of my body to some external cues

I felt like there was no free will, that I am just a dumb robot reacting to stuff outside.

3. Factfulness: Ten Reasons We’re Wrong About the World, Hans Rosling

I often come across people who ruminate about “the good old days” when the world was a better place and everything was honest and fair.(Wow! Did they ever pick up a book in history?).

It is a human habit to glorify everything in the past. Believe it or not, the world has gotten better for human beings.

In Factfulness Hans Rosling, the founder of Gap Minder finds hope for humanity with his statistics. He defines and justifies his metrics for measuring the world’s progress, showing how much it has changed in years and where we are headed.

The book is a ray of hope in the gloomy, saddening world of mainstream media.

4. The Bonobo and the Atheist, Frans De Waal

Ah, religion! The topic people either love to love or love to hate.

De Waal, despite being a non-believer himself, argues for the positive role of religion in the world and its role in morality. He observes what parts of human morality emerge from “external” factors like preaching and religion, and what parts are inherent to being biologically human.

He offers evidence of inherent empathy and morality which he observed in his years of primate research and his work with chimpanzees. The book is a refreshingly positive perspective on being human and helps you develop empathy for believers if you are a nonbeliever and vice versa.

5. Thinking Fast and Slow

This work by Daniel Kahneman is about the psychology behind economic decisions. Kahneman went on to win the Nobel prize for Economics.

WithThinking Fast and Slow, You will understand your reasoning behind various everyday decisions around money and risk, while Kahneman Pitts them against the “actual” logical thing to do. You might end up feeling very stupid, even if you are a statistician with years of experience behind you.

On the one hand, the book helps you understand the wiring of your brain (or the lack of it) and on the other, it teaches you how the market sets you up to fall for its tricks. It is a fun and interesting read.

Here are some more hand-curated books in this “Get-smart-quick” genre, if you are through with the list above (Which will take you a good while, by the way).

Our Inner Ape by Frans De Waal (Zoology)

Man’s Search for Meaning, Victor Frankl (Philosophy and psychology)

What the Dog Saw and Other Adventures, Malcolm Gladwell (Mixed bag of topics)

The Design of Everyday Things, Norman Donald (Engineering and Design by ex-Apple designer)

The Body, A guide for occupants, Bill Bryson (Biology)

Sapiens, Yuval Noah Harari (History)

Guns, Germs and Steel, Jared Diamond (History)

That’s all for now. I will definitely update this list in the future and I will pull all my hair out if it does not make you smarter ;)

title: Hair Secret That Instagram Models Won't Tell You
content:
Hair Secret That Instagram Models Won't Tell You

From yet another naturally curly girl with a lot of hair.

Ifhair is one of your greatest problems in life, congratulations! your other real problems are probably much smaller. I had curly hair all my life, and at a point in my life, I got sucked into the curly girl moment popularized by Instagram.

They were over-the-top times of my life. Though they gave me some tips, they had their quirks. Sometimes they reduced my entire life to my hair. Sometimes they made me happy and proud of my head. Here is what you need to know.

The Curly Girl Method

It is the 100th time you are seeing that ad in your feed.

One part of your heart knows this is going to be yet another gimmick for your 13$. You are way past saying “But 13$ is too much for shampoo!” Somewhere at the back, hope saysMaybe this is the magic product!

At the supermarket.

You walk through the hair-aisle slowly, intently scanning for familiar Instagram products. A bright purple package with “Curls” written in a big twirly beautiful font catches your eye. Again, you drop it into the shopping cart although“We still have a tonne of product at home”.

Welcome to the life of a naturally curly woman.

The real hair journey

I tried literally every hair thing there was to try on the internet.DIY?Been there.Silicone/Parabenfree? Done that. Oh, the trend is silicone again?“Yes sir! I am right behind you!”

For a long time, my hair played along. And boy did it play along! I felt like a luscious mermaid. I got the attention that my childhood military-boy-hairdo did not get me. I realized pretty women live in a different world. People bought the products I bought, strangers asked me who I am and where I come from.

Whether any of those products played a part, I cannot tell. Actually, I can. They did not. 3 years after leaving the zone, I am using my favorite drugstore shampoo and conditioner (Not silicone-free, by the way) and hair masks my mom taught me when I was 11. Nothing else really stuck.

Discoveries that actually helped

I cannot say that all the time spent on Instagram and Youtube pages was a waste of time.(Although it did waste some money, and time is money so money is time?).

I did learn a valuable few tips. Unfortunately for the influencers, none of them ask you to buy more products.

These things really worked to better my natural hair :

I stayed far from any combs. I actually don’t own a regular comb anymore, and that was a great idea. Just detangle in the shower with wet hair and conditioner.

Conditioner is good for dry hair.

Hair masks from your fridge and pantry > hair masks from a plastic/glass bottle. Go natural.

Don’t shampoo often. Once a week is enough number of times. An hour before that, slather your hair up in oil for extra protection. Most plant oils will do.

Don’t iron, don’t chemically treat it, don’t color (guilty).

Don’t. I repeat DONT stress yourself about frizz or imperfect hair days. It is just hair. While you are at it, don’t stress yourself on other things either.

Eat good things and sleep well.

That’s it. That is really all you have to do for caring well for your hair. (Unless you are hosting the Emmys and want to look impeccable. In which case, go practice your jokes,theywill do your hair for you!)

Also, do you notice something? All those tips make you a few dollars richer, not poorer.

Then, is social media lying?

Technically no. Those product-selling ladies probably really have the hair they show off. Good for them. But it isextremelyunlikely that all that was just the magic of the products they are selling.

Here is a story from my life. I know a girl from my college popular for her good skin. This was 8 years ago. Now she is an Instagram model selling skincare products. She recently got a golden youtube icon sent to her for her 100 thousandth subscriber. What do we learn from this?

They take to skin, hair, lips, waist, butt, other care because they had good skin, hair, lips, butt or other in the first place!

If you notice that you are good at something, you just do more of it. People selling products on social media are no different. Their products didn’t make their hair, they had good hair in the first place.

What should you do?

First, stop punching a hole in your pocket and be product-conservative. Choose one that you like and be done with it.

Use homemade masks and plant oils to show love for your hair once in a while, that should do it.

Eat well, sleep well, love your hair and yourself how you are. I am not repeating a cliche here. Really,accept yourself, like you accept your laptop without complaining, about its silvery body and pressy keypads.

After you did all that, chill out! It is going to be fine, its just hair!

I am Sruthi Korlakunta, read me onMedium, or write me onLinkedIn. I like hearing from people! yes, you too!
